proposals.title: Adaptive Graph Re-Encoding for Retrosynthetic Planning: Capturing Structural Drift in Monte Carlo Tree Search
proposals.motivation: Current retrosynthesis planners such as RetroGNN encode the target molecule graph once at the root of the search tree and reuse that fixed representation throughout all downstream expansions. As the search tree deepens and candidate intermediates diverge significantly from the original target, this static encoding becomes an increasingly poor proxy for the true synthesizability landscape. Addressing this representational staleness is critical for improving route quality in large-scale virtual screening campaigns where search trees routinely exceed dozens of branching steps.
proposals.hypothesis: Periodically re-encoding the molecular graph at strategically chosen nodes in the retrosynthetic search tree—triggered by a measurable structural-drift criterion—will yield more accurate synthesizability estimates and higher-quality synthetic routes than a single root encoding, while maintaining a computational cost well below full per-node GNN inference.
proposals.method: We propose an adaptive re-encoding schedule integrated into the Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework used by RetroGNN. A lightweight structural-drift detector, based on Tanimoto similarity between the current intermediate's Morgan fingerprint and the last-encoded ancestor, triggers a fresh GNN forward pass when the similarity drops below a learned threshold. Between refresh events, node-level value estimates continue to use the most recent cached encoding. The drift threshold and refresh frequency are jointly optimized with the GNN parameters via a bilevel optimization procedure on a held-out set of known synthetic routes from the USPTO dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) the first systematic study of representational staleness in tree-search-based retrosynthesis, quantifying how prediction accuracy degrades as a function of search depth; (2) an adaptive re-encoding mechanism that recovers most of the accuracy of expensive per-node inference at a fraction of the cost; and (3) empirical evidence on USPTO-50k and USPTO-full benchmarks showing improved route solvability and reduced invalid-step rates compared to both single-encoding and naive periodic-refresh baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has treated the molecular graph encoding as a one-shot preprocessing step for efficiency, no study has examined or exploited the trade-off between encoding freshness and computational budget within the search tree. The idea of coupling a structural-drift trigger to selective re-encoding is non-obvious because it requires recognizing that the GNN embedding space is not uniformly transferable across the chemical transformations encountered during retrosynthetic decomposition.

proposals.title: Subtree-Aware Structural Encodings for Retrosynthetic Route Prediction: Beyond Root-Centric Feature Propagation
proposals.motivation: Current retrosynthetic planning models such as RetroGNN propagate root-level molecular features down synthesis trees, implicitly assuming that descendant nodes share sufficient structural similarity with the target molecule for these features to remain informative at every branching decision. This assumption degrades for deep synthesis trees where late-stage intermediates bear little resemblance to the root target, leading to poor disconnection predictions precisely where creative retrosynthetic reasoning is most needed. Replacing this global root-centric encoding with localized subtree-specific structural representations could unlock more accurate and chemically meaningful route predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: Encoding each node in a retrosynthetic tree with a compressed representation of its local subtree neighborhood, rather than relying on features propagated from the root molecule, will yield significantly improved prediction accuracy for late-stage retrosynthetic disconnections and produce shorter, more synthesizable routes overall.
proposals.method: We propose Subtree-Structural Encoding Networks (SubSEN), which replace root-feature propagation with a hierarchical message-passing scheme operating over local subtree neighborhoods. For each node in the synthesis tree, we compute a fixed-dimensional structural encoding by applying a graph neural network to the k-hop induced subtree rooted at that node, then compress this representation via a learned pooling operator inspired by DiffPool. These local encodings are combined with a lightweight global context vector derived from the root via cross-attention, allowing the model to balance local structural detail against global target awareness. We evaluate on standard retrosynthesis benchmarks (USPTO-50K, PaRoutes) measuring per-step accuracy stratified by tree depth, end-to-end route validity, and computational cost relative to RetroGNN and baseline methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic empirical test of whether local subtree structure or global root structure is more informative for retrosynthetic decision-making at varying tree depths, establishing new understanding of information flow in synthesis planning models. The proposed SubSEN architecture offers a drop-in replacement for root-feature propagation modules in existing retrosynthetic GNNs, with expected gains concentrated in the long-horizon planning regime that matters most for practical drug discovery. The depth-stratified evaluation protocol itself will serve as a diagnostic tool for future synthesis planning research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on improving molecular representations or search strategies for retrosynthesis, no existing method has questioned the fundamental assumption that root-level features remain informative throughout the synthesis tree. Our subtree-specific encoding with learned compression directly challenges this assumption and introduces a principled mechanism for adapting representational granularity to local chemical context, an approach that is non-obvious because root propagation has been treated as architecturally neutral rather than as an inductive bias with testable consequences.

proposals.title: Runtime-Aware Learning Objectives for Graph Neural Network Surrogates of Retrosynthesis Planning
proposals.motivation: Surrogate models for computationally expensive scientific software, such as retrosynthesis feasibility predictors, are conventionally trained to maximize classification agreement with an expert oracle. However, as demonstrated in recent retrosynthesis surrogate work, improvements in top-1 accuracy or node-level fidelity do not reliably translate into reduced wall-clock screening time, because the computational cost distribution across molecules is highly skewed and architecture-dependent latency is non-trivial. This disconnect means the field may be optimizing the wrong objective, leaving large practical speedups on the table.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing standard cross-entropy expert-imitation losses with a runtime-aware training objective—one that explicitly penalizes surrogate inference cost and weights misclassification loss by the oracle compute time saved per molecule—will yield surrogate models that achieve substantially greater end-to-end wall-clock speedup on virtual screening campaigns than accuracy-matched baselines, even if their top-1 agreement with the expert is lower.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage approach. First, we profile per-molecule oracle runtime for a large compound library and train a lightweight runtime predictor (a small GNN or random forest on molecular descriptors) to serve as a differentiable runtime surrogate. Second, we formulate a cost-sensitive loss function for the main surrogate GNN that combines a weighted binary cross-entropy—where each sample's weight is proportional to its estimated oracle runtime—with an explicit inference-cost regularization term that penalizes architectural choices increasing the surrogate's own latency (e.g., number of message-passing layers, hidden dimension) via a Lagrangian relaxation. We will benchmark on the RetroGNN synthesizability task using the ZINC and Enamine REAL libraries, measuring both classification metrics and true end-to-end screening throughput under fixed hardware budgets.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first training framework for scientific surrogate models that directly optimizes for computational throughput rather than oracle agreement. We expect to demonstrate that runtime-aware surrogates can accelerate virtual screening by 2-5x beyond accuracy-optimized counterparts at equivalent hardware cost, fundamentally changing how the community evaluates and trains such models. The framework generalizes beyond retrosynthesis to any setting where a cheap surrogate filters inputs to an expensive oracle.
proposals.novelty: While cost-sensitive learning and model compression are established fields, no prior work has combined oracle-runtime-weighted loss functions with inference-cost regularization specifically for scientific surrogate models. The key non-obvious insight is that optimizing for the wrong proxy metric (accuracy) can actively harm the true objective (throughput), and that a principled cost-aware reformulation resolves this misalignment without requiring architectural changes to the surrogate itself.

proposals.title: SolverGNN: Learning Variable-State Representations from Raw Solver Signals for Branch-and-Bound
proposals.motivation: Modern machine-learning augmented branch-and-bound solvers rely on handcrafted variable features—such as pseudocosts, fractionality, and domain statistics—that require substantial domain expertise to design and may omit informative solver-state patterns. As mixed-integer programming instances grow in scale and diversity, fixed feature sets become a bottleneck: they transfer poorly across problem families and cannot capture dynamic solver phenomena that emerge during search. Automating the extraction of variable representations directly from raw solver signals would democratize ML-guided solving and unlock richer, instance-adaptive decision policies.
proposals.hypothesis: A graph neural network that operates on the raw bipartite constraint–variable graph annotated only with unprocessed solver-state signals (LP relaxation values, bound changes, constraint slacks, and branching history counts) can learn node-level representations that match or exceed the predictive power of expert-designed feature vectors for variable selection, while generalizing better to unseen problem families.
proposals.method: We propose SolverGNN, a message-passing neural network applied to the bipartite graph linking constraints and variables at each branching step. Instead of feeding in the standard 72-dimensional engineered feature vector, each node receives only raw, minimally normalized solver signals: current LP value, lower and upper bounds, objective coefficient, constraint activity, and a short history buffer of recent bound tightenings. A multi-layer Graph Attention Network with edge-type-aware attention aggregates information across the bipartite structure, producing per-variable embeddings that are scored for branching. The model is trained via imitation learning on strong branching decisions using the Ecole framework, with a curriculum that mixes MILP families of increasing heterogeneity to encourage transferable representations.
proposals.contribution: This work would establish that learned solver-state representations can replace handcrafted feature engineering in ML-for-branching pipelines without sacrificing—and potentially improving—branching quality and solving time. It provides a systematic ablation quantifying which raw signals carry the most information, offering new insight into what solvers implicitly encode. The resulting architecture serves as a drop-in replacement for the feature-extraction front-end of any GNN-based branching policy, lowering the barrier to applying ML in operations research.
proposals.novelty: Prior hybrid approaches treat feature engineering as a prerequisite and focus model design on the scoring head; SolverGNN inverts this by making the representation itself the learned component, showing that attention-based message passing over raw solver telemetry captures cross-variable dependencies that no fixed feature vector can express. This challenges the prevailing assumption that domain-expert features are necessary for competitive ML-augmented solvers.

proposals.title: Learning Latent Solver-State Representations for Hybrid Neuro-Symbolic Constraint Solving
proposals.motivation: Hybrid neuro-symbolic solvers for combinatorial optimization (e.g., SAT, MILP) rely on handcrafted, fixed features to represent solver state for neural heuristics, limiting adaptability and performance. This manual feature engineering is a bottleneck, analogous to pre-deep-learning computer vision, preventing models from discovering more powerful, task-specific representations directly from raw solver dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learned, low-dimensional latent representation of the solver's internal state, derived directly from raw variable and constraint activity traces, will outperform fixed handcrafted features in guiding neural search heuristics, leading to faster convergence and solving harder problem instances.
proposals.method: We will develop a graph neural network (GNN) encoder that operates on the dynamic constraint-variable bipartite graph of a solver (e.g., a SAT solver). The encoder will process raw, time-varying signals (e.g., variable assignments, clause activities, conflict counts) as node and edge features. Using a contrastive or predictive self-supervised objective, we will train this encoder to produce informative node embeddings. These learned embeddings will then replace the handcrafted features in an existing hybrid solver's policy network, which will be fine-tuned via reinforcement learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general framework for automating feature extraction in hybrid solvers, reducing domain expertise needed for new problem domains. It will demonstrate measurable performance gains on benchmark datasets (e.g., SATLIB) and contribute a methodology for learning representations from structured, algorithmic processes.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been applied to static problem graphs, learning dynamic state representations directly from a solver's *runtime signals* to replace a fixed feature set is a novel direction for neuro-symbolic reasoning. It merges representation learning from Bengio's earlier work on latent variable models with the structured reasoning of recent hybrid solvers.

proposals.title: Depth as Input: Scale-Invariant Depth Encodings for Robust Representation Learning in Vision Models
proposals.motivation: Current approaches to depth-aware vision tasks often relegate depth information to a secondary role, using it only to reweight losses rather than as a first-class input signal. This is primarily driven by well-documented distribution shift concerns: absolute depth values vary drastically across sensors, scenes, and scales, making naive depth conditioning brittle. However, discarding depth as an input forfeits rich geometric structure that could substantially improve spatial reasoning, object segmentation, and scene understanding, particularly in safety-critical applications such as medical imaging and autonomous navigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that depth can be effectively incorporated as a direct input signal—without incurring distribution shift degradation—by encoding it through scale-invariant transformations such as rank-based depth features and normalized sinusoidal positional encodings, yielding representations that are both more geometrically informed and more robust to domain shifts than loss-reweighting alternatives.
proposals.method: We propose a Depth-as-Input (DaI) module that transforms raw depth maps into scale-invariant representations via two complementary pathways: (1) a rank-based encoding that replaces absolute depth values with their local percentile ranks within spatial neighborhoods, eliminating scale dependence while preserving ordinal structure; and (2) a normalized Fourier positional encoding that maps depth to a fixed-bandwidth frequency space, analogous to positional encodings in transformers but adapted for continuous depth signals. These encodings are fused with RGB features via cross-attention layers and trained end-to-end. We will evaluate on monocular depth estimation benchmarks (NYUv2, KITTI), cross-domain transfer settings (synthetic-to-real), and downstream tasks including colonoscopy landmark detection, comparing against loss-reweighting baselines and standard depth concatenation approaches.
proposals.contribution: This work challenges the prevailing assumption that depth signals are too fragile for direct conditioning and establishes a principled framework for scale-invariant depth encoding. We expect to demonstrate improved cross-domain generalization (reducing depth-related distribution shift by a significant margin) and stronger downstream task performance, particularly in medical imaging where depth cues from endoscopic geometry are informative but highly variable. The resulting DaI module would be a portable architectural component applicable to any vision model that benefits from geometric reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored depth as auxiliary supervision or loss weighting, treating depth as a directly encoded input through rank-based and Fourier normalization is unexplored. The non-obvious insight is that ordinal preservation combined with frequency-space normalization can decouple geometric information from scale-specific artifacts, enabling depth conditioning that transfers across domains where absolute depth statistics differ fundamentally.

proposals.title: Towards Treat Depth As An Input Signal Rather Than Only As Loss Weighting: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to The paper avoids using depth directly because of distribution shift concerns and instead reweights l. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treat depth as an input signal rather than only as loss weighting can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates assumption challenge principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: The paper avoids using depth directly because of distribution shift concerns and instead reweights losses by relative depth. A challenge is to learn d.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the treat depth as an input signal rather than only as loss weighting problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Depth-Conditioned Branching Policies for Neural Combinatorial Search
proposals.motivation: Modern neural branch-and-bound and retrosynthesis planners apply a single learned policy uniformly across all levels of the search tree, yet the information landscape changes dramatically from root to leaf: early nodes require broad, exploratory branching under high uncertainty, while deep nodes benefit from cheap, narrow heuristics that exploit accumulated context. This mismatch wastes computation—expensive models are invoked where simple rules suffice, and shallow models are asked to make high-stakes strategic decisions they are ill-equipped for.
proposals.hypothesis: A depth-conditioned branching controller that allocates model capacity as a learned function of search depth will achieve superior cost-quality Pareto fronts compared to a single fixed-capacity brancher, because the optimal exploration-exploitation trade-off and the marginal value of additional computation shift systematically as the search deepens.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-controller architecture that selects among a discrete set of stage-specific branching policies (ranging from lightweight GNN scorers to full transformer-based evaluators) conditioned on normalized search depth, subtree uncertainty estimates, and incumbent solution quality. The controller is trained end-to-end via a two-phase procedure: first, each stage-specific policy is pre-trained independently on depth-stratified subproblems extracted from solved search trees; then the meta-controller is fine-tuned with REINFORCE using a cost-penalized objective that trades off solution quality against total neural-network inference cost. We evaluate on mixed-integer linear programming benchmarks (MIPLIB), retrosynthesis planning (USPTO-50k), and graph combinatorial optimization tasks from BenchmarkingGNN suites.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes that the one-policy-fits-all-depths assumption in learned search is a significant and correctable bottleneck, providing both a principled framework for depth-adaptive branching and empirical evidence across three distinct search domains. The resulting system is expected to reduce neural inference cost by 30-50% at matched solution quality, or improve solution quality at matched wall-clock time, offering a practical recipe for deploying learned search at scale.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored variable selection and node selection as monolithic learned policies, no existing approach explicitly models the cost-benefit asymmetry across search depths as a first-class design axis. The insight that search depth induces a natural curriculum—from strategic to tactical decisions—and that this curriculum should be mirrored by a heterogeneous policy architecture, challenges the implicit uniformity assumption in all current neural combinatorial optimization frameworks.

proposals.title: Universal Branching Policies via Family-Conditioned Graph Neural Networks for Mixed-Integer Programming
proposals.motivation: Learned branching policies for mixed-integer programming (MIP) solvers have shown strong performance but are typically trained and evaluated on a single combinatorial problem family, limiting their deployment in shared solver services, cloud optimization platforms, and heterogeneous industrial workloads. As MIP-as-a-service offerings grow, a brancher that must be retrained per problem type becomes impractical, yet no systematic study has examined whether a single policy can match or exceed family-specific specialists across diverse MIP distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: A family-conditioned graph neural network brancher, which augments bipartite variable-constraint representations with learned problem-family embeddings, can match the performance of per-family specialist branchers across multiple MIP problem distributions while enabling positive transfer that improves sample efficiency on under-represented families.
proposals.method: We extend the bipartite GNN architecture of Gasse et al. (2019) with a family-conditioning mechanism: each training instance is tagged with a problem-family identifier that is embedded and fused into node features via FiLM-style modulation layers. We train on a mixture of at least five canonical NP-hard families (set cover, combinatorial auction, capacitated facility location, maximum independent set, and mixed-integer knapsack) using a multi-task imitation learning objective against strong branching labels. We compare three regimes: (1) per-family specialists, (2) a naive universal model without conditioning, and (3) our family-conditioned universal model, measuring primal-dual integral, solving time, and node count on held-out instances, including an unseen sixth family to test zero-shot generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first rigorous empirical and architectural study of universal learned branching across heterogeneous MIP families, establishing whether cross-family transfer is beneficial or detrimental. It introduces FiLM-conditioned bipartite GNNs as a practical mechanism for multi-distribution solver learning and delivers actionable guidance for deploying learned branchers in real-world mixed-workload settings such as cloud optimization services.
proposals.novelty: While prior learned branching work implicitly assumes single-family deployment, we explicitly challenge this assumption by designing an architecture that treats problem-family identity as a first-class conditioning signal, revealing whether the bipartite variable-constraint representation already captures sufficient cross-family structure or whether explicit family modulation is necessary for effective transfer.

proposals.title: Online Meta-Adaptation for Neural Combinatorial Optimizers: Bridging Synthetic Training and Industrial Deployment Distributions
proposals.motivation: Neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) models are overwhelmingly trained on synthetically generated random instances (e.g., uniform-random TSP or VRP), yet real industrial workloads exhibit structured correlations, heavy-tailed demands, and non-stationary distributional shifts that violate the i.i.d. assumption underlying offline training. This distribution mismatch leads to significant performance degradation at deployment time, undermining the practical promise of learned solvers. Closing this sim-to-real gap for combinatorial optimization is critical for NCO to transition from academic benchmarks to industrial-scale logistics, scheduling, and supply-chain applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework that trains NCO models to rapidly adapt their policy parameters from a small buffer of real deployment instances—using gradient-based online adaptation with a learned loss surrogate—will consistently outperform fixed offline-trained models on structured industrial distributions, and that the adaptation efficiency scales favorably with the degree of distributional shift between synthetic training and deployment.
proposals.method: We propose a two-phase approach. First, during meta-training, we expose an attention-based constructive NCO policy (building on the POMO architecture) to a diverse task distribution generated by a parameterized instance generator spanning uniform, clustered, and grid-structured problem families, optimizing for fast few-shot adaptation via a MAML-style bilevel objective with a learned inner-loop loss function. Second, at deployment, the model maintains a sliding-window replay buffer of recently encountered industrial instances and performs lightweight online policy adaptation using natural-gradient updates constrained by a KL trust region to prevent catastrophic forgetting of general solving competence. We will evaluate on real-world logistics benchmarks (CVRPLIB, TSPLIB industrial subsets) and proprietary scheduling traces, measuring solution quality, adaptation speed, and robustness under non-stationary workload drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first systematic framework for online adaptation of neural combinatorial optimizers to deployment distributions, providing both theoretical analysis of the generalization gap between synthetic and structured instances and a practical algorithm that industrial users can deploy without retraining from scratch. We expect to demonstrate 5-15% solution quality improvements over offline-trained baselines on structured industrial benchmarks and to characterize scaling laws that describe how adaptation sample efficiency varies with distributional shift magnitude, giving practitioners actionable guidance on when and how to adapt learned solvers.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation and meta-learning are well-studied in supervised learning, their application to autoregressive combinatorial construction policies is non-trivial because the action space is combinatorial, reward signals are sparse, and the policy must maintain feasibility constraints throughout adaptation. Our combination of a learned inner-loop loss surrogate with trust-region online updates specifically designed for sequential construction policies is, to our knowledge, the first approach that addresses the unique challenges of distribution shift in neural combinatorial optimization without requiring access to optimal solutions from the target distribution.

proposals.title: Selective Imitation: Confidence-Aware Learned Branching with Expert Heuristic Fallback for Mixed-Integer Programming
proposals.motivation: Learned branching policies trained via imitation learning on strong branching demonstrations have shown impressive speedups on in-distribution MIP instances, yet they silently degrade on out-of-distribution or adversarial states where the learned model lacks reliable signal. Current deployments force an all-or-nothing choice between the ML policy and classical heuristics like reliability pseudocost branching (RPB), leaving significant performance on the table. A selective mechanism that detects when the learned policy is likely to err and defers to proven expert rules would yield a robust hybrid solver that captures ML gains without catastrophic failures.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned branching policy augmented with a calibrated confidence estimator can identify branch-and-bound states where its predicted variable scores are unreliable, and selectively deferring to reliability pseudocost branching or strong branching on those states will produce solve-time performance that strictly dominates both the pure ML policy and the pure heuristic baseline across heterogeneous MIP benchmark distributions.
proposals.method: We extend a GNN-based imitation learning brancher (following the Gasse et al. bipartite graph architecture) with an auxiliary confidence head trained to predict whether the primary policy's top-ranked variable matches the strong branching oracle's choice. The confidence head is trained on a binary classification objective using held-out solver states, and we apply temperature scaling for post-hoc calibration. At inference time, if the calibrated confidence falls below a learned threshold, the solver falls back to RPB or, budget permitting, limited strong branching. The threshold is tuned on a validation set of heterogeneous MIP instances to minimize total solving time. We evaluate on standard NeurIPS ML4CO benchmarks (set covering, combinatorial auctions, facility location, item placement) as well as deliberately constructed distribution-shift scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled abstention framework for ML-guided combinatorial optimization that breaks the implicit assumption that a learned policy must handle every solver state. It provides the first empirical characterization of where and why learned branchers fail, offering interpretable confidence maps over the branch-and-bound tree. The resulting hybrid solver is expected to be deployment-ready, offering ML-level speedups on familiar instances while gracefully degrading to expert-level performance on novel ones, directly addressing a key barrier to industrial adoption of ML in solvers.
proposals.novelty: While selective prediction and learning-to-defer have been studied in classification, applying calibrated abstention within a sequential decision process like branch-and-bound is non-trivial because errors compound across tree depth and the cost of a bad branching decision is state-dependent. Our approach is the first to integrate confidence-aware deferral into learned branching, challenging the prevailing assumption that the ML policy should uniformly replace the classical heuristic at every node.

proposals.title: HetBranch: Hardware-Aware Dynamic Branching for Retrosynthesis Feasibility Estimation on Heterogeneous Compute
proposals.motivation: RetroGNN and similar graph neural network surrogates for retrosynthesis assume a fixed, CPU-only deployment target, forgoing accelerator-level graph computation even when GPUs or TPUs are intermittently available. In real pharmaceutical and materials-science pipelines, compute clusters often expose shared or spot accelerators whose availability fluctuates over time. Exploiting these transient resources could dramatically improve throughput and model fidelity without sacrificing the robustness of CPU-only fallback inference.
proposals.hypothesis: A hardware-aware dynamic branching architecture that routes expensive message-passing computation to available accelerators while maintaining a lightweight CPU-resident fallback pathway can match or exceed the throughput of CPU-only surrogate models under average cluster conditions, and approach full GNN accuracy when accelerators are available, without requiring retraining or model duplication.
proposals.method: We propose HetBranch, a co-designed inference framework comprising (1) a shared stem encoder that extracts molecular graph features on CPU, (2) a lightweight linear branch that produces fast, approximate synthesizability scores on CPU alone, and (3) a deep GNN branch dispatched to accelerators when a runtime hardware monitor detects available GPU or TPU resources. A gating controller, trained via reinforcement learning with a reward balancing latency and prediction quality, decides per-query which branch to invoke. We evaluate on standard retrosynthesis benchmarks (USPTO-50k, Pistachio) comparing HetBranch against CPU-only RetroGNN, full-GPU GNN inference, and static ensemble baselines across simulated heterogeneous cluster traces derived from real cloud spot-instance availability logs.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) the first hardware-aware adaptive inference framework for graph-based molecular property prediction, (2) a principled RL-based gating mechanism that balances accuracy and latency under stochastic accelerator availability, and (3) empirical evidence quantifying the accuracy-throughput Pareto frontier achievable by heterogeneous deployment versus fixed-hardware assumptions. The framework generalizes beyond retrosynthesis to any GNN workload deployed in mixed-hardware environments.
proposals.novelty: Prior surrogate models treat hardware homogeneity as a fixed design constraint rather than a variable to optimize over. HetBranch is non-obvious because it couples model architecture decisions—branch depth, message-passing rounds—directly to a runtime hardware availability signal, requiring co-design of the learning objective and the systems-level scheduler rather than treating them independently.

proposals.title: Beyond Benchmarking: A Controlled Study of Size-Generalization in Graph Neural Networks Across Architectures, Distillation, and Regularization Regimes
proposals.motivation: Graph neural networks are increasingly deployed on combinatorial and scientific problems where test instances are substantially larger than training instances, yet systematic understanding of what drives size-generalization remains fragmented. The Benchmarking GNN study revealed that even top-performing architectures overfit catastrophically on problems like Maximum Independent Set when train and test graphs differ in size, exposing a critical gap between in-distribution accuracy and out-of-distribution extrapolation. A controlled, multi-factor study is urgently needed to disentangle the contributions of architecture, training signal, and regularization to size-generalization before practitioners can reliably scale GNN solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: Size-generalization in GNNs is governed primarily by the interaction between message-passing depth, the presence of auxiliary self-supervised losses that encode structural invariants, and explicit regularization, rather than by architecture family alone; consequently, a lightweight GNN with appropriate auxiliary losses and regularization can match or exceed larger, more expressive architectures on out-of-distribution graph sizes.
proposals.method: We propose a full-factorial experimental design crossing four architecture families (GCN, GIN, GAT, PNA) with three distillation strategies (no distillation, soft-label distillation from a large-graph-trained teacher, feature-map distillation), three auxiliary loss types (none, graph-size prediction, substructure counting), and three regularization methods (dropout, DropEdge, spectral normalization) on five benchmark tasks spanning combinatorial optimization and molecular property prediction. Training is performed on small graphs (up to 100 nodes) and evaluation targets graphs 2x to 10x larger, using a standardized protocol with fixed compute budgets and hyperparameter sweeps via Bayesian optimization. Interaction effects will be quantified through ANOVA and ablation analysis to identify which factor combinations yield robust extrapolation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive empirical map of how architecture, distillation, auxiliary losses, and regularization jointly affect size-generalization in GNNs, replacing anecdotal findings with statistically grounded guidelines. The resulting factor-interaction analysis will let practitioners select minimal-cost configurations that generalize reliably, and the open-sourced benchmark suite will serve as a standardized testbed for future generalization research. Additionally, the study will clarify whether distillation from large-graph teachers can serve as a practical shortcut for improving extrapolation without access to large graphs at deployment time.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied individual factors like DropEdge or distillation in isolation, no existing study crosses architecture choice with training-signal augmentation and regularization in a single controlled design, making it impossible to detect interaction effects that dominate in practice. Our factorial approach, combined with the explicit focus on out-of-distribution size extrapolation rather than in-distribution accuracy, provides a uniquely actionable decomposition of the generalization problem.

proposals.title: Scaling Walls in Learned Branching: Mapping the Extrapolation Frontier of Hybrid GNN Branchers Trained on Medium-Scale Instances
proposals.motivation: Graph neural network branchers for mixed-integer linear programming have shown strong performance when trained and tested on instances of similar size, yet real-world deployment demands generalization to formulations orders of magnitude larger than any training distribution. Current evaluations typically train on small instances and test on modestly larger ones, leaving unknown the precise scaling threshold beyond which learned branching policies degrade. Characterizing this extrapolation frontier is essential for deciding when to trust a learned brancher versus falling back to classical strong branching in production solvers.
proposals.hypothesis: Hybrid branchers that combine a GNN-based scoring model at the root node with lightweight, root-conditioned heuristic models at deeper nodes will generalize reliably up to a quantifiable instance-size multiplier, beyond which the root-node GNN embedding no longer captures sufficient problem structure, causing the conditioned cheap models to collapse toward random branching.
proposals.method: We will generate benchmark families (set cover, combinatorial auctions, capacitated facility location) at four controlled scales: small (baseline), medium (2-4x), big (8-10x), and 2x-big (16-20x variables and constraints). Hybrid branchers following the architecture of Gasse et al. (2019) will be trained under three regimes: small-only, medium-only, and a curriculum mixing small and medium instances with importance-weighted sampling. Generalization will be evaluated by tracking primal-dual gap closure, node counts, and solving time on the big and 2x-big test sets, with strong branching and reliability branching as oracle and practical baselines. We will additionally perform representational probes on the GNN embeddings at the root node across scales, using centered kernel alignment to quantify when the learned bipartite graph representation loses structural fidelity relative to the strong-branching signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic extrapolation map for GNN-based branching policies, identifying concrete instance-size thresholds at which learned models fail and quantifying the gap between training-scale performance and out-of-distribution degradation. It will establish whether medium-scale or curriculum training provides a practical recipe for extending the useful range of learned branchers. The representational analysis will offer mechanistic insight into why root-conditioned architectures lose fidelity, informing the design of next-generation branching architectures with explicit scale-equivariant inductive biases.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that reports generalization anecdotally or at a single extrapolation factor, this study treats the extrapolation limit itself as the primary scientific quantity to be measured, introducing controlled scaling protocols and representational diagnostics that reveal the structural mechanism of failure rather than merely observing performance decline.

proposals.title: Stress-Testing Hybrid Learning-and-Search MILP Solvers: A Comprehensive Evaluation Across Diverse Instance Distributions and Industry Benchmarks
proposals.motivation: Recent hybrid approaches that combine graph neural networks with classical branch-and-bound heuristics for Mixed-Integer Linear Programming have shown promising results, yet their evaluation has been confined to a narrow set of synthetic problem families such as set covering, combinatorial auctions, capacitated facility location, and maximum independent set. Real-world MILP instances from supply chain scheduling, chip verification, and energy network design exhibit fundamentally different constraint structures, variable interactions, and root-node relaxation landscapes that may expose brittleness in learned branching and node-selection policies. A rigorous cross-distribution evaluation is urgently needed before practitioners can trust these hybrid solvers in production settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Hybrid GNN-guided MILP solvers that perform well on standard synthetic benchmarks will exhibit significant performance degradation—measured by solve time, node count, and optimality gap—on structurally heterogeneous instance families where the branch-and-bound tree topology diverges sharply from training distributions, and this degradation can be systematically characterized and mitigated through distribution-aware fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will curate a diverse benchmark suite spanning at least eight additional MILP families drawn from MIPLIB 2017 stratified clusters, real-world logistics instances from the Google OR-Tools repository, and procedurally generated problems with tunable constraint density and coefficient heterogeneity. Each hybrid solver—including GNN-based branching policies and learned cut-selection modules—will be evaluated against state-of-the-art expert heuristics (reliability branching, strong branching with limited lookahead) using a controlled experimental protocol that measures primal-dual integral, solving time, and tree size across matched computational budgets. We will further apply transfer learning and domain-adaptive fine-tuning strategies to quantify the gap between zero-shot generalization and distribution-specific adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, cross-distribution robustness analysis of hybrid learning-based MILP solvers, identifying specific structural properties—such as constraint sparsity patterns, objective coefficient variance, and branching factor profiles—that predict when learned policies fail relative to classical heuristics. The resulting benchmark suite and evaluation protocol will become a community resource for standardized comparison. Additionally, the fine-tuning analysis will yield practical guidelines for deploying learned MILP components in new problem domains with minimal retraining cost.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has demonstrated that GNN-based policies can match or exceed expert heuristics on curated synthetic instances, no systematic study has characterized the failure modes of these methods across the full structural diversity of practical MILPs. Our approach of coupling a structurally annotated benchmark taxonomy with transfer-learning diagnostics provides a principled framework for understanding when and why hybrid solvers generalize, moving beyond aggregate accuracy to instance-level interpretability of solver behavior.

proposals.title: Distribution-Tuned Expert Branching Heuristics: Disentangling Specialization Gains from Learning Gains in Neural Branch-and-Bound
proposals.motivation: Recent neural branching policies such as FiLM-augmented GNN imitation learners have demonstrated impressive improvements over classical expert heuristics like Reliability Pseudo-cost Branching (RPB) on standard MIP benchmarks. However, these comparisons pit a distribution-trained model against a broad-purpose heuristic that uses no distributional information, conflating the benefit of specialization with the benefit of learning. Establishing whether learned models genuinely capture structure beyond what a well-tuned classical heuristic can exploit is critical for guiding future research investment in ML for combinatorial optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: Problem-class-specialized variants of RPB and Strong Branching approximations—tuned via lightweight hyperparameter search over scoring weights, look-ahead depths, and reliability thresholds on the same training distribution used by neural models—will close a substantial portion of the performance gap reported for learned branching policies, revealing that a significant share of the reported gains stems from distribution specialization rather than from the representational capacity of neural architectures.
proposals.method: We will select four canonical MIP benchmark families (Set Cover, Combinatorial Auction, Capacity-Constrained Facility Location, and Maximum Independent Set) following prior protocol. For each family we construct distribution-tuned expert heuristics by parameterizing RPB and Hybrid Strong/Pseudo-cost Branching with family-specific scoring weights, reliability counters, and candidate pre-filtering rules, optimizing these parameters via Bayesian hyperparameter search (Tree-structured Parzen Estimators) on the same training instances used by GNN-based learners. We then benchmark these tuned experts against both vanilla RPB and state-of-the-art learned branching policies (GNN imitation learning, FiLM-enhanced models) measuring solving time, dual-bound integral, and node count on held-out test instances of matched difficulty.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first controlled ablation separating distribution-specialization effects from learning effects in neural branching, yielding calibrated baselines that the community can adopt for fairer evaluation. It will produce an open-source tuning pipeline for generating problem-class-specific expert heuristics, lowering the barrier for rigorous benchmarking. The results will clarify the marginal value of neural function approximation and inform where future architectural innovations should focus.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats classical branching rules as fixed baselines, no systematic study has applied modern hyperparameter optimization to specialize these heuristics per problem distribution before comparing against learned policies. This reframes the evaluation question from 'can ML beat experts?' to the more scientifically precise 'can ML beat experts given equal access to distributional information?'—a distinction that is non-obvious yet fundamental to understanding the true source of performance gains.

proposals.title: Gradient-Stabilized End-to-End Training of Root-Encoder to Decision-Tree Architectures via Adaptive Path Regularization
proposals.motivation: Hierarchical models that pair a shared root encoder with tree-structured decoders—common in retrosynthesis planning, parsing, and hierarchical classification—often face a painful trade-off: freezing the root encoder is fast and stable but leaves performance on the table, while end-to-end backpropagation through deep decision trees can yield vanishing or exploding gradients that destabilize the encoder. As these architectures scale to larger trees in molecular design and program synthesis, a principled understanding of when and why end-to-end training helps—and how to make it reliably beneficial—is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The instability of root-to-tree backpropagation arises primarily from path-length-dependent gradient variance across tree branches, and can be resolved by an adaptive per-path regularization scheme that normalizes gradient contributions by effective depth, enabling end-to-end training to consistently outperform root-only extraction across diverse problem families without sacrificing convergence speed.
proposals.method: We propose Stability-Aware Path Regularization (SAPR), which instruments the backward pass through tree decoders to track per-path gradient norms and applies a learned, depth-conditioned rescaling factor before gradients reach the root encoder. Concretely, we combine three techniques: (1) a gradient variance monitor that estimates second-moment statistics across tree paths using exponential moving averages, (2) an adaptive clipping-and-scaling module conditioned on path depth and branching factor, and (3) a warmup curriculum that begins with shallow-tree gradients and progressively unlocks deeper paths as the encoder stabilizes. We will evaluate on retrosynthesis route prediction (using the USPTO benchmark following RetroGNN protocols), hierarchical text classification, and mathematical expression generation, comparing SAPR against frozen-encoder baselines, naive end-to-end training, and existing gradient stabilization methods such as gradient clipping and spectral normalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide both a practical training algorithm—SAPR—that makes end-to-end root-to-tree training reliably beneficial without manual tuning, and a theoretical characterization of when end-to-end adaptation helps most (relating it to the mismatch between pre-trained encoder representations and task-specific tree structure). We expect to demonstrate consistent improvements of 5-15% on synthesizability prediction and parsing accuracy over root-frozen baselines while matching their convergence speed, establishing a new default training protocol for hierarchical encoder-decoder architectures.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic gradient stabilization methods that treat the computation graph uniformly, SAPR exploits the known tree topology to apply path-aware, depth-conditioned regularization—an approach that is non-obvious because it requires treating different backward paths through the same decoder asymmetrically based on structural rather than purely numerical properties of the gradient signal.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning the Distillation Protocol: Problem-Family-Adaptive Selection of Knowledge Distillation, Expert Distillation, and Multiple-Head Ensembling Objectives
proposals.motivation: Current practice in neural combinatorial optimization applies a single distillation or auxiliary-loss protocol uniformly across all problem families, yet empirical evidence shows that knowledge distillation (KD) degrades performance on combinatorial auctions while helping set cover and other families, and the relative advantage of expert distillation (ED) versus multiple-head ensembling (MHE) shifts depending on problem structure. This heterogeneity wastes practitioner effort on per-family tuning and leaves significant performance on the table. A principled, learnable mechanism for protocol selection would both advance our theoretical understanding of when and why each auxiliary objective helps and provide immediate practical gains across diverse combinatorial optimization benchmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning agent that conditions on lightweight structural features of a problem family—such as constraint density, variable-constraint ratio, and objective landscape curvature—can reliably select the distillation protocol (KD, ED, MHE, or none) that maximizes downstream solver quality, outperforming any fixed-protocol baseline and matching or exceeding oracle family-specific selection.
proposals.method: We formulate protocol selection as a contextual bandit over the discrete action space {KD, ED, MHE, null} where the context is a feature vector extracted from a small sample of training instances per problem family. The meta-learner is a graph neural network that ingests the bipartite variable-constraint graph of sampled instances and outputs a protocol distribution. Training proceeds in two phases: (1) an offline phase that constructs a meta-dataset by running each protocol on a diverse suite of problem families (set cover, maximum independent set, combinatorial auctions, facility location, capacitated vehicle routing) and recording final primal gap as reward; (2) an online phase using REINFORCE with a learned baseline that fine-tunes the selector on held-out families to test generalization. We additionally investigate a soft-mixing variant where the selector outputs a convex combination of protocol losses, enabling smooth interpolation rather than hard switching.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (i) the first formal treatment of distillation-protocol selection as a meta-learning problem in neural combinatorial optimization, (ii) a lightweight, GNN-based selector that automates what is currently a manual per-family design choice, and (iii) an extensive empirical study across at least five problem families quantifying when and why each protocol succeeds or fails. The resulting framework reduces practitioner overhead and is expected to unlock compounding gains as new auxiliary objectives are proposed, since each can be absorbed into the selector's action space without architecture redesign.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats the choice of auxiliary training objective as a fixed hyperparameter, we recast it as a learnable function of problem structure, drawing on meta-learning principles rarely applied to the distillation-objective design axis. The combination of a GNN-based structural featurizer with a contextual-bandit protocol selector is, to our knowledge, unprecedented and offers a principled answer to the empirically observed but theoretically unexplained family-dependent performance of KD, ED, and MHE.

proposals.title: Scale-Aware Regularization for Combinatorial Optimization on Graphs: Preventing Catastrophic Degradation Across Instance Sizes
proposals.motivation: Graph neural networks trained on small combinatorial optimization instances frequently exhibit catastrophic performance degradation when evaluated on larger instances, a phenomenon particularly acute for NP-hard problems like Maximum Independent Set (MIS) where the solution structure shifts qualitatively with scale. Current regularization and validation practices treat this scale shift as a minor hyperparameter tuning issue, yet recent benchmarking evidence shows it is a first-class failure mode that undermines the practical utility of learned solvers. Addressing this gap is urgent because real-world deployment of GNN-based combinatorial solvers demands reliable generalization across the full range of industrially relevant instance sizes.
proposals.hypothesis: Family-specific scale shift in combinatorial optimization problems induces a systematic distributional mismatch between small training instances and large test instances, and this mismatch can be explicitly modeled and counteracted through scale-aware regularization objectives and multi-resolution validation protocols, yielding GNN solvers that degrade gracefully rather than catastrophically as instance size grows.
proposals.method: We propose a three-pronged approach: (1) a scale-conditional regularization term that penalizes the divergence between the model's internal representations on small and progressively larger instances during training, implemented via an auxiliary loss that aligns graph-level embeddings across size buckets using optimal transport; (2) a curriculum-based multi-resolution validation scheme that monitors approximation ratio trajectories across instance-size brackets and triggers early stopping only when cross-scale performance stabilizes; and (3) a theoretical analysis grounding the regularizer in structural properties of the MIS solution space, leveraging known phase transitions in random graph ensembles to define family-specific scale-shift profiles. Experiments will benchmark against state-of-the-art GNN solvers on MIS, Minimum Vertex Cover, and Graph Coloring across synthetic and real-world graph families ranging from tens to tens of thousands of nodes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish scale shift as a formally characterized failure mode in learned combinatorial optimization, providing both a theoretical framework linking problem-specific phase transitions to generalization breakdown and a practical toolkit of regularization and validation techniques. The proposed methods are expected to substantially close the performance gap between small-instance-trained models and dedicated large-instance solvers, enabling a single trained model to maintain competitive approximation ratios across two or more orders of magnitude in instance size. Beyond combinatorial optimization, the scale-aware regularization principles should transfer to any domain where GNNs must generalize across structurally shifting graph families.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats size generalization as a data augmentation or architecture design problem, our approach directly regularizes the learned representation space to be invariant to scale-induced distributional shifts, informed by the combinatorial structure of the target problem family. The integration of phase-transition theory from random graph combinatorics into the design of machine learning regularizers is, to our knowledge, unprecedented and offers a principled alternative to empirical tuning of generalization heuristics.

proposals.title: Capacity Scaling Laws for FiLM-Conditioned GNN Solvers on Hard Combinatorial Optimization Instances
proposals.motivation: Recent hybrid neural-classical solvers using FiLM conditioning on graph neural networks show promising results on combinatorial optimization benchmarks but exhibit degraded generalization when evaluated on instances that are 2x larger or structurally harder than those seen during training. The supplement to prior work explicitly flags that observed scaling failures may stem from architectural capacity bottlenecks rather than fundamental limitations of the hybrid paradigm. Resolving this ambiguity is critical before the community either abandons or doubles down on FiLM-based approaches for NP-hard problems.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically increasing the hidden-layer width and depth of the cheap GNN backbone in FiLM-conditioned combinatorial solvers will recover a substantial fraction of the generalization gap observed on harder set cover instances, following predictable capacity scaling laws analogous to those seen in language model scaling, thereby demonstrating that prior failures are attributable to under-parameterization rather than to intrinsic limits of feature-wise linear modulation for combinatorial reasoning.
proposals.method: We conduct controlled scaling experiments on weighted set cover instances drawn from established benchmark generators at 1x, 2x, and 4x the baseline problem size, as well as structurally harder variants with increased constraint density. For each difficulty tier, we train FiLM-conditioned GNN models across a grid of hidden dimensions (64 to 1024) and network depths (4 to 16 message-passing layers), measuring both primal gap relative to CPLEX and out-of-distribution generalization to unseen instance sizes. We fit power-law scaling curves relating model capacity (parameter count and effective width) to solver quality, and compare against capacity-matched baselines without FiLM conditioning to isolate the contribution of the modulation mechanism under scaled architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for neural combinatorial optimization solvers, providing the community with concrete guidelines on how much model capacity is needed to maintain solver quality as instance difficulty grows. It will definitively resolve whether the generalization failures reported in prior FiLM-based solvers are architectural or methodological in origin, directly informing the design of next-generation hybrid solvers. The resulting scaling curves and trained checkpoints will serve as a practical resource for practitioners deciding compute-quality tradeoffs when deploying learned solvers.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws have been extensively studied for language models, no analogous analysis exists for neural combinatorial optimization, where the relationship between model capacity and problem hardness is confounded by the combinatorial explosion of the solution space. Our approach of disentangling FiLM conditioning effectiveness from raw capacity through matched-parameter ablations provides a methodology transferable to any modulated architecture applied to structured discrete problems.

proposals.title: BatchBranch: GPU-Aware Batching and Scheduling for Concurrent Neural Branching in Mixed-Integer Linear Programming
proposals.motivation: Recent advances in learning-to-branch have shown that graph neural network (GNN) policies can match or exceed expert branching heuristics in mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solvers, yet these models are deployed one instance at a time, leaving GPU resources severely underutilized. In practice, applications such as multi-objective optimization, scenario-based stochastic programming, and retrosynthesis planning require solving hundreds or thousands of related MILPs concurrently, creating an ideal but unexploited opportunity for GPU batching. Designing systems-level scheduling that dynamically batches branching queries across concurrent solver processes would unlock the throughput needed to make learned branching practical at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: A scheduling framework that dynamically groups branching inference requests from multiple concurrent MILP solvers into GPU-efficient batches can achieve near-linear throughput scaling with the number of simultaneous instances, without degrading per-instance solution quality, by exploiting the asynchronous and heterogeneous timing of branch-and-bound node evaluations across solvers.
proposals.method: We propose BatchBranch, a middleware layer between a set of concurrent MILP solver processes and a shared GPU-resident GNN branching policy. The system uses an asynchronous request queue with adaptive batching: solver threads submit bipartite graph representations of their current branch-and-bound nodes to a central dispatcher, which forms variable-size batches using a deadline-aware policy that balances GPU utilization against solver idle time. We employ dynamic padding and graph coarsening to handle heterogeneous instance sizes within a single batch, and implement priority scheduling so that solvers closer to proving optimality receive lower-latency inference. The framework will be implemented on top of Ecole and SCIP, with the GNN policy following the bipartite graph convolution architecture of Gasse et al. (2019), and evaluated on standard MILP benchmark families as well as multi-objective optimization scenarios requiring hundreds of concurrent solves.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic study of GPU batching strategies for learned MILP branching under concurrent workloads, filling a gap between single-instance ML-for-optimization research and real-world deployment requirements. We expect to demonstrate that BatchBranch achieves 5-20x throughput improvement over naive sequential GPU inference while maintaining branching decision quality within 2% of the single-instance baseline. The resulting open-source framework will make neural branching viable for large-scale combinatorial optimization pipelines in drug discovery, supply chain planning, and multi-objective design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on improving the quality of individual branching decisions via imitation or reinforcement learning, no existing approach addresses the systems-level challenge of serving a learned brancher to many solvers simultaneously. Our adaptive batching with deadline-aware scheduling is non-obvious because branch-and-bound trees exhibit highly irregular and unpredictable node evaluation patterns across instances, making naive fixed-size batching either wasteful or latency-inducing; our solution treats this as an online scheduling problem with learned load prediction.

proposals.title: Gated Affine Hypermodels: Bridging the Cost-Generalization Gap Between HyperSVM and FiLM for Combinatorial Transfer
proposals.motivation: HyperSVM offers extremely low per-instance cost by generating linear classifier weights from a hypernetwork, yet its expressiveness saturates on larger, more structured problem instances. FiLM-based modulated MLPs generalize far better on such instances but introduce substantially higher computational overhead due to their multi-layer feature-wise conditioning. This leaves a practical gap in the architecture spectrum: practitioners must choose between a model that is cheap but limited and one that is powerful but expensive, with no principled intermediate option that trades off cost and generalization smoothly.
proposals.hypothesis: By introducing a family of gated affine hypermodels—architectures that generate low-rank affine modulation parameters for a shallow MLP through a shared hypernetwork with learned gating over modulation depth—it is possible to recover a significant fraction of FiLM's generalization advantage on larger problem instances while keeping computational cost within a small constant factor of HyperSVM.
proposals.method: We propose Gated Affine Hypermodels (GAH), in which a compact hypernetwork produces low-rank affine transformation parameters (scale and shift) for each layer of a shallow two- or three-layer MLP, with a learned binary or soft gating mechanism that selectively activates modulation at each layer based on instance complexity. The rank of the generated affine parameters is controlled by a bottleneck dimension, allowing smooth interpolation between HyperSVM (rank-1, single layer, no gating) and full FiLM (full-rank, all layers modulated). We will evaluate GAH on combinatorial optimization benchmarks of varying instance size, measuring both out-of-distribution generalization and wall-clock inference cost relative to HyperSVM and FiLM baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled architectural family that fills the unexplored region between hypernetwork-generated linear models and fully modulated deep networks, providing practitioners with a tunable cost-accuracy knob. It will yield both theoretical analysis of the expressiveness-cost tradeoff as a function of modulation rank and gating sparsity, and empirical Pareto frontiers on standard benchmarks demonstrating that GAH achieves 70-90% of FiLM's generalization gain at only 1.5-3x HyperSVM's cost.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats hypernetwork-generated classifiers and feature-wise modulation as separate paradigms, GAH unifies them into a single parametric family via rank-controlled affine generation and learned depth gating, revealing that the gap between HyperSVM and FiLM is not a discrete jump but a traversable continuum with favorable intermediate operating points.

proposals.title: From Lab to Plant Floor: A Deployment Study of Hybrid ML-Guided Branching in Production Mixed-Integer Programming Solvers
proposals.motivation: Machine-learning-augmented branching policies have demonstrated strong node-count reductions on academic benchmarks, yet no rigorous study has characterized their behavior inside long-running, CPU-bound industrial solver deployments where tail latency, numerical reproducibility, and interaction with presolve, cutting planes, and incumbent heuristics are critical. Without deployment evidence, solver vendors and operations-research teams cannot justify integrating learned components into mission-critical scheduling, logistics, or supply-chain pipelines. Closing this evidence gap is essential for translating a decade of ML-for-combinatorial-optimization research into real-world impact.
proposals.hypothesis: Hybrid ML-guided branching strategies, when carefully integrated with solver internals such as presolve reductions, cut management, and node-selection heuristics, can maintain at least 90% of their benchmark speed-ups in production MIP workloads while satisfying industrial requirements on P99 latency variance (within 15% of the deterministic baseline) and bit-level reproducibility across solver restarts.
proposals.method: We will instrument two open-source solvers (SCIP and HiGHS) with ONNX-Runtime inference hooks at the branching callback, deploying three learned branching policies (a GNN strong-branching imitator, a reinforcement-learned policy, and a hybrid portfolio selector) on a curated suite of 200+ real production MIP instances from public industrial repositories (MIPLIB 2017 industry set, Google OR-Tools benchmarks, and partner supply-chain models). Each configuration will be evaluated over 72-hour continuous solve campaigns on commodity CPU servers, collecting per-node inference overhead, wall-clock solve-time distributions, deterministic tick counts for reproducibility audits, and interaction profiles showing how learned branching decisions propagate through cut selection and primal heuristic firing. We will apply changepoint detection on latency time-series and statistical reproducibility tests (bitwise and tolerance-based) to quantify deployment readiness.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic deployment-grade evaluation framework for ML-augmented branching, providing practitioners with concrete guidelines on when learned branchers help, hurt, or introduce unacceptable variance in production solvers. The open-source instrumentation toolkit and the reproducibility audit protocol will become reusable infrastructure for the solver community. The empirical findings will identify specific solver-internal interactions (e.g., cut-branching feedback loops) that either amplify or attenuate the gains seen on isolated benchmarks, directly informing the next generation of solver-aware ML architectures.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior benchmarking work that evaluates ML branchers in isolation on single instances, this study treats the solver as a complex stateful system and measures emergent deployment phenomena—latency tails from GC pauses or cache effects during inference, reproducibility failures from floating-point non-determinism in neural forward passes, and cascading interactions between learned branching and downstream solver components—that only manifest in sustained production workloads.

proposals.title: BranchNAS: Hardware-Aware Neural Architecture Search for Cost-Constrained Retrosynthetic Feasibility Predictors
proposals.motivation: Retrosynthetic feasibility scoring models like RetroGNN must operate under strict wall-clock budgets in virtual screening pipelines that evaluate millions of candidates. Current designs hand-tune encoder depth, head complexity, hidden widths, and prediction-refresh schedules without principled exploration of the Pareto frontier between predictive quality and solver runtime. Importing hardware-aware neural architecture search, which has matured in efficient deep learning for edge deployment, offers a systematic way to discover brancher architectures that maximize screening throughput under a fixed compute envelope.
proposals.hypothesis: A hardware-aware NAS framework that jointly searches over graph encoder topology, lightweight classification heads, hidden dimensionality, and score-refresh frequency will yield retrosynthetic feasibility predictors that achieve equivalent or superior ranking quality to hand-designed RetroGNN variants while reducing per-molecule inference cost by at least 40%, because the design space contains favorable accuracy-latency trade-offs that manual tuning cannot efficiently explore.
proposals.method: We formulate a differentiable NAS supernet whose searchable blocks include GNN encoder layers (GCN, GIN, GAT cells), pooling operators, and multi-resolution cheap prediction heads, with a latency look-up table calibrated on target hardware (GPU and CPU). The search objective is a scalarized combination of binary cross-entropy on synthesizability labels and a measured-latency penalty, optimized via a one-shot gradient approach akin to FBNet. A secondary outer loop searches discrete refresh schedules that control how often the expensive retrosynthesis oracle re-labels the training set, treating the refresh interval as a categorical architecture hyperparameter whose cost is amortized over the screening campaign.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first NAS-derived family of retrosynthetic feasibility predictors explicitly optimized for deployment cost, producing a Pareto set of models spanning quick-filter to high-fidelity regimes. It establishes a reusable search space and latency-modeling methodology transferable to other molecular property prediction tasks that face analogous quality-versus-throughput trade-offs. The resulting models will lower the computational barrier for large-scale virtual screening in drug discovery pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While hardware-aware NAS has been applied to vision and language models, its transfer to molecular graph networks for synthesizability prediction is unexplored; the additional search dimension of oracle-refresh scheduling—treating data-generation cost as an architectural choice—has no precedent in either the NAS or cheminformatics literature.

proposals.title: SparseRootFormer: Sparse Attention Encoders for Molecular Retrosynthesis Root Structures with Hardware-Aware Speed-Quality Trade-offs
proposals.motivation: Retrosynthetic analysis requires encoding complex molecular graph structures to predict feasible synthetic routes, yet current GNN-based approaches like RetroGNN suffer from message-passing bottlenecks that scale poorly on modern accelerator hardware. Sparse attention mechanisms from the transformer literature offer a compelling alternative, as they exploit the highly parallel matrix-multiply units on GPUs far more efficiently than iterative neighborhood aggregation. Bridging these two paradigms is timely given the rapid growth of virtual screening libraries that demand both accuracy and throughput at unprecedented scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Sparse attention encoders that treat molecular root structures as sets of atom-bond tokens with learned, chemically-informed sparsity patterns can match or exceed GNN message-passing accuracy on retrosynthetic feasibility prediction while achieving substantially higher throughput on GPU accelerators, because the regular compute patterns of attention align better with modern hardware than the irregular memory access of graph convolutions.
proposals.method: We propose SparseRootFormer, a transformer-based encoder that represents molecular root structures as sequences of atom tokens augmented with bond-type positional encodings, applying sparse attention patterns derived from molecular topology (bond connectivity within k-hops) and learned chemical priors. Specifically, we combine fixed structural sparsity masks extracted from the molecular graph with adaptive top-k attention heads that learn task-relevant long-range atom interactions, following principles from sparse transformer architectures such as BigBird and Longformer adapted to graph-structured inputs. The model is trained end-to-end on retrosynthetic feasibility labels using the same benchmarks as RetroGNN, with systematic ablations comparing dense attention, random sparsity, topology-only sparsity, and hybrid sparsity across multiple GPU architectures to characterize the speed-quality Pareto frontier.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled method for transferring sparse attention mechanisms from NLP to molecular graph encoding, providing both a new architecture (SparseRootFormer) and a systematic hardware-aware benchmarking framework for comparing attention-based and message-passing encoders in computational chemistry. The resulting encoder is expected to enable 3-10x throughput improvements for virtual screening pipelines without sacrificing predictive accuracy, directly accelerating drug discovery workflows. Additionally, the chemically-informed sparsity patterns offer interpretability advantages, revealing which long-range atomic interactions the model deems critical for synthesizability assessment.
proposals.novelty: While graph transformers have been explored, no prior work systematically designs sparsity patterns specifically for retrosynthetic root encoding by combining molecular topology constraints with learned chemical attention priors, nor benchmarks such architectures against GNN baselines with explicit hardware-efficiency metrics. The integration of domain-specific structural sparsity with adaptive attention heads creates a uniquely balanced architecture that neither pure graph methods nor generic sparse transformers can achieve.

proposals.title: MoE-Branch: Mixture-of-Experts Conditional Computation for Adaptive Graph Neural Network Inference
proposals.motivation: Graph neural networks face a fundamental tension between expressive power and computational cost: deeper, more expressive architectures impose prohibitive overhead on large-scale graphs, while lightweight models sacrifice accuracy on structurally complex subgraphs. Conditional computation, which activates only relevant model components per input, has transformed the scalability of language models and vision transformers, yet remains largely unexplored for per-node adaptive inference in GNNs. Bridging this gap is urgent because real-world graph tasks—from retrosynthesis feasibility screening to anatomical landmark detection in medical imaging—demand both speed and reliability across heterogeneous node neighborhoods.
