proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight per-node gating network that dynamically routes each node to one of several specialized computation heads—FiLM-style affine modulation, HyperNetwork-generated SVM classifiers, or a full expert sub-network fallback—will achieve accuracy comparable to the most expressive monolithic GNN baselines while reducing average per-node FLOPs by 40-60%, because graph nodes exhibit highly skewed complexity distributions where most nodes can be classified by cheap transformations and only a minority require expensive nonlinear experts.
proposals.method: We propose MoE-Branch, a drop-in message-passing layer augmented with a trainable gating module that receives each node's aggregated neighborhood embedding and outputs a sparse routing distribution over three expert heads: (1) a FiLM-like head applying learned affine feature modulation for structurally simple nodes, (2) a HyperSVM head that generates per-node linear classifier weights via a small hypernetwork for intermediate-complexity nodes, and (3) a full multi-layer expert head for rare high-complexity nodes. The gating network is trained end-to-end with a load-balancing auxiliary loss adapted from Switch Transformer to prevent expert collapse, combined with a computational budget regularizer that penalizes average routing cost. We evaluate on the standard benchmarks from the Benchmarking GNNs framework (ZINC, PATTERN, CLUSTER, and ogbg-molhiv) as well as on a retrosynthesis feasibility task inspired by RetroGNN, measuring both predictive performance and wall-clock inference time across varying sparsity budgets.
proposals.contribution: This work makes three contributions: first, it establishes the first systematic framework for per-node conditional computation in GNNs, demonstrating that heterogeneous expert routing is both trainable and effective at the node level; second, it provides empirical Pareto frontiers showing that MoE-Branch matches or exceeds top Benchmarking GNN baselines at 40-60% reduced computation, directly addressing the time-accuracy trade-off identified as a core challenge in GNN scalability; third, it offers practical guidelines for expert head design and load-balancing strategies specific to graph-structured data, where node degree distributions and neighborhood heterogeneity create routing dynamics qualitatively different from those in sequence or grid models.
proposals.novelty: While mixture-of-experts and conditional computation are well-studied in NLP and vision, their application to per-node routing in GNNs is non-obvious because graph nodes lack the regular positional structure that makes token-level gating straightforward—node complexity depends on local topology, feature heterogeneity, and task-specific difficulty in ways that require a topology-aware gating signal rather than a simple feature-based router. The combination of heterogeneous expert types (affine modulation, generated classifiers, and full sub-networks) within a single routing framework is itself novel and motivated by the observation that graph node classification difficulty follows a heavy-tailed distribution poorly served by uniform-capacity architectures.

proposals.title: Towards Mixture-Of-Experts Branchers With Conditional Computation: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to Use a lightweight gating network to choose among FiLM-like, HyperSVM-like, or expert fallback heads . This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mixture-of-experts branchers with conditional computation can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates method transfer principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: Use a lightweight gating network to choose among FiLM-like, HyperSVM-like, or expert fallback heads per node. This imports conditional computation fro.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the mixture-of-experts branchers with conditional computation problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Solver-State Contrastive Pretraining: Self-Supervised Representation Learning for Branch-and-Bound Variable Selection
proposals.motivation: Machine learning approaches to combinatorial optimization increasingly rely on imitation learning from expert solvers, yet the learned variable and constraint embeddings are trained end-to-end from scratch on each downstream task, ignoring the rich structural regularities shared across problem instances and search-tree states. Self-supervised pretraining has transformed NLP and vision by learning transferable representations before task-specific fine-tuning, but this paradigm remains unexplored for solver-state representations in mixed-integer linear programming (MILP). Developing pretrained embeddings for branch-and-bound states would reduce sample complexity, improve generalization to unseen instance distributions, and provide a principled alternative to the ad-hoc auxiliary diversity losses currently used to prevent representation collapse.
proposals.hypothesis: Contrastive pretraining of bipartite graph neural network embeddings—using instance discrimination across MILP instances and temporal coherence across nearby branch-and-bound tree states as self-supervised objectives—will yield variable-selection policies that generalize better to out-of-distribution instances and require significantly fewer expert demonstrations than end-to-end imitation learning baselines.
proposals.method: We encode each branch-and-bound state as a bipartite graph of variable and constraint nodes using a GNN architecture following Gasse et al. (2019). During pretraining, we construct positive pairs from (i) augmented views of the same MILP instance (e.g., random constraint permutations, variable relabeling, and objective perturbations) and (ii) temporally adjacent states in the same search tree, while negative pairs are drawn from different instances within a batch. We optimize an InfoNCE-style contrastive loss over pooled graph-level and node-level representations jointly. The pretrained encoder is then fine-tuned with standard imitation learning on strong branching demonstrations. We evaluate on heterogeneous MILP benchmark families (set cover, combinatorial auctions, facility location, and mixed instance collections) measuring closed gap, solving time, and few-shot transfer to held-out problem classes.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first self-supervised pretraining framework specifically designed for branch-and-bound solver states, establishing that transferable structure exists across diverse MILP families at the embedding level. We expect to demonstrate that pretrained models match or exceed fully supervised imitation learning with 5-10x fewer labeled demonstrations, and that the learned representations transfer across problem classes without catastrophic degradation. The framework provides a modular, reusable encoder that can serve as a foundation for multiple downstream solver-learning tasks beyond variable selection, including node selection and cutting-plane generation.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is well-studied in vision and graphs, its application to solver-state spaces is non-obvious because the relevant invariances—what makes two solver states semantically similar—are defined by optimization structure rather than perceptual similarity. Our key insight is that the branch-and-bound tree itself provides a natural curriculum of positive pairs through temporal locality, and that MILP symmetries (variable permutation, constraint scaling) offer domain-specific augmentations that no generic graph contrastive method would exploit.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Pretraining of Solver-State Embeddings for Improved Neural Combinatorial Optimization
proposals.motivation: Neural networks for combinatorial optimization (CO) often rely on imitation learning from expert solvers, which is data-inefficient and struggles with generalization. Pre-2026, self-supervised learning (SSL) has revolutionized representation learning in vision and language by leveraging unlabeled data, but its application to structured solver states (e.g., variable assignments, constraint graphs) in CO remains underexplored. This limits the ability to learn rich, transferable latent representations of the solving process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying contrastive or generative self-supervised pretraining to the evolving states of a combinatorial solver will yield more informative and generalizable node/variable embeddings, leading to improved sample efficiency and final solution quality in downstream imitation learning for CO tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a graph neural network (GNN) encoder for solver states, treating variable assignments and constraints as a dynamic graph. For pretraining, we will employ a multi-task SSL objective: (1) a contrastive loss (e.g., SimCLR) where positive pairs are states from nearby nodes in the solver's search tree, and (2) a masked variable/constraint prediction task inspired by BERT. This pretrained encoder will then be fine-tuned via behavioral cloning or DAgger to imitate a high-performance solver (e.g., a MILP solver's branching policy), replacing randomly initialized embeddings.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a framework for self-supervised representation learning in combinatorial search, potentially reducing the need for large labeled expert trajectories. It will provide empirical evidence on benchmark CO problems (e.g., SAT, MIS, TSP) showing that SSL-pretrained embeddings accelerate training convergence and enhance policy generalization compared to training from scratch or with simple auxiliary losses.
proposals.novelty: While SSL is common in other domains, its tailored application to the structured, sequential state space of a combinatorial solver—using search tree proximity for contrastive pairing—is novel. This moves beyond prior auxiliary diversity losses by creating a dedicated pretraining phase with richer, domain-specific objectives.

proposals.title: Confidence-Aware Retrosynthetic Planning: Calibrated Uncertainty Estimation for Selective Branching in Graph Neural Network-Based Synthesizability Prediction
proposals.motivation: Retrosynthetic planning models such as RetroGNN accelerate virtual screening by rapidly estimating synthesizability, yet they produce point predictions that offer no indication of when their rankings are unreliable. The original RetroGNN work reveals that prediction quality degrades substantially for certain molecular families and scales, meaning downstream decisions—such as which synthetic branches to expand—can be misguided without a reliability signal. Equipping these models with well-calibrated uncertainty estimates would allow chemists and automated planners to selectively defer to expensive but accurate retrosynthesis software only when the fast surrogate is likely wrong, improving both efficiency and trustworthiness.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating calibrated uncertainty estimation into graph neural network-based synthesizability predictors enables a selective prediction framework that can automatically identify unreliable branching decisions, achieving near-oracle screening performance by abstaining on the hardest molecular subsets while maintaining the computational speedup of the surrogate model on the majority of compounds.
proposals.method: We will augment RetroGNN-style architectures with three complementary uncertainty quantification strategies—MC-Dropout, deep ensembles, and spectral-normalized neural Gaussian processes (SNGP)—and train them on retrosynthesis feasibility datasets with temperature scaling for post-hoc calibration. A selective prediction module will use the calibrated confidence scores to rank molecules into act-or-defer categories: molecules above a learned confidence threshold are ranked by the fast model, while those below are routed to full retrosynthesis solvers. We will evaluate calibration quality (expected calibration error), selective prediction metrics (coverage-vs-accuracy curves), and end-to-end virtual screening hit rates across diverse molecular families, explicitly stratifying by the chemical scaffolds and molecular weight ranges identified as failure modes in prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled framework for confidence-aware retrosynthetic branching that bridges fast surrogate models and slow exact solvers, directly addressing the reliability gap in neural synthesizability estimation. It provides the first systematic comparison of uncertainty quantification methods for GNN-based retrosynthesis prediction, along with practical guidelines for when selective deferral improves screening outcomes. The resulting system should enable large-scale virtual screening campaigns that are simultaneously fast and trustworthy, reducing wasted synthesis effort on false-positive candidates.
proposals.novelty: While uncertainty estimation has been explored for molecular property prediction, its application to retrosynthetic feasibility ranking with explicit selective prediction and deferral to exact solvers is unexplored. The combination of graph-structured uncertainty quantification with a coverage-accuracy tradeoff framework tailored to the heterogeneous failure modes of synthesizability models—where certain chemical families systematically mislead the surrogate—represents a non-obvious integration of calibration theory with practical computational chemistry workflows.

proposals.title: Size-Depth Curriculum Learning for Scale-Generalizable Graph Neural Networks in Combinatorial Search
proposals.motivation: Graph neural networks trained on small problem instances consistently fail to generalize to larger, real-world-scale inputs—a critical barrier for deploying learned solvers in retrosynthesis planning, combinatorial optimization, and other search-intensive domains. Current training regimes expose models to fixed-size instances, causing them to overfit to size-specific structural regularities rather than learning transferable reasoning patterns. A principled curriculum that progressively increases both instance complexity and search depth could bridge this generalization gap, directly addressing a limitation observed across GNN benchmarks and retrosynthetic planning tools like RetroGNN.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-dimensional curriculum that jointly schedules training over increasing graph instance sizes and increasing search-tree depths will yield GNN models that generalize significantly better to out-of-distribution larger instances and deeper search horizons than models trained on uniform or single-dimension curricula, because gradually exposing the model to compounding structural complexity encourages learning of scale-invariant compositional representations.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-axis curriculum learning framework for GNN-based search models. The first axis progressively increases instance size (number of nodes/edges), while the second axis increases the required search depth (number of recursive expansion steps). Training begins on small graphs requiring shallow search and advances along both axes according to a competence-based pacing function that promotes the model to harder tasks only after mastering the current difficulty level. Concretely, we implement this on top of message-passing GNNs benchmarked on retrosynthetic route prediction (extending RetroGNN) and NP-hard combinatorial problems (e.g., TSP, SAT from standard GNN benchmarks), using a self-paced learning objective that reweights the loss by an anti-curriculum term penalizing already-mastered instances. We compare against uniform sampling, size-only curriculum, depth-only curriculum, and standard self-paced learning baselines across multiple generalization gaps (2x, 5x, 10x instance size increases).
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic study of joint size-depth curriculum learning for GNN-based combinatorial search, establishing empirically grounded pacing strategies that enable reliable out-of-distribution generalization. It offers a practical, architecture-agnostic training protocol that can be layered onto existing GNN solvers—including retrosynthesis tools—without modifying their inference procedure. The resulting framework is expected to substantially reduce the overfitting-to-small-instances phenomenon, making learned search methods viable for industrial-scale molecular design and logistics problems.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning has been explored for sequence models and single-task difficulty scaling, no prior work co-schedules instance size and search depth as orthogonal curriculum axes for graph-structured combinatorial problems. The insight that search depth introduces a fundamentally different complexity dimension from graph size—one compositional, the other structural—motivates a factored curriculum design that is non-obvious from either the curriculum learning or GNN generalization literatures alone.

proposals.title: HyperBranch: Meta-Learning Adaptive Branch-and-Bound Policies Across MILP Problem Families via Hypernetwork-Generated Variable Selection
proposals.motivation: Learned branching policies for mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) solvers have shown strong performance on individual problem distributions, yet they degrade sharply when deployed on unseen problem families, requiring expensive retraining. This brittleness limits practical adoption in operations-research pipelines where diverse MILP formulations arrive continuously. A meta-learning framework that enables rapid adaptation with minimal data from a new problem family would dramatically broaden the applicability of neural branching and reduce the computational overhead of specialization.
proposals.hypothesis: A hypernetwork that conditions branching-policy parameters on a compact latent representation of the MILP problem family can match or exceed family-specific trained models after only a small number of gradient steps on instances from a new family, significantly outperforming both from-scratch training and naive fine-tuning in the few-shot regime.
proposals.method: We propose HyperBranch, a two-level architecture: (1) a family encoder that maps a small support set of bipartite graph representations of MILP instances to a family embedding using a permutation-invariant graph neural network aggregator, and (2) a hypernetwork that generates the weights of a GNN-based variable-selection policy conditioned on this embedding. Training follows a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) outer loop over a distribution of MILP families (set cover, combinatorial auctions, facility location, scheduling), where the inner loop performs a few gradient updates on family-specific strong-branching imitation targets. At test time, given a handful of instances from an unseen family, the system produces an adapted brancher without full retraining. We additionally introduce a task-similarity regularizer that encourages smooth interpolation in the family embedding space, facilitating zero-shot transfer to structurally intermediate problem types.
proposals.contribution: HyperBranch will provide the first systematic meta-learning framework for MILP branching that transfers across structurally distinct problem families, establishing benchmarks for few-shot solver adaptation. It will demonstrate that hypernetwork-based parameter generation, when combined with graph-level family encodings, yields branching policies that generalize with 5-10x less data than fine-tuning baselines. The resulting open-source framework and cross-family evaluation protocol will serve as a foundation for future research on solver generalization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work conditions branching on per-instance features or trains family-specific models, no existing approach treats the problem family itself as a first-class meta-learning task and generates entire policy networks accordingly. The combination of bipartite-graph family encoders with hypernetwork weight generation for solver components is unexplored and non-obvious, bridging the meta-learning and combinatorial optimization communities in a way that neither has pursued independently.

proposals.title: CompressRetro: Quantization, Structured Pruning, and Compiler-Driven Optimization for Real-Time Retrosynthesis Prediction on Commodity CPUs
proposals.motivation: Graph neural networks for retrosynthetic feasibility scoring, such as RetroGNN, have demonstrated orders-of-magnitude speedups over full retrosynthesis software, yet their deployment in large-scale virtual screening campaigns still demands GPU infrastructure that is unavailable in many pharmaceutical and academic settings. Model compression techniques—quantization, structured pruning, and ahead-of-time compilation—have revolutionized NLP and vision inference on edge devices, but their systematic application to graph-structured scientific models remains largely unexplored. Closing this gap would democratize high-throughput synthesizability screening by enabling sub-millisecond per-molecule inference on standard CPU hardware.
proposals.hypothesis: Combining post-training mixed-precision quantization (INT8/INT4), structured channel pruning guided by sensitivity analysis on message-passing layers, and ahead-of-time compilation via frameworks such as TVM or TorchInductor can reduce RetroGNN-class model latency by 5-10x on commodity CPUs while preserving at least 95% of the original top-k retrosynthetic route prediction accuracy.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage compression pipeline applied to graph neural network architectures trained for retrosynthetic feasibility estimation. First, we perform layer-wise sensitivity analysis to identify which message-passing and readout layers tolerate aggressive structured pruning (removing entire channels) without degrading route-ranking metrics. Second, we apply quantization-aware fine-tuning with mixed-precision assignment, quantizing attention and aggregation operators to INT8 while keeping chemically sensitive bond-feature embeddings at FP16. Third, we compile the pruned-quantized model using TVM's graph-level and operator-level optimizations, fusing scatter-gather operations characteristic of GNN inference into CPU-vectorized kernels. We evaluate on established retrosynthesis benchmarks (USPTO-50k, PaRoutes) measuring latency, throughput, and route-prediction accuracy against the uncompressed baseline and against ONNX Runtime defaults.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes the first systematic benchmark for model compression applied to GNN-based retrosynthesis predictors, providing practitioners with Pareto-optimal configurations balancing speed and accuracy on CPU targets. It contributes reusable compression recipes—including GNN-specific pruning heuristics for message-passing architectures—that generalize beyond retrosynthesis to other molecular property prediction tasks. By demonstrating that million-molecule screening campaigns can run on a single multi-core server rather than a GPU cluster, the work substantially lowers the compute barrier for computational chemistry in resource-constrained laboratories.
proposals.novelty: While quantization and pruning are mature for CNNs and Transformers, their interaction with irregular graph topologies, variable-size molecular inputs, and scatter-gather aggregation operators introduces unique challenges that existing compression toolchains do not address. Our approach is the first to co-optimize structured pruning decisions with compiler-level kernel fusion specifically targeting the sparse, dynamic computation graphs characteristic of molecular GNNs, yielding compression ratios unattainable by applying standard recipes naively.

proposals.title: Bandit-Guided Policy Switching for Adaptive Branching in Branch-and-Bound Solvers
proposals.motivation: Neural branching policies such as FiLM-augmented GNNs have demonstrated strong variable-selection quality in mixed-integer linear programming, yet their per-node inference cost can dominate total solve time on easy subtrees. Conversely, cheap solver heuristics like reliability branching degrade on hard subproblems where learned policies shine. A principled mechanism for switching among policies of varying cost and quality at each node would capture the best of both worlds, but no existing framework treats this as an explicit online decision problem under nonstationary rewards.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the per-node choice among a portfolio of branching policies—ranging from expensive learned models to lightweight heuristics—as a contextual bandit problem with search-state features will reduce total solving time compared to any single fixed policy, by adaptively allocating expensive inference only where it yields sufficient dual-bound improvement.
proposals.method: We formulate policy selection at each branch-and-bound node as a contextual bandit whose context vector encodes current search statistics (gap, depth, LP relaxation features, recent bound improvements) and whose arms correspond to a FiLM-conditioned GNN, a distilled single-layer scoring head, and the solver's default reliability branching. We employ a variant of LinUCB with discounted rewards to handle the nonstationarity of the search process, where the reward signal is the dual-bound improvement normalized by wall-clock cost of the selected policy. The bandit is trained online within each solve instance, requiring no offline reward labels, and its exploration is warm-started from a small calibration set of heterogeneous MIP instances.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first online bandit framework for adaptive branching-policy switching inside branch-and-bound, bridging the online learning and combinatorial optimization communities. It provides a principled, theoretically grounded mechanism to manage the cost-quality trade-off that currently limits deployment of neural branching in production solvers. We expect 15-30% wall-clock speedups over the best fixed neural policy on standard MILP benchmarks while maintaining solution quality.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats neural branching as a monolithic replacement for solver heuristics, our approach is non-obvious in recognizing that the optimal policy varies within a single solve and that this variation can be captured by lightweight bandit algorithms operating on search-state context—importing online learning theory into a setting where it has not been applied despite being a natural fit.

proposals.title: DAgger-Branch: Dataset Aggregation for Distribution-Shift-Robust Neural Branching in Mixed-Integer Programming
proposals.motivation: Neural network policies trained via imitation learning on strong branching decisions have shown promise for accelerating mixed-integer programming (MIP) solvers, yet they suffer from a fundamental covariate shift: the branch-and-bound tree states encountered at deployment under the learned policy diverge significantly from those generated by the expert during training. This mismatch compounds along the depth of the search tree, leading to cascading errors that degrade solver performance on harder instances. Addressing this gap is critical for making learned branching policies reliable enough for production MIP solvers.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying DAgger-style online dataset aggregation—where the learned branching policy is periodically rolled out within the solver and the resulting tree states are re-labeled by the strong branching expert—will produce a training distribution that faithfully covers the states the hybrid policy actually visits, thereby significantly reducing the tree size and solving time compared to standard behavioral cloning of expert trajectories.
proposals.method: We extend the GNN-based branching architecture of Gasse et al. (2019) with an iterative DAgger training loop: in each round the current learned policy is deployed inside SCIP to solve a batch of MIP instances, collecting bipartite graph representations of the visited branch-and-bound nodes; these nodes are then labeled by querying the full strong branching oracle, and the augmented dataset is used to retrain the policy. To manage the computational cost of expert re-labeling deep in the tree, we introduce a selective annotation strategy that prioritizes nodes where the learned policy's confidence is lowest, reducing oracle calls by an estimated order of magnitude while preserving coverage of the critical distribution tail. We further incorporate a mixing schedule that interpolates between expert and learned policy rollouts across rounds, balancing exploration of novel states with stability of the training signal.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first principled application of interactive imitation learning to neural branching in MIP solvers, directly closing the train-deploy distribution gap that limits current approaches. We expect DAgger-Branch to reduce average solving time and tree size by 15-30% over behavioral cloning baselines on standard MIP benchmarks (MIPLIB, combinatorial auction, set cover), with the gains most pronounced on out-of-distribution instance sizes. The resulting framework also establishes a reusable protocol for applying interactive imitation learning to other sequential decision points within combinatorial solvers.
proposals.novelty: While DAgger is well-studied in robotics and game playing, its application to branching in branch-and-bound search trees is non-trivial because the state space is a dynamically growing tree rather than a fixed-horizon trajectory, and expert labeling (strong branching) is orders of magnitude more expensive per query than typical oracle calls—necessitating the novel selective annotation and policy-mixing components we propose.

proposals.title: Sub-Microsecond Learned Branching: Distilled Linear Scorers for Massively Parallel MILP Workloads
proposals.motivation: Modern operations-research pipelines frequently solve thousands of small-to-medium MILPs concurrently on shared CPU clusters—for example in column generation, Lagrangian decomposition, or scenario-based stochastic programming. State-of-the-art learned branchers based on graph neural networks or even lightweight MLPs impose per-node inference costs that become prohibitive when thousands of branch-and-bound trees compete for the same cores. Designing branching policies whose per-call cost approaches that of a single dot product would unlock neural branching gains in precisely the deployment regime where combinatorial explosion is most painful.
proposals.hypothesis: A linear scoring function distilled from a stronger GNN or MLP branching policy, combined with a small set of cheaply computed static variable features extracted once at the root node, can match or exceed the node-count efficiency of reliability pseudocost branching while adding less than one microsecond of overhead per branching decision, thereby reducing wall-clock time for massively parallel MILP workloads.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage pipeline. First, we train a full-capacity GNN branching policy via imitation learning on strong branching decisions across diverse MILP families (set cover, combinatorial auctions, facility location). Second, we extract a compact feature vector per variable at the root node—comprising LP relaxation statistics, objective coefficients, and constraint participation counts—and distill the GNN's branching scores into a single linear layer via regression, optionally augmented with a tiny two-layer bottleneck (hidden dimension ≤8) gated by a learned instance-difficulty scalar. Third, we benchmark throughput under a realistic parallel regime: N concurrent MILP solves sharing P CPU cores, measuring both node counts and wall-clock solve time as N/P grows from 1 to 256, comparing against SCIP's default branching, reliability pseudocosts, and the undistilled MLP teacher.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic study of the Pareto frontier between learned-brancher inference cost and branching quality under CPU contention. It delivers an open-source, solver-integrated ultra-light brancher that practitioners can drop into parallel MILP pipelines with negligible overhead. The empirical scaling curves will establish clear guidelines for when heavier neural branchers pay for themselves versus when the distilled linear scorer dominates.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on learning to branch has focused on improving node-count efficiency with increasingly expressive models, no study has explicitly optimized for the cost-per-decision axis under multi-tenant CPU sharing. The insight that root-node features can be computed once and amortized across all subsequent nodes, combined with aggressive distillation to a linear scorer, inverts the usual neural branching design philosophy and addresses a deployment bottleneck that the academic benchmarking paradigm—single MILP, dedicated resources—systematically overlooks.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Learned Branching Policies: Predicting Architecture Breakdown Thresholds Across Combinatorial Instance Sizes
proposals.motivation: Learned branching policies for mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) have shown strong performance on benchmark instances, yet practitioners lack principled guidance on when a given neural brancher will degrade as problem size grows. Current evaluations report accuracy on fixed-size benchmarks without characterizing the functional relationship between instance scale and solver performance, leaving a critical gap between research results and deployment decisions. Establishing predictive scaling laws—analogous to those transforming large language model research—would convert scattered empirical observations into actionable design tools for selecting or training branchers matched to expected workload sizes.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance of learned branching architectures (GNN-based, MLP-based, and hybrid imitation-learning branchers) follows power-law scaling relationships with respect to MILP instance size (measured by variable and constraint counts), and these relationships exhibit architecture-specific critical thresholds beyond which accuracy collapses and runtime overhead dominates solver gains, enabling predictive model selection without exhaustive per-size evaluation.
proposals.method: We will generate controlled MILP instance families (set cover, combinatorial auctions, facility location, maximum independent set) at four graduated scales—small (≤500 variables), medium (500–5000), big (5000–50000), and 2x-big (50000–100000)—using established generators from the Ecole library. For each scale, we train and evaluate three brancher architectures: a GNN-based policy following the bipartite graph formulation, an MLP policy operating on hand-crafted features, and a TreeGate hybrid that conditions on search-tree context. We measure solving time, primal-dual gap closure rate, branch-and-bound node count, and policy accuracy against strong branching oracles, then fit parametric scaling functions (power law, logistic saturation, and piecewise-linear models) using nonlinear least squares with bootstrap confidence intervals to identify breakpoint thresholds where each architecture transitions from beneficial to detrimental.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first systematic scaling-law characterization of learned branching policies, producing closed-form predictive equations that map expected instance size to recommended brancher architecture and anticipated performance envelope. The resulting design tool will allow operations research practitioners to select neural branchers without costly per-instance benchmarking, and will reveal fundamental computational bottlenecks—such as GNN message-passing saturation or feature-extraction overhead—that inform the next generation of scalable branching architectures. We will release all scaling curves, trained models, and instance generators as an open benchmark suite.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws have reshaped understanding of neural language models and supervised learning, no analogous framework exists for neural combinatorial optimization components embedded within classical solvers. Our approach is non-obvious because it treats the brancher not as a standalone predictor but as a component whose scaling behavior emerges from the interaction between neural inference cost and combinatorial search-tree structure—revealing phase-transition-like thresholds that cannot be predicted from either the neural or combinatorial perspective alone.

proposals.title: Multiscale Hybrid Inference Schedules: Hierarchical Checkpointing Between Expensive and Cheap Computation in Deep Networks
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning systems face a fundamental tension between inference quality and computational cost, with practitioners forced to choose between running expensive models everywhere or amortizing a single costly call across many cheap subsequent inferences. This binary framing leaves a large and unexplored design space of intermediate scheduling strategies that could yield superior quality-cost tradeoffs. As models scale to billions of parameters and are deployed in safety-critical and resource-constrained settings, principled methods for allocating heterogeneous computation across inference steps become essential.
proposals.hypothesis: A multiscale hierarchy of computation checkpoints—comprising infrequent expensive root evaluations, periodic moderate-cost shallow refresh steps, and frequent cheap deep inference calls—achieves strictly better quality-cost Pareto frontiers than either extreme of the spectrum (expensive everywhere or cheap-after-root-forever), because the hierarchical schedule can adaptively maintain representation fidelity at multiple temporal and structural scales.
proposals.method: We formalize the inference scheduling problem as a hierarchical policy over three or more computation tiers, each defined by model capacity, latency budget, and representation refresh rate. We parameterize schedules using a nested periodic structure (e.g., one root call every N steps, shallow refreshes every M < N steps, cheap inference otherwise) and optimize the schedule parameters via bilevel optimization, where the outer loop minimizes total compute subject to a quality floor and the inner loop trains lightweight adapter modules for the cheap and shallow tiers using knowledge distillation from the expensive tier. We evaluate on sequential decision-making benchmarks, autoregressive language modeling, and visual reasoning tasks, measuring both downstream accuracy and wall-clock cost across GPU clusters of varying scale.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces a formal framework for multiscale hybrid inference scheduling that generalizes prior work studying only the two extremes of computation allocation. We provide theoretical analysis showing conditions under which hierarchical schedules provably dominate flat schedules in quality-cost tradeoff, and we deliver practical algorithms for learning near-optimal schedules. The resulting methods enable practitioners to deploy large models in resource-constrained or latency-sensitive environments while maintaining substantially higher fidelity than pure amortization approaches.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats the choice between expensive and cheap inference as binary, our multiscale dual formulation reveals that hierarchical checkpoint schedules form a rich lattice of strategies with non-trivial interactions between tiers—the optimal refresh frequency at each level depends on the fidelity decay rate of representations below it, creating a coupled optimization problem that has no analogue in the single-checkpoint setting.

proposals.title: HybridSelect: Amortized GNN Embeddings at the Root with Lightweight Node-Selection Policies for Branch-and-Bound
proposals.motivation: Node selection—deciding which open node in the branch-and-bound search tree to explore next—is a critical decision that directly controls tree size and solver runtime, yet learned approaches remain dominated by simple best-first heuristics. Recent work on variable selection has shown that a hybrid strategy, running an expensive graph neural network only at the root node and propagating cheap per-node features thereafter, achieves near-GNN accuracy at a fraction of the cost. Transferring this same asymmetric-cost design to node selection is both natural and timely, as solvers routinely maintain thousands of open nodes where repeated full-GNN inference is infeasible.
proposals.hypothesis: A node-selection policy that computes rich GNN embeddings of the bipartite constraint–variable graph once at the root and then scores open nodes with a lightweight MLP over incrementally updated local features (dual bound gap, depth, branching history, and projected root embedding) will match or exceed the search-tree reduction of a full-GNN oracle selector while adding negligible overhead per node evaluation.
proposals.method: We encode the mixed-integer program at the root with a bipartite Graph Neural Network following the architecture of Gasse et al. (2019), producing per-variable and per-constraint embeddings. At each subsequent open node we construct a fixed-length feature vector composed of (i) the inherited root embedding projected through a learned linear map, (ii) cheap local statistics (relative dual bound gap, tree depth, pseudo-cost estimates, last branching variable identity), and (iii) a lightweight delta encoding of bound changes since the root. A small MLP scores each candidate node; the policy is trained via imitation learning on node-selection traces extracted from SCIP's oracle retrospective best-bound strategy on the MIPLIB 2017 benchmark collection and Ecole environments. We compare against best-first search, learned full-GNN node selection applied at every step, and the plunging-then-best-bound hybrid used in modern solvers, measuring solving time, node counts, and the Pareto front of accuracy versus inference overhead.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes a principled framework for cost-aware learned node selection in branch-and-bound, demonstrating that the expensive-root cheap-node paradigm generalizes beyond variable branching to a second core solver decision. We expect 30–50% node-count reductions relative to default SCIP node selection on heterogeneous MIPLIB instances with wall-clock speedups, closing the practicality gap that has prevented learned node-selection policies from deployment. The resulting open-source Ecole-compatible module will provide the community with a plug-and-play learned node selector.
proposals.novelty: While the hybrid expensive-root cheap-node decomposition has been explored for branching variable selection, no prior work transfers this design to node selection, despite the two problems being recognized duals within branch-and-bound. The key non-obvious insight is that root embeddings carry sufficient global structural information to remain predictive across a dynamically growing search tree, provided they are combined with incremental local features that capture the evolving optimization landscape at each open node.

proposals.title: Root-Conditioned Hybrid Models for Adaptive Cut Selection in Mixed-Integer Programming Solvers
proposals.motivation: Modern MILP solvers spend substantial time selecting which cutting planes to apply, yet default heuristics ignore instance-specific structure. Recent work demonstrated that a hybrid recipe combining cheap root-node-conditioned models with expensive expert policies yields strong branching decisions at a fraction of the cost. Cut generation exhibits an analogous quality-versus-computation trade-off—aggressive cut rounds tighten the dual bound but slow each LP relaxation—making it a natural next domain for the hybrid paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight graph neural network policy trained on root-node LP features can replicate the cut-selection quality of computationally expensive scoring rules (such as efficacy-weighted filtering or bound-improvement ranking), and a hybrid scheduler that delegates only ambiguous decisions to the expensive scorer will close the dual gap comparably to full expert scoring while reducing cut-selection overhead by at least 50%.
proposals.method: We encode candidate cuts and the current LP relaxation as a bipartite graph and train a GNN policy via imitation learning on trajectories collected from an expensive oracle that scores cuts by their dual-bound improvement. At the root node we extract conditioning features (constraint statistics, objective coefficients, initial LP gap) that parameterize a fast scoring head. During solving, the fast head filters the cut pool; only candidates with uncertain scores are forwarded to the full GNN scorer. We benchmark on the MIPLIB 2017 collection and NeurIPS ML4CO datasets, measuring primal-dual integral, total solving time, and the fraction of decisions delegated to the expensive model.
proposals.contribution: This work extends the hybrid cheap-versus-expensive decision framework from variable branching to cutting-plane management, establishing its generality as a solver-design principle. It provides the first learned adaptive cut selector that explicitly manages its own inference budget, and delivers practical solver speed-ups by reducing the per-round overhead of cut evaluation without sacrificing dual-bound quality.
proposals.novelty: While learned branching policies are well studied, learned cut selection remains nascent and no prior work applies a hybrid fast/slow delegation architecture to this setting. The root-conditioning mechanism, which amortizes instance understanding computed once at the root across all subsequent cut rounds, is a non-obvious design choice that exploits the empirical observation that cut effectiveness is largely predictable from root LP structure.

proposals.title: Cheap-and-Deep: Hierarchical Neural Branching via Direction Prediction and Candidate Pruning in Branch-and-Bound
proposals.motivation: Modern learned branching policies for mixed-integer programming (MIP) solvers achieve strong variable-selection performance but impose significant per-node inference cost, limiting their practicality on large instances. The branching decision naturally decomposes into complementary sub-decisions—which variable, which direction, and which candidates merit expensive evaluation—yet current methods conflate these into a single monolithic scorer. Exploiting the structural information already available at the root LP relaxation to cheaply resolve the easier sub-decisions before invoking a costly GNN scorer would substantially improve the scaling behavior of learning-based branch-and-bound.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight neural head trained on root-node LP features can accurately predict branching direction and prune unpromising candidate variables, so that a full-precision GNN scorer need only be invoked on a small filtered subset, yielding solver performance comparable to full scoring at a fraction of the computational cost.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage hierarchical architecture. The first stage is a shallow MLP head conditioned on root-LP statistics (reduced costs, fractionality, objective coefficients, constraint activity) that jointly predicts branching direction for each candidate variable and outputs a pruning score used to retain only the top-k candidates. The second stage applies a graph convolutional network, following the bipartite variable-constraint formulation of Gasse et al. (2019), only to the filtered candidate set. Both stages are trained end-to-end via imitation learning on strong branching decisions collected from SCIP, with an auxiliary direction-prediction loss derived from the sign of the strong-branching score differential. We evaluate on standard MIP benchmarks (Set Cover, Combinatorial Auction, Capacitated Facility Location) measuring solving time, node count, and per-node inference cost across scaling regimes.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled task decomposition of learned branching that separates cheap, structure-informed sub-decisions from expensive scoring, enabling learned branching to scale to instances 5-10x larger than current GNN-based methods handle in practice. It also provides the first systematic study of branching-direction prediction as an independent learning target, establishing baselines and transferability results that the MIP-ML community can build upon.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats variable selection as an atomic decision, we show that decomposing it into direction prediction and candidate pruning—using only information from the root relaxation—creates a natural inverse problem whose cheap solution dramatically reduces the workload of the expensive forward scorer, an insight that has not been explored in the learned branching literature.

proposals.title: When Not to Learn: A Meta-Predictor for Identifying Failure Modes of Learned Branching in Mixed-Integer Programming
proposals.motivation: Learned branching policies based on graph neural networks have shown impressive gains on many combinatorial optimization benchmarks, yet their performance degrades unpredictably on certain problem families and at larger scales—sometimes losing to classical heuristics like reliability pseudocost branching (RPB). Understanding and predicting these failure modes is critical for deploying ML-augmented solvers in production, where a single poor branching decision cascade can waste orders of magnitude more compute than a conservative fallback. A principled meta-predictor that flags when to distrust the learned policy would make hybrid solvers robust enough for real-world operations research pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight meta-classifier trained on cheaply extractable instance features (constraint matrix statistics, LP relaxation gap, variable-constraint graph topology, and early search-tree signals) can reliably predict, before or during the search, whether a learned branching policy will underperform RPB on a given instance or search phase, enabling an adaptive switching strategy that matches or exceeds the better of the two on every problem family.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage approach. First, we construct a large-scale diagnostic dataset by running both a GNN-based learned branching policy and RPB on diverse MIP families (set cover, combinatorial auctions, facility location, independent set, and scheduling) across multiple scales, recording per-instance performance gaps and per-node branching quality metrics. Second, we train a gradient-boosted meta-classifier and a small GNN meta-predictor on static instance features and dynamic search-phase features respectively, targeting a binary label (learned-policy-wins vs. heuristic-wins) as well as a continuous performance-gap estimate. The meta-predictor is integrated into SCIP via a callback that switches branching strategy at configurable tree-depth checkpoints, with the switching threshold tuned via Bayesian optimization on a held-out validation set.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a systematic empirical taxonomy of when and why learned branching fails, decomposed by problem structure, scale, and search phase; (2) an open-source meta-prediction framework that wraps any learned branching policy and adaptively falls back to classical heuristics; and (3) evidence that the 'inverse problem' perspective—learning when not to use ML—yields more reliable solver performance than either pure-ML or pure-heuristic strategies, establishing a new best practice for hybrid combinatorial optimization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on improving learned branching policies, this proposal inverts the question: rather than asking how to learn better, we ask when learning hurts. This inverse framing is non-obvious because it requires characterizing the failure surface of neural policies in a structured way, and it yields a practically deployable switching mechanism that is agnostic to the specific learned policy, making it composable with future advances in ML for branch-and-bound.

proposals.title: Budget-Conditioned Early-Exit Networks: Learning Branching Policies That Adapt Inference Depth to Hardware Constraints
proposals.motivation: Deep learning models are increasingly deployed across a heterogeneous spectrum of hardware—from edge CPUs to multi-GPU clusters—yet most architectures are trained for a single fixed compute regime, requiring costly retraining or distillation for each target platform. This rigidity wastes resources and limits equitable access to capable AI systems, a concern central to responsible AI deployment. A single model that gracefully degrades or scales its computation in response to an explicit hardware-budget signal would dramatically reduce deployment friction and democratize access to performant inference.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep network augmented with learned branching policies conditioned on a continuous hardware-budget token can match the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier of independently trained models across at least three distinct compute regimes (strict CPU, moderate shared-GPU, high-throughput accelerator), without requiring separate training runs or architecture modifications at deployment time.
proposals.method: We propose Budget-Conditioned Branching Networks (BCBNets), which embed a scalar or low-dimensional budget descriptor as a conditioning signal into a multi-exit transformer or residual network. At each candidate exit point, a lightweight gating module—parameterized as a small MLP receiving the budget token concatenated with intermediate representations—decides whether to exit or continue deeper computation. Training uses a budget-sampling curriculum: each mini-batch samples a hardware-budget level uniformly, and the loss combines task accuracy with a differentiable cost penalty calibrated to wall-clock latency profiles measured on representative hardware. We employ straight-through Gumbel-Softmax estimators for discrete exit decisions during training and validate on image classification (ImageNet), language understanding (GLUE), and retrosynthesis feasibility prediction benchmarks across CPU, single-GPU, and multi-GPU settings.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled framework for budget-conditioned inference that unifies early-exit, adaptive-depth, and model-scaling paradigms under a single trained policy; (2) empirical evidence that budget conditioning incurs minimal overhead compared to budget-specific baselines while eliminating the combinatorial cost of per-platform optimization; and (3) practical guidelines for calibrating budget tokens to real hardware profiles, enabling plug-and-play deployment across heterogeneous infrastructure without retraining.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior early-exit or once-for-all network approaches that treat compute cost as a static constraint during training, our method treats the hardware budget as a first-class input variable that the model reasons about at inference time, enabling smooth interpolation across compute regimes never explicitly seen during training—an approach that is the scaling dual of fixed-target deployment assumptions prevalent in current adaptive inference literature.

proposals.title: Dual-Metric Benchmarking of Learned Branching Policies Under Multi-Objective MILP Enumeration Workloads
proposals.motivation: Recent learned branching policies for mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) are evaluated almost exclusively on single-instance solving time, ignoring the dominant deployment pattern in which a solver must process large batches of structurally related instances—as arises in multi-objective optimization via the epsilon-constraint method or objective-space enumeration. This gap matters now because GPU-accelerated branchers show diminishing or even negative returns when amortized across portfolios of related subproblems, yet no standardized benchmark captures this regime. A dual benchmark that jointly measures per-instance solution quality and aggregate portfolio throughput is essential for guiding the next generation of practical learned solvers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that learned branching policies optimized for single-instance performance exhibit a Pareto-suboptimal trade-off between per-instance node reduction and portfolio-level wall-clock throughput when evaluated on multi-objective MILP enumeration workloads, and that policies explicitly trained on batched, structurally correlated instance streams can recover a superior dual-metric frontier.
proposals.method: We construct benchmark suites by generating multi-objective MILP families from established problem classes (multi-objective facility location, network design, and knapsack) and producing instance batches via systematic epsilon-constraint decomposition. We evaluate state-of-the-art learned branchers—including GNN-based imitation-learning policies and reinforcement-learning branchers—under a dual-metric protocol that records both per-instance integrality gap closure and end-to-end portfolio completion time, explicitly accounting for GPU inference latency and batch scheduling overhead. We then train a portfolio-aware branching policy using a multi-task graph neural network that shares representations across correlated subproblems and optimizes a scalarized dual objective combining node efficiency and throughput.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes the first standardized dual-metric benchmark for learned MILP branching under realistic multi-objective enumeration workloads, enabling the community to diagnose when GPU inference costs erode single-instance gains. It provides empirical scaling laws relating instance correlation, batch size, and the per-instance-versus-throughput trade-off across problem families. Finally, the portfolio-aware training protocol offers a concrete recipe for building learned branchers that remain competitive in deployment settings where thousands of related subproblems must be solved sequentially or in parallel.
proposals.novelty: Existing benchmarks treat each MILP instance as independent, masking the overhead structure that dominates real multi-objective campaigns. By jointly benchmarking per-instance quality and portfolio throughput on correlated instance streams, we expose a previously hidden failure mode of GPU-based learned branchers and provide the first training framework that directly optimizes for the dual metric, bridging the gap between academic evaluation and operational solver deployment.

proposals.title: Beyond Scalar Pitch: Evaluating Rich Spectro-Temporal Conditioning Representations for Neural Audio Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Recent neural vocoders and audio synthesis models have reported negative or marginal results when conditioning on scalar pitch (F0) and periodicity, leading some researchers to abandon conditioning-based paradigms altogether. However, scalar F0 is a lossy compression of the complex quasi-periodic structure of voiced sounds, discarding harmonicity profiles, micro-modulations, and phase relationships that carry perceptually critical information. Revisiting this assumed limitation is timely because modern differentiable signal-processing layers now make it feasible to extract and condition on richer spectro-temporal descriptors at training scale.
proposals.hypothesis: The failure of pitch and periodicity conditioning in neural audio synthesis stems not from an intrinsic limitation of conditioning-based architectures but from the poverty of scalar F0 and single-valued periodicity as conditioning signals; substituting structured multi-dimensional representations—harmonic amplitude envelopes, instantaneous frequency trajectories, and subband analytic phase summaries—will recover and surpass the synthesis quality of unconditioned models while retaining explicit pitch controllability.
proposals.method: We propose a controlled ablation study across three conditioning representation tiers: (1) baseline scalar F0 plus periodicity, (2) a mid-level representation comprising per-harmonic amplitude envelopes extracted via a differentiable harmonic-plus-noise decomposition, and (3) a rich representation combining instantaneous frequency contours estimated through analytic signal computation with learned subband phase summary vectors. Each representation tier will be injected via Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) layers into a shared WaveNet-style backbone and a parallel HiFi-GAN generator, trained on the same multi-speaker dataset (VCTK and an internal singing voice corpus). We will evaluate using objective metrics (PESQ, UTMOS, F0-RMSE, voiced/unvoiced error rate) and a MUSHRA-style listening test, with statistical significance assessed via paired bootstrap tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence disentangling whether conditioning failure in neural vocoders is architectural or representational, directly informing the design of next-generation controllable synthesis systems. By open-sourcing the extraction pipeline and FiLM-injection modules, we lower the barrier for the community to adopt richer conditioning without redesigning entire model architectures. If confirmed, the results reopen the conditioning paradigm as a viable path toward interpretable, steerable audio generation.
proposals.novelty: Prior work has treated the negative pitch-conditioning result as evidence against the conditioning paradigm itself, conflating representation adequacy with architectural capacity. Our proposal is non-obvious because it reframes a widely accepted negative result as a representation-engineering problem and introduces subband analytic phase summaries—never previously used as conditioning features—as a compact yet information-rich alternative to raw phase, which is notoriously difficult to model directly.

proposals.title: Phase-Anchored Non-Autoregressive Generation: Disentangling Initial Phase Ambiguity from Parallel Decoding Limitations
proposals.motivation: Non-autoregressive (NAR) models promise significant speedups for sequence generation but consistently underperform autoregressive baselines, particularly in domains with continuous-valued outputs such as audio waveform and spectrogram synthesis. The prevailing explanation attributes this gap to the fundamental difficulty of modeling dependencies without sequential factorization, yet recent evidence suggests that much of the degradation concentrates in phase-sensitive reconstruction rather than in amplitude or high-level structure. Identifying and resolving the true bottleneck—initial phase ambiguity rather than parallelism per se—would reframe the design space for efficient generative models across speech, music, and scientific signal domains.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary quality gap between autoregressive and non-autoregressive generative models in phase-sensitive domains arises not from the absence of sequential token dependencies, but from the lack of a canonical initial phase anchor; providing or inferring this anchor enables NAR models to match autoregressive quality while retaining full parallel decoding speed.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage framework: (1) a lightweight phase-anchor module—a small autoregressive or variational network that predicts or samples a canonical initial phase vector from a global conditioning representation, (2) a phase-canonicalization layer that normalizes all target frames relative to this anchor before training, removing the rotational degeneracy in the loss landscape, and (3) a standard NAR transformer decoder that generates all remaining frames in parallel conditioned on the inferred anchor. We will evaluate on speech synthesis (LJSpeech, LibriTTS) and music generation (MusicNet) using both reconstruction metrics (multi-resolution STFT loss, phase error) and perceptual metrics (PESQ, FAD), comparing against fully autoregressive, fully non-autoregressive, and iterative-refinement baselines. Controlled ablations will isolate the contribution of the phase anchor from other architectural differences.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic empirical test of whether initial phase ambiguity, rather than non-autoregression itself, is the dominant bottleneck in parallel signal generation. If confirmed, it offers a principled and lightweight architectural fix—phase anchoring—that preserves the O(1) decoding advantage of NAR models while closing much of the quality gap. Beyond audio, the phase-canonicalization principle generalizes to any domain where outputs exhibit gauge-like symmetries that create spurious multimodality in the loss landscape, potentially benefiting molecular conformation generation and time-series forecasting.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored iterative refinement and knowledge distillation to improve NAR quality, no study has isolated initial phase ambiguity as a separable and independently solvable sub-problem. The insight that a single cheap inference step—phase anchoring—can break a rotational degeneracy and convert a hard multimodal prediction problem into a unimodal one amenable to parallel decoding is non-obvious and challenges the assumption that sequential factorization is inherently necessary for phase-coherent generation.

proposals.title: Adaptive Memory Selection for Autoregressive Audio Synthesis: Learning Phoneme- and Timbre-Dependent Context Windows
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive audio models condition on a fixed number of previous samples, yet the temporal dependencies in audio signals vary dramatically—fricatives require only milliseconds of context while reverb tails and sustained bass notes demand hundreds of milliseconds or more. This mismatch means fixed-context models either waste computation on short-memory signals or underfit long-range dependencies, limiting both efficiency and perceptual quality. Developing context-adaptive mechanisms is critical for scaling neural audio synthesis to diverse, real-world acoustic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: An autoregressive audio model equipped with a learned memory selection mechanism that dynamically adjusts its effective context window based on local signal characteristics will achieve superior perceptual quality and lower reconstruction error compared to fixed-context baselines, while using fewer total conditioning samples on average.
proposals.method: We propose a gated memory controller module inserted before the autoregressive decoder that predicts, for each generation step, both a context length and an attention mask over a maximum-length history buffer. The controller is a lightweight recurrent network that receives a compressed representation of recent spectral features and outputs a soft selection distribution over past samples, trained end-to-end with the reconstruction loss augmented by a sparsity penalty on context usage. We evaluate on LibriSpeech, NSynth, and a reverberant room-impulse-response dataset, measuring Fréchet Audio Distance, per-phoneme segmental SNR, and computational cost relative to fixed-context WaveRNN and SampleRNN baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first learnable, signal-adaptive context mechanism for sample-level autoregressive audio models, providing both a practical architectural module and an empirical characterization of how optimal context length varies across phonetic and timbral categories. The resulting models are expected to improve perceptual quality on heterogeneous audio while reducing average inference cost, and the learned context patterns will offer interpretable insights into the temporal memory structure of natural sounds.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats context length as a fixed hyperparameter or uses uniform self-attention windows, our approach learns a content-dependent dynamic context policy directly from data, drawing on principles from adaptive computation and sparse attention but applying them at the raw waveform sample level—a regime where the diversity of required memory spans is far greater than in text or symbolic sequences.

proposals.title: Replacing Fully Connected Conditioning Stacks with Explicit Autocorrelation and Periodicity Operators in Deep Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Fully connected (FC) conditioning layers are ubiquitous in modern generative architectures, yet their computational role remains poorly understood. Recent work hypothesizes that FC stacks succeed because they implicitly learn autocorrelation structure that small convolutional filters cannot capture efficiently. If this hypothesis is correct, replacing learned FC layers with principled signal-processing operators should yield equivalent or superior performance with far fewer parameters, offering both interpretability gains and architectural efficiency at a time when model compression and mechanistic understanding are critical research priorities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the representational advantage attributed to fully connected conditioning stacks can be fully recovered—or exceeded—by substituting them with explicit differentiable autocorrelation estimators and learnable periodicity detectors, demonstrating that FC layers in these architectures are not learning abstract nonlinear mappings but are instead serving as inefficient proxies for classical DSP primitives.
proposals.method: We propose a drop-in replacement module called AutoCorr-Condition, comprising three components: (1) a differentiable autocorrelation layer that computes windowed autocorrelation via FFT-based Wiener-Khinchin estimation, (2) a learnable harmonic basis bank parameterized by frequency, phase, and decay that captures periodic structure, and (3) a lightweight gating mechanism that blends these operator outputs before injection into the main network. We will evaluate this module by replacing FC conditioning stacks in three representative architectures—WaveNet-style vocoders, FiLM-conditioned image generators, and neural audio synthesis models—comparing parameter count, training convergence, and output quality (FID, MOSNet, reconstruction loss) against FC baselines and ablations on standard benchmarks including LJSpeech, CIFAR-10, and NSynth.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a mechanistic explanation for why FC conditioning layers are effective, grounding an architectural choice in signal-processing theory rather than heuristic design. It provides a practical, parameter-efficient alternative module that researchers can adopt in any conditioning pipeline. More broadly, it advances the program of reverse-engineering learned representations by showing that supposedly general-purpose dense layers can be decomposed into interpretable, domain-specific operators—connecting deep learning architecture design to classical signal processing in a falsifiable way.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted that FC layers may capture correlation structure, no study has directly tested whether purpose-built autocorrelation and periodicity operators can serve as a functionally equivalent replacement. Our approach is non-obvious because it challenges the prevailing assumption that the flexibility of dense layers is essential, proposing instead that their success is an artifact of over-parameterization relative to a structured signal-processing solution that can be specified a priori.

proposals.title: Chunked Autoregressive Waveform Synthesis Without Pre-Aligned Conditioning: Learning Alignment Jointly with Generation
proposals.motivation: Chunked autoregressive models have demonstrated strong performance in spectrogram inversion tasks where the conditioning signal is temporally aligned with the target waveform by construction. However, many practically important generation scenarios—such as direct text-to-waveform synthesis or generation from weakly aligned control signals—lack this convenient alignment. Understanding whether chunked autoregressive mechanisms can jointly learn alignment and high-fidelity waveform generation would significantly broaden their applicability and reduce reliance on intermediate representations like spectrograms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chunked autoregressive waveform synthesis does not fundamentally require pre-aligned conditioning signals; instead, a learned alignment module integrated into the chunked generation framework can jointly infer temporal correspondence and produce waveforms of comparable quality to systems operating with oracle alignment, provided the model is equipped with an explicit soft-alignment mechanism between conditioning and chunk boundaries.
proposals.method: We propose augmenting a chunked autoregressive waveform generator with a differentiable monotonic attention module that learns to align unaligned conditioning sequences (e.g., phoneme or token sequences) to chunk boundaries during training. The model processes variable-length conditioning inputs through a location-sensitive attention mechanism that produces per-chunk context vectors, which are then consumed by the autoregressive chunk generator. We will systematically compare three settings: (1) oracle-aligned spectrograms (upper bound), (2) learned alignment from phoneme sequences, and (3) learned alignment from raw text embeddings, evaluating on LJSpeech and LibriTTS using both objective metrics (PESQ, UTMOS, MCD) and subjective listening tests. Ablations will isolate the contribution of monotonicity constraints, chunk size, and attention initialization strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes the first systematic investigation of whether the chunked autoregressive paradigm generalizes beyond pre-aligned conditioning to settings requiring joint alignment learning. We expect to identify the conditions under which alignment-free chunked generation matches or approaches the quality of alignment-privileged baselines, and to characterize failure modes (e.g., chunk boundary artifacts, attention collapse) that arise when alignment must be inferred. The resulting framework would enable chunked autoregressive models to serve as unified end-to-end architectures for diverse audio generation tasks without requiring intermediate aligned representations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on chunked autoregressive synthesis has exclusively operated in the aligned-conditioning regime, and prior work on attention-based TTS has focused on frame-level autoregression, no study has examined the interaction between learned alignment and chunk-level autoregression. The non-obvious insight is that chunk boundaries introduce a coarser temporal granularity that may actually simplify the alignment problem compared to frame-level models, but simultaneously risk compounding alignment errors across chunks in ways that frame-level models do not.

proposals.title: Pitch-Periodic Dilated Convolutions: Expanding the Causal Receptive Field for Low-Latency Neural Waveform Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Autoregressive neural vocoders such as WaveRNN and LPCNet achieve high-quality speech synthesis but are constrained to small output chunks because enlarging the receptive field with standard dilated convolutions destroys the phase coherence needed for natural-sounding pitch periodicity. Generating larger chunks per forward pass is critical for reducing inference latency on edge devices, yet naive receptive-field expansion introduces audible artifacts at pitch-period boundaries. A principled architecture that scales the causal context window while preserving periodicity structure would simultaneously improve synthesis quality and real-time efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: A generator architecture that couples pitch-conditioned dynamic dilation schedules with harmonic-prior gating can maintain autoregressive phase bias across receptive fields spanning multiple pitch periods, enabling chunk sizes 4-8x larger than current models without degradation in pitch accuracy or perceptual quality.
proposals.method: We propose Pitch-Periodic Dilated Convolutions (PPDC), where dilation rates in causal convolutional layers are dynamically set as integer multiples of the local fundamental-period estimate, ensuring that each layer's receptive field aligns with harmonically meaningful time scales. A lightweight pitch estimator feeds a dilation-scheduling module that adjusts per-layer dilation factors in real time. We augment this with a harmonic-prior gating mechanism inspired by neural ODE stability constraints from Bengio and collaborators' work on learning causal structure, which multiplicatively gates hidden activations using a learned harmonic template so that phase continuity is structurally enforced rather than learned from data alone. Training uses a multi-resolution STFT loss combined with a novel periodicity-consistency penalty that measures phase coherence across chunk boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a new family of pitch-aware causal convolutions that decouple receptive-field growth from phase-coherence degradation, enabling autoregressive waveform generators to synthesize chunks of 4-8x larger size at equivalent or better perceptual quality. The harmonic-prior gating mechanism provides a theoretically grounded inductive bias for periodic signal generation that can be incorporated into any dilated-convolution-based vocoder. We expect measurable improvements in both real-time factor on CPU and MOS scores relative to fixed-dilation baselines such as WaveRNN and LPCNet.
proposals.novelty: Unlike fixed dilation schedules that treat all temporal scales uniformly, PPDC dynamically aligns convolutional receptive fields to the signal's own periodicity structure, making large-context autoregression pitch-aware by construction rather than by data-driven memorization. The combination of pitch-conditioned dynamic dilation with harmonic-prior gating is, to our knowledge, unexplored and offers a structurally motivated solution to a problem previously addressed only through ad hoc architectural tuning.

proposals.title: Seamless Stitching: Learned Transition Priors for Artifact-Free Chunk-Boundary Synthesis in Autoregressive Neural Vocoders
proposals.motivation: Chunked autoregressive GAN (CARGAN) vocoders enable real-time streaming synthesis by generating audio in fixed-size blocks, yet audible clicks and discontinuities persist at chunk boundaries, particularly for small chunk sizes and pitched musical signals where phase coherence is perceptually critical.
