These boundary artifacts limit deployment in low-latency music generation and high-quality voice synthesis, creating a gap between streaming efficiency and perceptual fidelity. Eliminating such artifacts without sacrificing the latency advantages of chunked generation would unlock practical real-time neural audio for interactive and creative applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly conditioning each chunk's initial hidden state and first few generated samples on a learned transition prior—trained to enforce waveform continuity, phase coherence, and spectral smoothness across boundaries—will suppress boundary clicks and reduce perceptual artifacts to inaudible levels, even at chunk sizes below 320 samples, without increasing inference latency.
proposals.method: We propose a lightweight Transition Continuity Module (TCM) inserted at each chunk boundary in the CARGAN generator: a small recurrent network that ingests the final overlap context of the previous chunk and produces a boundary embedding used to initialize the next chunk's generation. Training combines three objectives: (1) a multi-scale boundary STFT loss penalizing spectral discontinuity in a narrow window around each transition, (2) an adversarial boundary discriminator that receives only the stitched overlap region and must distinguish real from generated transitions, and (3) a differentiable instantaneous-frequency consistency loss enforcing phase continuity for harmonic signals. We will evaluate on both speech (LJSpeech, VCTK) and music (NSynth, MAESTRO) at chunk sizes of 160, 320, and 640 samples, measuring PESQ, periodicity error, a new Boundary Click Rate metric based on energy transient detection, and subjective MUSHRA listening tests.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) the first dedicated modeling framework for chunk-boundary artifact suppression in autoregressive neural vocoders, (2) a set of boundary-aware training objectives that can be integrated into any chunked waveform generator, and (3) systematic perceptual and objective benchmarks quantifying boundary artifacts across chunk sizes and audio domains. By decoupling transition modeling from the main generator, the approach preserves CARGAN's real-time capability while closing the quality gap with non-streaming models.
proposals.novelty: Prior chunked vocoders treat boundary handling implicitly through overlap-add or simple cross-fading, whereas our approach learns an explicit transition prior with dedicated adversarial and phase-coherence supervision—directly targeting the perceptual signatures of boundary artifacts rather than relying on generic waveform reconstruction losses to suppress them incidentally.

proposals.title: Signal-Adaptive Chunk Sizing for Neural Audio Synthesis via Learned Regime Detection
proposals.motivation: Current neural vocoders and waveform synthesizers process audio in fixed-size chunks, forcing a rigid tradeoff between temporal resolution for periodic signals and computational efficiency for stationary regions. This limitation produces either boundary discontinuities at chunk edges or periodicity artifacts within chunks, degrading perceptual quality in real-time synthesis applications. A method that dynamically adjusts processing granularity to match the local signal regime would simultaneously improve quality and throughput, addressing a key bottleneck in deployable speech and music generation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight learned policy network that estimates local signal regime—voicing state, periodicity confidence, and spectral stationarity—can select chunk sizes online such that synthesis quality matches or exceeds the best fixed chunk size per regime while reducing overall computation by allocating large chunks to stationary regions and small chunks only where periodicity demands fine resolution.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component architecture: (1) a regime classifier, implemented as a small recurrent network operating on mel-spectrogram frames, that outputs a categorical chunk-size decision (from a discrete set of 2–5 sizes) along with continuous periodicity confidence and stationarity scores; and (2) a chunk-adaptive neural vocoder backbone (e.g., modified HiFi-GAN or WaveGlow) that conditions its upsampling and synthesis layers on the selected chunk size. The regime classifier is trained jointly with the vocoder using a composite loss combining multi-resolution STFT reconstruction, a periodicity-aware loss penalizing pitch errors in voiced regions, and a regularization term encouraging larger chunks to promote computational savings. We employ straight-through Gumbel-Softmax estimation for the discrete chunk-size selection to enable end-to-end gradient flow.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first framework for online, learned chunk-size adaptation in neural audio synthesis, replacing the current practice of manual hyperparameter tuning with a principled, data-driven mechanism. It provides a formal decomposition of the chunk-size tradeoff into measurable signal-regime components—voicing, periodicity confidence, and stationarity—enabling interpretable and controllable synthesis behavior. The resulting system is expected to achieve state-of-the-art audio quality on standard vocoder benchmarks while demonstrating measurable latency reduction in streaming inference scenarios.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats chunk size as a static architectural choice, this approach frames it as a sequential decision problem conditioned on local signal properties, drawing on reinforcement-learning-inspired policy gradients within a generative model. The coupling of a regime-detection module with adaptive-resolution synthesis is non-obvious because it requires maintaining waveform phase coherence across variable-length segments, which we address through overlap-aware boundary conditioning.

proposals.title: Meta-Learned Dynamic Loss Rebalancing for Multi-Objective Autoregressive Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Autoregressive generative models optimized with composite losses (e.g., feature-matching plus mel-spectrogram reconstruction) suffer from shifting loss landscapes as conditioning contexts change across training phases, leading to artifacts such as metallic distortions in speech synthesis. Current practice relies on expensive manual hyperparameter sweeps to retune loss weights per domain or training stage, which does not scale to multi-domain or continually adapting systems. An automatic, principled mechanism for dynamically rebalancing these losses would improve both training efficiency and output quality across generative modalities.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning module that treats composite loss weights as learnable functions of training dynamics—specifically gradient magnitudes, loss curvature, and autoregressive conditioning statistics—can match or exceed the perceptual quality achieved by exhaustive manual tuning while eliminating the need for per-domain weight schedules.
proposals.method: We propose a lightweight auxiliary network, trained via online bi-level optimization, that ingests running statistics of per-loss gradients, loss values, and autoregressive context length, and outputs scalar weights for each loss component at every training step. The outer objective optimizes a held-out perceptual quality metric (e.g., PESQ or FID depending on modality) using implicit differentiation through the inner training loop, following recent advances in learning rate and hyperparameter scheduling. We validate on autoregressive speech synthesis (feature-matching + mel loss) and extend to autoregressive image generation (reconstruction + adversarial + perceptual losses) to demonstrate generality.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a general-purpose, modality-agnostic framework for automatic loss balancing in autoregressive generative training, removing a critical manual bottleneck. It provides theoretical grounding connecting dynamic loss weighting to multi-task learning and Pareto-optimal gradient descent, and delivers empirical evidence across speech and image domains showing artifact reduction and faster convergence compared to fixed and heuristic scheduling baselines.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior multi-task balancing methods (e.g., GradNorm, uncertainty weighting) that assume stationary task relationships, our approach explicitly models the non-stationarity introduced by autoregressive conditioning—where the effective difficulty and scale of each loss component shift as the model conditions on increasingly long generated contexts—making it uniquely suited to sequential generative training.

proposals.title: Phase-Aware Objectives for Neural Audio Synthesis: Discriminators and Losses That Explicitly Enforce Phase Continuity
proposals.motivation: Neural audio synthesis models predominantly optimize mel-spectrogram reconstruction and adversarial losses that operate on magnitude representations, leaving phase coherence as an implicit byproduct rather than a directly supervised quantity. This oversight leads to audible artifacts—buzzing, metallic coloring, and discontinuities at chunk boundaries—that degrade perceptual quality, especially in streaming and real-time applications. Because autoregressive generators implicitly track instantaneous-frequency state, there is a timely opportunity to design explicit phase-aware training signals that close this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing auxiliary losses and discriminator heads that directly evaluate phase-spectrum structure—specifically instantaneous-frequency smoothness, group-delay consistency, and cross-chunk phase continuity—will yield statistically significant improvements in perceptual audio quality over equivalent architectures trained with only mel-reconstruction and waveform-domain adversarial objectives, without increasing inference cost.
proposals.method: We propose a three-component framework: (1) a differentiable instantaneous-frequency continuity loss computed via the finite-difference of unwrapped STFT phase, penalizing sudden jumps that violate the harmonic model; (2) a phase-spectrum discriminator that receives complex-valued STFT frames (or equivalent real-imaginary pairs) and is trained adversarially to distinguish real from generated phase trajectories at multiple temporal resolutions; and (3) a chunk-boundary phase-consistency regularizer that minimizes the L1 distance between the predicted and extrapolated phase at overlap regions during streaming synthesis. These components are integrated as auxiliary objectives into an autoregressive neural vocoder backbone (e.g., a WaveRNN or SampleRNN variant) and evaluated against mel-only and multi-resolution STFT baselines on LJSpeech and VCTK using PESQ, POLQA, and a newly proposed Phase Continuity Score (PCS).
proposals.contribution: This work contributes the first systematic study of explicit phase-aware training objectives for neural vocoders, providing both the loss functions and an open-source discriminator architecture that can be dropped into existing GAN-based or hybrid autoregressive synthesis pipelines. We expect to demonstrate that phase-explicit supervision reduces perceptual artifacts measurably—targeting at least 0.15 PESQ improvement—and that the chunk-boundary regularizer substantially improves streaming quality, enabling lower-latency deployment without quality regression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored multi-resolution spectral losses and complex-valued representations, no existing approach decomposes the phase supervision problem into instantaneous-frequency smoothness, adversarial phase-trajectory discrimination, and explicit cross-chunk consistency as separable, composable objectives. The insight that autoregressive structure already maintains implicit phase state means these losses provide a complementary inductive bias rather than a redundant signal, which is non-obvious given that magnitude-focused losses have long been considered sufficient.

proposals.title: PolyPitch: Multi-Harmonic Phase Coupling for Polyphony-Aware Neural Waveform Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current neural waveform synthesis models leverage periodicity priors built around a single fundamental frequency (F0) trajectory, which works well for speech but degrades when multiple simultaneous pitches are present, as in polyphonic music. Polyphonic audio introduces beating patterns, overlapping harmonic series, and phase interactions that single-F0 assumptions cannot capture. Bridging this gap is critical for high-fidelity music generation and any audio domain where multiple pitched sources co-occur.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly conditioning waveform generators on a multi-pitch representation—where each detected pitch contributes its own phase trajectory and harmonic template—will reduce spectral artifacts and improve perceptual quality in polyphonic synthesis compared to single-F0 conditioned baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-pitch extension to periodicity-aware neural vocoders by replacing the single-F0 excitation signal with a superposition of per-pitch phase-locked sinusoidal banks, each derived from a multi-pitch estimator. A lightweight harmonic interaction module, implemented as a gated residual network, learns to model cross-pitch phase coupling and beating effects. The system is trained end-to-end on mixed polyphonic music datasets using a combination of multi-resolution STFT loss and a novel per-harmonic phase coherence loss that penalizes phase drift independently for each detected pitch.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic framework for injecting multi-pitch periodicity structure into neural audio synthesis, extending the single-F0 paradigm to polyphonic settings. It will produce a reusable multi-pitch excitation module compatible with existing vocoder architectures, along with a benchmark evaluation protocol for polyphonic waveform quality. The expected outcome is measurably improved synthesis of chords, ensemble passages, and multi-speaker scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats polyphonic synthesis as an undifferentiated sequence modeling problem or relies on source separation as preprocessing, our approach directly models the interaction of multiple concurrent harmonic structures within the generation process itself, avoiding error propagation from separation and capturing emergent phenomena like combination tones and beating that arise only from joint modeling.

proposals.title: Latency-Constrained Streaming Vocoding via Chunk-Causal Conditioning and Adaptive Degradation
proposals.motivation: Neural vocoders are increasingly deployed in real-time speech synthesis, dialogue systems, and live audio streaming, where end-to-end latency directly impacts user experience and system viability. While chunked autoregressive GANs (CARGAN) demonstrated that causal conditioning enables streaming synthesis, no principled framework exists for jointly optimizing chunk size, lookahead budget, and synthesis quality under hard latency constraints. Bridging this gap is critical for deploying high-fidelity neural vocoders on resource-limited edge devices and in interactive applications where round-trip latency must remain below perceptual thresholds.
proposals.hypothesis: A vocoder trained with a latency-aware objective that explicitly penalizes future-feature dependence and dynamically adjusts chunk boundaries can maintain perceptually comparable synthesis quality to offline models while satisfying strict per-chunk latency budgets, and will degrade gracefully—rather than catastrophically—when future conditioning features are partially or fully unavailable.
proposals.method: We extend the CARGAN framework in three ways. First, we introduce a latency-constrained training objective that augments the adversarial and multi-resolution STFT losses with a differentiable penalty proportional to the deviation of per-chunk inference time from a target latency budget, using a Lagrangian relaxation to balance quality and speed. Second, we design an adaptive chunk buffering mechanism that learns, via a lightweight policy network, to select chunk sizes and lookahead windows conditioned on the current spectral complexity of the input, allocating larger buffers for transient-rich segments and smaller ones for stationary regions. Third, we implement a stochastic feature masking curriculum during training—progressively dropping future mel-spectrogram frames with increasing probability—so the model learns robust causal representations that degrade smoothly rather than failing abruptly when future context is curtailed at inference time.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes the first unified framework for training streaming neural vocoders under explicit latency constraints, providing both a differentiable training formulation and a practical adaptive buffering strategy. It establishes quantitative degradation curves relating lookahead availability to perceptual quality metrics (PESQ, UTMOS), giving practitioners actionable guidance for latency-quality tradeoffs. The resulting system is expected to enable deployment of high-quality neural vocoding on mobile and embedded platforms where fixed-latency guarantees are mandatory.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior streaming vocoders that treat chunk size as a static hyperparameter and accept whatever quality results, our approach makes the latency-quality tradeoff a first-class learnable objective and introduces stochastic future-feature masking as a curriculum strategy—drawing on ideas from dropout regularization and causal representation learning—to produce models that are inherently robust to variable lookahead availability rather than merely tolerant of a single fixed configuration.

proposals.title: Perceptual Artifact Decomposition Metrics: Learning Artifact-Specific Objective Measures for Neural Vocoder Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current neural vocoder evaluation relies heavily on aggregate spectral metrics like pitch RMSE and mel-cepstral distortion, yet these correlate poorly with human perceptual preference because they fail to isolate the distinct artifact types that dominate listener judgments. Perceptually salient distortions such as boundary clicks at phoneme transitions, voiced-unvoiced decision errors, and periodicity glitches each degrade perceived quality through different psychoacoustic mechanisms, but no existing objective metric framework targets them individually. Designing artifact-decomposed metrics would enable more informative vocoder benchmarking, faster development iteration, and better loss functions for end-to-end speech synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: A set of specialized detectors trained to identify and quantify distinct vocoder artifact categories—boundary discontinuities, voicing decision errors, and periodicity perturbations—will each individually achieve stronger correlation with human perceptual ratings than pitch RMSE, and their learned combination will approach or exceed the predictive power of full-reference perceptual models like PESQ and ViSQOL on vocoder-degraded speech.
proposals.method: We will first construct a large-scale artifact-annotated dataset by having trained listeners label segments of vocoder-generated speech from multiple architectures (WaveNet, WaveRNN, HiFi-GAN, UnivNet) with fine-grained artifact tags covering boundary clicks, voiced-unvoiced misclassifications, sub-harmonic distortions, and metallic buzzing. Using this dataset, we train a multi-task deep neural network with artifact-specific detection heads built on a shared learned auditory representation, leveraging perceptually motivated front-ends such as learnable gammatone filterbanks. Each head outputs both a binary detection score and a continuous severity estimate per short-time frame, and we aggregate these into per-utterance artifact-specific scores. Finally, we learn a lightweight fusion model mapping the artifact-specific scores to overall perceptual quality, and we validate the entire framework against large-scale mean opinion score listening tests across diverse speaker and language conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled taxonomy of neural vocoder artifacts grounded in psychoacoustic analysis, a publicly released artifact-annotated speech corpus, and a modular evaluation toolkit that provides interpretable per-artifact quality scores rather than a single opaque number. The artifact-decomposed metrics will serve as targeted diagnostic tools enabling vocoder developers to pinpoint and address specific failure modes, and the learned fusion model will offer a new state-of-the-art objective predictor of perceptual vocoder quality. Beyond evaluation, the differentiable artifact detectors can be incorporated as auxiliary loss terms during vocoder training to directly penalize perceptually dominant distortions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats speech quality prediction as a monolithic regression task, our approach decomposes evaluation into artifact-specific channels aligned with distinct perceptual failure modes, yielding both interpretability and improved predictive accuracy. The combination of psychoacoustically motivated artifact taxonomy with learned detection heads on shared auditory representations is, to our knowledge, the first framework that provides actionable, artifact-level diagnostic feedback for neural vocoder development.

proposals.title: Adaptive Autoregressive Context Windows via Learned Acoustic Conditioning for Unified Speech and Music Generation
proposals.motivation: Current neural audio generation models use fixed-length autoregressive contexts, forcing a compromise between the short dependencies of speech and the long-range temporal structure of music. This mismatch leads to either wasted computation on speech or insufficient context for musical coherence, and becomes acute as unified audio models are expected to handle diverse signal types within a single architecture. A principled mechanism for dynamically allocating context length would improve both quality and efficiency across audio domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned context selection module, conditioned on local spectral and temporal features of the input signal, can dynamically predict the optimal autoregressive history length per generation step, matching or exceeding the quality of domain-specific fixed-context models while reducing average computational cost.
proposals.method: We propose augmenting a Transformer-based autoregressive audio codec language model with a lightweight context gate network—a small auxiliary network that ingests a sliding window of mel-spectrogram features and outputs a soft attention mask over past hidden states, effectively modulating the receptive field at each decoding step. Training proceeds in two phases: first, oracle context lengths are estimated by measuring reconstruction loss as a function of context size across a large corpus of speech (LibriLight) and music (MusicCaps, FMA); second, the gate network is trained jointly with the main model via a combined objective of reconstruction quality and a context-length regularization penalty that encourages parsimony. We evaluate on speech continuation, music continuation, and mixed-domain audio editing tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first adaptive-context mechanism for autoregressive audio generation that eliminates the need for domain-specific context engineering. It provides empirical evidence quantifying how optimal context length varies with signal type, frequency content, and reverberation characteristics, offering new insight into the temporal dependency structure of natural audio. The resulting model is expected to serve as a more efficient and flexible backbone for multi-domain audio generation systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats context length as a global hyperparameter or relies on fixed hierarchical structures, our approach learns a data-driven, instance-level context allocation policy, drawing on meta-learning principles to generalize across acoustic domains without manual tuning—an idea that, despite its simplicity, has not been explored in the audio generation literature.

proposals.title: Differentiable Phase-Vocoder Integration for Neural Waveform Generation: Bridging Classical DSP and Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: Neural vocoders such as HiFi-GAN and WaveGlow produce high-fidelity audio but treat phase as an implicit byproduct of learned convolutions, leading to phase discontinuities and tonal artifacts especially in pitch-sensitive applications. Classical phase vocoders have solved phase coherence for decades through explicit instantaneous-frequency tracking and phase accumulation, yet this principled mechanism has not been transferred into end-to-end trainable generators. Closing this gap matters now because real-time speech synthesis, singing voice conversion, and music generation all demand both the quality of neural methods and the phase consistency guarantees of DSP.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding a differentiable phase-vocoder update rule—where instantaneous frequency estimates drive explicit phase accumulation—inside a neural waveform generator will yield measurably improved phase coherence and perceptual quality compared to purely learned generators, without sacrificing inference speed.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid generator architecture in which a neural backbone (e.g., a multi-receptive-field convolutional network) predicts frame-wise instantaneous frequency and amplitude envelopes, and a differentiable phase-vocoder module performs cumulative phase integration via a straight-through-estimator-augmented summation to maintain gradient flow. The phase-vocoder layer converts these accumulated phases and amplitudes into a waveform via an inverse short-time Fourier transform, and the entire pipeline is trained end-to-end with a combination of multi-scale spectral loss, phase-aware loss on group delay deviation, and adversarial losses from multi-period and multi-scale discriminators. We will benchmark on LJSpeech and VCTK, measuring PESQ, POLQA, phase distortion metrics, and MOS, comparing against HiFi-GAN, BigVGAN, and Vocos baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled inductive bias for phase modeling in neural audio synthesis by showing that classical DSP operations can be made fully differentiable and integrated as architectural modules rather than post-processing steps. It provides the community with a reusable differentiable phase-vocoder layer that can be inserted into existing generator architectures, and it establishes that explicit phase tracking reduces artifacts in challenging scenarios such as unseen speakers and expressive prosody. The approach exemplifies a broader paradigm of transferring mature signal-processing algorithms into neural network components with formal guarantees.
proposals.novelty: While prior neural vocoders learn phase implicitly and classical vocoders track it explicitly but are not trainable, no existing method embeds the full phase-vocoder update equation as a differentiable layer inside a GAN-trained generator. The non-obvious insight is that the cumulative summation in phase accumulation, despite being a recurrence, admits efficient parallel-scan computation and stable gradients when paired with instantaneous-frequency parameterization, making it practical for real-time synthesis without autoregressive bottlenecks.

proposals.title: Harmonic-Plus-Residual Neural Vocoding: A Source-Filter Hybrid Architecture with Explicit Pitch Excitation for Robust Speech Synthesis
proposals.motivation: State-of-the-art neural vocoders such as HiFi-GAN and WaveNet still exhibit systematic artifacts on sustained vowels, high-pitched speakers, and tonal languages—failure modes that correlate strongly with pitch and periodicity modeling errors. Classical speech DSP solved harmonic structure decades ago via source-filter decomposition and sinusoidal modeling, yet modern end-to-end systems discard this inductive bias, forcing networks to rediscover periodicity from data alone. Bridging these paradigms is timely because recent work on differentiable digital signal processing (DDSP) has demonstrated that hybrid architectures can be both expressive and parameter-efficient, but no systematic framework exists for integrating source-filter priors into large-scale neural vocoding pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: A vocoder architecture that factorizes speech generation into a deterministic harmonic excitation component—synthesized via a differentiable oscillator bank conditioned on explicit F0 and harmonic amplitudes—and a learned stochastic residual module will produce higher-fidelity speech with fewer pitch-related artifacts than purely neural baselines, while requiring significantly fewer parameters and less training data to converge.
proposals.method: We propose a two-branch generator: (1) a harmonic branch that takes predicted F0 contours and harmonic amplitude envelopes as input and produces a quasi-periodic excitation signal through a bank of differentiable sinusoidal oscillators followed by a lightweight learned all-pole filter modeling the vocal tract transfer function; (2) a residual branch consisting of a compact WaveNet-style or GAN-based module that captures aperiodic components (fricatives, transients, breathiness) by modeling the difference between the target waveform and the harmonic reconstruction. Both branches are trained jointly end-to-end with a multi-resolution STFT loss, a pitch-weighted spectral loss that emphasizes harmonic structure, and an adversarial loss for perceptual quality. We will evaluate on LJSpeech, VCTK (multi-speaker), and a tonal-language corpus (Mandarin) to stress-test periodicity handling across diverse conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled architectural framework that unifies classical source-filter theory with modern neural generation, providing both a theoretical justification grounded in speech production physics and empirical evidence of superiority on pitch-sensitive evaluation metrics. The explicit harmonic prior is expected to yield substantial improvements on difficult cases—high F0 speakers, singing voice, and tone languages—where current systems degrade, while simultaneously reducing model size and training cost. Additionally, the modular design enables interpretable control over voice characteristics (e.g., breathiness, harmonics-to-noise ratio) that monolithic neural systems cannot offer.
proposals.novelty: While DDSP introduced differentiable oscillators for musical instruments, our approach is non-obvious in three ways: (1) we decompose the neural vocoder itself into a physics-informed harmonic pathway and a learned residual pathway trained adversarially, rather than using a single additive-synthesis model; (2) we incorporate a differentiable all-pole filter within the harmonic branch to capture formant structure, coupling source-filter theory directly into the gradient flow; and (3) we introduce a pitch-weighted spectral loss that forces the model to prioritize the very harmonic structures where neural vocoders systematically fail.

proposals.title: Phase-Locked Recurrent Oscillators for Chunk-Coherent Neural Vocoding
proposals.motivation: Autoregressive and flow-based neural vocoders achieve high-fidelity speech synthesis yet still suffer from audible discontinuities at chunk boundaries in streaming settings, because each chunk is generated without an explicit model of the underlying periodic structure. Control and communications engineering solved an analogous synchronization problem decades ago with phase-locked loops (PLLs), which maintain a running estimate of phase and frequency that smoothly tracks a reference signal. Importing this inductive bias into neural audio synthesis is timely because real-time, low-latency speech generation is now a deployment bottleneck for conversational AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A recurrent hidden-state module whose update rule is structurally analogous to a digital phase-locked loop—decomposing its state into explicit phase and frequency components updated via a learned phase-error signal—will reduce inter-chunk phase discontinuities and improve perceptual quality in streaming neural vocoding compared to standard GRU or LSTM hidden states of equal capacity.
proposals.method: We design a Phase-Locked Recurrent Unit (PLRU) cell that maintains a two-dimensional oscillator state (instantaneous phase and frequency) and updates it each frame using a differentiable phase-detector network that compares predicted and observed spectral phase, a learned loop filter that smooths the error, and a numerically-controlled oscillator step that advances the phase estimate. The PLRU replaces the recurrence in a WaveRNN-style vocoder backbone; the oscillator state is carried across streaming chunks so that each new chunk begins phase-aligned with the previous one. We train on LJSpeech and LibriTTS with a multi-resolution STFT loss augmented by an explicit phase-continuity penalty at chunk boundaries, and evaluate using PESQ, POLQA, and a boundary-discontinuity metric defined as the mean absolute phase jump at chunk seams.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a novel recurrent cell design that embeds classical PLL dynamics inside a learnable neural module, providing a principled inductive bias for periodic signal tracking; (2) empirical evidence that explicit phase-frequency state decomposition outperforms generic recurrent states for streaming vocoding quality; and (3) an analysis connecting the learned loop-filter bandwidth to the stability-responsiveness trade-off familiar from PLL theory, offering interpretability uncommon in neural audio synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While prior neural vocoders treat phase implicitly through large autoregressive or flow-based models, no existing architecture encodes the phase-locked loop's structural decomposition—phase detector, loop filter, numerically-controlled oscillator—as a differentiable recurrent cell. This transfers a well-understood control-theoretic mechanism into deep generative modeling, yielding an interpretable hidden state whose components map directly onto physical signal properties rather than opaque activation vectors.

proposals.title: Overlap-Add Autoregressive GANs: Eliminating Boundary Artifacts in Chunked Neural Sequence Generation via Classical Windowing
proposals.motivation: Chunked autoregressive generation is essential for real-time and memory-efficient deployment of GANs on long sequences such as audio waveforms, time-series, and even image scanlines, yet naive chunk boundaries introduce audible clicks or visible seams that degrade output quality. The overlap-add (OLA) framework with carefully designed analysis-synthesis windows has been the standard solution for boundary-artifact-free reconstruction in speech coding and STFT processing for decades, but this principled signal-processing technique has not been systematically adapted to the latent-space and adversarial-training setting of modern generative models. Bridging this gap is timely because chunked neural vocoders and streaming generative models are rapidly entering production, and ad-hoc crossfade heuristics currently used lack theoretical grounding and waste compute.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a learnable overlap-add windowing scheme into the chunk interface of autoregressive GANs will eliminate boundary discontinuities while preserving the causal, local-context advantages of chunked generation, achieving output quality statistically indistinguishable from full-sequence generation at a fraction of the memory cost.
proposals.method: We propose OLA-GAN, which partitions the generation target into overlapping chunks whose overlap ratio and window shape are co-optimized with the generator and discriminator. Concretely, the generator produces chunks with symmetric tapered extensions (initialized as Hann windows but parameterized as learnable basis functions), and a differentiable OLA reconstruction layer merges them before the discriminator sees the full sequence; an auxiliary multi-scale boundary discriminator explicitly penalizes discontinuities at chunk junctions. We enforce the Princen-Bradley perfect-reconstruction constraint as a soft regularizer so that the learned windows provably sum to unity in the overlap region, and we train with a composite loss combining adversarial, multi-resolution spectral, and a novel boundary-smoothness term computed on first- and second-order finite differences across chunk edges.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first principled framework for artifact-free chunked autoregressive GAN generation grounded in classical signal-processing theory, delivering a plug-in OLA module that can be retrofitted to existing architectures such as HiFi-GAN, WaveGAN, and streaming diffusion vocoders. We expect to demonstrate that OLA-GAN matches or exceeds full-sequence baselines in perceptual quality metrics (PESQ, POLQA, FID) while reducing peak memory by 4-8x, enabling real-time streaming on edge devices. The learnable window framework also opens a new design axis for any generative model operating on sequential data in chunks.
proposals.novelty: While overlap-add is textbook in linear signal processing, its transfer to nonlinear, adversarially-trained generative models is non-obvious because GAN generators do not satisfy the superposition principle; our key insight is that enforcing the perfect-reconstruction constraint in a differentiable reconstruction layer, combined with a boundary-aware discriminator, recovers the seamlessness guarantees of classical OLA even in the learned, nonlinear setting. This reframing turns a fixed signal-processing primitive into a trainable module with provable reconstruction properties, which has not been explored in the generative modeling literature.

proposals.title: Neural Bayesian Filtering for Latent Phase and Periodicity Estimation in Streaming Audio
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning models for audio processing typically operate on fixed-length chunks, discarding inter-chunk temporal coherence of periodic structure such as pitch and voicing. This leads to boundary artifacts, phase discontinuities, and degraded performance on low-frequency or quasi-periodic signals. Bayesian filtering offers a principled framework for maintaining uncertainty over latent acoustic state across time, yet its integration with learned feature extractors remains largely unexplored in audio.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network augmented with a differentiable Bayesian filtering module—maintaining explicit posterior distributions over phase, voicing state, and fundamental frequency across streaming audio chunks—will achieve superior periodicity tracking and noise robustness compared to purely feedforward or autoregressive baselines, particularly in low-SNR and ambiguous spectral regions.
proposals.method: We propose a hybrid architecture combining a learned front-end encoder (operating per-chunk) with a differentiable extended Kalman filter (EKF) or particle filter that propagates beliefs over a structured latent state comprising instantaneous phase, voicing probability, and harmonic amplitudes. The state-transition and observation models are parameterized by small neural networks trained end-to-end via reparameterized gradient estimation. We evaluate on pitch tracking (PTDB-TUG, MDB-stem-synth), voice activity detection, and speech enhancement benchmarks, comparing against CREPE, PYIN, and Transformer-based streaming baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces a principled mechanism for streaming audio models to carry calibrated uncertainty over periodic latent structure across arbitrary chunk boundaries, eliminating ad-hoc overlap-add heuristics. It establishes new state-of-the-art on streaming pitch tracking under noise while providing interpretable posterior estimates that signal unreliable regions—critical for downstream safety-aware speech systems.
proposals.novelty: While Kalman filters have a long history in signal processing, their differentiable integration as learned latent-state modules within modern deep audio architectures is unexplored. The key non-obvious insight is that explicitly factoring the latent space into phase, voicing, and spectral components—mirroring known acoustic physics—yields a structured prior that dramatically improves sample efficiency and robustness compared to black-box recurrent alternatives.

proposals.title: Temporal Seam Critics: Transferring Video-Domain Transition Discriminators to Eliminate Boundary Artifacts in Chunk-Based Neural Audio Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Chunk-based neural vocoders such as CARGAN enable low-latency streaming audio synthesis but produce audible click artifacts at chunk boundaries, degrading perceptual quality and limiting deployment in real-time applications. Video generation has independently developed sophisticated temporal consistency discriminators that penalize visual seam artifacts at frame boundaries and shot transitions, yet these architectural insights have not been transferred to the audio domain. Bridging this cross-modal gap is timely because both domains share the fundamental problem of enforcing smooth continuity across independently generated temporal segments.
proposals.hypothesis: A temporal seam critic architecture, adapted from video-domain transition discriminators to operate on spectro-temporal representations at audio chunk boundaries, will significantly reduce perceptible click artifacts in chunk-based neural vocoders without increasing inference latency, because the underlying temporal discontinuity structure is analogous across modalities.
proposals.method: We propose Temporal Seam Critics (TSC), a family of auxiliary discriminator modules that operate specifically on short windows straddling chunk boundaries in the mel-spectrogram and waveform domains. Drawing from video temporal consistency discriminators that evaluate motion coherence and luminance continuity across frame transitions, we design a multi-scale 1D convolutional critic that receives concatenated overlapping boundary regions from adjacent chunks and learns to distinguish real continuous audio from chunk-boundary-containing synthesized audio. The TSC loss is combined with the existing CARGAN adversarial and multi-resolution STFT losses during training, requiring no architectural changes to the generator and adding zero overhead at inference time since the critic is only used during training. We further introduce a boundary-focused data augmentation strategy that synthetically creates hard negative examples by splicing unrelated audio segments, forcing the critic to learn fine-grained continuity features.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled cross-modal transfer framework showing that temporal consistency enforcement techniques from video generation can be systematically adapted to audio synthesis, opening a broader research direction in modality-agnostic temporal coherence methods. Concretely, we expect TSC-augmented CARGAN to achieve measurable reductions in boundary artifacts as assessed by both objective metrics (spectral discontinuity, PESQ, periodicity error at boundaries) and subjective listening tests, while maintaining the low-latency streaming capability. The approach generalizes beyond CARGAN to any chunk-based or streaming neural audio system, including real-time text-to-speech and music generation.
proposals.novelty: While temporal discriminators are well-studied in video synthesis, their explicit transfer to audio chunk-boundary artifact suppression is unexplored; the non-obvious insight is that the spectro-temporal structure of audio chunk seams is sufficiently analogous to video frame transitions that discriminator architectures and training strategies can cross the modality boundary with minimal adaptation. Additionally, framing boundary artifacts as a discriminative rather than purely reconstructive problem enables the model to learn perceptually relevant continuity features that handcrafted overlap-add or crossfade heuristics cannot capture.

proposals.title: Periodicity-Aware Training of Neural Audio Synthesizers via Differentiable Autocorrelation and Cepstral Auxiliary Losses
proposals.motivation: Neural audio synthesis models trained with mel-spectrogram reconstruction losses often produce artifacts in voiced and harmonic regions because the mel basis smears fine periodic structure across frequency bands. Classical signal analysis provides precise, well-understood descriptors of periodicity—autocorrelation, cepstrum, and harmonic peak ratios—yet these have not been systematically incorporated as differentiable training objectives. Bridging this gap is timely because recent vocoders and codec-based speech models are reaching perceptual quality ceilings that better periodicity supervision could break through.
proposals.hypothesis: Adding differentiable auxiliary losses derived from short-time autocorrelation and real-cepstrum representations to the training objective of neural audio synthesizers will significantly reduce pitch and voicing errors compared to mel-only and multi-resolution STFT baselines, without increasing inference cost.
proposals.method: We implement three differentiable auxiliary loss terms: (1) a normalized short-time autocorrelation distance computed on windowed waveform frames, penalizing discrepancies in lag-domain periodicity between generated and reference audio; (2) a real-cepstral envelope loss that compares liftered cepstral coefficients capturing spectral envelope and harmonic spacing; and (3) a harmonic peak ratio loss that measures the energy concentration at integer multiples of the estimated fundamental frequency. All three are computed via standard PyTorch FFT operations and require no additional learned parameters. We integrate these losses into HiFi-GAN and EnCodec decoder training as weighted auxiliary terms alongside existing adversarial and mel losses, sweeping loss weights via Bayesian hyperparameter optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic study of classical periodicity descriptors as differentiable training losses for neural audio synthesis, yielding a modular loss toolkit that can be dropped into any waveform-generation pipeline. We expect measurable improvements in gross pitch error, voicing decision error, and PESQ on standard speech benchmarks (LJSpeech, VCTK), along with improved harmonic fidelity in singing voice synthesis. The approach demonstrates a principled method-transfer strategy: importing decades of DSP knowledge directly into gradient-based learning rather than relying on learned discriminators to implicitly discover periodic structure.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses multi-resolution spectral losses or learned period discriminators, no existing approach directly supervises the autocorrelation and cepstral domains that classical speech analysis identifies as the canonical representations of periodicity. The non-obvious insight is that these fixed, well-characterized transforms provide complementary gradient signals to mel losses—autocorrelation enforces fine temporal regularity while the cepstrum constrains harmonic envelope shape—yielding a richer error surface without adding learnable parameters or inference overhead.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Temporal Memory for Autoregressive Audio: A Scaling Study on Long-Context Modeling of Low-Frequency Content, Reverberation, and Musical Structure
proposals.motivation: Current autoregressive audio generation models operate on fixed context windows that are fundamentally too short to capture low-frequency waveform periods, room reverberation tails (often exceeding one second), and long-form musical structure such as verse-chorus repetition. This mismatch leads to well-documented artifacts including bass thinning, premature reverb truncation, and structural incoherence in generated music. As generative audio models move toward professional-quality synthesis, solving the long-context problem is both scientifically important for understanding temporal hierarchy in sequence models and practically critical for real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical memory architecture that maintains multiple temporal resolutions—operating at sample-level, frame-level, and section-level granularities—will enable autoregressive audio models to faithfully reproduce low-frequency content, realistic reverberation decay, and coherent long-form musical structure, with perceptual quality scaling log-linearly with effective context length across these three dimensions.
proposals.method: We propose a three-tier hierarchical autoregressive architecture: (1) a local sample-level transformer operating on short windows for high-frequency detail, (2) a mid-level recurrent memory module with learned downsampling that compresses frame-level representations to capture bass fundamentals and reverb envelopes at 50-500ms scales, and (3) a sparse global attention mechanism over section-level embeddings for multi-second structural coherence. We will conduct a systematic scaling study varying effective context length from 0.5s to 60s across datasets of anechoic music, room impulse responses of varying RT60, and full multi-instrument arrangements. Evaluation will combine spectral fidelity metrics (log-spectral distance below 200Hz), perceptual reverb coherence scores (energy decay curve correlation), and human listening tests for structural plausibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first empirical scaling laws relating effective context length to perceptual quality in autoregressive audio generation, decomposed across frequency bands and structural timescales. The hierarchical memory architecture will provide a reusable module that can be integrated into existing audio generation frameworks to extend their temporal reach without quadratic attention cost. The resulting scaling curves will offer practical guidance for practitioners on how to allocate computational budget across temporal resolutions for specific audio generation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored longer contexts for language and image generation, no systematic scaling study has characterized how autoregressive context length interacts with the distinct physical timescales inherent in audio—wave periods, acoustic decay, and musical form. The multi-resolution memory hierarchy is specifically designed around these physical timescales rather than treating audio as a generic sequence, enabling principled compression that preserves perceptually critical long-range dependencies while remaining computationally tractable.

proposals.title: Subband Chunked Autoregression: Frequency-Adaptive Temporal Resolution for Scalable Sequence Modeling
proposals.motivation: Autoregressive models for long sequences face a fundamental tension between context length and computational cost. Recent work on chunked autoregression and neural audio codec models reveals that low-frequency content demands longer temporal dependencies while high-frequency detail can be adequately captured with shorter context windows. By allocating computational resources according to the intrinsic temporal scale of each frequency band, we can simultaneously extend effective context and reduce overall complexity, addressing a critical bottleneck in scaling generative sequence models.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-rate autoregressive architecture that decomposes input sequences into frequency subbands and assigns each subband a chunk size inversely proportional to its center frequency will achieve superior long-range coherence on low-frequency structure while maintaining generation quality on high-frequency detail, outperforming uniform-chunk baselines at equivalent or lower computational cost.
proposals.method: We propose a subband chunked autoregression (SCA) framework that first decomposes sequential data into K frequency bands via a learned filterbank or fixed QMF analysis. Each subband is processed by a dedicated autoregressive transformer operating at a band-specific temporal resolution: low-frequency bands use large chunks with extended causal context windows, while high-frequency bands use smaller chunks with shorter context. Cross-band conditioning is achieved through a lightweight hierarchy where coarse (low-frequency) predictions condition fine (high-frequency) generation at each step, inspired by multi-scale latent variable models. We validate on neural speech synthesis and music generation benchmarks, measuring perceptual quality (PESQ, FAD), long-range coherence (pitch tracking accuracy over 30-second windows), and FLOPs relative to uniform-chunk and full-sequence autoregressive baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces a principled framework for frequency-aware computational allocation in autoregressive models, demonstrating that matching temporal resolution to signal bandwidth yields better scaling behavior than uniformly increasing context length. It provides both theoretical analysis connecting subband chunk sizes to information-theoretic rate-distortion bounds and empirical evidence across audio generation tasks, establishing a new design axis for efficient long-context generative modeling.
proposals.novelty: While multi-scale and hierarchical generation have been explored, no prior work systematically ties autoregressive chunk granularity to frequency-band bandwidth in a unified architecture. The key non-obvious insight is that the optimal chunk size is not a single hyperparameter but a spectrum of values dictated by the signal's spectral structure, and that this decomposition yields superlinear computational savings because low-frequency bands, which require the longest context, also have the lowest sample rates after decimation.

proposals.title: TinyCARGAN: Knowledge Distillation and Structured Pruning of Chunked Autoregressive GAN Vocoders for Real-Time Speech Synthesis on Edge CPUs
proposals.motivation: Neural vocoders have achieved remarkable speech quality, yet deploying them on resource-constrained edge devices remains challenging due to high computational demands. CARGAN introduced a chunked autoregressive approach that preserves pitch accuracy through explicit autoregressive structure, but its sequential chunk dependencies and model size still exceed the compute budgets of mobile and embedded CPUs. Enabling high-fidelity, pitch-faithful speech synthesis on edge hardware would unlock offline voice assistants, accessibility tools, and low-latency on-device TTS in settings where cloud inference is impractical or undesirable.
proposals.hypothesis: A combination of task-aware structured pruning, chunk-level knowledge distillation from a full-size CARGAN teacher, and post-training integer quantization can reduce model size and inference cost by 4-8x while retaining perceptually equivalent speech quality and the superior pitch tracking that distinguishes CARGAN from non-autoregressive GAN vocoders.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage compression pipeline. First, we apply structured pruning guided by Taylor-expansion importance scores to the WaveNet-style residual blocks within the CARGAN generator, removing up to 60% of channels while monitoring pitch RMSE degradation. Second, we perform chunk-level knowledge distillation where the pruned student is trained to match both the waveform output and intermediate feature maps of the original CARGAN teacher, using a combined adversarial plus multi-resolution STFT loss augmented with a pitch-contour consistency term. Third, we apply dynamic fixed-point INT8 quantization with quantization-aware fine-tuning to further reduce latency on ARM and x86 edge CPUs. We benchmark on LJSpeech and VCTK, measuring RTF on Raspberry Pi 4 and smartphone-class processors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematically compressed autoregressive GAN vocoder targeting edge CPUs, establishing Pareto frontiers between model size, real-time factor, and perceptual quality (PESQ, UTMOS) for this architecture class. The pitch-contour distillation loss we introduce provides a principled mechanism for preserving the fundamental-frequency accuracy that motivates autoregressive vocoders, and our ablation study will clarify which compression stages most affect pitch fidelity versus general quality. We will release pretrained TinyCARGAN checkpoints and an ONNX inference runtime, enabling practitioners to deploy high-quality pitch-faithful TTS on commodity hardware.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge distillation and pruning have been applied to non-autoregressive vocoders like HiFi-GAN, no prior work addresses the unique challenge of compressing chunked autoregressive generators where inter-chunk dependencies create sequential bottlenecks distinct from fully parallel models. Our pitch-contour distillation loss explicitly transfers the autoregressive pitch advantage rather than treating compression as a generic model-shrinking exercise, ensuring the compressed model retains the very property that justified the architectural choice.

proposals.title: Phase-Coherent Chunked Autoregressive Waveform Synthesis at 48 kHz: Scaling Temporal Precision for Full-Band Audio Generation
proposals.motivation: Current chunked autoregressive waveform models have demonstrated compelling results at 22.05 kHz for speech, but real-world music production and high-fidelity audio applications demand 44.1 or 48 kHz sampling rates where phase discontinuities at chunk boundaries become perceptually salient. As generative audio models move toward universal deployment in music, film, and interactive media, understanding whether chunked phase bias corrections remain effective under doubled temporal resolution is critical for bridging the gap between research prototypes and production-grade synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that naively scaling chunked autoregressive waveform synthesis from 22.05 kHz to 48 kHz will expose perceptually significant phase and amplitude discontinuities at chunk boundaries, but that a frequency-dependent phase bias correction mechanism—learned jointly with the waveform generator—can restore boundary coherence across the full audible spectrum without requiring proportional increases in chunk overlap or model capacity.
proposals.method: We will train chunked autoregressive waveform models at 22.05, 44.1, and 48 kHz on a combined corpus of speech (LibriTTS) and music (MusicNet, MUSDB18) to systematically measure how boundary artifacts scale with sample rate. We introduce a learnable frequency-band-adaptive phase bias module that conditions chunk-boundary blending on short-time Fourier transform sub-bands, allowing the model to allocate correction capacity preferentially to high-frequency components most sensitive to phase misalignment. We evaluate using both objective metrics (multi-resolution STFT loss, perceptual evaluation of speech quality, Fréchet audio distance) and controlled listening tests targeting boundary-region artifacts across frequency bands.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic empirical characterization of how chunked autoregressive boundary artifacts scale with sampling rate across speech and music domains. The proposed frequency-adaptive phase bias module offers a lightweight, drop-in architectural component that enables existing chunked models to operate at full-band rates without architectural overhaul. The resulting open-source model and evaluation protocol will establish benchmarks for high-fidelity neural audio synthesis at professional sample rates.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats chunk boundary correction as a uniform operation, our approach recognizes that phase sensitivity is frequency-dependent—high-frequency harmonics and transients require sub-sample precision that low-frequency components do not. Decomposing the bias correction across frequency bands is non-obvious because it reverses the typical assumption that time-domain chunking artifacts should be addressed purely in the time domain, instead leveraging spectral structure to make the correction both more targeted and more parameter-efficient.

proposals.title: Bidirectional Chunked Neural Vocoding: Exploiting Acausal Context for Artifact-Free Offline Waveform Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Causal autoregressive and causal-chunked vocoders are designed for streaming applications where future context is unavailable, but the majority of speech and music synthesis workloads—dataset preprocessing, audiobook generation, text-to-speech corpus creation—operate fully offline and gain nothing from the causal constraint. Imposing causality in these offline settings leaves reconstruction quality on the table, particularly at chunk boundaries and in phase-ambiguous spectral regions where forward-only context is insufficient. A principled bidirectional formulation that treats offline synthesis as the mathematical dual of causal streaming could close this quality gap while preserving the computational advantages of chunked processing.
proposals.hypothesis: A chunked neural vocoder that conditions each waveform chunk on both left-context and right-context embeddings will produce measurably lower boundary artifacts and superior phase reconstruction compared to an equivalent causal-only chunked model, without requiring full-sequence autoregression, because the additional future context resolves the phase ambiguities and transient mismatches that arise at chunk boundaries under unidirectional generation.
proposals.method: We propose a bidirectional chunked vocoder architecture in which a shared encoder produces latent representations from mel-spectrogram inputs, and two context networks—a forward-causal and a backward-causal transformer—each summarize left and right waveform context into fixed-dimensional conditioning vectors per chunk. These are fused via a lightweight cross-attention gate before feeding into a chunk-level flow-matching or diffusion decoder that synthesizes each waveform segment. Training uses a two-phase curriculum: first, the model is trained with teacher-forced ground-truth context from both directions; second, it is fine-tuned with its own previously generated chunks to handle inference-time distribution shift. We benchmark on LJSpeech, LibriTTS, and a music synthesis dataset, measuring PESQ, UTMOS, mel-cepstral distortion, and a novel boundary-discontinuity metric computed from cross-chunk spectral flux.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes offline chunked vocoding as a formally distinct regime from streaming synthesis, providing both the theoretical duality framework and a practical architecture that other offline audio systems can adopt. We expect 0.1–0.3 PESQ improvement and substantial boundary artifact reduction over causal-chunked baselines at matched computational cost, demonstrating that the bidirectional context is especially beneficial for phase-sensitive and transient-heavy signals. The released model and evaluation toolkit will serve as a new baseline for offline neural vocoding research.
proposals.novelty: While bidirectional models are standard in discriminative audio tasks, their application to generative chunked waveform synthesis is unexplored because the field has conflated the streaming constraint with the chunked-processing paradigm. Our key insight—that offline chunked generation admits a principled acausal dual where right-context conditioning resolves the phase and boundary ambiguities inherent to causal-only chunk synthesis—has not been formalized or empirically validated, and the two-phase curriculum that bridges teacher-forced and self-conditioned bidirectional context is a novel training strategy for this setting.

proposals.title: Harmonic Bandwidth Extension via Phase-Aware Chunked Autoregression: Scaling Dual Conditioning for Audio Super-Resolution
proposals.motivation: Audio bandwidth extension—recovering high-frequency content from narrowband signals—is critical for telephony enhancement, legacy media restoration, and enabling low-bitrate codecs to deliver perceptually transparent quality. Current approaches either produce spectrally flat hallucinations that lack harmonic fidelity or require expensive full-sequence diffusion passes that scale poorly to long-form audio. The convergence of efficient chunked autoregressive generation with explicit phase-structure modeling creates a timely opportunity to solve both the quality and scalability bottlenecks simultaneously.
proposals.hypothesis: A chunked autoregressive model that conditions each temporal chunk on both a coarse mel-spectrogram envelope and an explicit harmonic phase scaffold can reconstruct high-frequency bands with superior harmonic coherence and phase continuity compared to single-pass diffusion or GAN baselines, while scaling linearly in sequence length through localized chunk-wise generation.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-conditioned chunked autoregressive architecture (DualAR-BWE) that operates in two stages: first, a lightweight harmonic analysis module extracts fundamental frequency tracks and harmonic phase templates from the narrowband input; second, a transformer-based autoregressive decoder generates high-band waveform chunks, each conditioned on the local mel spectrogram (spectral envelope) and the harmonic phase scaffold (periodic structure prior). Chunk boundaries are managed via overlapping Hann-windowed segments with phase-locked stitching to ensure cross-chunk continuity. We scale the approach along a dual axis—model capacity (transformer depth and width) and conditioning resolution (mel bin count and harmonic order)—to characterize the scaling behavior of perceptual quality metrics (ViSQOL, PESQ) and harmonic distortion measures.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled framework that decomposes bandwidth extension into envelope hallucination and harmonic phase recovery, showing that explicit periodic-structure conditioning yields measurable gains in harmonic fidelity over purely spectral methods. The chunked autoregressive formulation provides the first systematic scaling analysis for audio super-resolution, establishing predictable quality-compute trade-off curves analogous to language-model scaling laws. Practically, the method enables real-time high-quality bandwidth extension suitable for streaming telephony and on-device audio enhancement.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior bandwidth extension systems that treat the missing band as an unconstrained generative problem, our approach introduces a harmonic phase scaffold as a structural inductive bias within a chunked autoregressive framework—combining the phase-continuity advantages of overlap-aware local generation with dual-axis scaling that separately characterizes the contributions of model size and conditioning granularity, a decomposition not previously explored in audio super-resolution.

proposals.title: Inverse Chunk Synthesis: Chunked Autoregressive Models for Speech Enhancement and Dereverberation
proposals.motivation: Speech enhancement and dereverberation remain challenging because reverberation introduces long-range temporal dependencies that exceed the effective context of frame-level models, causing artifacts and incomplete suppression of late reflections. Recent chunked autoregressive waveform models have demonstrated that generating audio with extended reverberant structure requires context-sensitive sequential synthesis over long windows, suggesting that the same architectural mechanism can be repurposed for the dual inverse problem. A principled framework that treats clean speech recovery as conditional chunk-wise generation would unify enhancement and dereverberation under a single generative paradigm, directly leveraging advances in neural audio synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: A chunked autoregressive generative model conditioned on degraded (noisy and reverberant) waveform segments can learn to sequentially synthesize clean speech continuations by implicitly inverting the room impulse response and noise corruption, achieving superior dereverberation and enhancement over frame-level discriminative baselines, particularly for long reverberation times (T60 > 0.7s) where context-dependent modeling is critical.
proposals.method: We propose Inverse Chunk Synthesis (ICS), a conditional chunked autoregressive model that takes overlapping segments of a degraded speech waveform as input context and autoregressively generates corresponding clean speech chunks. The architecture builds on a Transformer-based sequence model operating on discrete audio tokens obtained via a neural codec (e.g., EnCodec or SoundStream), where the encoder ingests degraded tokens and the decoder produces clean tokens chunk-by-chunk with cross-attention to the degraded context. Training uses paired clean-reverberant speech from simulated room impulse responses spanning diverse T60 values and noise conditions, with a multi-resolution adversarial loss and a perceptual loss to ensure waveform fidelity; we further introduce a chunk-overlap consistency loss that enforces agreement in overlapping output regions to eliminate boundary artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel generative framework that recasts speech enhancement and dereverberation as conditional sequential synthesis rather than spectral masking or regression, demonstrating that the same chunked autoregressive mechanism effective for generating reverberant audio can invert that process. We expect significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods on standard benchmarks (REVERB Challenge, DNS Challenge) especially in high-reverberation regimes, establishing scaling laws relating chunk size, context length, and dereverberation quality. The framework further provides a principled unification of enhancement, dereverberation, and bandwidth extension under a single autoregressive generation paradigm, opening new research directions in inverse audio problems.
proposals.novelty: While prior speech enhancement methods treat the problem as discriminative spectral mapping or diffusion-based generation, ICS is the first to exploit the duality between chunked autoregressive audio generation and its inverse—recognizing that the same sequential context mechanism that captures long reverberant structure during synthesis can decompose and remove it during conditional generation. The chunk-overlap consistency mechanism and the explicit scaling analysis of context length versus reverberation time are also novel, providing both practical and theoretical insights absent from existing approaches.

proposals.title: ChunkPLC: Chunked Autoregressive Adversarial Packet-Loss Concealment for Low-Bandwidth Interactive Audio
proposals.motivation: Real-time interactive audio applications such as VoIP and teleconferencing suffer severe quality degradation under packet loss, yet existing neural concealment methods assume high-bandwidth codecs and cannot operate within the strict latency and bitrate budgets of low-bandwidth streaming. The recent success of lightweight autoregressive generative models for waveform synthesis suggests that a dual formulation—treating packet repair as conditional generation over missing temporal chunks—can achieve perceptually transparent concealment at bitrates below 16 kbps. Addressing this gap is timely because the proliferation of bandwidth-constrained edge devices demands robust audio communication without relying on retransmission.
proposals.hypothesis: A chunked autoregressive GAN, conditioned on previously decoded audio frames and compact side information transmitted alongside the codec stream, can reconstruct missing waveform packets with higher perceptual quality (measured by PESQ and ViSQOL) and lower computational cost than current interpolation and autoencoder baselines, even under burst loss rates exceeding 20%.
proposals.method: We propose ChunkPLC, a causal generative model that segments the audio stream into fixed-length chunks aligned with the codec frame structure and autoregressively predicts missing chunks using a WaveGAN-style generator with multi-scale discriminators. The generator is conditioned on (i) a sliding context window of the K most recent successfully decoded chunks processed by a lightweight temporal convolutional encoder, and (ii) a low-overhead side-information vector (spectral envelope plus voicing flag, roughly 200 bits per frame) piggy-backed on future packets via a simple FEC-like scheme. Training employs a combination of multi-resolution STFT loss, feature-matching loss from the discriminators, and a perceptual loss derived from a pretrained wav2vec 2.0 encoder, with a curriculum that progressively increases burst-loss length during training to improve robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) the first adversarially trained packet-loss concealment system explicitly designed for sub-16 kbps interactive audio, demonstrating that generative modeling can serve as a natural dual to neural codec compression; (2) a lightweight side-information protocol that adds negligible bandwidth overhead yet substantially anchors the generative model during extended loss bursts; and (3) a systematic evaluation framework comparing neural PLC methods under realistic network trace conditions including bursty loss modeled by Gilbert-Elliott channels. We expect ChunkPLC to outperform the ITU-T G.711 Appendix I and recent deep-learning PLC baselines by at least 0.3 PESQ points at 20% random loss and 10% burst loss.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior neural PLC approaches that operate on high-bitrate PCM or rely on non-causal future context, ChunkPLC formulates concealment as a strictly causal, chunk-aligned conditional generation problem that directly interfaces with low-bandwidth codec frame boundaries, and introduces adversarial training with a curriculum over loss patterns—bridging the GAN-based speech synthesis literature with the largely overlooked problem of robust streaming under constrained bandwidth.

proposals.title: Frequency-Faithful Mode Regularization: Replacing Uniform Mass Allocation with Data-Proportional Coverage in Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Current mode regularization techniques in generative models such as GANs and VAEs assume that probability mass should be distributed fairly—roughly equally—across all modes, particularly during early training. However, real-world data distributions are often heavily imbalanced, with some modes being orders of magnitude more frequent than others. Enforcing uniform coverage can distort the learned distribution, suppress dominant modes, and amplify rare artifacts, ultimately degrading sample quality and calibration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing uniform mode mass allocation with a data-proportion-aware allocation scheme—where the regularizer encourages each mode to receive probability mass proportional to its empirical frequency in the training data—will yield generative models that better recover the true data distribution, as measured by improved FID, reduced reverse KL divergence, and more faithful frequency calibration across modes.
proposals.method: We propose a frequency-faithful mode regularization objective that re-weights the mode coverage penalty by estimated empirical mode frequencies obtained via a lightweight clustering step (e.g., k-means or Gaussian mixture model) applied to mini-batches during training. Concretely, we modify the regularization term in both GAN and VAE frameworks so that the target allocation vector is set to the normalized cluster frequencies rather than a uniform vector. We will evaluate on synthetic multi-modal benchmarks with known ground-truth imbalance ratios (e.g., stacked MNIST, ring-of-Gaussians) as well as natural image datasets (CelebA, LSUN) where attribute frequencies are skewed, comparing against standard mode regularization, VEEGAN, and PacGAN baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled alternative to the implicit fairness assumption in mode regularization, demonstrating that data-proportional allocation leads to better distributional fidelity without sacrificing mode coverage. It provides both a practical training algorithm compatible with existing generative architectures and a diagnostic framework—mode frequency calibration curves—for evaluating how well generative models recover heterogeneous mode structures. The results will inform best practices for training generative models on naturally imbalanced data, a setting ubiquitous in scientific and industrial applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has extensively studied mode collapse and mode coverage, the implicit assumption that all modes deserve equal probability mass has gone largely unchallenged. Our approach is non-obvious because it reveals that the seemingly beneficial goal of fairness in mode allocation can itself be a source of distributional error, and that a simple frequency-aware correction substantially changes the quality-diversity trade-off landscape.

proposals.title: Beyond Disjoint Manifolds: Characterizing GAN Training Dynamics Under Overlapping Support via Stochastic Decoders and Explicit Support Smoothing
proposals.motivation: The foundational analysis of GAN training pathologies by Arjovsky and Bottou (2017) rests on the assumption that the supports of the real and generated distributions are effectively disjoint on low-dimensional manifolds embedded in high-dimensional space, implying that an optimal discriminator can perfectly separate them, yielding uninformative gradients. However, real-world data pipelines introduce measurement noise, label noise, and stochastic augmentations that blur manifold boundaries, while modern architectures increasingly use stochastic decoders (e.g., VAE-GAN hybrids, diffusion-GAN bridges) whose outputs have full-dimensional support. Understanding whether the disjoint-manifold pathology genuinely governs practical GAN training, or whether naturally occurring and deliberately introduced overlap restores well-behaved gradients, is critical for both theoretical grounding and principled architecture design.
proposals.hypothesis: When the real data distribution is modeled with realistic observation noise or when the generator employs a stochastic decoder, the supports of the real and generated distributions overlap in a measurable region, and within this overlap the discriminator's gradient field provides informative, non-vanishing direction toward the true data distribution, fundamentally altering the convergence landscape relative to the classical disjoint-manifold analysis.
proposals.method: We propose a three-pronged investigation. First, we develop a theoretical framework extending the Arjovsky-Bottou manifold analysis to noisy data models where observations are corrupted by additive Gaussian or Poisson noise of varying magnitude, deriving conditions on noise level relative to manifold separation under which discriminator gradients remain informative and quantifying the resulting gradient quality via Fisher information bounds.
