Second, we implement controlled experiments comparing three support-smoothing strategies—(a) additive instance noise on real and fake samples (Sønderby et al., 2017), (b) stochastic decoders parameterized as conditional Gaussians with learned variance, and (c) a novel kernel-density-based support expansion that convolves generator outputs with a bandwidth-adaptive kernel—measuring discriminator gradient norms, generator loss curvature, and FID/IS across CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, and synthetic manifold benchmarks with known ground-truth geometry. Third, we analyze the spectral properties of the discriminator's Jacobian under each regime to characterize how overlap affects the effective dimensionality of the gradient signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic theoretical and empirical characterization of GAN training dynamics when the disjoint-manifold assumption is explicitly violated. It will yield practical guidelines for calibrating noise injection and stochastic decoder variance to maintain gradient quality without sacrificing sample fidelity, and it will introduce spectral Jacobian analysis as a diagnostic tool for identifying when a GAN is operating in the pathological disjoint regime versus a healthy overlapping regime. These contributions bridge the gap between worst-case theoretical analyses and the empirically observed success of modern GANs.
proposals.novelty: While instance noise has been proposed as a heuristic stabilizer, no prior work rigorously characterizes the phase transition between disjoint and overlapping support regimes or quantifies how discriminator gradient informativeness scales with the degree of overlap. Our Fisher-information-based gradient quality bounds and spectral Jacobian diagnostics provide novel theoretical tools that move beyond binary disjoint-vs-identical support analyses to a continuous, quantitative understanding of the overlap spectrum.

proposals.title: Topology-Aware Learned Priors for Generative Adversarial Networks: Disentangling Mode Collapse from Prior Misspecification
proposals.motivation: Mode collapse in GANs is conventionally attributed to discriminator pathologies or training instability, yet the standard fixed isotropic Gaussian prior may itself be a root cause by failing to capture the topological structure of complex data manifolds. Understanding and correcting this prior misspecification is critical because it affects all downstream generation quality, and recent advances in normalizing flows and graph neural networks now make structured, learnable priors tractable. Resolving whether mode collapse originates in the prior or the adversarial dynamics would fundamentally reshape how the community designs and trains generative models.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant fraction of mode collapse in GANs is caused by topological mismatch between the fixed unimodal latent prior and the multi-modal, non-trivially connected structure of the true data distribution, and replacing the fixed prior with a learned structured prior that adapts its topology to the data will substantially reduce mode collapse without modifying the discriminator or training procedure.
proposals.method: We propose to replace the standard Gaussian prior with a learnable prior parameterized by a normalizing flow whose base distribution is a mixture model, trained jointly with the generator and discriminator via an augmented adversarial objective that includes a topological regularization term based on persistent homology signatures of the latent space. We will use graph neural networks to encode neighborhood structure of data samples into the prior's mixture components, allowing the prior to discover and represent disconnected or non-convex modes. Controlled ablation experiments on synthetic multi-modal distributions, CIFAR-10, CelebA, and LSUN will isolate the contribution of prior misspecification versus discriminator failure to overall mode collapse, measured via Fréchet Inception Distance, precision-recall metrics, and the number of recovered modes on stacked-MNIST benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic causal analysis separating prior-induced mode collapse from discriminator-induced mode collapse, establishing a new diagnostic framework for generative model failure modes. The proposed topology-aware learned prior offers a drop-in replacement for fixed priors in existing GAN architectures, improving mode coverage without architectural changes to the generator or discriminator. The topological regularization technique and GNN-based mixture discovery are general tools applicable beyond GANs to VAEs, diffusion models, and other latent-variable generative frameworks.
proposals.novelty: While learned priors have been explored in VAE contexts, no prior work has combined persistent homology-based topological regularization with GNN-structured mixture priors specifically to diagnose and remedy the causal contribution of prior misspecification to GAN mode collapse. The key non-obvious insight is that what appears to be a discriminator problem may be fundamentally a latent geometry problem, requiring tools from computational topology rather than adversarial training improvements.

proposals.title: Stochastic Geometric Decoding: Replacing Deterministic Generation with Noise-Injected Mappings for Improved Mode Coverage in Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Current geometric-regularized generative models rely on deterministic decoders that map each latent point to a single output, making them vulnerable to mode collapse and brittle point-to-point matching between latent and data manifolds. As generative models are deployed for increasingly safety-critical applications—from drug design to scientific simulation—understanding whether stochastic decoding can improve robustness and coverage is both theoretically important and practically urgent. This work directly addresses a core assumption in geometric approaches to generation that has gone largely unexamined.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the deterministic decoder with a conditional stochastic mapping—where each latent code generates a distribution over outputs rather than a single point—will improve coverage of nearby modes, reduce sensitivity to the geometric regularizer's hyperparameters, and in some regimes eliminate the need for explicit mode-covering penalties altogether.
proposals.method: We augment the decoder of a geometrically-regularized generative model (e.g., a Wasserstein autoencoder or regularized flow) with a learned conditional noise process, parameterized as a small normalizing flow or a Gaussian with learned heteroscedastic variance conditioned on the latent code. We train using a modified objective that combines the original geometric penalty with a stochastic reconstruction term estimated via importance-weighted samples. We systematically vary the noise magnitude and decoder flexibility across synthetic multi-modal benchmarks (e.g., ring-of-Gaussians, Swiss roll with clustered modes) and standard image benchmarks (MNIST, CelebA, CIFAR-10), measuring mode coverage via the number of captured modes, FID, and a proposed geometric distortion metric that quantifies how faithfully the decoder preserves local neighborhood structure.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first controlled empirical and theoretical analysis of how stochastic decoding interacts with geometric regularization in generative models, identifying precise regimes where injected noise helps versus hurts mode coverage. It offers practical guidelines for practitioners on when deterministic decoders suffice and when stochastic alternatives are preferable, along with a new geometric distortion metric for evaluating decoder fidelity. The findings will inform the design of more robust generative architectures, particularly for applications where missing modes carries high cost.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic decoders exist in VAE-like models, their interaction with geometric regularizers—which explicitly penalize manifold distortion—has not been studied; it is non-obvious whether adding noise to a geometry-preserving map would destroy the very structure the regularizer enforces or instead provide a complementary mechanism for mode coverage. This work isolates that interaction and reveals a phase-transition-like behavior where low noise improves coverage but high noise degrades geometric fidelity, a finding not predictable from either literature alone.

proposals.title: Disentangling Discriminator-Induced Mode Collapse from Generator-Capacity Collapse in Generative Adversarial Networks
proposals.motivation: Mode collapse and training instability in GANs are typically attributed to discriminator pathologies such as oversharp decision boundaries or vanishing gradients in unvisited density regions. However, limited generator expressiveness—stemming from architectural bottlenecks, insufficient network width, or poorly structured latent spaces—may independently cause missing modes, yet these two failure sources are routinely conflated. Cleanly separating these causes is essential for designing targeted regularization strategies rather than applying broad-spectrum stabilization heuristics that may mask one problem while exacerbating the other.
proposals.hypothesis: Generator-capacity collapse—arising from architectural bottlenecks and suboptimal latent geometry—is an independent and quantitatively significant cause of mode dropping that persists even when discriminator-induced collapse is fully mitigated, and regularization methods that directly target generator expressiveness will recover missing modes that discriminator-focused penalties alone cannot.
proposals.method: We design a controlled diagnostic framework using synthetic mixture-of-Gaussians and structured image datasets (Stacked MNIST, CIFAR-10) where the ground-truth mode count is known. First, we construct oracle discriminators (frozen, pre-trained classifiers with calibrated outputs) that provably avoid discriminator-induced pathologies, isolating any residual mode collapse as generator-originated. Second, we systematically vary generator capacity (width, depth, skip connections) and latent space structure (dimensionality, learned manifold priors via normalizing-flow reparameterizations) while measuring mode coverage via the birthday paradox test and reverse-KL proxy metrics. Third, we introduce generator-targeted regularizers—latent Jacobian penalties encouraging uniform coverage, capacity-aware spectral normalization on the generator, and learnable latent-to-data diffeomorphisms—and compare their mode-recovery efficacy against standard discriminator-side interventions (gradient penalty, spectral normalization on D, zero-centered penalties) using Fréchet Inception Distance and precision-recall curves.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic causal decomposition of mode collapse into discriminator-induced and generator-capacity components, quantifying each source's relative contribution across data complexities. It introduces a reusable oracle-discriminator diagnostic protocol that future GAN research can adopt to attribute training failures. Additionally, the proposed generator-side regularizers—particularly latent Jacobian penalties and flow-based reparameterization—offer practical tools that complement existing discriminator-focused stabilization, yielding measurably higher mode coverage on standard benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has extensively studied discriminator regularization as the primary remedy for GAN instability, no existing study isolates generator capacity as an independent causal variable by removing discriminator confounds via oracle substitution. The combination of oracle-discriminator diagnostics with generator-targeted latent-geometry regularization challenges the prevailing assumption that fixing the discriminator landscape is both necessary and sufficient for stable, mode-covering generation.

proposals.title: Mode-Adaptive Regularization Metrics: Learning Local Similarity Measures for Geometrically Heterogeneous Data Manifolds
proposals.motivation: Modern regularization and representation learning frameworks typically enforce a single global similarity metric (e.g., Euclidean, cosine, or a fixed kernel) across the entire data distribution, implicitly assuming homogeneous geometry. This assumption breaks down in practice: rare subpopulations, minority semantic modes, and tail categories often occupy manifold regions with curvature and density profiles that diverge sharply from dominant modes, leading to underrepresentation, mode collapse in generative models, and degraded fairness. Addressing this gap is critical for building robust, equitable AI systems—a concern central to responsible AI development and catastrophic risk mitigation.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing a single global similarity metric in regularization objectives with a collection of locally learned, mode-conditioned metrics will yield representations that better preserve the geometric structure of rare and underrepresented modes, measurably reducing mode collapse in generative models and improving downstream performance on minority subpopulations without degrading overall quality.
proposals.method: We propose Mode-Adaptive Metric Regularization (MAMR), which partitions the latent space into semantically coherent regions via a lightweight mixture-of-experts gating network and learns a distinct Mahalanobis-like metric per region through backpropagation. Concretely, we augment the training objective of a variational autoencoder or diffusion model with a regularization term that computes pairwise similarity using the locally selected metric, weighted by soft cluster assignments. The gating network is conditioned on both the input features and a learned mode embedding, and is trained end-to-end with an entropy-based load-balancing loss to prevent degenerate assignment. We evaluate on image generation benchmarks (CIFAR-100 long-tailed, CelebA with rare attribute combinations) and molecular generation (using synthesizability-aware splits), measuring per-mode FID, coverage, and density metrics alongside standard aggregate scores.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled, differentiable framework for learning heterogeneous local metrics within regularization objectives, replacing the rigid global-metric assumption; (2) empirical evidence that mode-adaptive metrics reduce mode collapse and improve tail-mode fidelity across vision and molecular generation tasks; and (3) theoretical analysis connecting local metric learning to information-geometric properties of the data manifold, providing sufficient conditions under which MAMR provably improves coverage of rare modes relative to global-metric baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored adaptive kernels and local metric learning in supervised settings, no existing approach integrates mode-conditioned metric selection directly into the regularization objective of deep generative models. The key non-obvious insight is that the metric itself—not just the model capacity or loss weighting—is a critical bottleneck for rare-mode preservation, and that jointly learning metrics and representations through a mixture-of-experts gating mechanism can break the symmetry that causes global metrics to be dominated by high-density regions.

proposals.title: Safety-Constrained Bilevel Optimization for Geometric and Mode Regularization in Deep Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Training deep generative models requires balancing geometric consistency, mode coverage, and sample fidelity through multiple regularization terms whose coefficients are notoriously sensitive to dataset and architecture choices. Current adaptive schemes treat these regularizers as soft penalties with online-tuned weights, leading to proxy exploitation, coefficient collapse, and brittle training dynamics that demand expensive per-dataset hyperparameter sweeps. A principled constrained optimization framework is needed to guarantee stable, diverse generation without the fragility of unconstrained heuristic balancing.
proposals.hypothesis: Reformulating geometric consistency and mode coverage as hard safety constraints within a bilevel optimization framework—where a slow outer-loop controller adjusts regularizer strengths inside bounded trust regions while a discrete phase detector prevents under-regularized instability, over-regularized oversmoothing, and mode-dropping regimes—will yield more robust generative model training with fewer dataset-specific hyperparameter adjustments than existing adaptive penalty methods.
proposals.method: We propose a bilevel control architecture in which the inner loop trains the generative model under fixed regularization coefficients, while the outer loop solves a constrained optimization problem over those coefficients using lagged validation metrics for stability, diversity (measured via kernel-based mode coverage statistics), and sample fidelity (FID/precision-recall). The outer controller operates within per-phase trust regions and enforces minimum regularization floors derived from theoretical bounds on gradient variance. A lightweight discrete phase detector, implemented as a hidden Markov model over rolling training statistics, classifies the current regime and selects the appropriate constraint set, preventing the controller from continuously overreacting to transient gradient noise. We validate on image generation (StyleGAN-family architectures on FFHQ, LSUN) and molecular graph generation (RetroGNN-style synthesizability models) to demonstrate cross-domain generality.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first formalization of adaptive regularization scheduling as a safety-constrained bilevel program, replacing ad-hoc penalty weighting with provably bounded coefficient trajectories. It delivers a practical, architecture-agnostic training wrapper that eliminates dataset-specific retuning while matching or exceeding the generation quality of manually tuned baselines. The discrete phase detection mechanism offers interpretable diagnostics of training health, enabling practitioners to identify and correct pathological regimes without post-hoc analysis.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior adaptive regularization methods that treat coefficient tuning as unconstrained online learning susceptible to proxy exploitation and runaway dynamics, our approach imposes explicit trust-region constraints and phase-dependent admissible ranges inspired by safe reinforcement learning, ensuring that no single regularization objective can be driven to trivial or degenerate values during training. The combination of a constrained bilevel formulation with a discrete regime detector is, to our knowledge, unprecedented in the generative modeling literature.

proposals.title: MetaCal: Self-Calibrating Generative Training via Jointly Learned Semantic Metrics and Gradient-Aware Adaptive Loss Weighting
proposals.motivation: Modern generative models require careful balancing of multiple training objectives—geometric alignment, reconstruction fidelity, and mode coverage—yet practitioners must hand-tune loss coefficients and fix similarity metrics before training begins, leading to brittle optimization and poor multimodal coverage. As generative architectures grow in complexity and are applied to safety-critical domains such as drug design and scientific simulation, the inability to adapt training dynamics in a principled, automated fashion becomes a significant bottleneck. A self-calibrating framework that co-evolves its own notion of similarity alongside its loss weighting strategy would eliminate this fragile manual process and improve both training stability and output quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Jointly learning a task-adaptive similarity metric and a gradient-signal-driven loss weighting controller during generative training yields superior multimodal coverage and sample fidelity compared to fixed-metric, fixed-coefficient baselines, because the co-adaptation allows early training to prioritize coarse geometric alignment under robust features while later stages can increase pressure on semantically meaningful mode separation as the learned metric matures.
proposals.method: We propose MetaCal, a two-component framework: (1) a parameterized similarity metric network trained end-to-end alongside the generator, mapping pairs of samples to a learned manifold distance that replaces fixed metrics such as FID-kernel distances or Wasserstein approximations; and (2) a lightweight meta-learned controller that observes per-objective gradient norms, mode coverage statistics from a diversity buffer, and sample fidelity scores at each training step, then outputs adaptive scalar weights for geometric, reconstruction, and regularization losses. The metric network is regularized via a contrastive proxy task on known semantic groupings, while the controller is trained with a bilevel optimization objective that maximizes validation-set mode coverage and sample quality. We validate on image generation benchmarks (CIFAR-10, LSUN, FFHQ), molecular generation tasks using graph neural network decoders inspired by RetroGNN-style architectures, and a controlled multimodal synthetic benchmark designed to expose mode collapse.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, fully differentiable framework for eliminating two persistent sources of human intervention in generative model training: metric selection and loss coefficient tuning. By demonstrating that co-learning these components improves both optimization stability and multimodal coverage across vision and molecular domains, we provide the community with a general-purpose training wrapper applicable to GANs, VAEs, and diffusion models. The bilevel optimization formulation and the curriculum-like behavior that emerges naturally—coarse alignment first, fine semantic separation later—offer new theoretical insight into adaptive training dynamics for deep generative models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored either learned metrics or adaptive loss weighting in isolation, no existing method jointly optimizes both in a single differentiable loop driven by explicit training-phase signals such as gradient smoothness and mode coverage. The emergent curriculum behavior—where the system autonomously transitions from robust coarse alignment to fine-grained semantic mode pressure—arises without any explicit scheduling, making the approach fundamentally different from hand-designed annealing strategies.

proposals.title: Towards Self-Calibrating Semantic Regularization Via Jointly Learned Metrics And Adaptive Loss Weighting: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to This project proposes to jointly learn a similarity metric for manifold matching and an adaptive con. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-calibrating semantic regularization via jointly learned metrics and adaptive loss weighting can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates fusion principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: This project proposes to jointly learn a similarity metric for manifold matching and an adaptive controller that weights geometric, reconstruction, an.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the self-calibrating semantic regularization via jointly learned metrics and adaptive loss weighting problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Regularization Schedules: Adaptive Weighting of Geometric and Mode-Covering Objectives in Deep Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models such as VAEs and normalizing flows rely on regularization terms—geometric priors enforcing latent smoothness and mode-covering penalties preventing posterior collapse—whose relative weights are typically hand-tuned and held fixed throughout training. This brittle setup leads to suboptimal trade-offs: early training benefits from strong regularization to stabilize optimization, while later stages require relaxed constraints to maximize sample fidelity on complex, large-scale datasets. An adaptive, principled mechanism for scheduling these weights would eliminate costly hyperparameter sweeps and improve both training robustness and final sample quality.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned weighting scheme that adjusts the coefficients of reconstruction, geometric, and mode-regularization losses according to phase-specific diagnostic signals—such as gradient variance, latent coverage statistics, and sample-level fidelity scores—will achieve Pareto-superior trade-offs between training stability and generation quality compared to fixed or simple annealing schedules.
proposals.method: We propose a bilevel optimization framework in which the regularization weights are treated as meta-parameters updated on a held-out validation objective, while the model parameters are optimized on the weighted training loss. Concretely, at each training phase we compute lightweight diagnostic features—gradient norm smoothness across layers, an approximate coverage metric via kernel density estimation in the latent space, and FID-based fidelity on a small validation batch—and feed these into a small auxiliary network (a gated recurrent controller) that outputs the next-step weighting coefficients. The outer loop minimizes a composite validation loss combining reconstruction accuracy and distributional coverage, using implicit differentiation to backpropagate through the inner optimization steps efficiently. We will evaluate on image benchmarks (CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, ImageNet 64×64) and a molecular generation task to test cross-domain generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a general-purpose, plug-in module for adaptive regularization scheduling applicable to any loss-decomposable generative model, removing a major source of manual tuning in generative modeling pipelines. We expect to demonstrate consistent improvements of 5-15% in FID and mode coverage metrics over fixed-weight and cyclical annealing baselines, with particular gains on larger and more heterogeneous datasets where static coefficients fail. The diagnostic-driven controller also provides interpretable training trajectories, offering insights into when and why different regularization pressures matter.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored simple annealing schedules (e.g., KL warm-up in VAEs) or fixed multi-objective weighting, no existing approach learns phase-adaptive regularization weights via bilevel optimization conditioned on real-time training diagnostics. The combination of gradient smoothness, coverage, and fidelity signals as meta-features for a recurrent controller is a non-obvious integration of meta-learning, multi-objective optimization, and generative modeling that goes well beyond standard hyperparameter scheduling.

proposals.title: Adversarial Metric Certification for Identifiable Cross-Domain Manifold Alignment
proposals.motivation: Cross-domain representation alignment is central to transfer learning, multi-modal fusion, and biological neural comparison, yet current approaches rely on a single learned metric that can silently absorb alignment error into geometric distortion, producing spurious correspondences indistinguishable from genuine shared structure. This failure mode is especially dangerous in safety-critical applications—clinical imaging, brain-computer interfaces, and AI alignment auditing—where false confidence in cross-domain transfer can propagate undetected. A principled framework that separates true structural correspondence from metric-induced artifacts is therefore an urgent methodological need.
proposals.hypothesis: If the metric used for manifold matching is itself adversarially certified—constrained to preserve local topology, maintain calibration under input perturbations, and yield consistent alignments across independently sampled anchor sets—then only genuinely shared geometric structure will survive the matching procedure, making the resulting correspondences identifiable and robust rather than artifacts of a single flexible embedding.
proposals.method: We propose a bilevel optimization framework: the inner loop trains a matching model (e.g., optimal-transport or graph-neural-network based aligner) against a family of metric networks, while the outer loop updates each metric network only when it simultaneously improves held-out semantic correspondence accuracy, cross-domain retrieval recall, and downstream transfer F1 while satisfying three hard constraints—(1) local Lipschitz bounds that enforce topology preservation via neighborhood-graph spectral agreement, (2) calibration stability measured by maximum calibration error under Jacobian-norm-bounded perturbations, and (3) cross-anchor consistency requiring that alignments computed from disjoint random anchor subsets agree above a Jaccard threshold. The matching model must perform well under the worst-case certified metric in the admissible family, yielding minimax-style robustness guarantees on alignment quality.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a falsifiable diagnostic protocol for manifold matching: if an alignment degrades under metric perturbation within the certified family, it is flagged as non-identifiable, providing practitioners with an actionable reliability certificate absent from current methods. It also provides the first theoretical connection between metric-learning regularization and the identifiability conditions studied in nonlinear ICA and causal representation learning, unifying two previously separate literatures. Empirically, we expect the framework to yield more transferable alignments on multi-modal benchmarks (vision-language retrieval, cross-species neural recordings) while exposing failure cases that conventional Procrustes or contrastive alignment methods silently accept.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats the metric as a fixed design choice or learns it end-to-end without safeguards, our approach treats metric variation as a first-class adversary: alignments must survive a family of topology-preserving metrics rather than exploit a single one, turning the metric from a hidden confound into an explicit identifiability test. This adversarial certification perspective bridges robust optimization with the emerging theory of representation identifiability, offering a mechanism—cross-anchor consistency under constrained metric perturbation—that has no direct precedent in either community.

proposals.title: Coverage-Aware Metric Learning for Manifold Matching: Unifying Alignment and Mode Recall in Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Generative models frequently suffer from mode dropping, where entire semantic subpopulations of the target distribution are ignored during training, yet standard evaluation metrics like FID conflate fidelity and coverage into a single scalar that obscures the failure. Existing remedies either bolt a separate classifier onto the generator to detect missing modes or rely on fixed feature distances that are insensitive to rare manifold regions. A learned metric that jointly optimizes for geometric alignment and mode coverage would provide both a more informative training signal and a more diagnostic evaluation tool, addressing a critical gap as generative models are deployed in safety-sensitive scientific and medical domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A similarity metric trained end-to-end with a coverage-estimation auxiliary objective will concentrate optimization pressure on under-represented modes, yielding generative models with significantly higher recall of rare semantic regions compared to models trained with fixed-distance optimal-transport losses, without sacrificing overall alignment quality.
proposals.method: We parameterize a deep metric network whose embedding space is trained with a bi-level objective: the inner level minimizes an entropy-regularized optimal-transport cost between generated and real sample embeddings for alignment, while the outer level maximizes a coverage-sensitive loss that up-weights embedding gradients in regions where local mass ratios deviate most from uniformity, estimated via kernel density ratio scoring in the learned space. The metric network is co-trained with the generator using alternating optimization, and we regularize the embedding geometry with a spectral normalization constraint to prevent representation collapse. We evaluate on standard image benchmarks (CIFAR-10, FFHQ) and a molecular conformation generation task, measuring precision-recall curves, rare-mode recall at controlled rarity levels, and comparing against Wasserstein-distance and classifier-based coverage baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a unified framework in which the distance metric itself becomes the instrument for detecting and correcting mode dropping, eliminating the need for separate ad-hoc coverage heuristics or auxiliary discriminators. It provides both a practical training algorithm that improves rare-mode recall in generative models and a theoretically grounded diagnostic metric that decomposes matching quality into alignment and coverage components. The framework generalizes across data modalities, offering immediate utility in domains such as drug design and medical imaging where minority modes carry outsized importance.
proposals.novelty: Existing approaches treat metric learning and coverage estimation as orthogonal problems; our key insight is that coupling them creates a feedback loop where the metric sharpens in precisely the manifold regions where coverage is poorest, producing an optimization landscape that is self-correcting with respect to mode collapse. This contrasts with prior optimal-transport methods that use fixed ground costs oblivious to distributional gaps, and with GAN-based diversity losses that lack a principled geometric foundation.

proposals.title: Coverage-Aware Metric Learning for Robust Manifold Matching in Generative Models
proposals.motivation: A core failure mode in generative models, particularly in GANs and flow-based models, is mode dropping, where the model fails to capture the full diversity of the data distribution. Pre-2026 methods for detecting and mitigating this, such as auxiliary discriminators or heuristic metrics, are often noisy, separate from the alignment objective, and do not provide a direct training signal for improving coverage on unlabeled data. This limits the reliability and safety of generative systems, especially in high-stakes domains like medical imaging (e.g., colonoscopy analysis) where missing rare but critical modes can have severe consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly learning a similarity metric that is sensitive to both geometric alignment and the statistical coverage of data modes, we can create a unified training signal that directly penalizes mode collapse and mass misallocation during manifold matching, leading to more robust and comprehensive generative models.
proposals.method: We will develop a neural network to parameterize a coverage-aware metric. This network will be trained with a multi-objective loss combining a contrastive or triplet loss for geometric alignment of matched data pairs with a novel coverage estimation loss. The coverage loss will be based on the divergence between the distribution of transport costs (e.g., Wasserstein distance computed under the learned metric) across mini-batches and a prior encouraging uniform mass allocation across latent semantic clusters identified dynamically. We will integrate this into a generative adversarial framework or an optimal transport matching setup, where the metric network provides adaptive costs that increase for under-represented modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, unified framework for evaluating and improving mode coverage in generative models directly through the learned metric. It will yield a new diagnostic tool for detecting mode collapse and a training mechanism that actively corrects for it, advancing beyond fixed metrics like FID or separate discriminators. The resulting models are expected to show improved performance on datasets with complex, multi-modal distributions, such as biomedical imagery.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight coupling of metric learning with explicit coverage estimation, making the metric itself a direct instrument for detecting and correcting mode dropping, rather than treating coverage as a separate, post-hoc diagnostic problem. This moves beyond prior work that uses fixed metrics or adversarial discriminators which lack explicit geometric and coverage coupling.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Geometric Similarity Metrics for Semantically-Aligned Manifold Matching in Deep Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models rely on implicit or hand-chosen similarity metrics—pixel-wise reconstruction, discriminator-derived distances, or classifier-based perceptual losses—yet the choice of metric profoundly shapes optimization landscapes, mode coverage, and sample quality. Despite this, no systematic framework exists for learning the metric itself or for understanding how metric geometry interacts with the topology of the data manifold. Developing principled, learnable similarity metrics is critical for bridging the gap between low-level reconstruction fidelity and high-level semantic coherence in generative modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned similarity metric, trained end-to-end to maximize semantic mode coverage and minimize manifold mismatch, will yield generative models with superior distributional fidelity compared to fixed metrics (pixel, discriminator, or classifier features), particularly on multi-modal data distributions where semantic structure diverges from low-level pixel geometry.
proposals.method: We propose a bi-level optimization framework in which an inner loop trains a generative model (e.g., a VAE or flow-based model) using a parameterized metric network, while an outer loop updates the metric parameters to minimize a distributional divergence (e.g., maximum mean discrepancy or sliced Wasserstein distance) computed on held-out semantic attributes. The metric network is instantiated as a lightweight encoder whose representation space defines a Riemannian geometry over the data manifold; we compare learned metrics against fixed baselines from several metric families (Lp, perceptual, contrastive) using controlled ablations on image and molecular graph benchmarks, leveraging graph neural network encoders for structured data.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic empirical and theoretical analysis of how similarity metric choice governs optimization dynamics and mode coverage in manifold-matching generative models. It introduces a practical meta-learning algorithm for end-to-end metric adaptation that generalizes across data modalities, and establishes diagnostic benchmarks for evaluating metric-induced geometry against ground-truth semantic structure, offering the community a principled toolkit for metric selection in generative modeling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats the similarity metric as a fixed architectural choice, we recast it as a learnable component optimized for downstream distributional quality via bi-level optimization—a perspective that unifies and subsumes existing perceptual and adversarial losses. The combination of Riemannian metric learning with meta-optimization over semantic mode coverage has not been explored, and connecting this framework to both continuous (image) and discrete (graph) data domains through GNN-based metric encoders is a non-trivial methodological advance.

proposals.title: Learning Semantic Metrics for Stable and Interpretable Manifold Alignment
proposals.motivation: Manifold matching and disentanglement methods, such as those used in domain adaptation or cross-modal learning, rely on predefined similarity metrics (e.g., pixel-wise L2, discriminator features). These metrics often optimize for low-level reconstruction fidelity, which can misalign semantic modes of variation and lead to unstable optimization or semantically meaningless interpolations. A learned, task-driven metric is needed to align latent spaces based on high-level concepts, which is crucial for robust applications in scientific domains like molecular property prediction or medical image analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: A similarity metric, parameterized by a neural network and learned jointly with the manifold alignment objective, will produce more stable optimization and better semantic correspondence between latent spaces than fixed, low-level metrics.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a metric network, structured as a small Siamese encoder, is trained to produce a distance function. This network will be optimized via a contrastive loss that maximizes mutual information between semantically paired data points across manifolds (e.g., different views of a molecule or colonoscopy video frames). We will integrate this learned metric into existing manifold matching architectures, such as variational autoencoders or cycle-consistent models, and benchmark against fixed metrics (pixel, VGG, classifier) on stability measures (gradient variance) and downstream task performance (e.g., classification of mucosal states in colonoscopy).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for learning geometry in latent space alignment, moving beyond heuristic metric choices. It will deliver concrete guidelines on metric design for stable optimization and demonstrate improved semantic disentanglement in applications like biomedical data analysis, contributing to more reliable and interpretable representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While metric learning is established in supervised settings, its integration as a tunable component within the manifold matching optimization loop—explicitly to control optimization dynamics and semantic alignment—is unexplored. This approach directly addresses the instability and semantic misalignment caused by standard metrics, a gap noted but not studied in prior work.

proposals.title: Harmonic Discriminator Potentials: Eliminating Spurious Critical Points in GAN Training via Elliptic Regularity
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks remain notoriously difficult to train, with mode collapse and vanishing gradients rooted in poorly shaped discriminator loss landscapes rather than in generator capacity alone. The geometry of the discriminator function in the region between real and generated data manifolds directly governs the quality of generator gradients, yet there is no principled theory characterizing which discriminator geometries are pathological and which guarantee informative flow. Drawing on the well-understood failure modes of artificial potential fields in robot motion planning—where harmonic and navigation-function constructions provably eliminate spurious attractors—offers a rigorous mathematical framework for diagnosing and curing these GAN training pathologies.
proposals.hypothesis: Unconstrained neural discriminators generically develop spurious critical points and broad plateaus in the inter-manifold region between real and generated data supports, and imposing harmonic or near-harmonic regularity—enforcing an elliptic PDE constraint on the discriminator's Laplacian—provably eliminates these pathological geometries, yielding monotone and informative generator gradients throughout training.
proposals.method: We will formalize the discriminator as a scalar potential field defined on the ambient data space with boundary conditions set by the real and generated data manifolds, then apply classical results from elliptic PDE theory (maximum principles, Harnack inequalities) to characterize the critical-point and curvature structure of unconstrained versus harmonically regularized discriminators. Concretely, we will (1) prove that generic ReLU-network discriminators admit interior saddle points and flat regions via Morse-theoretic analysis on interpolated data, (2) derive a Laplacian-penalized training objective that enforces approximate harmonicity and prove it eliminates interior extrema under mild manifold-separation assumptions, and (3) implement the regularizer as a tractable spectral penalty on the Hessian trace estimated via Hutchinson's method during standard GAN training on image benchmarks (CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ) to validate improved gradient informativeness, training stability, and sample quality.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first formal geometric theory linking discriminator critical-point structure to GAN training failure modes, grounding intuitions from the potential-field literature in rigorous PDE-based guarantees. It delivers a practical, computationally tractable harmonic regularization scheme that can be layered onto existing GAN architectures to reduce mode collapse and gradient vanishing without architectural changes. More broadly, it establishes a new bridge between classical navigation-function theory and modern generative modeling, opening a principled design space for discriminator regularization grounded in elliptic regularity rather than ad hoc penalties like gradient clipping or spectral normalization.
proposals.novelty: While spectral normalization and gradient penalties constrain the discriminator's Lipschitz constant, they do not address interior critical-point topology; our approach is the first to leverage elliptic maximum principles to provably rule out spurious discriminator extrema in the inter-manifold region, importing a structural guarantee from robotics navigation functions that has no precedent in generative modeling theory.

proposals.title: Geometric Regularity Theory for Discriminator Functions on Inter-Manifold Regions in Generative Adversarial Networks
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread empirical success, GAN training remains notoriously unstable, with mode collapse and vanishing gradients persisting as fundamental obstacles. A growing body of evidence suggests that the root cause lies in the geometric properties of discriminator functions in the high-dimensional void between the real data manifold and the generated manifold, yet no formal theory characterizes which discriminator geometries are pathological. Formalizing this connection between inter-manifold discriminator shape and gradient quality would provide the theoretical foundation needed to design principled, stability-guaranteed training algorithms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that GAN training instability is governed by the curvature and Lipschitz structure of the discriminator's level sets in the ambient space between disjoint real and generated manifolds: specifically, discriminator function classes whose gradients become nearly orthogonal to the geodesic connecting the two manifolds produce vanishing or misleading generator gradients, and that a quantifiable regularity condition on the discriminator's Hessian spectrum in these inter-manifold regions is both necessary and sufficient for stable gradient flow to the generator.
proposals.method: We will develop a differential-geometric framework that models the real and generated distributions as submanifolds embedded in high-dimensional ambient space and analyzes the discriminator as a smooth function on the tubular neighborhood between them. Using tools from Morse theory and the theory of optimal transport, we will classify discriminator critical point structures in inter-manifold regions and derive spectral conditions on the discriminator Hessian that guarantee generator gradients remain informative. We will validate the theory empirically by training GANs with discriminator regularizers derived from our conditions on standard image benchmarks (CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ) and measuring correlation between our proposed geometric indicators and training stability metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous geometric characterization of why certain discriminator architectures and regularization schemes succeed or fail, unifying disparate empirical findings—such as the effectiveness of spectral normalization, gradient penalties, and Wasserstein formulations—under a single theoretical framework. The derived regularity conditions will yield practical, architecture-agnostic discriminator regularizers with provable stability guarantees. Beyond GANs, the inter-manifold geometry framework will offer new analytical tools applicable to any adversarial learning setting where support mismatch between distributions is a concern.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied discriminator Lipschitz constraints or gradient penalties in isolation, no existing theory jointly characterizes the full second-order geometric structure of discriminators in the critical inter-manifold region or derives necessary and sufficient conditions for gradient informativeness. Our Morse-theoretic classification of discriminator critical points between disjoint manifolds is entirely new and reveals that the pathology is not merely about gradient magnitude but about the alignment of discriminator level-set geometry with the transport direction between manifolds.

proposals.title: Representation-Robust Multiscale Coverage Auditing: Turning Unidentifiable Mode Counting into Testable Coverage Guarantees for Unlabeled Data
proposals.motivation: Evaluating whether a generative model or dataset adequately covers the modes of a complex data distribution is critical for AI safety and fairness, yet existing approaches rely on a single learned embedding space whose geometry can arbitrarily distort coverage conclusions. As foundation models are increasingly trained on massive unlabeled corpora whose composition is poorly understood, the inability to reliably audit data coverage poses a concrete catastrophic-risk vector: systematic blind spots in training data propagate into deployed systems without detection. A framework that provides coverage guarantees stable across plausible representations would close this auditing gap and make dataset-level safety claims scientifically grounded.
proposals.hypothesis: Coverage diagnostics computed as consensus intervals over an ensemble of diverse self-supervised encoders yield auditing conclusions that are both more reliable and more informative than single-representation metrics: specifically, regions flagged as undercovered by all encoders correspond to genuine distributional gaps, while regions where encoders disagree reveal representation-sensitive structure that single-number scores silently average away.
proposals.method: We train an ensemble of structurally diverse self-supervised encoders—contrastive (SimCLR, BYOL variants), masked-autoencoder, and graph-neural-network-based manifold learners—on the target unlabeled dataset, then construct a consensus multiscale hierarchy by applying persistent-homology-guided clustering independently in each embedding and retaining only those local regions whose existence is stable across a majority of representations. For each consensus region at each scale, we estimate three diagnostics—rare-region recall, support coverage, and mass-allocation error—using kernel density ratio estimation between the audit target (model samples or a held-out split) and the reference dataset, reporting each as a confidence interval over the encoder ensemble rather than a point estimate. We validate the framework on controlled synthetic manifolds with known mode structure, on large-scale image benchmarks (ImageNet, LAION subsets) auditing popular diffusion models, and on molecular datasets where RetroGNN-style synthesizability predictors may exhibit coverage failures over rare chemical scaffolds.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, representation-agnostic auditing toolkit that replaces brittle single-embedding coverage scores with robust interval-valued diagnostics, enabling practitioners to make defensible claims about dataset and model coverage for the first time. It provides the first empirical characterization of how much coverage conclusions vary across plausible representation choices on real-world benchmarks, quantifying a source of evaluation uncertainty the field has largely ignored. The open-source toolkit and benchmark suite will serve as infrastructure for AI safety auditing, dataset documentation, and responsible deployment review.
proposals.novelty: While prior coverage metrics implicitly assume a fixed, correct representation, our key insight is to recast coverage auditing as a consensus inference problem over the space of valid encoders, transforming an ill-posed question (how many modes exist?) into a well-posed one (which coverage claims are invariant to representation choice?). The marriage of persistent-homology-based hierarchical clustering with self-supervised encoder ensembles is, to our knowledge, unprecedented and provides both theoretical grounding via topological stability results and practical scalability through amortized encoder inference.

proposals.title: Species-Richness Estimators for Mode Coverage: Statistically Grounded Evaluation of Generative Models Without Classifiers
proposals.motivation: Evaluating whether a generative model captures the full diversity of a target distribution remains an open problem, with existing metrics like FID and precision-recall relying on classifier embeddings that conflate perceptual quality with distributional coverage. Mode collapse and missing-mode detection currently require labeled taxonomies or surrogate classifiers, both of which introduce biases and cannot quantify how much of the distribution remains unseen. Ecology has faced an analogous challenge for decades—estimating unseen species richness from incomplete surveys—and has developed principled nonparametric estimators that are directly transferable to generative evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: Good-Turing frequency-of-frequency analysis, Chao-style lower bounds, and coverage-based rarefaction applied to unsupervised cluster occupancy statistics in a learned feature space can provide statistically valid lower bounds on the number of missing modes and the uncovered probability mass of a generative model, outperforming classifier-dependent mode-dropping diagnostics in both accuracy and calibration.
proposals.method: We partition generated and reference samples into local modes using unsupervised structure in a self-supervised feature space (e.g., DINO or MAE embeddings): specifically, we apply k-nearest-neighbor density estimation and spectral clustering to define discrete mode identities without labels. We then construct frequency-of-frequency vectors (the number of modes observed exactly once, twice, etc.) and apply Chao1, Chao2, and Good-Turing estimators to infer the number of unobserved modes and the missing probability mass, along with analytic confidence intervals via coverage-based rarefaction curves. We validate these ecology-inspired metrics against controlled synthetic distributions with known mode structure and benchmark them on ImageNet, LSUN, and CIFAR-10 generation tasks, comparing against classification-based recall, improved precision-recall, and rarity scores.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first family of nonparametric, classifier-free generative coverage metrics with provable statistical guarantees—lower bounds on missing modes and confidence intervals on uncovered mass—bridging ecological statistics and deep generative modeling. The proposed estimators are model-agnostic, work on unlabeled data, and decompose coverage failure into rare-mode under-representation versus complete mode absence, providing actionable diagnostic information that current metrics lack. We expect these tools to become standard complements to FID and precision-recall in generative model development.
proposals.novelty: While ecological estimators are well-known in biodiversity science, their application to generative model evaluation is non-obvious because it requires solving the mode-identity problem in continuous high-dimensional feature spaces without labels—a challenge we address through unsupervised partitioning in self-supervised representation spaces combined with sensitivity analysis over partition granularity. The resulting framework is the first to offer finite-sample statistical guarantees (confidence intervals and lower bounds) for mode coverage, rather than point estimates that lack uncertainty quantification.

proposals.title: Attribute-Aware Mode Coverage Estimation with Adaptive Rare-Condition Regularization for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models routinely drop rare modes corresponding to minority attributes, unusual viewpoints, and infrequent attribute conjunctions, yet standard evaluation metrics like FID and IS only measure aggregate distributional quality and are blind to which semantic subpopulations are missing. This failure mode poses both scientific and societal risks: scientifically it obscures whether a model has truly learned the data distribution, and socially it can erase underrepresented groups from generated outputs. A fine-grained, conditional coverage diagnostic coupled with a training signal that directly counteracts detected gaps would close the loop between evaluation and optimization, addressing a critical limitation in current generative modeling practice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decomposing mode coverage into attribute-conditioned slices and feeding per-slice recall deficits back as an adaptive regularization term will significantly improve recall of rare attribute combinations without degrading overall sample quality, and that this coupled estimate-and-regularize loop will be more label-efficient than uniform upsampling or post-hoc reweighting because it concentrates corrective pressure precisely where empirical mode collapse is detected.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component architecture: (1) a conditional coverage estimator that partitions the data manifold into semantic slices defined by available attribute labels or, when annotations are sparse, by clusters inferred via a weakly-supervised attribute discovery module trained with contrastive learning on partial labels and nearest-neighbor consistency; and (2) an adaptive regularizer that converts per-slice coverage gaps into loss penalty weights updated on an exponential moving average schedule each training epoch. The coverage estimator uses a kernel-based support measure comparing generator outputs to held-out real samples within each slice, producing per-attribute recall scores and a probability-mass misallocation index. During training, the regularizer dynamically upweights the generator loss for slices whose recall falls below a configurable threshold, with a temperature-scaled softmax over slice deficits to avoid gradient instability from hard reweighting.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a unified framework that, for the first time, tightly couples conditional mode-coverage measurement with an adaptive training objective, producing both a diagnostic tool and a corrective mechanism in a single pipeline. It will yield fine-grained coverage reports that reveal exactly which rare attribute conjunctions remain missing and by how much, enabling researchers and auditors to perform targeted fairness and diversity assessments of generative models. We expect the method to achieve state-of-the-art rare-mode recall on benchmarks such as CelebA-HQ and ImageNet-LT while maintaining competitive FID, demonstrating that aggregate quality and minority-mode fidelity need not be traded off.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats mode coverage as a single scalar diagnostic or applies uniform diversity regularizers, our approach creates a closed feedback loop where coverage measurement directly and differentiably informs per-slice training pressure, making the regularization automatically adaptive to the model's current failure modes. The weakly-supervised attribute discovery component further distinguishes our method by enabling fine-grained coverage estimation even when attribute annotations cover only a small fraction of the data, removing a key practical barrier to deployment.

proposals.title: Discriminator-Free Mode Coverage Estimation for Generative Models via Kernel Density Signatures
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of generative models on unlabeled data relies on auxiliary discriminators or classifiers to detect missing modes, introducing bias from the proxy model's own blind spots. As generative models are increasingly deployed in safety-critical domains such as drug design and medical imaging, accurate and self-contained mode-coverage metrics are essential to ensure that rare but important data subpopulations are not silently dropped during generation.
proposals.hypothesis: A kernel-based density signature method that compares local neighborhood statistics between real and generated samples in a learned representation space can estimate mode coverage, rare-mode recall, and probability-mass allocation with higher accuracy and lower bias than discriminator-dependent approaches, without requiring labeled data or a separate classifier.
proposals.method: We propose computing localized kernel density estimates in the feature space of a self-supervised encoder (e.g., a pretrained contrastive model) for both real and generated distributions, then identifying candidate modes as high-density clusters in the real data via persistent homology. Mode coverage is quantified by measuring the fraction of real-data topological features matched by generated samples within an adaptive bandwidth, while rare-mode recall is estimated through a reweighted nearest-neighbor divergence that upweights low-density regions. We validate on synthetic mixture-of-Gaussians benchmarks with known ground-truth modes, then scale to image and molecular generation tasks, comparing against FID, precision-recall, and discriminator-based coverage metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces a family of discriminator-free mode-coverage metrics that decompose generative quality into coverage breadth, rare-mode recall, and mass-allocation fidelity, providing practitioners with interpretable diagnostics beyond aggregate scores like FID. The metrics are theoretically grounded in kernel density estimation and topological data analysis, offering provable consistency guarantees under mild smoothness assumptions. We release an open-source evaluation toolkit to standardize mode-coverage benchmarking across generative modeling research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing approaches that outsource mode detection to a learned discriminator—inheriting that model's own coverage gaps—our method directly estimates topological mode structure from density signatures in a self-supervised feature space, making the evaluation independent of any task-specific auxiliary model. The combination of persistent homology for mode identification with reweighted kernel divergences for rare-mode sensitivity is, to our knowledge, unexplored in the generative model evaluation literature.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Compositional Coverage Regularization for Faithful Generation of Rare Semantic Combinations
proposals.motivation: Conditional generative models systematically under-represent rare attribute combinations—unusual pose–object–background triples are either dropped entirely or rendered through shortcut correlations that bind rare attributes to stereotyped contexts. This failure is especially consequential in safety-critical domains such as medical imaging and autonomous driving, where the long tail of semantic compositions carries disproportionate decision-making weight. Existing data-augmentation and re-weighting schemes treat rarity as a marginal per-attribute problem, ignoring the combinatorial structure of the joint semantic space and the label noise that pervades rare annotations.
proposals.hypothesis: A generator regularized to match the joint support of a learned compositional rarity map—where each coverage constraint is weighted by its estimated annotation uncertainty—will produce significantly more faithful and diverse rare-attribute combinations than marginal re-weighting baselines, without sacrificing overall generation quality or amplifying label noise.
proposals.method: We first construct a structured rarity map by fitting a sparse log-linear model over discrete attribute, pose-bin, and background-cluster features from the training set, yielding per-combination rarity scores and heteroscedastic uncertainty estimates derived from annotation agreement and neighborhood consistency. A coverage regularization term is added to the generator objective that penalizes shortfall between the empirical generation distribution over these combinations and the target support, with each term down-weighted in proportion to its annotation uncertainty via a learned attenuation function. A counterfactual recombination pipeline uses attribute-disentangled editing guided by CLIP-space directions to synthesize plausible images pairing rare attributes with diverse contexts, and a dedicated minority critic—a small auxiliary discriminator conditioned on the rarity map—explicitly penalizes generations where rare attributes co-occur only with their most frequent backgrounds or viewpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work reframes rare-generation failure from a single-label frequency problem to a joint compositional coverage problem, providing both a principled regularization framework and a practical counterfactual augmentation pipeline. The uncertainty-weighted formulation offers the first mechanism to prevent noisy rare-label annotations from destabilizing training, a known but unaddressed failure mode in long-tail generation. We expect measurable gains on compositional coverage metrics across CelebA-HQ attribute combinations, LSUN scene–object co-occurrences, and a medical skin-lesion benchmark where rare dermoscopic pattern combinations are clinically significant.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior long-tail generation methods that treat attributes independently and apply uniform re-weighting, our approach models rarity over the combinatorial attribute–pose–background space and modulates each coverage constraint by annotation uncertainty, preventing the well-documented but unaddressed problem of noisy rare labels dominating the training signal. The minority critic architecture, which conditions on the structured rarity map to explicitly detect context-stereotyped shortcuts, is a new adversarial mechanism with no direct precedent in the generative modeling literature.

proposals.title: Occupancy-Aware Mode Regularization: Borrowing from Ecological Survey Models to Preserve Rare Attribute Combinations in Conditional Generative Networks
proposals.motivation: Conditional generative models systematically under-represent rare attribute combinations, collapsing them into majority modes—a failure that limits their reliability in safety-critical applications such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, and fair synthetic data generation. This mode-dropping problem mirrors the well-studied ecological challenge where rare species appear absent in surveys not because they are truly missing but because detection probability is low, a distinction formalized by hierarchical occupancy models. Given the growing deployment of generative models in domains where tail-distribution fidelity has real-world consequences, a principled regularization framework that explicitly prevents the erasure of rare conditions is both timely and necessary.
proposals.hypothesis: Generative models that incorporate a hierarchical occupancy-inspired regularization term—one that separates low training-set frequency from true generative incapacity and applies rarity-scaled penalties with partial pooling across semantically related attributes—will achieve substantially higher fidelity on rare attribute combinations without degrading overall sample quality, compared to standard conditional training, reweighting baselines, and existing mode-coverage objectives.
proposals.method: We introduce Occupancy-Aware Mode Regularization (OAMR), which augments the generator's loss with a conditional penalty term that is activated whenever a requested rare attribute tuple is visually absent or semantically replaced in the output, as measured by a pre-trained attribute classifier. Penalty magnitudes are set via inverse-frequency weighting with an uncertainty-aware correction estimated from a beta-binomial occupancy model fitted to training-set attribute co-occurrence counts, directly paralleling ecological detection-prevalence decomposition. A hierarchical prior over related attribute groups (e.g., hair color variants, rare medical lesion subtypes) enables partial pooling so that extremely scarce conditions borrow statistical strength from neighboring attributes, stabilizing gradients. Training employs targeted minibatch construction that guarantees a minimum proportion of rare-attribute samples per batch, implemented as a stratified sampler over attribute-tuple strata.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a formal connection between ecological occupancy modeling and generative mode coverage, providing a theoretically grounded rarity-aware regularization framework that generalizes beyond ad-hoc reweighting; (2) a practical, architecture-agnostic training procedure (OAMR) applicable to GANs, diffusion models, and VAEs that demonstrably improves rare-attribute fidelity on CelebA-HQ, DermNet, and a synthetic multi-attribute benchmark; and (3) empirical evidence that hierarchical attribute sharing prevents the gradient instability typically caused by naive inverse-frequency scaling, establishing a new best practice for conditional generation under long-tailed attribute distributions.
proposals.novelty: While reweighting and oversampling are well-known remedies for class imbalance, no prior work in generative modeling has formalized the ecological distinction between detection failure and true absence as a structured regularization objective, nor has hierarchical partial pooling across attribute ontologies been used to stabilize rare-condition generation. The combination of occupancy-theoretic penalty design with targeted minibatch stratification yields a principled framework that is both more theoretically motivated and more robust than existing approaches.

proposals.title: Inverse Consistency Regularization with Attribute-Conditioned Latent Posteriors for Minority-Aware Conditional Generation
proposals.motivation: Conditional generative models routinely suffer from mode dropping on rare attribute combinations—side views, unusual backgrounds, and infrequent label intersections are under-represented in the learned generator, leading to poor fidelity and reduced coverage precisely where diversity matters most. Current reconstruction-based objectives force each sample into a single latent code, collapsing the inherent ambiguity of many-to-one mappings and disproportionately penalizing minority conditions. Addressing this failure mode is critical for deploying generative models in safety-sensitive and fairness-aware applications, aligning with broader concerns about robust and trustworthy AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the deterministic encoder with an attribute-conditioned posterior that predicts a distribution over plausible latent codes, and regularizing the generator to remain consistent with this posterior under each condition, will significantly reduce conditional mode dropping and improve generation fidelity and coverage for rare attribute combinations without degrading performance on majority conditions.
proposals.method: We introduce an inverse consistency regularizer that pairs a conditional generator G(z, c) with an amortized attribute-conditioned inference network q(z | x, c) outputting a mixture-of-Gaussians posterior over latent codes. During training, for each generated sample x = G(z, c), the regularizer penalizes the KL divergence between the forward sampling distribution and the inferred posterior q(z | x, c), weighted by an importance factor inversely proportional to the empirical frequency of condition c so that rare labels receive stronger consistency enforcement. We further employ a set-valued inverse mapping that maintains a small particle set of plausible latent codes per example, updated via Stein variational gradient descent, ensuring that ambiguous samples are not collapsed to a single mode. The full objective combines adversarial or diffusion-based generation loss, the frequency-weighted inverse consistency term, and a mutual information lower bound between conditions and generated outputs to prevent attribute information loss.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled regularization framework that unifies uncertainty-aware inference with conditional generation, providing both theoretical grounding through the connection to conditional cycle-consistency and practical gains in minority-condition fidelity. Experiments on multi-attribute face generation, compositional scene synthesis, and rare-class medical image generation will demonstrate measurable improvements in conditional FID, attribute classification accuracy, and coverage metrics for tail-distribution conditions. The framework is generator-architecture-agnostic and can be applied as a drop-in regularizer for GANs, VAEs, and diffusion models.
proposals.novelty: While cycle-consistency and encoder-based regularization have been explored independently, conditioning the inverse mapping on requested attributes and weighting consistency enforcement by label rarity is new—it transforms the regularizer from a global reconstruction signal into a targeted, uncertainty-aware fairness mechanism. The use of set-valued latent posteriors via particle-based inference to preserve multiple valid explanations for ambiguous samples further distinguishes this approach from prior deterministic or single-Gaussian inverse methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Consistency Regularization for Robust Conditional Generative Modeling of Rare Attributes
proposals.motivation: Conditional generative models, such as conditional VAEs and GANs, often suffer from mode collapse and poor fidelity on minority or ambiguous conditions (e.g., rare disease phenotypes in medical imaging, unusual semantic combinations). This failure stems from a deterministic encoder objective that forces ambiguous or rare-condition samples into a single latent code, oversimplifying the true posterior and degrading coverage and fidelity precisely where robustness is most needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the deterministic encoder with an attribute-conditioned, uncertainty-aware inverse mapping that predicts a distribution of plausible latent codes, and regularizing the generator to be consistent with this posterior, will improve fidelity and coverage for rare and ambiguous conditions without sacrificing performance on common modes.
proposals.method: We will extend a conditional VAE framework. The encoder, E(x, a), will output parameters for a conditional Gaussian posterior q(z|x, a). A consistency regularizer will enforce that for a generated sample x' = G(z, a) with z ~ q(z|x, a), the latent code z' = E(x', a) remains probable under the original posterior. The strength of this regularizer will be weighted by the inverse frequency of attribute 'a' in the dataset. We will test on datasets with inherent ambiguity and label imbalance, such as medical imaging (colonoscopy frames with rare anatomical landmarks or pathologies) and multi-view object datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training objective that explicitly protects minority conditions by treating them as inherently uncertain, leading to more robust and fair conditional generative models. It will offer a principled method to mitigate conditional mode dropping, advancing the reliability of generative AI in high-stakes, imbalanced domains like healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While inverse consistency is known in unsupervised learning (e.g., cycle consistency), its application as an uncertainty-aware, attribute-conditioned regularizer specifically designed to protect rare and ambiguous labels in conditional generation is novel. It directly addresses the oversimplification of latent posteriors for minority data, a key failure mode not solved by standard reconstruction or adversarial losses.

proposals.title: Attribute-Conditional Mode Regularization: Preventing Minority Attribute Collapse in Conditional Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks and diffusion models systematically under-represent rare attribute combinations, producing high-fidelity samples for majority classes while silently dropping minority conditions such as unusual poses, rare backgrounds, or infrequent semantic pairings. This failure mode is particularly dangerous in fairness-sensitive applications—face generation, medical imaging, and data augmentation for long-tailed recognition—where the missing modes correspond precisely to underrepresented populations. Existing mode-coverage regularizers operate globally and cannot distinguish whether a rare attribute is being dropped because it is difficult or because it is infrequent, motivating attribute-aware regularization.
proposals.hypothesis: Conditioning the generator's diversity regularizer on class labels or semantic attributes—so that the penalty for mode collapse scales inversely with attribute frequency—will significantly improve recall of rare attribute combinations without degrading overall sample quality, because it decouples the coverage objective from the global distribution and applies targeted pressure where mode dropping is most likely.
proposals.method: We introduce Attribute-Conditional Mode Regularization (ACMR), which augments the generator loss with an auxiliary term that measures per-attribute-group coverage in a learned feature space. Concretely, we partition each mini-batch by its conditioning labels, compute a kernel-based diversity score (Maximum Mean Discrepancy or a Vendi-score variant) within each partition, and weight the resulting penalty inversely by the empirical attribute frequency so that rare groups receive stronger regularization pressure.
