The method is evaluated on CelebA (40 binary attributes), CelebA-HQ with compositional attribute queries, and a long-tailed medical skin-lesion dataset, comparing against unconditional diversity regularization, rejection-sampling baselines, and classifier-free guidance reweighting across both GAN (StyleGAN2-ADA) and diffusion (EDM) backbones.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first regularization framework that explicitly targets minority-attribute mode collapse by making the diversity objective attribute-aware, offering a principled and training-time solution that avoids the computational overhead of post-hoc rejection sampling or inference-time reweighting. We expect measurable gains in attribute-conditioned recall and FID-per-group metrics, especially for rare combinatorial attribute queries (e.g., side-view plus hat plus eyeglasses), establishing new benchmarks for conditional coverage evaluation. The approach also yields practical guidelines for choosing per-attribute regularization weights, bridging fairness-aware generation and mode-coverage theory.
proposals.novelty: While prior mode regularization methods treat coverage as a monolithic objective over the full data manifold, ACMR is the first to decompose the regularizer along semantic attribute axes and apply frequency-adaptive weighting, making the insight that global diversity metrics can mask severe per-subgroup collapse both explicit and actionable within the training loop.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Encoders for Reconstruction Regularization in Generative Models with Ambiguous Inverse Mappings
proposals.motivation: Reconstruction-based regularizers in generative models assume a deterministic encoder that maps each data point to a unique latent code, yet real data manifolds often admit multiple valid latent explanations for a single observation. This many-to-one ambiguity causes encoders to average over modes, collapsing fine-grained structure and producing overly smooth reconstructions that fail to capture the true complexity of the data distribution. Addressing this limitation is critical for scaling generative models to scientific domains—molecular design, visual neuroscience, medical imaging—where preserving multimodal latent structure is essential for downstream utility.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing deterministic point-estimate encoders with structured posterior encoders that explicitly represent multimodal uncertainty over latent codes will yield reconstruction regularizers that preserve complex data manifold geometry, improving both generation quality and latent space fidelity compared to standard single-mode encoder objectives.
proposals.method: We propose a mixture-density encoder network that outputs a mixture of Gaussians in latent space, trained with a modified ELBO objective augmented by a coverage-encouraging diversity term that penalizes mode collapse across mixture components. For each data point, the encoder produces K candidate latent codes with associated responsibilities; the reconstruction loss is computed as a responsibility-weighted expectation, and an entropy bonus on the mixture weights ensures exploration of alternative explanations. We further introduce a local Jacobian regularizer that encourages each mixture component to capture a geometrically coherent neighborhood of the data manifold, preventing degenerate solutions. The approach is architecture-agnostic and will be evaluated by integrating it into GAN, VAE, and flow-based generators across image (CelebA-HQ, LSUN), molecular (ZINC-250K), and neuroscience (calcium imaging) benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both a practical encoder module that can be dropped into existing generative frameworks and a theoretical analysis of when ambiguous inverse mappings degrade reconstruction regularization. We expect measurable improvements in FID, reconstruction diversity metrics, and downstream task performance in molecular property prediction and neural decoding, establishing that multimodal encoder objectives are a necessary component for faithful generative modeling of structured scientific data.
proposals.novelty: While variational encoders model uncertainty with a single Gaussian, and mixture-based priors have been explored, no prior work has specifically designed encoder objectives to handle the many-to-one ambiguity that arises in reconstruction regularization for generators. The combination of mixture-density encoding with geometric Jacobian constraints to preserve local manifold structure under ambiguous inversion is, to our knowledge, unexplored and addresses a gap between VAE-style inference and GAN-style generation.

proposals.title: Towards Better Encoder Objectives For Ambiguous Inverse Mappings: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to The regularizer relies on an encoder that maps data points back to latent codes so the generator can. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that better encoder objectives for ambiguous inverse mappings can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates future work principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: The regularizer relies on an encoder that maps data points back to latent codes so the generator can reconstruct them. Future work could investigate e.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the better encoder objectives for ambiguous inverse mappings problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Adaptive Phase Scheduling for Manifold-Diffusion GAN Training via Online Manifold Proximity Detection
proposals.motivation: Manifold-Diffusion GANs (MDGAN) decompose training into a manifold-alignment phase and a diffusion phase, but the transition point is manually tuned per dataset, limiting scalability and robustness. Miscalibrated scheduling leads to either premature diffusion on a poorly aligned manifold or wasted computation on redundant alignment steps. An automatic scheduling mechanism would make manifold-diffusion training broadly applicable without per-task hyperparameter searches.
proposals.hypothesis: An online estimator of the Hausdorff-like distance between the generator manifold and the data manifold, computed from discriminator feature statistics, can reliably trigger phase transitions and dynamic rebalancing, yielding sample quality equal to or better than oracle-tuned fixed schedules while eliminating the manual split entirely.
proposals.method: We propose a lightweight manifold proximity score computed from the Fréchet distance between discriminator intermediate-layer activations for real and generated batches, updated with exponential moving averages for stability. A controller uses this score with a threshold-crossing rule augmented by a patience mechanism to transition from alignment-dominated to diffusion-dominated training, and can interleave or rebalance phases via a softmax-weighted mixture of the two loss objectives. We validate on image benchmarks (CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, LSUN) by comparing FID and precision-recall curves against fixed-schedule MDGAN and ablate the proximity estimator against alternatives such as kernel MMD and classifier two-sample tests.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled, data-driven phase scheduling framework that removes the primary hyperparameter burden of manifold-diffusion GAN training, (2) empirical evidence that adaptive scheduling matches or exceeds hand-tuned baselines across diverse image domains, and (3) analysis showing the learned schedule reveals interpretable training dynamics, providing insight into when and why manifold alignment saturates.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum and progressive training strategies exist for GANs, no prior work frames the manifold-alignment-to-diffusion transition as an online detection problem solvable with discriminator-derived manifold proximity statistics, nor explores continuous rebalancing of dual-phase GAN objectives as an alternative to hard switching.

proposals.title: Orthogonal Stabilization Decomposition: Disentangling the Failure Modes Addressed by Mode Regularization, Unrolled Optimization, and Feature Matching in GAN Training
proposals.motivation: Despite significant progress in GAN training stabilization, practitioners still face unpredictable training failures because existing techniques—mode regularization, unrolled optimization, spectral normalization, and feature matching—are typically studied in isolation or combined ad hoc without understanding their interaction effects. There is no systematic framework that identifies which specific failure modes (mode collapse, training divergence, vanishing gradients, oscillatory non-convergence) each stabilization component uniquely resolves versus which failures persist or are redundantly addressed. Building such a framework is critical now because GANs are being deployed in increasingly high-stakes domains including drug design and medical imaging, where silent failure modes can have serious downstream consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: Mode regularization, unrolled optimization, and feature-based stabilization methods address largely orthogonal subsets of GAN training failure modes, and their principled combination via a modular hybrid training framework will yield both improved generation quality and a diagnostic decomposition that predicts which stabilization components are necessary for a given data distribution and architecture.
proposals.method: We propose a controlled factorial experimental framework that systematically trains GANs under all 2^k combinations of k orthogonal stabilization techniques—specifically mode regularization, unrolled optimization (with varying unrolling depths), feature matching at multiple discriminator layers, and spectral normalization—across a diverse benchmark of datasets spanning low-dimensional synthetic distributions, standard image benchmarks (CIFAR-10, CelebA, LSUN), and structured data (molecular graphs). For each combination, we instrument training with fine-grained diagnostic probes measuring mode coverage via birthday paradox tests, gradient magnitude statistics, discriminator loss landscape curvature, and generator output diversity metrics at regular intervals. We then apply causal attribution analysis using Shapley value decomposition to quantify each component's marginal contribution to resolving specific failure types, and derive a lightweight meta-learned selector that recommends the minimal stabilization subset given early-training diagnostic signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic taxonomy of GAN failure modes mapped to their corresponding stabilization solutions, enabling practitioners to diagnose and resolve training pathologies with targeted interventions rather than expensive trial-and-error. The modular hybrid training framework and accompanying meta-selector will reduce the computational cost of GAN training by identifying minimal necessary stabilization components, and the factorial analysis will reveal previously unknown interaction effects—both synergistic and antagonistic—between popular stabilization methods. The resulting open-source diagnostic toolkit will serve as a practical resource for the growing community applying GANs to scientific domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has independently proposed and evaluated individual GAN stabilization techniques, no existing study performs a rigorous causal decomposition of their joint and marginal effects on distinct failure modes. The combination of factorial experimental design with Shapley-based attribution and a meta-learned component selector is a novel methodological contribution that transforms GAN stabilization from an art into a principled engineering discipline.

proposals.title: Manifold-Aligned Generation via Optimal Transport Plans with Mode-Mass Guarantees
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models such as VAEs and diffusion models often suffer from mode collapse or mode undercoverage because their training objectives rely on pointwise reconstruction or local score matching, which fail to enforce global geometric consistency between the learned and true data manifolds. Optimal transport (OT) provides a principled framework for comparing probability distributions that inherently accounts for the cost of moving mass across the support, making it uniquely suited to enforce faithful manifold alignment. Developing OT-based training objectives that provably preserve mode mass would address a fundamental limitation in generative modeling and improve reliability in safety-critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing or augmenting standard reconstruction losses with entropy-regularized optimal transport plans between mini-batch empirical distributions on the generated and data manifolds will yield generative models that achieve significantly higher mode coverage and lower Wasserstein distance to the true data distribution, without sacrificing sample quality.
proposals.method: We propose Manifold-Aligned Optimal Transport (MAOT), a training framework that computes Sinkhorn-divergence-based transport plans between encoded data representations and generated samples in a learned latent space, using geodesic costs estimated via a neural metric on the data manifold. The transport plan is incorporated as an auxiliary loss that penalizes mass imbalance across identified mode clusters, estimated online via a lightweight Dirichlet process mixture. We integrate MAOT into both VAE and score-based diffusion architectures, leveraging mini-batch OT with debiased Sinkhorn divergences for scalability, and validate on synthetic manifolds with known topology as well as standard image benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a theoretically grounded and practically scalable method for training generative models that explicitly align generated and data manifolds via optimal transport with mode-mass preservation guarantees. It provides the first systematic study connecting OT plan structure to mode coverage in deep generative models, along with diagnostic tools for quantifying per-mode mass fidelity. The resulting models are expected to demonstrate state-of-the-art mode coverage on benchmarks known for mode-collapse pathologies, such as Stacked MNIST and CelebA attribute subgroups.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used Wasserstein distances as evaluation metrics or simple loss surrogates, our approach is non-obvious in that it leverages the structure of the transport plan itself—not just the scalar divergence—to construct per-mode mass-balance penalties, combined with neural geodesic cost estimation that respects the intrinsic geometry of the data manifold rather than relying on ambient Euclidean distances.

proposals.title: Homotopy-Scheduled Objective Morphing: Continuation Methods for Bridging Geometric and Adversarial Training Losses
proposals.motivation: Training generative models with adversarial objectives suffers from well-documented instabilities—mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and sensitivity to hyperparameters—particularly in the early phases of learning when the generator is far from the data manifold. Supervised geometric losses (e.g., L1, perceptual, or feature-matching losses) provide stable, well-conditioned gradients early on but plateau in sample quality. A principled framework for smoothly interpolating between these regimes would combine the optimization-friendly landscape of geometric losses with the distributional sharpness of adversarial objectives, directly addressing a core bottleneck in generative modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuous homotopy schedule that parameterizes the training objective as a convex combination L(λ) = (1−λ)·L_geometric + λ·L_adversarial, where λ is advanced from 0 to 1 according to a curriculum tied to training progress indicators, will yield faster convergence, improved final sample quality, and greater training stability compared to fixed-weight or abrupt-switching baselines, because it maintains a connected path of well-conditioned loss landscapes throughout training.
proposals.method: We formulate the training objective as a one-parameter family of loss functions and apply numerical continuation methods to trace the solution path as the homotopy parameter λ increases. Concretely, we define λ(t) via an adaptive schedule governed by monitoring signals such as the discriminator's classification accuracy and the generator's gradient signal-to-noise ratio: λ advances only when the model demonstrates sufficient competence at the current difficulty level. We implement predictor-corrector steps inspired by classical arc-length continuation to avoid bifurcation points in the loss landscape, using short rollbacks when training diagnostics detect instability. Experiments span conditional image synthesis (on CIFAR-10, LSUN-Bedroom, and ImageNet 256×256) using StyleGAN2 and diffusion-GAN hybrid architectures, comparing against fixed-weight ablations, linear annealing, and curriculum-GAN baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a theoretically grounded homotopy framework that unifies geometric and adversarial training under continuation theory, providing formal conditions under which the solution path is smooth and trackable; (2) a practical adaptive scheduling algorithm with monitoring-based step-size control that requires no additional hyperparameter tuning beyond the base objectives; and (3) extensive empirical evidence that homotopy-scheduled training improves FID and precision-recall metrics by a significant margin while reducing training time to convergence and eliminating common failure modes such as mode collapse and training divergence.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and loss-weighting schedules exist in generative modeling, they are typically ad hoc linear anneals lacking theoretical grounding. Our approach is the first to apply formal continuation and homotopy methods from numerical analysis—including predictor-corrector tracking and bifurcation detection—to the adversarial training problem, transforming loss scheduling from a heuristic into a principled optimization strategy with convergence guarantees under mild smoothness assumptions.

proposals.title: Homotopy-Based Optimization for Stable Adversarial Training in Molecular Generation
proposals.motivation: Adversarial training, particularly in generative models for molecular design, is notoriously unstable and sensitive to initialization due to the complex, non-convex nature of the learned discriminator loss landscape. This instability hinders the reliable discovery of novel, valid molecules with desired properties. Pre-2026 work has shown that simpler geometric losses (e.g., reconstruction or distance-based losses) are easier to optimize but yield less expressive models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a homotopy method, which starts optimization with a stable geometric loss and continuously deforms the objective function towards a powerful adversarial loss, will yield more stable training, better convergence, and ultimately higher-quality generated molecules than direct adversarial optimization.
proposals.method: We will develop a homotopy parameter, λ, that blends a geometric loss (e.g., a VAE's reconstruction loss or a metric learning loss on molecular fingerprints) with a GAN's adversarial loss. Training begins at λ=0 (pure geometric loss) and λ is gradually increased to 1 (pure adversarial loss) according to an annealing schedule based on model performance. We will implement this within a state-of-the-art molecular graph generative architecture (e.g., a GraphVAE or JT-VAE framework coupled with a graph-based discriminator) and test it on standard molecular datasets like ZINC or QM9.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled optimization framework to stabilize adversarial training for generative models, specifically in the challenging domain of molecular design. It will demonstrate empirically that homotopy methods can lead to improved sample validity, diversity, and property optimization compared to standard adversarial training baselines.
proposals.novelty: While homotopy methods are known in numerical optimization, their application to stabilize adversarial training in deep generative models for scientific discovery is novel. This approach systematically bridges the gap between the easy optimization of geometric priors and the high sample quality of adversarial objectives.

proposals.title: Persistent Homology Diagnostics for Mode Coverage and Manifold Fidelity in Deep Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models such as GANs and diffusion models are evaluated primarily through sample-level metrics like FID and IS, which conflate texture quality with topological fidelity and fail to detect subtle mode dropping or incorrect manifold connectivity. Since real-world data distributions often exhibit nontrivial topological structure—disconnected components corresponding to semantic classes, loops arising from continuous attribute variation—a diagnostic framework that directly quantifies these structural properties is urgently needed. Topological data analysis (TDA), particularly persistent homology, offers a mathematically principled lens to compare the coarse shape of learned and target distributions, filling a critical blind spot in generative model evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: Persistent homology-based summaries of generated and real data distributions provide a more sensitive and interpretable diagnostic of mode collapse, mode invention, and manifold structural mismatch than existing sample-quality metrics, enabling targeted identification of which topological features (connected components, holes, voids) a generator fails to reproduce.
proposals.method: We propose computing multi-scale persistent homology on point-cloud samples from both real data and generator output, operating in learned representation spaces to mitigate the curse of dimensionality. Persistence diagrams are compared via the sliced Wasserstein distance on persistence measures, yielding per-dimension topological discrepancy scores (β₀ for connected components/modes, β₁ for loops, β₂ for voids). These scores are integrated into a differentiable topological regularization term using recent advances in differentiable persistence (e.g., topological autoencoders), enabling end-to-end training that penalizes topological mismatch alongside reconstruction or adversarial losses.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a diagnostic toolkit—TopoDiag—that decomposes generative model failures into interpretable topological categories (missing modes, spurious modes, broken or fused manifold components), providing actionable feedback beyond scalar quality scores; (2) a differentiable topological regularizer that demonstrably reduces mode collapse on standard benchmarks (Stacked MNIST, CIFAR-10, CelebA) as measured by both classical and topological metrics; and (3) theoretical analysis connecting Betti number discrepancies in persistence diagrams to mode-counting accuracy under mild density assumptions.
proposals.novelty: While persistent homology has been applied to data analysis broadly, its systematic use as a per-dimension diagnostic decomposition of generative model failure modes—combined with a differentiable training signal operating in learned latent spaces rather than ambient pixel space—has not been explored. The non-obvious insight is that projecting TDA into representation space sidesteps the well-known computational and statistical barriers of high-dimensional persistent homology, making topological diagnostics practical for modern generative models at scale.

proposals.title: Lyapunov-Guided Training of Generative Adversarial Networks: A Control-Theoretic Framework for Stabilizing Adversarial Dynamics
proposals.motivation: GAN training remains notoriously unstable, suffering from mode collapse, oscillatory dynamics, and vanishing gradients despite numerous heuristic remedies. Existing stabilization techniques—spectral normalization, gradient penalties, progressive growing—lack a unified theoretical foundation that explains when and why training diverges. Control theory offers a mature mathematical toolkit for analyzing and stabilizing coupled dynamical systems, yet its systematic application to adversarial generative models remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: The generator-discriminator interaction in GANs can be modeled as a nonlinear feedback control system, and constructing a Lyapunov function over the joint parameter space enables the design of adaptive learning rate controllers that provably prevent training trajectories from leaving stable regions, thereby reducing mode collapse and improving convergence reliability without sacrificing sample quality.
proposals.method: We formulate GAN training as a continuous-time dynamical system where the generator and discriminator define coupled ordinary differential equations over their respective parameter spaces. We derive candidate Lyapunov functions based on the integral of the discriminator's classification margin and the generator's distributional divergence, then use LaSalle's invariance principle to characterize the stability basin around Nash equilibria. From this analysis, we design a feedback controller that adaptively modulates per-layer learning rates and gradient clipping thresholds based on real-time estimates of the Lyapunov function's time derivative, implementing this as a lightweight wrapper around standard optimizers like Adam.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first rigorous Lyapunov stability analysis of GAN training dynamics that translates directly into a practical algorithmic intervention, bridging the gap between control-theoretic guarantees and deep generative modeling. The adaptive controller is expected to reduce training failure rates across architectures (DCGAN, StyleGAN, BigGAN) while maintaining or improving FID and IS scores on standard benchmarks. Beyond GANs, the framework establishes a methodology for applying stability analysis to other adversarial or multi-agent learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has drawn loose analogies between GANs and game theory or dynamical systems, no existing approach constructs explicit Lyapunov certificates for the joint generator-discriminator system and derives closed-form feedback control laws from them. The key non-obvious insight is that treating the discriminator's loss landscape curvature as a control-relevant state variable enables stability guarantees that are architecture-agnostic rather than tied to specific regularization schemes.

proposals.title: Fokker-Planck Generative Dynamics: Mode-Covering Generative Models via Particle Diffusion under Learned Potentials
proposals.motivation: Mode collapse remains a central failure mode of adversarial generative models, producing high-quality but insufficiently diverse samples. While methods such as MDGAN introduce a diffusion-like second phase, they rely on adversarial heuristics rather than principled dynamics, leaving mode coverage improvements fragile and hard to diagnose. A rigorous statistical-physics formulation connecting generator sample evolution to Fokker-Planck dynamics would provide both theoretical guarantees on mode coverage and practical algorithms with tunable exploration-exploitation trade-offs.
proposals.hypothesis: If generator samples are treated as interacting particles evolving under a learnable potential with controlled diffusion, then shaping the drift and noise terms to minimize a Fokker-Planck-derived free-energy functional will provably spread probability mass toward undercovered modes while preventing density collapse, yielding better mode coverage than adversarial or score-matching baselines without sacrificing sample quality.
proposals.method: We model each generator output as a particle whose position evolves via a Langevin stochastic differential equation with a parameterized potential learned end-to-end. The corresponding Fokker-Planck equation governs the time evolution of the generated density; we derive a variational free-energy objective combining a KL term that penalizes deviation from the data distribution with a repulsive entropy term that discourages particle concentration. The potential is parameterized by a neural network trained jointly with the generator via a particle-based estimator of the Fokker-Planck residual, using kernel density estimates for the entropy gradient and a critic network for the drift term. Training alternates between (i) simulating particle trajectories for a fixed number of Langevin steps and (ii) updating the potential parameters to reduce the free-energy gap, with an adaptive diffusion coefficient schedule that anneals from high exploration to fine-grained fitting.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a theoretically grounded generative framework that replaces adversarial mode-covering heuristics with explicit Fokker-Planck dynamics, providing verifiable convergence conditions tied to free-energy descent. Empirically, it is expected to achieve superior mode coverage on standard benchmarks such as Stacked MNIST, CIFAR-10, and synthetic mixture-of-Gaussians tasks compared to GAN variants and score-based diffusion models, while maintaining competitive FID scores. The framework also yields interpretable diagnostics: the learned potential landscape directly visualizes where the model allocates probability mass, enabling researchers to detect and debug residual mode neglect.
proposals.novelty: Unlike score-based diffusion models that denoise from a fixed prior or adversarial methods that lack explicit density dynamics, our approach learns the potential governing particle motion so that the stationary distribution of the Fokker-Planck equation matches the data distribution by construction. The explicit free-energy decomposition into energy and entropy terms provides a principled, tunable mechanism for balancing sample quality against mode coverage, a trade-off that existing methods address only implicitly.

proposals.title: Active Hard-Example Mining via Missing-Mode Detection for Mode-Complete Generative Modeling
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks and variational autoencoders notoriously suffer from mode dropping, producing high-quality samples that nonetheless fail to cover the full data manifold. Existing remedies such as diversity-promoting regularizers or mixture-based architectures treat all training examples uniformly and cannot adapt to the specific modes the current model is failing to capture. A targeted, active-learning-inspired approach that identifies and prioritizes precisely those real examples falling on missing modes would close this gap efficiently, reducing both training cost and residual mode collapse.
proposals.hypothesis: A third-party missing-mode discriminator trained to distinguish held-out real data from generated samples can reliably identify under-covered manifold regions, and feeding those hard examples back into training via importance reweighting and prioritized replay will yield generators with significantly higher mode coverage without sacrificing sample fidelity.
proposals.method: We train an auxiliary classifier (the missing-mode detector) on a mixture of real test images and generator outputs, using its per-sample confidence scores to rank real examples by how poorly they are represented in the generated distribution. High-scoring hard examples are aggregated into a priority replay buffer whose sampling weights are updated each training epoch. The encoder-generator pair (applicable to both VAE and GAN families) then receives a composite training signal: the standard reconstruction or adversarial loss on uniformly sampled data, plus a boosted loss term on the reweighted hard-example mini-batches that steers the latent space toward under-covered regions. We further introduce a curriculum schedule that anneals the hard-example ratio from low to high, preventing early-training instability.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, model-agnostic active-mining framework that converts any external mode-coverage diagnostic into a closed-loop training signal for generative models. Empirically, we expect measurable improvements in mode coverage metrics (number of covered modes, reverse KL divergence, and FID-recall) on standard benchmarks including Stacked MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CelebA, while maintaining or improving FID-precision. The framework also provides interpretable diagnostics—the hard-example queue itself reveals which data sub-populations the generator struggles with, offering actionable insight for practitioners.
proposals.novelty: While missing-mode detection has been proposed as an evaluation tool, no prior work closes the loop by actively mining the detected hard examples and reweighting them to steer generator training. Combining a third-party discriminator with importance-weighted replay and curriculum scheduling is non-obvious because naively upweighting hard examples risks destabilizing adversarial training; our annealed curriculum and dual-loss formulation address this instability in a theoretically motivated way.

proposals.title: Calibrated Density-Ratio Estimation for Quantitative Missing-Mode Detection in Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks and related models frequently suffer from mode dropping, yet current evaluation metrics like FID and IS provide only aggregate quality scores that obscure which modes are missing. The standard GAN discriminator implicitly estimates density ratios between real and generated distributions, but this estimator is poorly calibrated, architecture-dependent, and collapses when the discriminator achieves near-perfect separation. Reliable, interpretable per-mode coverage scoring would enable targeted model debugging and principled comparison of generative approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the raw discriminator output with a formally calibrated density-ratio estimator—built on telescoping mixtures of the real and generated distributions and post-hoc calibration—yields a coverage diagnostic that is statistically consistent, architecture-agnostic, and directly interpretable as a quantitative missing-mode score.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage pipeline: (1) construct a sequence of interpolating distributions between the real data and generator output using noise-bridged mixtures, then train lightweight ratio estimators (uLSIF or spectral series estimators) on adjacent pairs to avoid the degenerate perfect-separation regime; (2) compose these telescoping ratios to recover the full density ratio, applying isotonic regression or Venn-Abers calibration to each stage to ensure well-calibrated probability outputs; (3) aggregate calibrated ratios within learned cluster assignments to produce per-mode coverage scores with confidence intervals. We validate on standard image benchmarks (CIFAR-10, FFHQ) and a synthetic multimodal distribution where ground-truth mode frequencies are known, comparing against classifier two-sample tests and precision-recall metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first principled framework that converts GAN-style discriminator signals into calibrated, per-mode coverage scores with statistical guarantees. It decouples the evaluation mechanism from the generator architecture, enabling fair cross-model comparison and interpretable diagnostics. The telescoping ratio construction also offers a practical recipe for stable density-ratio estimation in high dimensions that may find uses beyond generative model evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While density-ratio estimation and classifier calibration are individually well-studied, their composition through telescoping noise bridges for mode-level generative model evaluation is new. The non-obvious insight is that calibrating intermediate ratios along a noise schedule, rather than calibrating a single end-to-end discriminator, sidesteps the high-dimensional perfect-separation problem and yields provably consistent coverage scores.

proposals.title: Temporal Manifold-Diffusion Regularization for Mode-Preserving Video Generation
proposals.motivation: Current video generation models, whether GAN-based or diffusion-based, systematically drop rare temporal modes such as unusual motions, infrequent scene transitions, and low-probability action sequences, leading to outputs that collapse toward average dynamics. Manifold-aware regularization has shown promise for preserving appearance diversity in image generation, but its extension to the temporal domain—where mode collapse manifests as missing motion patterns rather than missing visual categories—remains unexplored. Addressing this gap is critical for applications ranging from realistic simulation environments to scientific video synthesis where rare events carry disproportionate informational value.
proposals.hypothesis: A diffusion-based video generation model augmented with a temporal manifold-alignment regularizer—which penalizes discrepancies between the learned temporal latent manifold and the empirical manifold of training video dynamics—will preserve significantly more rare motion modes and temporal transitions than unregularized baselines, without sacrificing overall generation quality.
proposals.method: We construct a temporal manifold-diffusion framework that operates in two stages: first, we learn a low-dimensional temporal manifold from training videos using a sequence-aware variational encoder that maps short video clips to trajectories on a Riemannian manifold, capturing both common and rare dynamical modes via a kernel density estimate over trajectory embeddings. Second, we introduce a manifold-alignment regularization term into the denoising objective of a video diffusion model, penalizing the KL divergence between the distribution of generated temporal trajectories projected onto this manifold and the empirical training distribution, with particular up-weighting of low-density regions corresponding to rare modes. We evaluate on standard benchmarks (UCF-101, Kinetics-600) using both conventional metrics (FVD, IS) and a novel Temporal Mode Recall metric that measures coverage of rare action and transition categories.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first principled extension of manifold-based diversity regularization from static images to sequential video data, offering both a theoretical framework connecting temporal mode collapse to manifold geometry and a practical regularization method compatible with modern video diffusion architectures. We expect to demonstrate measurable improvements in rare-mode recall—particularly for infrequent actions and unusual scene transitions—while maintaining competitive FVD scores, establishing that manifold-aware training is a general principle applicable beyond the image domain. The proposed Temporal Mode Recall metric itself fills an evaluation gap, giving the community a tool to diagnose temporal mode collapse systematically.
proposals.novelty: While prior manifold-alignment regularizers operate on static feature spaces, our approach models the geometry of temporal trajectories on learned Riemannian manifolds, requiring new formulations for geodesic-aware density estimation over video dynamics rather than pixel appearances. The combination of trajectory-level manifold learning with density-sensitive regularization in the diffusion denoising objective is, to our knowledge, unprecedented and addresses a failure mode—temporal mode collapse—that existing diversity-promoting techniques for video generation do not explicitly target.

proposals.title: Temporal Manifold Alignment for Mode-Preserving Video Generation with Diffusion Models
proposals.motivation: Current video generation models, including diffusion models, often suffer from mode collapse, failing to capture the full diversity of real-world dynamics, especially rare motions and transitions. This limitation is critical for applications like medical video synthesis (e.g., colonoscopy) or scientific simulation, where preserving all temporal modes is essential for realism and downstream analysis. A principled approach to regularize the generative manifold of sequential data is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending the manifold alignment regularization framework—previously applied to static image GANs—to temporal diffusion models will enforce a better match between the latent manifolds of real and generated video sequences, thereby preserving rare temporal modes and improving the diversity and fidelity of generated dynamics.
proposals.method: We will adapt a video diffusion model (e.g., based on a 3D U-Net or transformer architecture) and introduce a novel regularization loss. This loss will minimize the sliced Wasserstein distance between the distributions of features extracted from real and generated video clips using a pre-trained spatiotemporal encoder (e.g., a SlowFast network). The alignment will be applied across multiple hierarchical layers of the diffusion denoiser, explicitly encouraging the model to maintain the structural and dynamical manifold of the training data throughout the generation process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new training objective for video diffusion models that mitigates temporal mode collapse. It will yield a framework for generating more diverse and realistic video sequences, with quantitative and qualitative evaluations on benchmarks and a domain-specific dataset (e.g., colonoscopy video). The method will offer insights into the geometry of temporal manifolds in generative models.
proposals.novelty: While manifold alignment has been explored for static images, its application to preserve the diversity of *temporal dynamics* in video generation with diffusion models is novel. The core idea of explicitly aligning spatiotemporal feature manifolds to regularize sequential data generation is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Provable Dynamics of Regularized Adversarial Games on Low-Dimensional Analytic Benchmarks
proposals.motivation: Despite extensive empirical progress in stabilizing generative adversarial training, theoretical understanding of why specific regularizers prevent mode collapse or training oscillation remains fragmented. Low-dimensional analytic games—where equilibria, basins of attraction, and bifurcation boundaries can be computed in closed form—offer the missing bridge between intuition from toy Gaussian experiments and the complexity of high-dimensional practice. A rigorous catalog of provable behaviors in such settings would ground future regularizer design on solid mathematical foundations rather than heuristic tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: For a family of two-player zero-sum and non-zero-sum games on low-dimensional continuous distributions (mixtures of 1-D and 2-D Gaussians, Dirac rings, and bimodal targets), gradient-penalty and spectral-norm regularizers each induce a distinct, analytically characterizable dynamical regime—specifically, there exist sharp regularization thresholds below which limit cycles persist and above which the dynamics provably converge to the target equilibrium without mode loss.
proposals.method: We construct a graded suite of analytic benchmark games: (1) a single-Gaussian location game, (2) a symmetric mixture-of-two-Gaussians game, and (3) a ring-of-Diracs discrete-mode game, each parameterized so that generator and discriminator updates yield ODEs or discrete-time maps amenable to Lyapunov and Jacobian analysis. For each game and each of three representative regularizers (gradient penalty, spectral normalization, and consensus optimization), we derive closed-form expressions for fixed points, compute eigenvalues of the Jacobian at equilibrium to classify stability, and identify bifurcation boundaries as a function of regularization strength. Numerical continuation methods will validate the analytic predictions and visualize the full bifurcation diagrams.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first unified analytic benchmark suite for adversarial training dynamics, complete with closed-form convergence and mode-coverage guarantees for standard regularizers. The sharp thresholds and bifurcation diagrams will provide practitioners with actionable guidance on regularization strength selection and give theorists a concrete reference set for testing new convergence proofs. The benchmark suite itself will be released as a lightweight open-source library for reproducible dynamical analysis of GAN-type algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While prior analytic studies typically examine a single game–regularizer pair in isolation, this proposal systematically crosses multiple analytic games with multiple regularizers to expose comparative structure—revealing, for instance, that regularizers preserving all modes in symmetric settings can still shift equilibria in asymmetric ones, a phenomenon invisible in any single-benchmark analysis.

proposals.title: Geometric Discriminator Regularization: Controlling Off-Manifold Gradients as the Dual to Generator-Side Losses in Adversarial Autoencoders
proposals.motivation: Adversarial autoencoders and related hybrid GAN-VAE models rely on heavy reconstruction losses to stabilize training because the discriminator provides unreliable gradient signals off the data manifold. Existing approaches regularize the generator or encoder side, but this treats the symptom rather than the cause: the poorly shaped discriminator landscape itself. Directly controlling discriminator geometry could yield more stable training with simpler generator objectives, a problem that grows more pressing as these models scale to high-dimensional data.
proposals.hypothesis: Imposing explicit geometric constraints on the discriminator—specifically Lipschitz smoothness, controlled gradient norm in off-manifold regions, and curvature penalties on intermediate representations—will produce a well-shaped energy landscape that provides reliable training signal to the generator, thereby reducing or eliminating the need for pixel-level reconstruction losses while maintaining or improving sample quality and latent space structure.
proposals.method: We propose a dual regularization framework applied directly to the discriminator of adversarial autoencoders. Concretely, we introduce three complementary penalties: (1) a gradient-norm penalty that enforces approximate 1-Lipschitz continuity not just along interpolations between real and fake samples but across a neighborhood of the data manifold estimated via noise perturbation; (2) a Hessian trace regularizer on the discriminator's intermediate layers that controls curvature and prevents sharp, unreliable decision boundaries; and (3) a spectral normalization schedule that adaptively tightens constraints as training progresses. We evaluate on standard benchmarks (CelebA-HQ, CIFAR-10, LSUN) comparing against spectral normalization, gradient penalty, and R1/R2 regularization baselines, measuring FID, reconstruction quality, and latent disentanglement while systematically ablating the reconstruction loss weight.
proposals.contribution: This work reframes discriminator regularization as the formal dual problem to generator-side reconstruction losses, providing both theoretical grounding via convex duality arguments and practical algorithms that simplify hybrid GAN-VAE training. We expect to demonstrate that a geometrically well-regularized discriminator allows significant reduction (50-80%) of reconstruction loss weight with no degradation in sample quality, while improving training stability and convergence speed. The framework offers a principled toolkit for practitioners designing adversarial generative models at scale.
proposals.novelty: While gradient penalties and spectral normalization are known techniques, our contribution is the systematic treatment of discriminator geometry as the dual optimization problem to generator-side losses—showing that these are formally complementary rather than independent design choices. The combination of off-manifold gradient control with intermediate-layer curvature penalties, guided by this duality perspective, has not been explored and yields a unified framework that subsumes prior regularization strategies as special cases.

proposals.title: Towards Discriminator-Side Geometry Regularization As The Dual Problem: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to The paper regularizes the generator and encoder because the discriminator is an unreliable learned t. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discriminator-side geometry regularization as the dual problem can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates scaling dual principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: The paper regularizes the generator and encoder because the discriminator is an unreliable learned target. The natural dual problem is to regularize t.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the discriminator-side geometry regularization as the dual problem problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Dual-Purpose Encoders: Leveraging Generative Model Inference Networks as Universal Representation Extractors for Retrieval, Clustering, and Semi-Supervised Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern generative models such as VAEs and energy-based models train encoder networks to approximate posterior inference, yet these encoders are typically discarded after generation and never evaluated as standalone representation learners. Given that these encoders are optimized to compress data into structured latent spaces that capture salient variation, they may yield high-quality embeddings competitive with or complementary to contrastive and self-supervised methods. Systematically characterizing when and why generative encoders transfer well to discriminative tasks would unify two largely separate lines of representation learning research and reduce the computational cost of training separate models for generation and understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Encoders trained jointly with generators via variational or amortized inference objectives learn latent representations that, without any fine-tuning, match or exceed purpose-built contrastive encoders on downstream retrieval, clustering, and semi-supervised classification benchmarks, particularly in low-data regimes where the generative training signal provides an implicit regularization advantage.
proposals.method: We will train a family of VAE and hierarchical VAE architectures with varying encoder capacities across image (CIFAR-100, ImageNet subsets), text (AG News, Wikipedia), and molecular graph (ZINC, QM9) domains, systematically comparing frozen encoder representations against contrastive baselines (SimCLR, BYOL) and supervised embeddings on k-NN retrieval, k-means clustering NMI, and label-efficient semi-supervised accuracy. We will introduce a lightweight adapter protocol that re-uses the encoder's learned posterior parameters as task-specific features and employ mutual information estimators to quantify how much task-relevant information the generative objective preserves versus discards. Ablations will isolate the contributions of reconstruction fidelity, KL regularization strength, and encoder depth to downstream transferability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive benchmark quantifying when generative encoders serve as effective general-purpose representations, providing practitioners with clear guidelines on when a single generative training run can replace separate representation learning pipelines. It will introduce a formal information-theoretic framework linking variational inference tightness to downstream task performance, offering principled criteria for encoder architecture selection. The findings will demonstrate that dual-purpose training yields substantial computational savings and improved data efficiency in semi-supervised settings, advancing the unification of generative and discriminative representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has anecdotally noted that VAE encoders capture meaningful structure, no systematic study has benchmarked generative encoders head-to-head against modern contrastive methods across multiple modalities and downstream tasks with controlled capacity. Our information-theoretic analysis of the generative-discriminative transfer gap provides a new theoretical lens that explains when inference networks retain task-relevant information and when the generative objective induces beneficial implicit regularization not present in purely discriminative objectives.

proposals.title: Towards Use The Encoder As An Inference Model For Downstream Tasks: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to Because the method already trains an encoder, the inverse problem of latent inference comes for free. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that use the encoder as an inference model for downstream tasks can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates scaling dual principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: Because the method already trains an encoder, the inverse problem of latent inference comes for free. That suggests studying whether the learned encod.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the use the encoder as an inference model for downstream tasks problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: MoE-Mode: Mixture-of-Expert Generators for Explicit Mode Decomposition in Generative Adversarial Networks
proposals.motivation: Mode collapse remains one of the most persistent failure modes in generative adversarial networks, where a single generator systematically under-represents minority modes of the data distribution. Existing approaches impose regularization or diversity penalties on a monolithic generator, but these scale poorly as distributional complexity grows. Decomposing mode coverage across specialized expert generators offers a principled alternative that converts the implicit burden of mode preservation into an explicit, modular allocation problem aligned with recent advances in mixture-of-experts architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: A mixture-of-expert generator architecture, where a learned routing encoder assigns training examples to specialized sub-generators based on detected latent modes, will achieve significantly higher mode coverage and lower reverse KL divergence than equivalent-capacity monolithic generators, particularly as the number of true data modes increases.
proposals.method: We propose MoE-Mode, comprising a lightweight mode-detection encoder that maps inputs to a discrete routing distribution over K expert generators, each specialized to a subset of data modes. The encoder is trained jointly with the experts and a shared discriminator using a combination of adversarial loss, a load-balancing auxiliary loss (preventing expert collapse), and a mode-coverage reward estimated via a kernel density probe on generated samples. We employ a Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization for differentiable routing during training and evaluate on StackedMNIST (1000 modes), CIFAR-10 conditional generation, and a synthetic Gaussian mixture benchmark with controlled mode counts ranging from 10 to 500.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic framework for converting mode preservation from a single-model regularization problem into an explicit decomposition problem via mixture-of-experts, establishing both theoretical grounding in terms of capacity allocation per mode and empirical evidence of superior scaling behavior. We expect to demonstrate that MoE-Mode achieves near-complete mode coverage where monolithic generators fail, while maintaining sample quality, and that the learned routing discovers semantically meaningful mode partitions without supervision. The framework also offers a diagnostic tool: expert utilization statistics directly reveal which modes are underserved.
proposals.novelty: While mixture-of-experts has been explored for discriminative tasks and conditional generation, applying it specifically as a mode-decomposition mechanism with a jointly learned mode-detection router reframes mode collapse as a routing and specialization problem rather than a regularization problem. This dual perspective—shifting from constraining a single generator to architecting cooperative specialists—is non-obvious because it requires solving the coupled challenge of unsupervised mode discovery and balanced expert assignment simultaneously.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Mode Coverage: How Latent Dimensionality, Prior Topology, and Encoder Invertibility Govern Generative Model Fidelity
proposals.motivation: Deep generative models are increasingly deployed at scale, yet practitioners lack principled guidelines for choosing latent space configurations—dimensionality, prior geometry, and encoder design—that reliably capture the full support of complex data distributions. The observation that DCGAN performance degrades under latent dimension changes on CelebA while regularized models remain robust suggests the existence of systematic scaling relationships between latent structure and mode coverage that have not been formally characterized. Understanding these relationships is critical for building generative models that do not silently drop modes as architectures and datasets scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Mode coverage in deep generative models follows predictable scaling laws governed by the interaction of three factors—latent dimensionality, prior topology, and encoder invertibility—such that regularized models with topologically matched priors exhibit power-law scaling of mode coverage with latent dimension, while unregularized models exhibit a phase-transition-like collapse beyond a critical dimension mismatch ratio.
proposals.method: We propose a systematic empirical and theoretical study across GANs, VAEs, and normalizing flows on datasets of increasing complexity (MNIST, CIFAR-10, CelebA, LSUN, ImageNet subsets). For each model family, we vary latent dimensionality across two orders of magnitude, swap prior topologies (Gaussian, uniform hypersphere, toroidal, learned manifold priors), and modulate encoder invertibility via architectural constraints and regularization strength. Mode coverage is measured using established metrics—number of recovered modes on synthetic mixtures, FID-vs-recall Pareto curves, and the recently proposed coverage and density metrics—while intrinsic dimensionality estimators characterize the effective latent manifold. We fit parametric scaling laws relating these structural choices to mode coverage and identify critical transition points using finite-size scaling analysis borrowed from statistical physics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first unified scaling laws connecting latent space design choices to mode coverage across generative model families, providing practitioners with actionable guidelines for configuring latent spaces at any scale. The theoretical framework grounded in manifold dimensionality and topological alignment will explain why certain model-prior combinations are inherently more robust, resolving longstanding empirical puzzles about GAN training instability under architectural changes. The resulting diagnostic toolkit will enable researchers to predict mode dropping before it occurs, significantly reducing wasted computation in large-scale generative modeling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied mode collapse and latent dimensionality in isolation, no existing study jointly characterizes the three-way interaction of dimension, topology, and invertibility as a scaling phenomenon. The application of finite-size scaling analysis from statistical physics to identify sharp phase transitions in mode coverage as a function of latent-data dimension mismatch is, to our knowledge, entirely new to the generative modeling literature.

proposals.title: Beyond Basic Emotions: Uncertainty-Aware Continuous Affect Prediction from Video via Distributional Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: Most video-based emotion recognition systems discretize affect into six or seven basic categories following Ekman's taxonomy, yet psychological evidence increasingly shows that naturalistic stimuli—especially cinematic content—elicit blended, context-dependent, and culturally variable emotional responses that these rigid categories cannot capture. This mismatch between label ontology and ground-truth affective experience introduces systematic annotation noise, inflates reported accuracies on ambiguous samples, and limits the ecological validity of deployed systems. Addressing this gap is timely because large-scale video understanding models now have sufficient representational capacity to learn richer affective structures, provided the supervision signal is redesigned.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing categorical emotion targets with a hybrid supervision framework—combining continuous valence-arousal-dominance (VAD) regression and calibrated distributional outputs that explicitly model annotator disagreement—will yield representations that better capture mixed and subtle affect, improving both predictive accuracy on ambiguous stimuli and calibration of uncertainty estimates compared to standard seven-class softmax baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a transformer-based multimodal architecture that jointly processes facial, vocal, and contextual scene features from movie clips and outputs both a point estimate in continuous VAD space and a learned probability distribution over the affect manifold using evidential deep learning to capture epistemic uncertainty. Training employs a multi-task loss combining VAD regression (concordance correlation), a distributional divergence term that fits per-sample annotation histograms rather than majority-vote labels, and a contrastive regularizer that structures the latent space so that perceptually similar affective states cluster together. We benchmark on re-annotated subsets of existing movie-clip datasets (e.g., AFEW, MOSEI) where we collect dense continuous annotations alongside the original categorical labels, enabling direct comparison of taxonomies.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled supervision framework that unifies categorical, dimensional, and uncertainty-aware affect modeling within a single architecture, (2) empirical evidence quantifying the information lost by the seven-class assumption on naturalistic video stimuli, and (3) a re-annotation protocol and benchmark that provides continuous and distributional ground truth for widely used movie-clip datasets. Together these advances move the field toward ecologically valid affective computing that acknowledges the inherent ambiguity of human emotional expression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored dimensional affect models or soft labels independently, no existing approach jointly learns calibrated epistemic-uncertainty distributions over a continuous affect manifold from multimodal video while explicitly treating annotator disagreement as signal rather than noise. Grounding this in evidential deep learning—rather than simple label smoothing or mixture-of-experts—provides theoretically motivated uncertainty estimates that degrade gracefully on out-of-taxonomy emotions, a property critical for real-world deployment.

proposals.title: Temporal Emotion Decomposition: Replacing Clip-Level Labels with Frame-Aware Sparse Supervision for Affective Video Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current affective computing models assign a single emotion label to an entire video clip, yet human emotional expression is inherently dynamic—a 10-second clip may traverse surprise, amusement, and neutral states. This label-granularity mismatch injects systematic noise into training, degrading both accuracy and interpretability. With the growing deployment of emotion-aware systems in healthcare and human-computer interaction, resolving this supervision bottleneck is both timely and consequential.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing monolithic clip-level emotion labels with a temporally decomposed, sparse supervision framework—where only high-confidence frames receive labels and intermediate frames are treated as latent variables—will yield more accurate and temporally coherent emotion predictions than standard clip-level training, without requiring exhaustive frame-by-frame annotation.
proposals.method: We propose Temporal Emotion Decomposition (TED), a two-stage framework. First, a temporal attention module scores each frame's alignment with the clip-level label, producing a soft relevance mask; frames below a learned threshold are treated as unlabeled, converting the problem into a semi-supervised setting within each clip. Second, we train a temporal variational autoencoder that models the emotion trajectory as a latent stochastic process, using the sparse high-confidence frame labels as anchor points and inferring emotions for unlabeled frames via amortized variational inference. The model is trained end-to-end with a composite objective combining supervised cross-entropy on anchor frames, a KL-regularized temporal prior, and a consistency loss encouraging smooth transitions between adjacent frame predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled framework for learning from clip-level labels without assuming temporal label homogeneity, directly addressing a widely acknowledged but unresolved weakness in affective computing pipelines; (2) empirical evidence on multiple benchmarks (Aff-Wild2, AFEW, MER) that sparse intra-clip supervision outperforms both clip-level and naive frame-level label propagation baselines; and (3) a reusable temporal attention-based relevance scoring module applicable to any weakly supervised video understanding task beyond emotion recognition.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored multiple-instance learning for video, no existing method explicitly models intra-clip emotion dynamics as a latent stochastic process with learned sparse anchor selection. The combination of temporal variational inference with adaptive frame-level confidence thresholding is, to our knowledge, unprecedented in affective computing and offers a generalizable alternative to the pervasive one-label-per-clip assumption.

proposals.title: Beyond the Primary Face: Relational Graph Networks for Multi-Person Affective Understanding in Cinematic Scenes
proposals.motivation: Current video emotion recognition pipelines overwhelmingly rely on detecting and cropping the face of a single primary subject, discarding the rich affective signals carried by secondary characters, inter-person dynamics, and reaction shots. This assumption is particularly fragile in cinematic contexts where directors deliberately use ensemble compositions, shot-reverse-shot editing, and crowd reactions to convey emotional tone. Challenging this single-face bottleneck is essential for building systems that understand affect the way human viewers do—through social and relational context.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the relational structure among multiple detected faces in a scene via a dynamic graph neural network will yield significantly more accurate video-level emotion predictions than pipelines that extract features from only the primary detected face, especially in multi-person and interaction-heavy scenes.
proposals.method: We construct a per-frame social graph where each detected face is a node (encoded by a pretrained facial action unit model) and edges encode spatial proximity, gaze direction, and temporal co-occurrence across frames. A temporal graph neural network—specifically a variant of message-passing GNN with gated recurrent aggregation inspired by benchmarking advances in graph network architectures—propagates relational information across characters and time steps. The resulting graph-level embedding is fused with a global scene descriptor and fed to a classifier for video-level emotion labels. We train and evaluate on standard movie emotion benchmarks (e.g., MovieGraphs, EmoReact) with controlled ablations that compare single-face, multi-face-independent, and our relational-graph conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic empirical challenge to the primary-face assumption in video emotion recognition, quantifying how much affective signal is lost when secondary characters and inter-person dynamics are ignored. It introduces a graph-based relational module that is architecture-agnostic and can be retrofitted into existing pipelines, offering a practical path to more context-aware affective computing. The analysis will also yield a taxonomy of scene types (e.g., dyadic dialogue, ensemble, reaction shot) where relational modeling provides the largest gains, guiding future dataset and model design.
proposals.novelty: While multi-face detection exists in prior work, no existing approach models the dynamic relational graph structure among characters as a first-class input for emotion prediction; combining temporal GNNs with facial action unit features for inter-character affect propagation is unexplored. The explicit ablation framework isolating single-face versus relational-context contributions is itself a methodological contribution that reframes how the field evaluates affective pipelines.

proposals.title: Scene-Level Emotion Recognition: Disentangling Facial, Postural, and Contextual Cues via Modality-Ablated Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art video emotion recognition systems derive the majority of their predictive power from facial features, implicitly encoding the assumption that emotion is a face-centric phenomenon. This creates fragile models that fail when faces are occluded, low-resolution, or culturally ambiguous, and it neglects rich affective signals from body posture, scene context, camera language, and object interactions. Challenging this assumption is timely because real-world deployment scenarios—surveillance, cinematic analysis, assistive robotics—routinely encounter conditions where facial evidence is weak or misleading.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scene-level graph neural network that explicitly models relational structure among body pose, environmental context, camera framing, and facial features will match or exceed face-centric baselines on standard emotion benchmarks, and will significantly outperform them on a new evaluation split where facial cues are deliberately degraded or contradicted by scene context.
proposals.method: We construct a heterogeneous scene graph for each video clip where nodes represent detected faces, body pose skeletons, salient objects, and global scene descriptors (camera angle, lighting, color palette), and edges encode spatial and temporal relationships. We train a message-passing graph neural network—building on benchmarked GNN architectures—with a systematic modality-ablation protocol: face-only, face-removed, body-only, context-only, and full-graph conditions. To rigorously test our hypothesis, we curate a 'Contradictory Cue' evaluation set from existing emotion datasets (EMOTIC, BoLD, CAER) plus newly annotated cinematic clips where annotators confirm that scene context contradicts or dominates facial expression.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic, graph-structured decomposition of emotion cues across modalities in video, quantifying exactly how much predictive signal resides outside the face. It delivers a reusable benchmark and evaluation protocol for context-dominant emotion recognition, and it offers a principled GNN architecture that gracefully degrades when any single modality is absent. These contributions directly inform safer and more robust affective computing systems for real-world deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has incorporated context as an auxiliary boost to facial features, no study has inverted the hierarchy to treat the face as just one node in a scene-level relational graph and systematically measured performance under facial ablation. The modality-ablation GNN protocol with a purpose-built contradictory-cue benchmark provides a novel experimental paradigm for stress-testing the face-centric assumption that dominates the field.

proposals.title: Beyond Fixed Mouth Crops: Learning Pose-Aware Adaptive Facial Subregion Attention for Audio-Visual Speech Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current audio-visual speech recognition systems rely on a fixed, globally defined bounding box assumed to capture the mouth region across all speakers and poses. This assumption breaks down under head rotation, speaker morphological variation, and occlusion, silently degrading performance on in-the-wild data. Replacing this brittle preprocessing step with a learned, adaptive mechanism is timely given advances in spatial attention and landmark-free face analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned spatial attention module that dynamically selects informative facial subregions on a per-frame, per-speaker basis will outperform fixed global mouth crops for visual speech recognition, and will reveal that regions beyond the canonical mouth area—such as jaw contour, cheeks, and nasolabial folds—carry complementary phonemic information exploited by the model.
proposals.method: We propose a differentiable spatial attention network (FaceRegionNet) inserted between the face detector and the visual encoder of an audio-visual speech recognition pipeline. FaceRegionNet takes the full face image and, conditioned on estimated 3D head pose and optional speaker embedding, produces a soft spatial attention map that is multiplied element-wise with the face crop before feeding into the downstream visual encoder. The module is trained end-to-end with the speech recognition loss, using no explicit region supervision, on LRS2, LRS3, and VoxCeleb2 benchmarks. We additionally apply Grad-CAM and information-theoretic probing to analyze which facial subregions the model learns to attend to across phoneme classes and pose conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a drop-in, pose-aware spatial attention module that replaces heuristic mouth cropping and improves word error rate on standard lip-reading benchmarks especially under large pose variation, (2) an empirical analysis revealing that learned attention extends beyond the mouth to jaw and cheek regions for specific phoneme categories, challenging the field's implicit assumption that the mouth alone is sufficient, and (3) a systematic ablation quantifying the performance cost of the fixed-crop assumption across pose angles and speaker demographics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored attention within already-cropped mouth regions, no existing method questions the upstream decision of where to crop in the first place. Our approach is non-obvious because it shows that the seemingly innocuous preprocessing choice of a fixed bounding box constitutes an information bottleneck, and that end-to-end learned spatial selection over the full face discovers phoneme-dependent multi-region strategies that a human designer would not prescribe.

proposals.title: Beyond Fixed Temporal Bins: Adaptive-Resolution Video Descriptors for Facial Affect Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current video emotion recognition pipelines compress face streams into a fixed number of temporal slots (typically ten), discarding fine-grained timing, variable-duration expressions, and the uneven salience of affective events. This rigid quantization is an unexamined design choice inherited from early pooling heuristics, yet human emotional displays are inherently aperiodic and vary dramatically in duration. Replacing this assumption with adaptive temporal representations is timely given recent advances in set-based and continuous-time architectures that can natively handle irregular sequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing fixed-bin temporal pooling with a learned, variable-resolution temporal descriptor—one that preserves original frame timing and allocates representational capacity proportional to emotional salience—will yield significantly improved facial affect recognition accuracy and temporal localization compared to uniform ten-slot baselines, especially on videos with heterogeneous expression dynamics.
proposals.method: We propose Adaptive Temporal Attention Pooling (ATAP), which replaces uniform binning with a two-stage mechanism: (1) a lightweight temporal saliency network that scores each frame embedding for affective informativeness, producing a continuous importance curve, and (2) a differentiable soft-clustering module that groups frames into a variable number of segments whose boundaries are learned end-to-end via a combination of reconstruction and downstream classification losses.
