The backbone face encoder is kept fixed (e.g., a pretrained AffectNet model), and ATAP operates on its output sequence using multi-head cross-attention with learned temporal position encodings that respect absolute timestamps rather than ordinal indices. We evaluate on AFEW, Aff-Wild2, and MELD benchmarks, comparing against uniform-bin, NetVLAD, and Transformer-mean baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (i) the first systematic empirical analysis showing how and why fixed temporal binning degrades video-level affect recognition, (ii) the ATAP module—a drop-in replacement for uniform pooling that is architecture-agnostic and adds negligible parameters, and (iii) evidence that salience-adaptive temporal resolution improves not only classification accuracy but also interpretability, as learned segment boundaries align with annotated expression onsets and offsets. The findings generalize the broader lesson that temporally non-uniform representations better match the statistics of human behavior.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive computation and variable-length representations have been explored in language and speech, the specific assumption of fixed-slot temporal descriptors in facial video analysis has never been formally questioned or ablated. Our approach is non-obvious because it reframes a seemingly innocuous engineering choice—how many bins to use—as a fundamental representational bottleneck, and resolves it through learned saliency-driven segmentation rather than simply increasing the bin count.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Interaction Networks for Emotion Recognition: Why Late Fusion of Unimodal Specialists Misses Emergent Affective Signals
proposals.motivation: Most multimodal emotion recognition systems train specialist models on individual modalities (audio, video, text) and combine their predictions through late fusion, implicitly assuming that emotional cues are independently decodable from each modality. However, affective states frequently manifest through cross-modal contingencies—such as prosodic-facial synchrony or incongruence between verbal content and vocal tone—that cannot be captured by any single specialist. Understanding how and when these emergent interaction effects dominate unimodal signals is critical for building robust affective computing systems and for advancing our theoretical understanding of how humans encode and decode emotion across sensory channels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intermediate fusion architectures, which allow learned cross-modal interactions during representation learning, will significantly outperform late fusion of independently trained specialists on emotion recognition benchmarks, and that this advantage will be largest for complex or ambiguous affective states where cross-modal contingencies carry the most diagnostic information.
proposals.method: We propose a controlled comparison across three fusion strategies—late, intermediate, and early—using a shared transformer backbone with modality-specific tokenizers for audio (wav2vec 2.0 features), video (facial action unit sequences), and text (pretrained language model embeddings). Intermediate fusion is implemented via cross-modal attention layers inserted at varying depths, allowing us to systematically measure where in the representation hierarchy cross-modal interactions contribute most. We evaluate on IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSEI, and MELD, stratifying results by annotator agreement to isolate cases of affective ambiguity, and employ representation similarity analysis and probing classifiers to characterize what cross-modal features the intermediate fusion layers learn that late fusion cannot recover.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic, depth-controlled analysis of when and why late fusion fails for multimodal emotion recognition, moving beyond aggregate accuracy to pinpoint the specific affective phenomena that require cross-modal interaction during learning. The mechanistic analysis of learned representations will offer concrete design principles for practitioners choosing fusion strategies, and the ambiguity-stratified evaluation protocol can serve as a standard diagnostic for future multimodal affective computing research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared fusion strategies on aggregate metrics, our contribution is non-obvious in two ways: first, we introduce a depth-controlled cross-modal attention framework that disentangles where in the representational hierarchy fusion matters most, and second, we demonstrate that the advantage of intermediate fusion is not uniform but concentrated on ambiguous affective states, challenging the implicit assumption that fusion strategy choice is task-general rather than instance-dependent.

proposals.title: Instance-Adaptive Multimodal Fusion via Sparse Gating: Beyond Static Per-Class Weights for Audio-Visual Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art multimodal fusion methods for tasks such as audio-visual speech recognition and deepfake detection rely on globally learned, static per-class or per-model weights to combine modality streams. However, the informativeness of each modality—motion, lip appearance, and audio—varies dramatically across individual clips due to occlusion, background noise, speaking style, and recording conditions. This mismatch between a fixed weighting scheme and highly variable per-instance modality reliability leaves significant performance on the table and limits robustness in real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing static global fusion weights with a lightweight, input-conditioned sparse gating mechanism will yield statistically significant improvements in multimodal recognition accuracy and calibration, and that the learned gating patterns will exhibit interpretable specialization that correlates with measurable modality reliability indicators such as signal-to-noise ratio and visual clarity.
proposals.method: We propose a Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (SMoE) fusion module inserted between frozen or fine-tuned unimodal encoders and the final classifier. Each expert corresponds to a candidate fusion strategy (e.g., audio-dominant, visual-dominant, motion-dominant, balanced), and a lightweight context encoder processes concatenated modality embeddings to produce a top-k sparse gating distribution via a noisy top-k softmax. The gating network is trained end-to-end with a combined task loss and a load-balancing auxiliary loss to prevent expert collapse. We evaluate on three benchmarks—LRS3 for audio-visual speech recognition, FakeAVCeleb for deepfake detection, and VGGSound for audio-visual event classification—comparing against static learned weights, simple attention-based fusion, and recent dynamic fusion baselines. Ablations systematically vary k, the number of experts, and the context encoder capacity to characterize the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic empirical challenge to the assumption that static global fusion weights are sufficient for multimodal recognition, demonstrating consistent gains from instance-adaptive sparse gating across diverse audio-visual tasks. It delivers an open-source, plug-in fusion module that can be inserted into existing multimodal pipelines with minimal overhead, along with an interpretability analysis linking gating decisions to per-clip modality quality metrics. The findings establish practical design guidelines for when dynamic fusion is worth its added complexity versus when static weights suffice.
proposals.novelty: While dynamic attention over modalities has been explored, the specific combination of sparse expert selection with explicit load balancing for multimodal fusion is underexplored, and no prior work has rigorously tested the conditions under which static per-class weights break down versus remain competitive. Our approach uniquely bridges the Mixture-of-Experts literature from large language models with the multimodal fusion problem, providing both a performant method and a diagnostic framework for modality reliability.

proposals.title: Soundtrack Shortcuts: Quantifying Spurious Music-Label Correlations in Video Emotion Benchmarks and Their Impact on Affective Model Robustness
proposals.motivation: Video-based emotion recognition systems increasingly fuse audio, visual, and textual streams, yet the audio channel conflates two fundamentally different signals: the human subject's vocal expression and the cinematic soundtrack imposed by content creators. Because popular affective computing benchmarks (e.g., MELD, MUStARD, CMU-MOSEI) are drawn from movies, TV shows, and online videos where background music is editorially aligned with scene emotion labels, models can exploit soundtrack cues as low-cost shortcuts rather than learning genuine affective representations. This matters now because deployed sentiment and emotion APIs are being applied to domains—telehealth, customer service, education—where no soundtrack exists, creating a silent distribution shift that degrades real-world performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art multimodal emotion recognition models trained on entertainment-sourced benchmarks learn spurious correlations between non-diegetic soundtrack features (tempo, key, instrumentation) and emotion labels, such that (a) selectively removing or replacing soundtrack components significantly degrades benchmark accuracy while leaving human annotator agreement unchanged, and (b) models exhibiting higher soundtrack reliance show larger performance drops when evaluated on naturalistic, soundtrack-free affective data.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage investigation. First, we develop an audio source-separation pipeline using pre-trained models (e.g., Demucs, Spleeter) to decompose benchmark audio into speech, music, and ambient components, then train probing classifiers on isolated soundtrack features to quantify the mutual information between music cues and emotion labels across five major benchmarks. Second, we construct controlled intervention sets: for each clip we generate soundtrack-swapped (congruent music replaced with incongruent music), soundtrack-removed, and original versions, then measure the change in predictions of six representative multimodal models (including MulT, MISA, and UniMSE) under each condition, using causal mediation analysis to isolate the soundtrack's contribution to the final prediction. Third, we curate a small naturalistic evaluation corpus from unscored webcam and telehealth recordings (with IRB approval) to benchmark cross-domain transfer, comparing vanilla models against versions fine-tuned on soundtrack-ablated data using invariant risk minimization (IRM) and spectral decoupling to suppress shortcut reliance.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic audit of soundtrack-induced shortcut learning in affective computing, delivering (1) a publicly released toolkit for audio-decomposed benchmark evaluation that the community can apply to new datasets, (2) quantitative evidence and causal estimates of how much current benchmark performance is attributable to editorial sound design rather than genuine emotion expression, and (3) a validated debiasing protocol combining source separation with invariant learning objectives that improves out-of-domain generalization on naturalistic affective data. Together these contributions establish methodological standards for more rigorous evaluation of multimodal emotion models before clinical or commercial deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied modality ablation in multimodal sentiment analysis, no study has specifically isolated non-diegetic soundtrack from diegetic speech audio and quantified its role as a confound using causal intervention. Our approach bridges the shortcut-learning literature from robust ML with affective computing evaluation practice, revealing a systematic bias that is invisible under standard modality-dropout analysis because both speech and music reside in the same audio channel.

proposals.title: Dynamic Over Static: Self-Supervised Pretraining on Facial Action Dynamics Outperforms Static-Image Features Under Label Scarcity
proposals.motivation: Recent work on automated facial analysis under data scarcity defaults to pretraining on large static-image datasets such as ImageNet or VGGFace, implicitly assuming that appearance-level features transfer better than temporal ones. Yet the downstream tasks of interest—pain detection, emotion recognition, action unit estimation—are fundamentally about how faces change over time, not how they look in a single frame. Determining whether dynamic pretraining can surpass static pretraining is critical for guiding data collection and model design in clinically important, label-scarce facial analysis domains.
proposals.hypothesis: Self-supervised pretraining on unlabeled facial video that explicitly captures temporal deformation patterns (action unit onset, apex, offset dynamics) will yield representations that transfer more effectively to downstream facial behavior tasks than representations pretrained on orders-of-magnitude more labeled static face images, particularly when downstream labeled data is scarce.
proposals.method: We will pretrain a spatiotemporal encoder using a contrastive predictive coding objective on large-scale unlabeled facial video corpora (VoxCeleb2, YouTube Faces), where the pretext task requires predicting future facial motion fields from past frames, forcing the network to learn action-unit-like temporal primitives. We compare this dynamic pretraining against static-image pretraining (ImageNet, VGGFace2 supervised, and DINO self-supervised on face crops) by fine-tuning identical downstream heads on DISFA, BP4D, and EmotioNet under controlled label budgets (1%, 5%, 10%, 100% of labels). We further perform a systematic ablation isolating temporal depth, motion magnitude filtering, and hybrid static-then-dynamic pretraining schedules to identify when and why dynamic signals dominate.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first controlled, large-scale comparison establishing whether dynamic facial pretraining is fundamentally more transferable than static-image pretraining for facial behavior analysis under data scarcity. It will deliver an open-source temporally-pretrained facial encoder and concrete guidelines for practitioners on when to invest in video-based versus image-based pretraining. The findings will reshape data-collection priorities in clinical and affective computing communities by quantifying the label-efficiency gains from temporal pretraining.
proposals.novelty: While self-supervised video representation learning is well studied for action recognition, no prior work has directly pitted temporal facial pretraining against the dominant static-image paradigm in a fair, budget-controlled comparison. The non-obvious insight is that the inductive bias toward modeling how faces deform—rather than how they appear—may yield a stronger prior for behavior tasks, even when static datasets are vastly larger and carry curated labels.

proposals.title: Selective Abstention in Mixture-of-Experts: When Overfit Modality Specialists Should Stay Silent
proposals.motivation: Multimodal fusion systems routinely retain expert modules that overfit to training distributions because they occasionally contribute useful signal on ambiguous inputs. This practice introduces systematic noise at inference time, degrading calibration and robustness on out-of-distribution data. Understanding when an expert should abstain rather than contribute is critical for deploying reliable multimodal systems in safety-sensitive domains such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, and affective computing.
proposals.hypothesis: Equipping each modality expert with a learned abstention mechanism—where participation is gated by an input-conditional confidence threshold—will improve ensemble calibration and out-of-distribution generalization compared to standard always-participate mixture-of-experts, without sacrificing in-distribution accuracy on the rare disambiguating cases where overfit experts are genuinely helpful.
proposals.method: We propose Abstention-Gated Mixture-of-Experts (AG-MoE), in which each expert is augmented with a lightweight meta-learned confidence head trained via a secondary objective that penalizes contribution when the expert's predictive uncertainty exceeds a learned, input-dependent threshold. The confidence head is optimized using a combination of proper scoring rules and a sparsity-inducing penalty that encourages experts to abstain by default and participate only when their epistemic confidence is high. We validate AG-MoE on multimodal sentiment analysis (CMU-MOSEI), audiovisual speech recognition (LRS3), and medical multimodal fusion benchmarks, comparing against standard MoE, top-k gating, and post-hoc calibration baselines under both in-distribution and distribution-shift conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic framework for expert-level abstention in multimodal fusion, offering both a principled training objective and a practical architecture that can be retrofitted onto existing MoE systems. It establishes empirical evidence that selective silence outperforms forced participation for overfit experts, yielding improved calibration, better OOD robustness, and interpretable per-expert participation maps that reveal which modalities are genuinely informative for which input regimes.
proposals.novelty: While gating mechanisms in MoE select among experts for efficiency, they do not permit full abstention—every input must be routed somewhere. Our approach is distinct in that it treats non-participation as a first-class action with its own learned policy, challenging the implicit assumption that retaining all experts and letting the gate modulate weights is sufficient to contain overfitting damage.

proposals.title: Latent Temporal Label Assignment for Weakly Supervised Video Emotion Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current video emotion recognition systems naively propagate a single clip-level label to every constituent frame, injecting substantial label noise that degrades learned representations and confounds temporal modeling. This is particularly problematic because emotional expressions are inherently sparse and transient within a clip—only a subset of frames genuinely conveys the annotated emotion while others are neutral or ambiguous. Addressing this weak supervision bottleneck is critical for scaling emotion recognition to large, coarsely annotated video datasets without expensive frame-level annotation.
proposals.hypothesis: A latent temporal assignment mechanism that jointly learns to segment clips into emotion-relevant and emotion-irrelevant intervals during training will yield more accurate and temporally calibrated emotion predictions than models that treat every frame as equally labeled, even without any frame-level supervision.
proposals.method: We propose a two-branch architecture: a backbone temporal encoder (e.g., a Transformer or temporal convolutional network) that produces frame-level feature representations, paired with a differentiable latent assignment module that outputs per-frame soft relevance scores via a constrained attention mechanism. The relevance scores gate the contribution of each frame to the clip-level prediction through a weighted aggregation, and training uses a combination of clip-level classification loss and an information-theoretic regularizer (minimizing mutual information between relevance scores and a uniform prior) to prevent degenerate solutions where all frames are selected or discarded. We further introduce a temporal smoothness penalty based on total variation of the relevance scores to encourage contiguous emotion segments rather than scattered frame selections.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled weakly supervised framework that automatically discovers which temporal segments within a video clip are emotionally salient, eliminating the need for costly frame-level annotations. The approach provides interpretable temporal attention maps that can be used for emotion onset/offset detection as a byproduct, and we expect it to improve clip-level classification accuracy by 3-5% on standard benchmarks (e.g., AFEW, CAER) by reducing label noise during training. The framework generalizes beyond emotion recognition to any video task where clip-level labels are temporally sparse.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior multiple-instance learning approaches that treat frames as independent instances, our method explicitly models temporal structure through smoothness constraints and information-theoretic regularization, jointly learning both the relevant segments and the emotion classifier end-to-end. The combination of gated temporal aggregation with a principled anti-collapse regularizer is a non-obvious design choice that prevents the well-known degenerate solutions in latent variable models for weak supervision.

proposals.title: Landmark-Conditioned Mouth Alignment for Robust Visual Speech Recognition
proposals.motivation: Visual speech recognition systems that rely on heuristic bounding-box cropping of the mouth region are sensitive to pose variation, scale changes, and speaker identity, introducing irrelevant background pixels that degrade learned representations. As deployment scenarios expand to unconstrained video—teleconferencing, surveillance, and assistive devices—robustness to geometric variability becomes a practical bottleneck. A principled, keypoint-driven mouth localization module would directly address this fragility while remaining lightweight enough for real-time use.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing fixed heuristic mouth cropping with a differentiable spatial alignment conditioned on facial landmarks will yield more pose- and scale-invariant mouth representations, significantly improving visual speech recognition accuracy under geometric variability without increasing inference cost.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage pipeline: first, a lightweight facial landmark detector (e.g., a compact HRNet or coordinate regression network) extracts a canonical set of lip and jaw keypoints; second, a differentiable spatial transformer module uses the predicted landmarks to compute a thin-plate-spline or affine warp that maps each frame's mouth region into a normalized canonical coordinate frame before feature extraction. The entire pipeline—landmark predictor, spatial transformer, and downstream visual speech encoder—is trained end-to-end with a combined loss that penalizes landmark regression error and speech recognition cross-entropy, enabling the alignment module to learn task-relevant geometric normalization rather than relying on generic face alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a drop-in, differentiable mouth alignment module that can replace heuristic cropping in any bag-of-mouths or lip-reading architecture, providing consistent improvements under pose and scale variation. We expect to demonstrate measurable gains on standard benchmarks (LRS2, LRS3) particularly in challenging head-pose subsets, establishing that learned geometric normalization is a low-cost but high-impact preprocessing step. The approach also provides interpretable intermediate outputs (landmark heatmaps and warp fields) that facilitate error analysis and model debugging.
proposals.novelty: While facial landmark detection and spatial transformer networks are individually well-studied, their joint end-to-end optimization specifically for visual speech recognition—where the alignment objective is driven by phoneme discrimination rather than generic face registration—has not been explored. This task-conditioned alignment learns to emphasize articulatory landmarks most informative for speech, yielding a normalization that is qualitatively different from standard face alignment pipelines.

proposals.title: Unbiased Fusion via Stratified Out-of-Fold Meta-Training: Eliminating Information Leakage in Multi-Model Synthesizability Prediction
proposals.motivation: Fusion models that combine predictions from multiple base learners are increasingly used in computational chemistry pipelines such as virtual screening and de novo drug design, yet they typically train on in-sample base-model outputs that are systematically overconfident. This train-test distribution mismatch inflates stacking performance during development and degrades generalization to novel chemical scaffolds. A principled out-of-fold meta-training framework would close this gap and is now tractable given modern distributed training infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing naïve train-set base-model outputs with rigorously constructed K-fold or leave-one-out out-of-fold predictions during fusion-model training will produce calibrated, unbiased meta-features that significantly improve generalization of synthesizability predictors on structurally novel compounds, without requiring retraining of the base models at inference time.
proposals.method: We propose a stratified out-of-fold meta-training pipeline in which each base model (e.g., RetroGNN, graph neural network regressors, fingerprint-based classifiers) is retrained on K complementary folds of the training data, and its held-out predictions are aggregated to form the full meta-training set for the fusion layer. To manage computational cost, we introduce a budget-aware fold-allocation strategy that assigns more folds to high-variance base models and fewer to stable ones, guided by a bias-variance decomposition estimated via bootstrap. The fusion model itself is trained with a calibration-aware loss combining cross-entropy with an expected calibration error penalty, ensuring that the resulting predicted synthesizability scores are both discriminative and well-calibrated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of out-of-fold meta-training for molecular property prediction fusion, delivering an open-source pipeline that researchers can drop into existing multi-model workflows. We expect measurable improvements in both discrimination (AUROC) and calibration (ECE) on benchmark synthesizability datasets, along with practical guidelines for fold-count selection under computational budgets typical of academic labs.
proposals.novelty: While K-fold stacking is well-known in tabular machine learning, its principled application to graph-based molecular property predictors—where data splitting must respect chemical scaffold structure to avoid leakage—and its combination with calibration-aware fusion training have not been explored. The budget-aware fold-allocation scheme, which adaptively assigns computational resources based on per-model variance, is a new contribution that makes leave-one-out-quality meta-features practical at scale.

proposals.title: Adaptive Temporal Tokenization: Learning Variable-Length Video Representations via Differentiable Segment Compression
proposals.motivation: Current video understanding pipelines impose a fixed temporal grid—typically ten evenly spaced slots—onto clips of wildly varying dynamics, discarding fine-grained onset, duration, and rhythm cues that are critical for action recognition, temporal grounding, and medical video analysis. This rigid quantization is analogous to using a fixed vocabulary in NLP before byte-pair encoding: it works but leaves significant performance on the table. A learnable, variable-length temporal aggregation mechanism would let representations adapt to content complexity, preserving salient temporal structure where it matters and compressing redundant segments where it does not.
proposals.hypothesis: A differentiable temporal tokenizer that learns to segment and compress video clips into a variable number of latent temporal tokens—guided by downstream task loss—will capture onset, duration, and rhythm patterns more faithfully than fixed-grid pooling, yielding measurable gains on temporally sensitive benchmarks without increasing total token count at inference time.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage architecture: (1) a lightweight temporal boundary predictor, implemented as a small recurrent or causal transformer network operating on frame-level features, that outputs soft segment boundaries via a Gumbel-softmax or differentiable top-k mechanism, and (2) a segment encoder that aggregates frames within each predicted segment using cross-attention pooling into a variable number of latent tokens. The entire pipeline is trained end-to-end with the downstream objective plus an information-bottleneck regularizer that penalizes excessive token count, encouraging the model to use more tokens only when temporal detail is informative. We benchmark on temporally demanding tasks including FineGym, Breakfast Actions, and colonoscopy phase segmentation, comparing against fixed-grid baselines, uniform sampling, and adaptive frame-selection methods.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (i) a principled, fully differentiable mechanism for learning temporal granularity from data rather than engineering it by hand, (ii) empirical evidence that variable-length temporal tokenization improves performance on fine-grained temporal reasoning tasks while maintaining or reducing computational cost, and (iii) analysis showing that the learned segmentation boundaries align with semantically meaningful temporal events, providing interpretable decompositions of video structure.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior adaptive sampling or keyframe selection methods that choose a fixed subset of frames, our approach learns to produce a variable number of composite temporal tokens whose count and span adapt per-clip. The integration of an information-bottleneck objective to regulate temporal resolution is, to our knowledge, unexplored in video representation learning and offers a theoretically grounded alternative to heuristic pooling schedules.

proposals.title: Bridging the Domain Gap: Adversarial and Curriculum-Based Adaptation for Transferring Face Representations from Web Imagery to Cinematic Video
proposals.motivation: State-of-the-art facial analysis models are predominantly trained on large-scale web-crawled still-image datasets such as VGGFace2 and MS-Celeb-1M, yet their deployment on cinematic video benchmarks like AFEW reveals significant performance degradation due to distributional shift in pose, lighting, motion blur, and artistic framing. Closing this domain gap without sacrificing the rich representations learned from abundant web data is critical for real-world affective computing and video understanding applications. As cinematic footage increasingly serves as a testbed for robust perception, principled domain adaptation methods are needed to ensure models generalize beyond their training distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining adversarial domain alignment in a shared feature space with a curriculum strategy that progressively introduces harder cinematic samples will yield face representations that retain the discriminative power of web-trained models while substantially reducing the web-to-cinema domain gap, outperforming both naive fine-tuning and standard unsupervised domain adaptation baselines on AFEW and related movie-face benchmarks.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework: first, a domain-adversarial neural network (DANN) head is attached to a pretrained face encoder to learn domain-invariant features by minimizing a domain classification loss while maximizing task performance on labeled web-face data and unlabeled AFEW frames. Second, we introduce a curriculum-based self-training loop that ranks unlabeled cinematic samples by prediction confidence and progressively incorporates pseudo-labeled movie frames into supervised training, using temporal consistency across adjacent video frames as an auxiliary regularizer to filter noisy pseudo-labels. We further explore multi-source adaptation by treating distinct web datasets (e.g., FER+, AffectNet) as separate source domains with individual alignment heads, enabling the model to selectively transfer from the most relevant source distribution for each target sample.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of domain adaptation techniques specifically tailored to the web-face to movie-face transfer setting, delivering both a practical framework and rigorous ablations across adaptation components. We expect to establish new state-of-the-art results on AFEW and related cinematic emotion recognition benchmarks while producing publicly available domain-adapted feature extractors. The findings will also yield general insights into how visual domain shift interacts with facial attribute tasks, informing future work on robust face analysis in unconstrained video.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is well-studied in object recognition, its application to the specific and practically important web-to-cinema face transfer problem remains underexplored, particularly the combination of adversarial alignment with curriculum pseudo-labeling guided by video temporal coherence. Our multi-source formulation that treats heterogeneous web-face datasets as distinct domains with selective transfer is a non-obvious design choice that addresses the overlooked problem of source-domain heterogeneity in face representation learning.

proposals.title: Instance-Adaptive Modality Gating: Learning Per-Example Reliability Scores for Robust Multimodal Fusion
proposals.motivation: Multimodal learning systems that naively fuse audio, visual, and motion streams suffer from modality-specific overfitting, where a poorly calibrated submodel can corrupt the joint prediction for individual instances even when its aggregate performance appears acceptable. Recent analyses reveal that the informativeness of each modality varies dramatically across examples—audio may dominate for one clip while facial cues are essential for another—yet standard fusion mechanisms apply fixed or globally learned combination weights. Developing instance-wise reliability estimation is critical for deploying robust multimodal systems in safety-sensitive domains such as healthcare and autonomous driving, where a single corrupted modality channel can have catastrophic consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, learned reliability gating module that produces per-instance, per-modality confidence scores—trained with a calibration-aware objective—will improve multimodal fusion accuracy and robustness compared to fixed or attention-only fusion strategies, particularly on out-of-distribution and modality-corrupted examples.
proposals.method: We propose a Modality Reliability Network (MRN) that sits between unimodal encoders and the fusion layer. For each input instance, MRN computes a scalar reliability score per modality using a small auxiliary network that ingests both the unimodal representation and a learned estimate of its predictive uncertainty via Monte Carlo dropout or deep ensembles. These reliability scores are normalized via a temperature-scaled softmax and used to gate each modality's contribution before late fusion. The training objective combines task loss with an explicit calibration penalty (expected calibration error on held-out modality subsets) and a regularizer that encourages the gating network to down-weight modalities whose per-instance confidence is misaligned with actual correctness. We evaluate on multimodal sentiment, emotion recognition, and audio-visual speech benchmarks under both clean and synthetically corrupted modality conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a principled, trainable mechanism for instance-level modality selection in multimodal fusion, moving beyond global attention weights to example-specific reliability estimation grounded in calibration theory. It will yield state-of-the-art robustness to modality-specific noise and distribution shift, and offer interpretable per-instance explanations of which modalities the model trusts and why. The framework is architecture-agnostic and can be retrofitted onto existing multimodal pipelines with minimal overhead.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard multimodal attention, which learns soft weights without explicit calibration guarantees, our approach jointly optimizes for task performance and per-modality calibration at the instance level, creating a gating signal that is both uncertainty-aware and robust to submodel overfitting—a combination not addressed by prior fusion methods.

proposals.title: EmotionMotionNet: Learning Disentangled Emotion-Specific Body Motion Representations via Contrastive Pretraining on Expressive Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Current video-based emotion recognition systems rely heavily on motion features transferred from action recognition models, which capture generic activity patterns rather than the subtle body dynamics that convey affective states. This domain mismatch leads to overfitting on spurious activity cues rather than learning the expressive head gestures, postural shifts, and socially meaningful movements that humans use to perceive emotion. As affective computing matures toward real-world deployment in mental health monitoring, human-robot interaction, and social signal processing, there is an urgent need for motion representations that are explicitly grounded in emotion-relevant body dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: A motion representation pretrained with emotion-aware contrastive objectives over body keypoint trajectories, head pose dynamics, and facial action unit temporal patterns will yield substantially more transferable and robust emotion recognition than generic action-recognition features, particularly on clips where activity context provides weak or misleading cues for affect.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we pretrain a spatiotemporal graph neural network on large-scale body keypoint sequences extracted from in-the-wild video, using a contrastive learning objective that groups clips by annotated emotional valence and arousal while pushing apart clips sharing the same activity label but differing in affect. The graph structure encodes anatomically meaningful joint hierarchies, with dedicated subgraph modules for facial action unit dynamics, head gesture trajectories, and upper-body postural flow. Second, we fine-tune the learned representations on downstream emotion recognition benchmarks, comparing against action-recognition transfer baselines. We employ information-bottleneck regularization to encourage the network to discard activity-specific motion patterns and retain only emotion-discriminative dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first purpose-built motion representation for affective computing that explicitly disentangles emotion-relevant dynamics from activity-correlated confounds. It will provide an open pretrained encoder and a diagnostic benchmark partitioning video clips by motion-emotion alignment strength, enabling the community to rigorously evaluate when and why activity-based transfer fails for emotion tasks. The resulting representations are expected to improve generalization on out-of-distribution scenarios where activity context is absent or misleading.
proposals.novelty: While prior work transfers motion features from action recognition or learns holistic video embeddings, no existing approach explicitly disentangles emotion-linked body dynamics from activity patterns using contrastive objectives with joint emotion and activity supervision. The combination of graph neural networks over anatomical keypoint hierarchies with an information-bottleneck constraint that actively suppresses activity-specific motion signals is, to our knowledge, unexplored in affective computing.

proposals.title: Detector-Free Face Tracking via Latent Temporal Representations Robust to Intermittent Detection Failure
proposals.motivation: Modern video-based face analysis pipelines are brittle: they depend on per-frame face detectors whose failures cascade into broken tracks, lost identity associations, and degraded downstream tasks such as emotion recognition or speech modeling. As face analysis moves into unconstrained, in-the-wild settings—surveillance, clinical monitoring, assistive technology—detector dropouts caused by occlusion, motion blur, or extreme pose become increasingly common yet remain poorly addressed. A principled representation-learning approach that absorbs detector failure as a natural mode of operation, rather than treating it as an exception to be patched with heuristics, is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A temporal latent-variable model trained end-to-end on raw video frames can maintain continuous face identity tracks through extended detector-failure windows by learning to propagate appearance and motion representations forward in time, achieving significantly lower track fragmentation than detection-then-track pipelines augmented with state-of-the-art heuristic smoothing and re-identification.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture combining (1) a variational recurrent encoder that ingests raw video frames and maintains a latent state summarizing face identity, position, and appearance even when no detection is available, and (2) a gated fusion module that integrates bounding-box detections when present but gracefully degrades to pure latent-state prediction when detections are missing. Training uses a masking curriculum: during learning, detector outputs are randomly dropped at increasing rates (0–100%), forcing the model to reconstruct face locations and identity embeddings from raw-frame context alone. We additionally introduce a contrastive temporal consistency loss that encourages the latent trajectory to remain smooth and identity-preserving across masked intervals, drawing on ideas from self-supervised temporal representation learning.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) the first end-to-end trainable face tracking framework explicitly designed for robustness to arbitrary-length detector failure, eliminating the need for hand-crafted fallback heuristics; (2) a masking-curriculum training strategy that systematically teaches temporal models to operate under partial observability; and (3) comprehensive benchmarks on challenging datasets (e.g., multi-face conversation videos, clinical recordings) demonstrating reduced track fragmentation, improved identity consistency, and graceful degradation curves as detector dropout rate increases.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats detection failure as a post-hoc problem to be solved by Kalman-filter interpolation or re-identification modules, our approach reconceives tracking as latent-state inference in a partially observable system, unifying detection-present and detection-absent regimes within a single learned representation. The masking curriculum is a non-obvious training strategy that converts what is normally a pipeline vulnerability into an explicit training signal, enabling the model to discover fallback cues—such as body context, scene geometry, and motion patterns—that hand-designed systems rarely exploit.

proposals.title: Source-Disentangled Emotion Recognition in Cinematic Audio via Variational Inference over Speech, Music, and Noise Streams
proposals.motivation: Movie audio is a dense mixture of dialogue, background music, and ambient noise, each carrying distinct and sometimes conflicting emotional signals. Current multimodal emotion recognition systems treat the audio track as a monolithic input, conflating lexical-prosodic cues with musical sentiment and irrelevant acoustic noise, which limits both interpretability and accuracy. Principled source disentanglement would let models attribute emotional predictions to specific audio components, enabling more robust and explainable affective computing.
proposals.hypothesis: A variational autoencoder framework that infers factored latent representations for speech, music, and noise from mixed cinematic audio will yield emotion predictions that are both more accurate and more interpretable than models operating on unseparated mixtures, because each latent factor captures a distinct and independently informative emotional channel.
proposals.method: We propose a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) with three structured latent subspaces corresponding to speech, music, and noise, trained jointly on a source-separation objective and an emotion classification objective. First, a pre-trained audio source separation backbone (e.g., an adapted Conv-TasNet) produces initial soft estimates of each stream; these are refined by the CVAE encoder into disentangled latent codes regularized by a total-correlation penalty to enforce independence. An attention-based fusion module then learns stream-specific emotion contributions, weighting prosodic, musical, and ambient cues before a final classification head. We train and evaluate on large-scale movie corpora with emotion annotations (e.g., extended IEMOCAP and CMU-MOSEI augmented with professionally mixed soundtracks), using ablations that silence individual latent streams to quantify each source's emotional contribution.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first end-to-end framework that jointly disentangles cinematic audio sources and performs emotion recognition over the factored representations, offering per-source emotional attribution scores that prior monolithic models cannot deliver. It establishes quantitative benchmarks for source-aware affective computing and introduces a total-correlation regularization strategy adapted from disentangled representation learning to the audio-emotion domain. The resulting interpretability—knowing whether an emotional shift is driven by a character's tone, the musical score, or ambient sound design—has direct applications in film analysis, content recommendation, and accessible media.
proposals.novelty: While source separation and emotion recognition have been studied independently, no prior work jointly optimizes disentanglement and affective prediction under a single variational objective with explicit independence constraints across audio streams. The use of total-correlation penalization to enforce factored emotional representations in mixed audio is a non-obvious transfer from the visual disentanglement literature championed by Bengio and colleagues, applied here to a fundamentally different modality where temporal dynamics and cross-stream leakage pose unique challenges.

proposals.title: Lexical-Acoustic-Visual Mixture of Experts for Multimodal Emotion Recognition: Disentangling When Words Complement, Reinforce, or Contradict Nonverbal Cues
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal emotion recognition systems fuse facial and acoustic streams but largely ignore the semantic content of speech, despite strong evidence from psychology that verbal content carries independent affective signals. As large-scale pretrained language models have matured, incorporating transcript-derived lexical features as a first-class modality is now computationally tractable and can expose systematic patterns of agreement and contradiction between what a speaker says and how they say it. Understanding these cross-modal dynamics is critical for building robust, interpretable affective computing systems and for advancing theories of human emotional communication.
proposals.hypothesis: Adding a lexical expert branch that encodes transcript or subtitle text alongside existing acoustic and visual expert branches within a mixture-of-experts framework will significantly improve emotion classification accuracy, and the learned gating mechanism will reveal interpretable regimes where verbal content complements nonverbal cues (e.g., sarcasm detection, masked emotions) versus where it is redundant.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stream mixture-of-experts architecture where each expert processes one modality: (1) a vision transformer fine-tuned on facial action units, (2) a wav2vec 2.0-based acoustic encoder capturing prosody and paralinguistic features, and (3) a pretrained language model (e.g., RoBERTa) encoding automatically transcribed or subtitle-derived text. A learned sparse gating network dynamically weights expert contributions per sample, enabling the model to adapt to varying informativeness across modalities. We train and evaluate on established benchmarks (IEMOCAP, CMU-MOSEI, MELD) using both gold transcripts and ASR-generated transcripts to assess robustness, and we introduce a novel concordance-discordance analysis that quantifies when lexical sentiment aligns with or contradicts acoustic-visual affect signals.
proposals.contribution: This work provides three contributions: first, a principled mixture-of-experts framework that treats text as a peer modality rather than a late-fusion afterthought, yielding state-of-the-art multimodal emotion recognition; second, an interpretable concordance-discordance metric that characterizes cross-modal agreement patterns, enabling fine-grained analysis of phenomena like sarcasm, emotional suppression, and cultural display rules; third, a systematic study of ASR noise robustness that establishes practical guidelines for deploying lexical features in real-world affective computing pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior multimodal fusion approaches typically concatenate or attend over all modalities uniformly, our sparse gating mechanism explicitly learns when lexical content is informative versus when it should be suppressed, providing both performance gains and interpretable evidence about the distinct roles of verbal and nonverbal channels. The concordance-discordance analysis framework is, to our knowledge, the first systematic method for quantifying cross-modal emotional agreement at the sample level in mixture-of-experts emotion recognition.

proposals.title: Attention-Based Multiple-Instance Learning for Robust Anatomical Landmark Detection in Weakly Labeled Colonoscopy Videos
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning approaches for anatomical landmark detection during colonoscopy inherit frame-level labels from clip-level annotations, introducing substantial label noise that degrades model performance and clinical reliability. Multiple-instance learning (MIL) offers a principled framework for learning from such weakly supervised data, as only a subset of frames within a clip genuinely express the annotated landmark. Addressing this noise is critical for deploying reliable automated detection systems that can assist endoscopists in real-time navigation and reduce miss rates for clinically significant landmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating each colonoscopy video clip as a bag of frame-level instances and applying attention-based multiple-instance learning will yield superior landmark detection accuracy compared to models trained with naively propagated clip-level labels, by automatically identifying and up-weighting the informative frames while suppressing noisy non-landmark frames within each clip.
proposals.method: We propose an attention-based MIL framework where each clip is represented as a bag of frame embeddings extracted by a convolutional backbone, and a gated attention mechanism learns instance-level importance scores to aggregate frame representations into a clip-level prediction. The attention weights serve dual purposes: producing a robust clip-level classification and generating pseudo frame-level localizations of landmark presence without requiring per-frame annotations. We will further incorporate a consistency regularization term that encourages temporal smoothness of attention scores across adjacent frames, exploiting the video structure to improve instance-level discrimination.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled weakly supervised training paradigm for colonoscopy landmark detection that eliminates the need for expensive frame-level annotations while matching or exceeding fully supervised baselines. The learned attention maps provide interpretable frame-level localization as a byproduct, offering clinicians transparent evidence for model predictions. Additionally, this approach establishes a general template for applying MIL to other medical video analysis tasks where clip-level labels are abundant but frame-level annotations are scarce.
proposals.novelty: While MIL has been applied to pathology images and radiology, its application to temporal medical video with attention-based instance selection and temporal consistency regularization is unexplored. The combination of gated attention pooling with video-specific temporal priors addresses a gap between static-image MIL methods and the sequential, noisy-label structure inherent in endoscopic video data.

proposals.title: Contrastive Audiovisual Self-Supervision for Label-Efficient Multimodal Emotion Recognition
proposals.motivation: Affective computing models typically require large corpora of human-annotated emotion labels, which are expensive, subjective, and culturally biased. Unlabeled audiovisual data—speech paired with facial video—is abundant yet underexploited as a pretraining signal. A self-supervised framework that learns shared audio-video representations before any emotion labels are introduced would dramatically reduce annotation dependence while capturing cross-modal affective cues that unimodal supervised pipelines miss.
proposals.hypothesis: A contrastive pretraining objective that aligns temporally co-occurring audio and video segments in a shared embedding space will learn emotion-relevant features that transfer to downstream affective classification, matching or exceeding fully supervised multimodal baselines when only 5–10% of emotion labels are available for fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We propose AV-EmoCLR, a two-stage framework. In Stage 1 we pretrain a dual-encoder architecture (a Vision Transformer for face crops and a wav2vec-style audio encoder for speech) using an InfoNCE contrastive loss over temporally aligned audio-video pairs drawn from large unlabeled conversational corpora such as VoxCeleb2 and AVSpeech; hard negatives are mined by sampling misaligned temporal windows within the same speaker to force the model to encode expressive dynamics rather than identity. In Stage 2 a lightweight cross-modal attention fusion head is fine-tuned on small labeled emotion benchmarks (IEMOCAP, MELD, CMU-MOSEI) under controlled label-fraction experiments ranging from 1% to 100%. We compare against supervised-only baselines, AudioMAE and VideoMAE self-supervised pretraining, and prior audiovisual contrastive methods lacking emotion-aware negative sampling.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic study of contrastive audiovisual pretraining specifically designed for low-resource emotion recognition, introducing an intra-speaker temporal hard-negative mining strategy that disentangles identity from affect. We expect to demonstrate that cross-modal self-supervision closes the gap with fully supervised systems at a fraction of the labeling cost, offering a practical path toward scalable affective AI that aligns with responsible data-efficiency goals.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing audiovisual contrastive methods (e.g., AV-HuBERT, AVE-CLIP) that target speech recognition or event detection, AV-EmoCLR introduces emotion-oriented intra-speaker hard negatives that force the shared embedding to capture affective prosody and facial micro-expressions rather than linguistic content or speaker identity—a non-obvious design choice that repurposes the paired modality structure for a fundamentally different downstream task.

proposals.title: Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks for Cross-Corpus Facial Expression Recognition: Bridging Controlled and In-the-Wild Domains
proposals.motivation: Facial expression recognition models trained on controlled datasets such as Google faces or the Toronto Face Database suffer large accuracy drops when deployed on naturalistic video data like AFEW, due to shifts in pose, illumination, resolution, and occlusion. Current practice relies on hand-engineered preprocessing pipelines—face registration, histogram equalization, and illumination normalization—that are brittle, dataset-specific, and discard potentially useful variation. A learned domain-adaptation approach would replace these manual steps with an end-to-end trainable system, improving both robustness and scalability across facial expression corpora.
proposals.hypothesis: A domain-adversarial training objective that forces a shared feature extractor to produce representations invariant to the source domain (controlled lab imagery vs. movie frames) will significantly reduce cross-corpus expression classification error compared to standard fine-tuning and manual normalization baselines, without requiring paired data or target-domain labels.
proposals.method: We adopt the Domain-Adversarial Neural Network (DANN) framework, attaching a gradient-reversal layer between a shared convolutional feature extractor and a domain discriminator, while a separate branch performs expression classification on labeled source data. The feature extractor is trained on a combination of Google faces and TFD as source domains and AFEW video frames as the unlabeled target domain, optimizing a minimax objective that minimizes expression classification loss while maximizing domain confusion. We further introduce a class-conditional alignment variant that matches per-expression feature distributions across domains using pseudo-labels on the target set, mitigating negative transfer when class-conditional distributions differ across corpora.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic evaluation of domain-adversarial adaptation for cross-corpus facial expression recognition spanning both still-image and video-frame domains. It is expected to demonstrate that learned domain invariance can match or exceed the accuracy of labor-intensive manual preprocessing pipelines while generalizing more gracefully to unseen recording conditions. The resulting framework will offer a drop-in replacement for hand-engineered normalization in any expression recognition pipeline, lowering the barrier to deploying affect-sensing systems in unconstrained real-world environments.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation has been explored in object recognition, applying adversarial domain alignment specifically to bridge the controlled-to-wild gap in facial expression recognition—and doing so across heterogeneous source corpora with a class-conditional alignment mechanism—has not been systematically studied. The integration of gradient-reversal-based invariance learning with per-expression distribution matching addresses the unique challenge that expression classes themselves exhibit domain-dependent appearance statistics, a subtlety ignored by vanilla domain adaptation.

proposals.title: Domain-Adversarial Feature Alignment for Cross-Domain Facial Expression Recognition
proposals.motivation: Facial expression recognition (FER) models trained on curated datasets like Google and TFD fail to generalize to in-the-wild video datasets like AFEW due to domain shift in illumination, pose, and background. This limits the deployment of robust, real-world affective computing systems. Current solutions rely on manual pre-processing, which is not scalable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a domain-adversarial neural network can learn domain-invariant latent representations that explicitly remove dataset-specific cues, thereby aligning the feature distributions of controlled (Google/TFD) and wild (AFEW) face image domains to improve cross-domain FER accuracy.
proposals.method: We will employ a Domain-Adversarial Neural Network (DANN) architecture with a shared feature extractor (e.g., ResNet), a label predictor for expression classification, and a domain classifier. The feature extractor will be trained to maximize domain classifier loss (via a gradient reversal layer) while minimizing expression classification loss, forcing it to learn domain-agnostic features. We will compare against standard fine-tuning and correlation alignment (CORAL) baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully learned, end-to-end alternative to manual image registration and normalization for cross-domain FER. It will demonstrate the utility of adversarial domain adaptation for aligning highly divergent facial image datasets, contributing a practical method for building more generalizable affective AI.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is known, its explicit application to align controlled lab photos with uncontrolled movie frames for FER, replacing manual pre-processing with learned adversarial alignment, is novel. The approach directly tackles the domain-specific cues (lighting, framing) that hinder generalization in this specific, socially-relevant task.

proposals.title: Stochastic Modality Dropout with Learned Fusion Gating for Robust Multimodal Perception Under Sensor Absence
proposals.motivation: Real-world multimodal systems frequently encounter missing or degraded sensor streams at deployment time due to hardware failures, occlusion, or environmental interference, yet most fusion architectures assume complete inputs and degrade catastrophically when modalities are absent. This brittleness is especially problematic in safety-critical applications where robustness to partial observation is essential. Developing training regimes and architectural components that explicitly prepare fusion models for arbitrary patterns of modality absence addresses a pressing gap between laboratory benchmarks and deployment reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: A training regime that combines structured stochastic modality dropout with a learned gating mechanism conditioned on modality availability indicators will produce fusion representations that degrade gracefully under arbitrary missing-modality patterns, matching or exceeding the accuracy of full-modality baselines even when up to half of input streams are absent at test time.
proposals.method: During training, each modality branch is independently dropped with a scheduled probability that anneals from low to high, forcing the shared fusion layers to learn redundant cross-modal representations. A lightweight gating network receives a binary availability vector and outputs per-modality attention weights that dynamically re-calibrate the fusion stack, effectively redistributing representational capacity to present modalities. We further regularize the latent space with a contrastive alignment loss that encourages the fused representation under any dropout mask to remain close to the full-modality representation, drawing on principles from denoising autoencoders and information bottleneck theory.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, architecture-agnostic training framework for missing-modality robustness that unifies modality dropout scheduling, availability-conditioned gating, and contrastive latent alignment into a single coherent method. We provide theoretical analysis connecting dropout rate scheduling to information-theoretic bounds on representation quality under partial observation. Empirical evaluation across multimodal benchmarks spanning audio-visual, sensor fusion, and medical imaging domains will establish new state-of-the-art robustness profiles under systematic and random modality ablation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior modality dropout approaches that treat dropout as a simple regularizer, our method couples stochastic ablation with a learned gating module and a contrastive alignment objective that jointly ensure the fusion representation remains semantically stable regardless of which modalities are present, providing both an architectural and an optimization-level solution rather than relying on either alone.

proposals.title: Bayesian Ensemble Calibration for Robust Multi-Expert Fusion Under Distribution Shift
proposals.motivation: Multi-expert fusion systems in deep learning often suffer from overconfident predictions and miscalibration, particularly when flexible combination models overfit to in-distribution data. Recent findings demonstrate that simple weighted averages can outperform complex learned fusion architectures precisely because they resist overfitting, yet they fail to account for epistemic uncertainty or adapt calibration under distribution shift. A principled Bayesian framework for ensemble calibration would bridge this gap, enabling robust model combination that is both well-calibrated and uncertainty-aware.
proposals.hypothesis: Bayesian model averaging with per-expert calibration via learned temperature scaling and Dirichlet-based posterior weighting will yield fusion predictions that are simultaneously better calibrated (lower expected calibration error) and more robust under distribution shift than both simple weighted averages and flexible learned fusion methods.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage approach: first, each expert model is individually calibrated using a Bayesian variant of temperature scaling where the temperature parameter is given a prior and inferred via variational inference, producing calibrated predictive distributions with uncertainty estimates. Second, we perform Bayesian model averaging over the calibrated experts using a Dirichlet prior on combination weights, with posterior inference conducted via stochastic variational methods that scale to large ensembles. The posterior over weights naturally contracts toward uniform (simple averaging) when data is scarce or experts disagree, providing an automatic interpolation between simple averaging and learned fusion. We evaluate on image classification, natural language inference, and structured prediction benchmarks under both in-distribution and shifted conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a theoretically grounded fusion framework that unifies the robustness advantages of simple averaging with the expressiveness of learned combination, automatically adapting its complexity to the available evidence. It delivers practical calibration improvements for safety-critical applications where overconfident ensemble predictions can lead to catastrophic downstream decisions. The framework also yields interpretable posterior weight distributions that quantify inter-expert agreement and identify when individual experts should be trusted or discounted.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing post-hoc calibration methods that treat calibration and combination as separate steps, our approach jointly infers calibration parameters and combination weights under a single Bayesian objective, allowing calibration quality to inform how experts are weighted. The Dirichlet posterior mechanism provides a principled explanation for why simple averages often dominate—they correspond to the maximum-entropy prior—and automatically recovers this behavior when the learned fusion would overfit.

proposals.title: InteractionGNN: Graph Neural Networks over Socio-Temporal Interaction Structures for Group Emotion Recognition in Movie Clips
proposals.motivation: Emotion recognition in naturalistic video remains dominated by single-face classifiers that ignore the rich social context humans rely on to interpret affect. In movies especially, a character's emotion is often conveyed not by their own facial expression alone but by the reactions of surrounding characters, spatial proximity, and turn-taking dynamics. Transferring graph-based relational modeling—already proven effective in molecular and social-network domains—to this multi-person affective setting could close a critical gap between isolated-face baselines and human-level contextual understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Encoding characters as nodes and their pairwise spatio-temporal relationships (co-occurrence, gaze direction, speaking-turn adjacency, and proxemic distance) as typed edges in a heterogeneous graph neural network will yield significantly more accurate group-level and individual emotion predictions in movie clips than models that process each face track independently, because the interaction structure carries affective information that is invisible to per-face representations.
proposals.method: We construct a dynamic heterogeneous graph for each movie scene: face-track detections become nodes whose features are extracted by a frozen vision encoder, while edges are typed by relation (spatial proximity, temporal co-occurrence, gaze mutual attention, and speaker–listener turn adjacency derived from an off-the-shelf active-speaker detector). A temporal message-passing GNN, extending the benchmarking framework of Dwivedi et al. (2020) with time-stamped edge convolutions and a graph-level readout with learnable pooling, propagates contextual cues across characters and time steps. The model is trained end-to-end on MovieGraphs and EMOTIC with a multi-task loss combining categorical emotion classification and valence-arousal regression, using curriculum learning that progresses from dyadic to multi-party scenes.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled, reproducible GNN architecture that treats social interaction topology as a first-class input for affective computing, providing a reusable module for any downstream video-understanding task; (2) empirical evidence quantifying how much affective information resides in relational structure versus individual appearance; and (3) a new evaluation protocol and interaction-graph annotations for movie emotion benchmarks that will enable future research on context-aware affect recognition.
proposals.novelty: While prior graph-based approaches in social behavior analysis model pedestrian trajectories or simple co-occurrences, InteractionGNN introduces heterogeneous, temporally evolving edge types specifically designed to capture the pragmatic signals (turn-taking, mutual gaze, proxemic shifts) that film theory identifies as primary carriers of emotional meaning—bridging GNN benchmarking methodology with affective science in a combination not previously explored.

proposals.title: Disentangled Audio Source Representations for Attributing Affective Predictions in Multimedia: Separating Prosody, Soundtrack, and Ambient Signals
proposals.motivation: Emotion recognition from video relies on complex audio mixtures where speech prosody, background music, and ambient sound effects co-occur, yet current end-to-end models treat the audio track as a monolithic input, making it impossible to attribute which acoustic source drives a given affective prediction. Disentangling these sources before classification would enable controlled ablation studies and improve both interpretability and robustness of affective computing systems. Given growing concerns about deploying opaque AI systems in high-stakes social contexts—a theme central to recent AI safety discourse—understanding what signals a model actually relies on is both a scientific and a societal imperative.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular pipeline that first separates speech, music, and ambient effects into independent latent streams, then classifies emotion from each stream independently and in combination, will reveal that speaker prosody and soundtrack mood contribute complementary but unequal predictive signals whose relative importance varies systematically across emotion categories, and that explicit source disentanglement improves both classification accuracy and attribution faithfulness compared to monolithic audio encoders.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage architecture: (1) a pretrained speech separation network (e.g., Conv-TasNet or SepFormer) augmented with a voice activity detection module to isolate speaker turns from non-speech audio, followed by a music-versus-ambient classifier to further partition the residual signal; (2) independent learned representation modules—building on self-supervised audio encoders such as HuBERT for speech prosody and a music-domain encoder for soundtrack features—that map each separated source into a shared affective embedding space; (3) a gated fusion mechanism with per-source attention weights that are themselves supervised to be interpretable, enabling post-hoc attribution of the final emotion prediction to each acoustic source. We train and evaluate on large-scale multimedia corpora (e.g., CMU-MOSEI, MovieGraphs) using both categorical and dimensional emotion labels, with systematic ablation over source combinations.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled methodology for source-level attribution in multimodal emotion recognition, providing the first systematic empirical evidence on the relative predictive power of prosody versus soundtrack versus ambient acoustics across emotion types. The disentangled architecture will serve as a diagnostic tool for auditing affective AI systems, revealing whether models exploit spurious acoustic cues (e.g., relying on dramatic background music rather than genuine emotional speech). By bridging speech separation and affective computing, the framework opens a new design axis for transparent and controllable multimedia understanding systems.
proposals.novelty: While speech separation and emotion recognition have each advanced independently, no prior work transfers source separation as an explicit attribution mechanism for affective prediction, nor systematically quantifies how each isolated audio stream contributes to emotion classification across categories. The combination of disentangled source representations with interpretable gated fusion provides a non-obvious connection between signal processing and AI interpretability that aligns with growing demands for accountable affective computing.

proposals.title: Value-Guided Active Curation: A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Prioritizing and Cleaning Web-Harvested Training Data
proposals.motivation: Large-scale web-mined datasets have become a dominant source of training data for deep learning, yet they contain substantial noise, mislabeling, and distribution mismatches that degrade model performance. Current approaches either apply coarse heuristic filters that discard valuable examples or require exhaustive manual review that does not scale. A principled framework that combines data valuation with active learning can direct scarce human annotation effort to the highest-impact subset of harvested data, dramatically improving data efficiency and final model quality.
proposals.hypothesis: By estimating per-example data values through gradient-based influence functions and using these estimates to guide an active learning acquisition strategy, human annotators can be directed to verify and clean only the web-mined examples whose inclusion would maximally improve downstream task performance, yielding models that match or exceed those trained on fully curated datasets while requiring annotation of less than 15 percent of the harvested pool.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage iterative pipeline. First, a data valuation module estimates the marginal contribution of each candidate web example using a lightweight approximation to Data Shapley values computed via TracIn-style gradient inner products against a small trusted validation set. Second, an active learning selector queries human annotators to verify, relabel, or discard the top-ranked examples according to a combined acquisition function that balances estimated data value, prediction uncertainty, and diversity in representation space. The annotated batch is incorporated into the training set and the valuation model is updated, repeating until a performance plateau or annotation budget is reached. We validate on image classification and video action recognition benchmarks using web-crawled extensions of standard datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work bridges data valuation theory and practical active learning into a unified curation framework specifically designed for noisy web-harvested data. It provides practitioners with a deployable pipeline that reduces annotation costs by an order of magnitude while maintaining or improving model accuracy compared to naive data augmentation from the web. Additionally, the framework produces interpretable data quality scores that can inform future crawling strategies and dataset documentation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior data valuation methods that operate post-hoc on fixed datasets, our approach integrates valuation directly into the acquisition loop, allowing the value estimates to adapt as the training distribution shifts with each annotation round. The combination of influence-function-based scoring with human-in-the-loop verification for web data is unexplored, and creates a feedback mechanism where human judgments continuously recalibrate the automated valuation model.

proposals.title: Active Data Valuation and Curation for Robust Learning from Noisy Web-Scale Datasets
proposals.motivation: The success of large-scale models hinges on vast datasets often harvested from the web, which contain significant noise, mislabeling, and irrelevant content. This noise degrades model performance and reliability, particularly in safety-critical domains like medical imaging (e.g., colonoscopy) or AI risk analysis. Efficiently identifying the most valuable data points for cleaning and annotation is a major bottleneck for scaling model development responsibly.
proposals.hypothesis: A human-in-the-loop active learning framework, guided by a learned data valuation function, can efficiently prioritize the most informative and cleanable examples from noisy web data, leading to models that achieve superior performance with less curated data than standard random or heuristic filtering.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, a core neural network model (e.g., a Vision Transformer or CNN) will be trained on a small, high-quality seed dataset (e.g., curated colonoscopy frames). Second, we will employ an acquisition function combining predictive uncertainty (e.g., Bayesian neural networks or Monte Carlo dropout) and a learned data valuation metric (inspired by Shapley value or influence function approximations) to score and rank harvested web data. The highest-scoring, most uncertain, and influential examples will be presented for human expert annotation (e.g., labeling normal mucosa or anatomical landmarks), iteratively refining both the model and the valuation function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable framework for dataset curation, reducing the human and computational cost of building large, high-quality datasets. It will contribute new methods for data valuation in the context of noisy external sources and demonstrate their impact on final model robustness and accuracy in domains like medical imaging.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is well-studied for clean datasets, its integration with data valuation techniques specifically for the noisy, web-scale data paradigm is novel. The approach moves beyond simple confidence thresholds by learning to value data points based on their expected contribution to model improvement and cleanliness, creating a tight human-AI curation loop.

proposals.title: CurriculumFusion: Self-Paced Curriculum Learning for Robust Multimodal Emotion Recognition from Noisy Audio-Visual Data
