Third, we augment beam search with a dynamic length and idiomaticity reranking module that applies learned correction factors conditioned on source-side features, evaluated against standard beam search and diverse beam search baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic decomposition of how length bias and literalness bias independently and jointly affect NMT output quality across typologically diverse language pairs. The proposed training and decoding corrections offer a practical, model-agnostic toolkit for reducing these biases, directly improving translation fluency on idiomatic constructions by an estimated 15-25% in human evaluation. The diagnostic framework itself becomes a reusable benchmark for auditing bias in any conditional text generation system.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified length bias in NMT decoding, no study has jointly diagnosed literalness bias as a coupled phenomenon rooted in the training objective itself. Our approach is non-obvious because it reframes what appears to be a decoding artifact as a fundamental misalignment between the maximum-likelihood objective and human translation preferences, requiring coordinated intervention at both training and inference time rather than simple post-hoc normalization.

proposals.title: Latent Phrase Induction from Raw Parallel Corpora: Eliminating Phrase-Table Supervision in Neural Translation Models
proposals.motivation: Current neural phrase-based and hybrid translation models still depend on phrase pairs extracted by classical SMT pipelines, inheriting their segmentation errors and coverage limitations. This dependency creates an artificial ceiling on translation quality and prevents end-to-end learning of compositional translation units. Removing this bottleneck is critical for building translation systems that can discover optimal bilingual units directly from data, especially for low-resource language pairs where statistical phrase extraction is unreliable.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural model equipped with a differentiable latent segmentation mechanism can induce translation-relevant phrase representations directly from raw parallel sentences and weak or no word alignments, matching or exceeding the translation quality of models trained on traditionally extracted phrase tables.
proposals.method: We propose a variational encoder-decoder architecture with a latent segmentation variable that jointly learns to segment source sentences into phrase-like spans and map them to target-language representations. The encoder uses a structured attention mechanism inspired by the CTC framework to marginalize over exponentially many segmentations, while a contrastive bilingual objective trained on parallel sentence pairs encourages the induced segments to capture cross-lingual correspondences without explicit alignment supervision. We compare three supervision regimes: full phrase-table supervision, weak guidance from unsupervised statistical alignments, and a fully unsupervised setting using only parallel sentences with a monolingual language model prior.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic framework for inducing bilingual phrase representations without any traditional SMT pipeline component, demonstrating that end-to-end learned segmentations can recover and surpass phrase-table quality on standard translation benchmarks. It establishes that latent structure discovery is a viable replacement for heuristic phrase extraction, opening a path toward fully neural compositional translation. The framework also yields interpretable phrase-level alignments as a byproduct, useful for translation analysis and education.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior hybrid models that treat phrase extraction as a fixed preprocessing step, our approach makes segmentation itself a latent variable optimized jointly with translation, avoiding the information bottleneck imposed by discrete phrase-table extraction. The combination of structured marginalization over segmentations with contrastive bilingual training is, to our knowledge, unexplored and provides a principled probabilistic alternative to pipeline-dependent phrase supervision.

proposals.title: Neural Phrase Table: Replacing Statistical Phrase Tables with Encoder-Decoder Target Phrase Generation for Statistical Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Statistical machine translation systems rely on phrase tables extracted via word alignment heuristics, which suffer from sparsity, lack of generalization to unseen phrase pairs, and excessive memory requirements. Neural encoder-decoder models have demonstrated the ability to learn continuous representations of source phrases that capture semantic and syntactic regularities, suggesting they can generate plausible target phrases on demand rather than relying on fixed lookup tables. Bridging neural phrase generation with the proven search and reordering infrastructure of phrase-based SMT could yield systems that combine the generalization capacity of neural models with the reliability of traditional decoding.
proposals.hypothesis: An encoder-decoder network trained to propose ranked target phrase candidates for each source phrase can replace or augment the conventional phrase table, and a hybrid system that interpolates neural phrase scores with traditional SMT features will achieve higher BLEU scores and improved lexical coverage on low-resource and morphologically rich language pairs compared to standard phrase-based baselines.
proposals.method: We train a recurrent encoder-decoder model with attention on phrase-aligned parallel corpora to generate target phrase candidates given source phrases, producing a scored n-best list per source span. We integrate these neural phrase proposals into the Moses phrase-based decoder as an additional feature function, allowing log-linear interpolation with traditional phrase table probabilities, lexical weighting, and language model scores. We evaluate three configurations—standard phrase table only, neural phrase table only, and a hybrid combining both—across language pairs of varying resource levels (French-English, German-English, English-Turkish) using BLEU, METEOR, and novel phrase coverage metrics that measure the proportion of test-set phrase pairs absent from the training phrase table but correctly generated by the neural model.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic comparison of neural versus statistical phrase tables within an otherwise identical phrase-based SMT pipeline, isolating the effect of phrase proposal mechanism from other architectural differences. It establishes whether neural phrase generation can improve translation of rare and unseen phrases, which is a key limitation of lookup-based phrase tables, particularly for morphologically rich and low-resource language pairs. The hybrid interpolation framework offers a practical upgrade path for existing SMT deployments without requiring a full transition to end-to-end neural MT.
proposals.novelty: Unlike end-to-end neural machine translation which replaces the entire SMT pipeline, this approach surgically replaces only the phrase table component while preserving the well-understood log-linear feature combination and beam search of phrase-based decoding, enabling controlled ablation of neural versus statistical phrase generation. The neural phrase table also introduces a generative capacity absent from conventional tables—synthesizing target phrases for source spans never observed in training—which provides a qualitatively different mechanism for handling data sparsity.

proposals.title: Adaptive Beam Search with Neural Pruning and Phrase-Level Caching for Efficient Open-Ended Phrase Generation in Statistical Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural phrase generation within SMT decoding pipelines remains prohibitively expensive due to the combinatorial explosion of candidate phrases and repeated model evaluations. Current approaches rely on exhaustive or naïve sampling, which scales poorly as phrase tables grow and target vocabulary expands. Developing efficient search strategies is critical for making neurally-guided SMT practical for real-time and large-scale translation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid search framework that combines adaptive beam search with a learned neural pruning policy and a locality-sensitive phrase cache can reduce the computational cost of open-ended phrase generation by an order of magnitude while preserving translation quality, as measured by BLEU score degradation of less than 0.5 points compared to exhaustive search.
proposals.method: We propose a three-component architecture: (1) an adaptive beam search that dynamically adjusts beam width per decoding step using a lightweight policy network trained via reinforcement learning to balance exploration and computational budget; (2) a neural pruning module that scores candidate phrase expansions and eliminates low-promise branches early, trained on distilled signals from a full-search oracle; and (3) a retrieval-augmented phrase cache using locality-sensitive hashing over source context embeddings to reuse previously decoded high-quality phrase fragments across similar contexts. The system is trained end-to-end on standard MT corpora (WMT14/WMT19) and evaluated against Moses and Transformer baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first unified framework that jointly optimizes search efficiency and translation quality for neural phrase generation within SMT decoding. It introduces a learnable pruning criterion that generalizes across language pairs and domains, reducing inference cost by 10-15x. The phrase caching mechanism offers a novel form of non-parametric memory for decoding that can be adopted by both SMT and neural MT systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior fixed-heuristic beam search or static pruning thresholds, our approach learns when to prune and how wide to search from the decoding dynamics themselves, treating search budget allocation as a sequential decision problem. The combination of learned adaptive search with retrieval-augmented caching for phrase-level reuse has not been explored in the MT literature.

proposals.title: Linguistic Probing of Gated Recurrent Units: What Do Reset and Update Gates Learn About Language Structure in Neural Machine Translation?
proposals.motivation: Gated recurrent units (GRUs) introduced a compact gating mechanism that rivals LSTMs on sequence modeling tasks, yet the linguistic phenomena captured by individual gates remain poorly understood. Understanding whether reset and update gates specialize for distinct linguistic functions—such as syntactic agreement, lexical fertility, or phrase boundary detection—would provide interpretability insights that inform both architecture design and training methodology. As the field increasingly demands explainable NLP systems, mechanistic understanding of foundational recurrent components remains an important open problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reset and update gates in GRU-based encoder-decoder translation models develop distinct linguistic specializations: reset gates will preferentially activate at phrase boundaries and during lexical reordering events, effectively controlling when prior context should be discarded, while update gates will track long-range syntactic dependencies such as subject-verb agreement and govern fertility (one-to-many word alignments), controlling how much historical information persists across timesteps.
proposals.method: We train GRU-based encoder-decoder models on parallel corpora for typologically diverse language pairs (English-German, English-Japanese, English-Arabic) to vary reordering complexity and morphological richness. We then apply diagnostic probing classifiers on gate activation vectors to predict linguistically annotated properties—phrase boundaries (from constituency parses), reordering distance (from word alignments), morphological agreement features, and fertility counts—at each timestep. We complement probing with controlled minimal-pair interventions, clamping individual gate values while measuring BLEU degradation on syntactically targeted test suites (e.g., subject-verb agreement across relative clauses, noun compound reordering).
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic, linguistically grounded analysis of functional specialization within GRU gates across typologically diverse translation settings. The results will yield concrete design guidance—for instance, whether gate dimensionality should scale with morphological complexity or reordering distance—and produce publicly released probing benchmarks and annotated gate activation datasets that enable reproducible interpretability research on recurrent architectures.
proposals.novelty: While prior interpretability work has examined what RNN hidden states encode, no study has isolated the distinct linguistic roles of reset versus update gates using controlled probing and causal intervention across diverse language pairs. The combination of correlational probing with causal clamping experiments on linguistically targeted test suites goes beyond standard representation analysis to establish functional, not merely correlational, gate specialization.

proposals.title: Neural Phrase-Table Pruning: Leveraging Continuous Phrase Representations for Denoising and Compression of Statistical Translation Models
proposals.motivation: Phrase tables in statistical machine translation systems grow combinatorially and contain vast numbers of noisy, redundant, or spuriously aligned entries that degrade translation quality and inflate decoding time. Existing count-based and significance-test pruning heuristics discard entries without understanding semantic plausibility, leaving systematic noise patterns intact. Continuous phrase representations learned by encoder-decoder networks capture fine-grained translational equivalence and neighborhood structure, offering a principled basis for identifying and removing low-quality entries at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Phrase-table entries whose learned vector representations fall outside high-density regions of the bilingual embedding space, or whose source-target cosine similarity falls below a learned plausibility threshold, are predominantly noisy or redundant, and removing them will improve both translation quality (BLEU) and decoding speed without sacrificing coverage on in-domain test sets.
proposals.method: We train a bilingual phrase encoder using the continuous-space translation model framework to embed source and target phrases into a shared vector space, then define a neural plausibility score combining cross-lingual cosine similarity with a density estimate from the phrase neighborhood graph. Entries scoring below a tuned threshold are pruned; additionally, near-duplicate clusters identified via locality-sensitive hashing in the embedding space are collapsed to a single representative entry. The pruned tables are evaluated in a standard phrase-based decoder (Moses) across multiple language pairs (French-English, German-English, Chinese-English) against significance-based and entropy-based pruning baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic framework for neural plausibility-driven phrase-table pruning, unifying semantic filtering and redundancy removal under a single learned representation. We expect to demonstrate that tables can be reduced by 50-70% with no loss or modest gains in BLEU, substantially accelerating decoding. The approach also yields interpretable diagnostics—visualizing which phrase neighborhoods are noisy—offering insights into alignment error patterns that inform upstream improvements.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior pruning methods that rely on surface statistics such as co-occurrence counts or Fisher significance tests, this approach exploits the geometric structure of learned bilingual embeddings to make semantically informed retention decisions, enabling the detection of plausible-but-redundant entries and implausible-but-frequent entries that statistical methods systematically misjudge.

proposals.title: Compositional Generalization for Rare and Unseen Phrase Translation via Retrieval-Augmented Encoder-Decoder Models
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation systems still struggle with rare and unseen multi-word phrases, producing hallucinated or under-translated outputs when source n-grams fall outside the frequent training distribution. This problem is acute in low-resource language pairs and specialized domains (e.g., biomedical, legal) where long-tail phrase distributions dominate. Improving compositional generalization to such phrases would make NMT systems substantially more reliable in real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: An encoder-decoder translation model augmented with a retrieval-based compositional memory and a frequency-aware contrastive training objective will generate more accurate translations of rare and previously unseen source phrases than standard transformer baselines, by learning to compositionally assemble sub-phrase representations rather than memorizing full-phrase mappings.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component extension to the standard transformer encoder-decoder architecture. First, we introduce a Compositional Phrase Memory (CPM) module that, at inference time, retrieves the k-nearest sub-phrase embeddings from a learned phrase bank and composes them via a small cross-attention layer to produce a prototype representation for unseen or rare source phrases. Second, we apply a frequency-stratified contrastive loss during training that up-weights rare phrase pairs and explicitly contrasts correct translations against near-miss distractors sampled from the phrase table, encouraging the decoder to distinguish fine-grained meaning differences. We evaluate on WMT benchmarks with controlled rare-phrase test sets and a newly constructed long-tail biomedical translation benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide (1) a principled, architecture-level mechanism for compositional phrase generalization that is compatible with any encoder-decoder NMT system, (2) a frequency-stratified training protocol that demonstrably reduces translation errors on rare and zero-shot phrases, and (3) new diagnostic evaluation benchmarks and metrics specifically designed to measure long-tail phrase translation quality. Together these contributions address a known but under-studied failure mode of neural translation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats rare-word handling through subword segmentation or copy mechanisms at the token level, our approach operates at the phrase level by retrieving and composing sub-phrase representations, bridging the gap between the compositional strengths of classical phrase-based SMT and the fluency of neural models. The frequency-aware contrastive objective is, to our knowledge, the first training signal explicitly designed to sharpen decoder decisions along the frequency spectrum of source phrases.

proposals.title: Domain-Adaptive Neural Phrase Scoring via Latent Domain Embeddings for Statistical Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Statistical machine translation systems suffer significant quality degradation when phrase tables trained on one domain are applied to another, yet rebuilding phrase tables from scratch for every new domain is computationally prohibitive. Prior work has shown that indiscriminate data aggregation across domains actively hurts translation quality, indicating that phrase-level distributional shifts are a core bottleneck. A lightweight, neural approach to adapting phrase scores across domains would make high-quality SMT accessible in low-resource and rapidly shifting domain settings without full system retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: Phrase-pair representations learned by a neural scoring model contain a separable domain-specific component, and by learning a latent domain embedding space it is possible to transform phrase scores from a source domain to a target domain with minimal parallel data, achieving translation quality competitive with in-domain trained phrase tables.
proposals.method: We propose training a neural phrase scoring network that jointly learns phrase-pair representations and a continuous latent domain embedding using a multi-task objective over heterogeneous domain corpora. At adaptation time, a small sample of target-domain monolingual or parallel data is used to infer the target domain embedding via amortized variational inference, which then modulates the phrase scoring function through feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) layers. We evaluate on multi-domain translation benchmarks (e.g., medical, legal, IT) using BLEU, METEOR, and domain-specific terminology accuracy, comparing against full in-domain retraining, simple mixture-model interpolation, and instance-weighting baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first framework for continuous, embedding-based domain adaptation of neural phrase scores that avoids discrete domain labels and full phrase table reconstruction. It offers a practical mechanism for rapid SMT domain transfer using orders of magnitude less target-domain data than retraining, and it yields theoretical insight into how phrase-level translation knowledge decomposes into domain-invariant and domain-specific factors.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior domain adaptation methods for SMT that operate at the corpus or model level through data selection or interpolation, this approach intervenes directly at the phrase representation level with a learned continuous domain manifold, enabling smooth adaptation to unseen domains without retraining and revealing the geometric structure of cross-domain phrase score variation.

proposals.title: PhraseIR: Approximate Nearest-Neighbor Retrieval over Learned Phrase Embeddings for Efficient Non-Autoregressive Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Non-autoregressive and phrase-table-augmented translation models must search over exponentially large candidate sets at decode time, creating a computational bottleneck that limits their practical deployment. Recent advances in approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search and dense retrieval have dramatically reduced lookup costs in open-domain question answering and passage retrieval, yet these techniques remain unexploited for structured phrase-level generation. Bridging this gap would convert an expensive combinatorial sampling problem into a tractable vector search, unlocking real-time phrase-based decoding at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Encoding target phrases into a learned vector space and replacing exhaustive phrase-table lookup with approximate nearest-neighbor retrieval will yield translation quality comparable to full beam search while reducing decoding latency by an order of magnitude, because the continuous embedding space captures soft semantic and syntactic similarities that allow high-recall retrieval with sub-linear search complexity.
proposals.method: We train a dual-encoder model that maps source context windows and target phrase candidates into a shared dense embedding space using a contrastive objective over aligned phrase pairs extracted from parallel corpora. At inference time, we build a FAISS-based HNSW index over all target-language phrase embeddings and, for each source span, retrieve the top-k nearest neighbors as candidate translations, which are then rescored by a lightweight cross-attention reranker. The system is integrated into a semi-autoregressive decoder that iteratively selects and stitches retrieved phrases, with the index updated via an online hard-negative mining schedule during fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first end-to-end framework that casts phrase-table decoding as a dense retrieval problem, providing both a training recipe for phrase-level contrastive embeddings and an efficient ANN-backed inference pipeline. We expect to demonstrate that retrieval-based decoding achieves BLEU scores within one point of autoregressive baselines on WMT benchmarks while operating five to fifteen times faster. The approach also opens a modular architecture where the phrase index can be updated, expanded to new domains, or shared across language pairs without retraining the decoder.
proposals.novelty: While dense retrieval has transformed passage-level and token-level search, no prior work applies ANN indexing at the phrase granularity for generation, which requires solving the unique challenges of variable-length span encoding and maintaining coherence across retrieved fragments. Our contrastive phrase embedding with online hard-negative mining is specifically designed to preserve both local lexical fidelity and broader contextual compatibility, a combination that naive adaptation of existing retrieval methods does not achieve.

proposals.title: Phrase-Difficulty Curriculum Learning for Neural Machine Translation: Scheduling Training by Length, Frequency, and Alignment Entropy
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation models are typically trained on randomly shuffled sentence pairs, ignoring the well-documented variance in phrase-pair difficulty arising from length, corpus frequency, and translational ambiguity. Curriculum learning has demonstrated consistent gains in vision and language modeling by presenting easy examples first, yet its application to NMT at the sub-sentential phrase level remains unexplored. Given that attention-based NMT systems are known to struggle disproportionately with rare, long, and highly multimodal phrase alignments, a principled difficulty-aware training schedule could yield both faster convergence and improved translation quality on the hardest tail phenomena.
proposals.hypothesis: A curriculum strategy that orders training examples by a composite phrase-difficulty score—combining phrase length, corpus frequency rank, and alignment entropy—will improve BLEU on rare and long-phrase test sets by at least 1.5 points over uniform-random baselines, while matching or exceeding overall translation quality, because early exposure to unambiguous patterns lets the attention mechanism learn reliable alignment primitives before confronting complex multimodal cases.
proposals.method: We define a phrase-difficulty scoring function d(s) = α·norm_len(s) + β·(1 − norm_freq(s)) + γ·H_align(s), where norm_len is normalized source-sentence length, norm_freq is log-frequency percentile, and H_align is the entropy of a word-alignment model (fast_align) over the sentence pair. Training proceeds in three curriculum stages—easy, medium, hard—determined by tercile thresholds of d(s), with each stage trained until validation perplexity plateaus before admitting harder data. We implement this atop a standard Transformer base model on WMT14 English-German and English-French, comparing against random-order, sentence-length-only curriculum, and competence-based curriculum baselines. We additionally conduct ablations isolating each difficulty component and measure attention-alignment quality using alignment error rate at each stage.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic study of sub-sentential phrase-difficulty curricula for NMT, introducing a linguistically grounded composite difficulty metric that unifies length, frequency, and alignment ambiguity. It establishes empirical evidence for whether structured exposure to progressively harder phrase phenomena improves both overall BLEU and targeted recall of rare long-distance translations. The proposed framework is model-agnostic and can be integrated into any attention-based NMT training pipeline with minimal overhead.
proposals.novelty: While prior curriculum learning for NMT has focused on sentence-level heuristics such as length or language-model scores, no work has decomposed difficulty into phrase-level linguistic dimensions—specifically alignment entropy as a proxy for translational multimodality. Combining frequency, length, and alignment ambiguity into a single curriculum schedule bridges insights from statistical MT phrase-table analysis with modern neural training dynamics, offering a non-obvious synthesis that existing sentence-level curricula cannot capture.

proposals.title: ExpertDecode: Mixture-of-Experts Decoder Heads for Structured One-to-Many Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation models typically learn a single averaged mapping from source to target, collapsing the inherent one-to-many nature of translation where a single phrase may have equally valid literal, idiomatic, or domain-specific renderings. This collapse leads to bland, generic translations and poor coverage of legitimate variation, a problem that worsens for morphologically rich and low-resource language pairs. Recent success of mixture-of-experts architectures in speech recognition and vision—where different experts naturally specialize on distinct input regimes—suggests a principled mechanism for structuring decoder diversity around translation modalities rather than relying on post-hoc reranking.
proposals.hypothesis: A mixture-of-experts decoder architecture in which expert sub-networks are trained with an auxiliary regime-classification loss will learn to specialize on distinct translation strategies (literal, idiomatic, domain-specific), producing higher-quality and more diverse translation outputs than standard beam search or sampling from a monolithic decoder, without requiring explicit annotation of translation type.
proposals.method: We augment a Transformer-based encoder-decoder translation model by replacing the final decoder feed-forward layers with a sparsely-gated mixture-of-experts module containing K expert sub-networks and a learned gating function conditioned on both source context and partial target prefix. To encourage meaningful specialization without manual labels, we introduce a two-stage training procedure: first, we pre-train the full model with standard cross-entropy on parallel corpora; second, we fine-tune with an added diversity-promoting auxiliary loss that maximizes mutual information between expert assignments and output surface-form clusters derived from unsupervised paraphrase mining. At inference time, we decode by selecting the top-scoring expert per phrase span or by sampling across experts to produce a ranked set of diverse translations.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first structured integration of mixture-of-experts decoding specifically designed to capture the one-to-many mapping in translation, yielding both a practical system that produces diverse high-quality translations on demand and an analytical framework for understanding how translation strategies distribute across experts. We expect measurable gains on diversity-aware metrics such as pairwise-BLEU and oracle-BLEU on WMT benchmarks and domain-adaptation test sets, along with interpretability insights showing that individual experts align with recognizable translation regimes such as formal register, colloquial idiom, and technical terminology.
proposals.novelty: While mixture-of-experts has been applied to scale model capacity in language models, its use as a structured mechanism for decomposing the inherently multi-modal output distribution of translation is unexplored. The combination of unsupervised regime discovery via paraphrase clustering with sparse expert gating removes the need for costly translation-type annotations and provides interpretable specialization that goes beyond what diverse beam search or latent variable models achieve.

proposals.title: Denoising Phrase Autoencoders: Learning Robust Compositional Phrase Representations via Input Corruption and Recovery
proposals.motivation: Modern NLP systems rely heavily on subword tokenization, which fragments meaningful phrases into arbitrary pieces, degrading downstream performance on tasks requiring compositional phrase understanding such as information extraction, semantic similarity, and retrieval. Despite advances in sentence-level and token-level representation learning, there is no principled framework for learning phrase-level embeddings that are invariant to tokenization artifacts, morphological variation, and noisy phrase boundary detection. Given the growing importance of phrase-level semantics in retrieval-augmented generation and structured knowledge extraction, robust phrase representations constitute a timely and impactful research direction.
proposals.hypothesis: Training encoder models with structured denoising objectives—where phrase inputs are systematically corrupted through subword shuffling, morphological perturbation, span deletion, and boundary noise—will yield phrase embeddings that are significantly more robust and semantically coherent than those produced by standard masked language modeling or contrastive learning alone, as measured by improved performance on phrase similarity benchmarks and downstream phrase-dependent tasks.
proposals.method: We propose the Denoising Phrase Autoencoder (DPAE), which operates on extracted phrase spans from large corpora. The model receives corrupted phrase inputs generated by four noise functions: (1) subword-level token dropout and reordering to simulate tokenization variance, (2) morphological perturbation via lemma substitution and affix manipulation, (3) span boundary jitter that shifts phrase start/end positions by one or two tokens, and (4) character-level noise injection mimicking OCR or extraction errors. A Transformer encoder maps the corrupted phrase to a fixed-dimensional embedding, and a lightweight decoder reconstructs the clean canonical phrase form. We further introduce a contrastive alignment term that pulls together embeddings of noisy variants of the same phrase while pushing apart embeddings of distinct phrases, combining reconstruction fidelity with discriminative structure in the learned space.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled denoising framework specifically designed for phrase-level representation learning, filling a gap between token-level and sentence-level methods. The resulting embeddings will provide plug-in improvements for phrase-dependent tasks including entity linking, keyphrase extraction, semantic search, and knowledge base population. We will release a benchmark suite of phrase similarity and robustness evaluations, along with pretrained phrase encoders, to catalyze further research on compositional sub-sentence representations.
proposals.novelty: While denoising autoencoders have been transformative at the token level (BERT) and sentence level (BART, T5), their systematic application to phrase-level representation learning with linguistically motivated corruption functions—spanning tokenization, morphology, and boundary noise simultaneously—is unexplored. The combination of reconstruction and contrastive objectives at the phrase granularity creates a representation space that is both generatively faithful and discriminatively structured, a property not achieved by existing phrase embedding methods.

proposals.title: Minimum Bayes Risk Training of Neural Phrase Scoring Models for End-to-End Translation Quality
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation pipelines typically train subcomponents—phrase scorers, reordering models, language models—by maximizing local conditional likelihoods, yet the true objective is sentence-level translation quality as measured by BLEU or human judgments. This mismatch means that locally optimal phrase scores can produce globally suboptimal translations when composed by the decoder. Statistical decision theory provides a principled remedy: train each component to minimize the expected downstream loss (translation risk) under the full decoding distribution, aligning training directly with evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing maximum-likelihood phrase-score training with a minimum Bayes risk (MBR) objective that accounts for the decoder's search distribution will yield significantly better sentence-level translation quality without requiring architectural changes, because it closes the gap between the local training surrogate and the global evaluation metric.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage approach: (1) a differentiable risk estimator that samples or enumerates high-probability translations from the current decoder, scores them with a smoothed sentence-level loss (e.g., expected BLEU loss), and backpropagates the risk gradient through the phrase scoring network; (2) a control-variate variance-reduction scheme inspired by REINFORCE-style policy gradient methods to make the risk gradient estimator practical at scale. Concretely, we parameterize phrase scores with a feedforward or shallow transformer encoder, decode with beam search augmented by Monte Carlo sampling, compute a cost matrix over the n-best list using smoothed BLEU, and update phrase-score parameters via the resulting expected-loss gradient. We evaluate on WMT benchmarks (En-De, En-Fr, Zh-En) comparing against maximum-likelihood baselines and sequence-level training alternatives such as minimum risk training applied only at the sequence level.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, decision-theoretic training framework that directly targets downstream translation utility at the phrase-scoring level, an underexplored locus of intervention in NMT pipelines. It provides both theoretical grounding—connecting Bayes-optimal scoring to end-to-end translation risk—and practical recipes including variance-reduced gradient estimators. We expect measurable BLEU improvements of 0.5–1.5 points on standard benchmarks and, more importantly, a reusable methodology for applying decision-theoretic training to other modular NLP pipelines where local likelihood and global utility diverge.
proposals.novelty: While minimum risk training has been applied at the full-sequence level, our approach uniquely pushes the decision-theoretic objective down to the phrase-scoring subcomponent, exposing a new optimization surface that sequence-level methods cannot reach. Combining Bayes risk minimization with control-variate gradient estimation at this granularity is, to our knowledge, unexplored and offers complementary gains to existing sequence-level techniques.

proposals.title: Graph-Based Manifold Regularization of Phrase Table Embeddings for Semi-Supervised Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation models learn continuous representations of phrase pairs, yet the rich relational structure among phrases—lexical similarity, syntactic alternation, alignment neighborhoods—remains unexploited as an inductive bias. In low-resource and domain-adaptation settings, labeled parallel data is scarce, making semi-supervised methods that leverage unlabeled structural information especially valuable. Treating the phrase table as a graph and enforcing manifold smoothness over its embeddings offers a principled way to inject this structure without additional parallel corpora.
proposals.hypothesis: Imposing graph Laplacian regularization over a phrase-table graph—where edges encode lexical overlap, syntactic similarity, and alignment co-occurrence—will produce smoother, more generalizable phrase embeddings, yielding significant BLEU improvements in low-resource translation settings compared to unregularized baselines.
proposals.method: We construct a phrase-pair graph from the phrase table by connecting entries that share sub-phrase lexical units, exhibit syntactic category overlap via dependency parses, or co-occur as alignment neighbors in the bitext. Edge weights are computed using a composite kernel over these features. We then add a graph Laplacian regularization term to the encoder-decoder training objective, penalizing large embedding differences between connected phrase nodes. We employ a scalable Graph Neural Network message-passing scheme inspired by spectral graph convolutions to propagate label and embedding information across the graph during training, enabling end-to-end differentiable optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces a formally grounded framework for injecting relational phrase-table structure into neural MT training via manifold regularization, bridging classical graph-based semi-supervised learning with modern sequence-to-sequence architectures. It provides empirical evidence across multiple low-resource language pairs that graph-smoothed phrase embeddings improve translation quality and data efficiency. The approach also yields interpretable phrase neighborhoods that expose the learned translation manifold's geometry.
proposals.novelty: While graph neural networks have been applied to molecular and social-network domains, their use as manifold regularizers over phrase-table topology in machine translation is unexplored. The combination of heterogeneous edge types—lexical, syntactic, and alignment-based—into a single regularization graph that directly shapes the encoder's embedding space is a non-obvious synthesis of structured prediction and semi-supervised learning paradigms.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Guided Active Learning for Human-in-the-Loop Phrase Table Refinement in Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation systems still struggle with rare, ambiguous, or domain-specific phrases where training data is sparse and phrase-table statistics conflict with model confidence. Human post-editing of full translations is expensive and wasteful, since most output segments are already adequate. A targeted triage mechanism that routes only the most uncertain or conflicting phrase pairs to human translators would dramatically improve cost-efficiency while focusing expert effort where it yields the greatest quality gains.
proposals.hypothesis: An active learning framework that selects phrase pairs for human annotation based on a joint uncertainty score—combining neural model predictive entropy with divergence from phrase-table log-probabilities—will achieve superior translation quality per unit of human effort compared to both random sampling and purely model-confidence-based selection strategies.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage triage pipeline integrated into a standard encoder-decoder NMT system. First, for each source phrase in a held-out or streaming corpus, we compute (a) Monte Carlo dropout-based predictive entropy over candidate translations and (b) a conflict score measuring the KL divergence between the neural posterior and the empirical phrase-table distribution. Phrase pairs exceeding a learned threshold on a composite acquisition function are routed to human translators for correction or expansion. Second, the validated annotations are incorporated into both the phrase table and the neural model via constrained fine-tuning with lexical-coverage rewards, following curriculum learning principles to prevent catastrophic forgetting. We evaluate on WMT benchmarks across high- and low-resource language pairs, measuring BLEU and COMET improvements per number of human annotations requested.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces a principled active learning acquisition function that unifies neural uncertainty and symbolic phrase-table statistics for human-in-the-loop machine translation. It provides the first systematic empirical study showing that disagreement between neural and statistical translation components is a strong signal for identifying high-value annotation targets. The resulting framework offers a practical, deployable triage system that reduces human annotation costs by an estimated 60-70% while matching or exceeding full post-editing quality.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior active learning approaches for NMT that rely solely on model uncertainty, our method exploits the tension between neural and symbolic translation knowledge sources as a novel acquisition signal. This cross-paradigm disagreement measure is non-obvious because it treats the legacy phrase table not as an obsolete component but as a complementary oracle whose divergence from the neural model reveals genuinely ambiguous or undertrained regions of the translation space.

proposals.title: Compact Neural Phrase Tables via Product Quantization of Learned Phrase Embeddings
proposals.motivation: Neural phrase-based translation and retrieval-augmented generation systems store large inventories of phrase embeddings that consume tens of gigabytes of memory, limiting deployment on commodity hardware and constraining the scale of candidate inventories. Compression techniques such as product quantization have proven effective in billion-scale nearest-neighbor search but have not been systematically adapted to the unique distributional properties of learned phrase representations in sequence-to-sequence models. Reducing the memory footprint of neural phrase tables without degrading translation or retrieval quality would democratize access to these powerful models and enable scaling to much larger phrase inventories under fixed memory budgets.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying optimized product quantization with end-to-end codebook learning to phrase embeddings and decoder hidden states will achieve 16-32x compression ratios while preserving at least 95% of downstream BLEU score and retrieval recall, because phrase embeddings occupy lower-dimensional manifolds than their ambient dimensionality suggests and their task-relevant structure can be captured by learned discrete codes.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage approach: first, we train a standard neural phrase table that encodes source-target phrase pairs into dense embeddings using a transformer encoder; second, we apply residual product quantization (RPQ) with jointly learned codebooks, where the quantization objective is co-trained with a distillation loss that preserves the original model's ranking of phrase candidates. We will compare RPQ against scalar quantization, locality-sensitive hashing, and knowledge distillation baselines across WMT translation benchmarks and open-domain question answering phrase retrieval tasks. We further introduce an asymmetric distance computation scheme where queries remain in full precision while the phrase table is quantized, balancing search accuracy against memory savings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of compression methods tailored to neural phrase tables, establishing Pareto frontiers between memory usage and task performance across multiple NLP benchmarks. We will release an open-source toolkit for compressing and serving phrase-level retrieval indices, lowering the barrier to deploying retrieval-augmented models. The findings will also yield insights into the intrinsic dimensionality and redundancy structure of learned phrase representations, informing future model design.
proposals.novelty: While product quantization is well studied for image retrieval and general vector search, its application to phrase-level NLP representations introduces unique challenges—phrase embeddings exhibit compositional semantic structure and variable-length source spans that standard codebook learning does not account for. Our end-to-end codebook distillation approach, which optimizes quantization codes directly against downstream task loss rather than reconstruction error alone, is a non-obvious departure from the standard compress-then-evaluate pipeline.

proposals.title: A*-Guided Retrosynthetic Decoding: Transferring Structured Search from Speech Recognition to Molecular Synthesis Planning
proposals.motivation: Retrosynthetic planning remains computationally prohibitive for large-scale virtual screening because exhaustive tree search over reaction templates scales poorly with target complexity. Speech recognition faced an analogous combinatorial explosion in decoding utterances from acoustic models and solved it decades ago with A* and beam-search algorithms that provide tunable cost-quality tradeoffs. Adapting these well-understood structured search methods to retrosynthesis could unlock full multi-step route generation at a fraction of current computational cost, directly addressing the scalability bottleneck identified in fast synthesizability estimation approaches like RetroGNN.
proposals.hypothesis: A* search with a learned retrosynthetic heuristic and diversity-promoting beam pruning can generate complete multi-step synthesis routes that match or exceed the quality of exhaustive retrosynthesis solvers while reducing wall-clock computation by an order of magnitude, and the beam width parameter provides a principled knob for trading off route diversity against search cost.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component system: (1) a graph neural network trained on successful retrosynthetic trajectories to serve as the A* heuristic function, estimating the remaining cost-to-go from any intermediate molecular state to commercially available building blocks; and (2) a modified beam-search decoder that maintains k candidate partial routes at each expansion step, using stochastic beam pruning inspired by diverse beam search in speech and NLP to prevent mode collapse onto a single synthesis pathway. The heuristic network is trained via self-play on routes extracted from the USPTO reaction dataset, and the full system is benchmarked against MCTS-based planners and RetroGNN-filtered exhaustive search on standard synthesizability benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes a formal bridge between structured decoding in sequence models and retrosynthetic tree search, providing the first systematic adaptation of A* and beam-search with learned heuristics to multi-step synthesis planning. The controllable diversity-cost tradeoff enables practitioners to request either a single cheapest route or a diverse portfolio of synthetic alternatives, a capability absent from current retrosynthesis tools. We expect this to make full route enumeration practical within the fast screening pipelines where only binary synthesizability scores are currently feasible.
proposals.novelty: While beam search has been used in single-step reaction prediction, no prior work has combined A* search with a GNN-learned heuristic specifically designed for multi-step retrosynthetic tree expansion, nor has the diversity-promoting beam framework from speech decoding been transferred to control the exploration-exploitation tradeoff in chemical space. The key non-obvious insight is that the admissibility guarantees of A* can be approximately preserved by the GNN heuristic through calibration on reaction feasibility scores, yielding near-optimal routes without exhaustive search.

proposals.title: Phase Transitions in Neural Machine Translation: Characterizing the Shift from Phrase-Level Decomposition to End-to-End Sequence Transduction
proposals.motivation: Encoder-decoder models with attention were originally validated on short phrases and then applied to full sentences, yet the field lacks a systematic characterization of when and why phrase-level decomposition stops being beneficial relative to end-to-end sequence transduction. Understanding this transition is critical for designing more efficient translation systems, particularly for low-resource languages where data scarcity may favor compositional phrase-level strategies. With the continued scaling of sequence-to-sequence models, identifying the precise regimes where monolithic end-to-end models surpass modular phrase-based pipelines has direct implications for architecture selection and training methodology.
proposals.hypothesis: There exists a critical sentence-length and training-data threshold beyond which end-to-end encoder-decoder models with attention decisively outperform phrase-decomposed translation pipelines, and this transition exhibits phase-transition-like behavior characterized by a sharp, predictable inflection in BLEU score as a function of sequence length and corpus size.
proposals.method: We will construct controlled experiments across six language pairs at varying resource levels, training both phrase-decomposed models (where sentences are segmented into noun, verb, and prepositional phrases translated independently then reassembled) and end-to-end attention-based encoder-decoder models on identical data. We systematically vary sentence length (5 to 80 tokens) and training corpus size (10K to 10M sentence pairs), measuring BLEU, chrF, and a novel compositional adequacy metric that scores preservation of cross-phrase dependencies such as agreement and anaphora. We apply statistical mechanics-inspired analysis to the performance curves, fitting sigmoidal transition functions to identify critical thresholds and compute scaling exponents that quantify the sharpness of the crossover.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first rigorous empirical characterization of the phrase-to-sentence scaling transition in neural machine translation, yielding actionable guidelines for when practitioners should prefer modular phrase-based architectures versus end-to-end models. The identified scaling laws and critical thresholds will inform efficient architecture selection, especially in low-resource settings where phrase decomposition may retain advantages. Additionally, the compositional adequacy metric we introduce offers a new evaluation dimension that captures cross-phrase linguistic coherence beyond surface-level n-gram overlap.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared phrase-based and neural approaches as competing paradigms, no study has systematically mapped the continuous transition between them as a function of sequence length and data scale, nor applied phase-transition analysis to identify sharp crossover thresholds. The framing of this transition as a scaling phenomenon with quantifiable critical exponents offers a new theoretical lens connecting statistical physics to neural sequence modeling.

proposals.title: Beyond Words: Subword and Character-Level Encoder-Decoder Models for Open-Vocabulary Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation systems that operate at the word level suffer from fixed vocabularies, poor handling of rare and morphologically rich words, and complete failure on out-of-vocabulary tokens. These limitations are especially severe for agglutinative and low-resource languages where productive morphology generates a combinatorial explosion of surface forms. A principled investigation of sub-word granularity in encoder-decoder architectures is urgently needed to make neural translation robust, open-vocabulary, and linguistically inclusive.
proposals.hypothesis: An encoder-decoder architecture operating on subword units derived from byte-pair encoding or character n-grams will match or exceed word-level translation quality on standard benchmarks while dramatically reducing unknown-token rates, and a purely character-level variant will further generalize to handle misspellings and novel morphological forms without any explicit segmentation.
proposals.method: We propose a systematic comparison of three granularity levels within the RNN encoder-decoder framework: word-level (baseline), subword-level using byte-pair encoding (BPE) with varying merge counts, and character-level with hierarchical encoding that first builds word-like representations from character sequences before feeding them to the sentence-level encoder. Each model will be trained on WMT English-German, English-French, and English-Turkish pairs, with Turkish providing a stress test for morphological richness. We will introduce controlled noise (typos, case variation) at test time to evaluate robustness, and perform ablations on BPE vocabulary size, character-level receptive field, and the effect of explicit morphological supervision as an auxiliary loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish clear empirical guidelines for choosing the unit of translation in neural encoder-decoder models, quantifying the trade-off between computational cost, translation quality, and vocabulary coverage across typologically diverse language pairs. It will demonstrate that sub-word models can be both more compact and more robust than word-level systems, opening neural machine translation to morphologically complex and low-resource languages. The resulting open-source models and segmentation analysis will serve as a foundation for future work on linguistically informed neural architectures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored character-level language modeling, no systematic study has compared word, subword, and character granularities within a single encoder-decoder translation framework across typologically diverse languages with controlled robustness tests. The combination of hierarchical character encoding with BPE ablations and morphological auxiliary losses offers a principled bridge between purely data-driven segmentation and linguistic structure that goes beyond simple vocabulary tricks.

proposals.title: Document-Context Encoder-Decoders: Scaling Learned Phrase Representations to Discourse-Level Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Current neural machine translation systems operate primarily at the sentence level, discarding inter-sentential cues such as pronominal anaphora, lexical cohesion, and topical consistency that human translators rely on. As encoder-decoder architectures have matured, extending their input span to full documents represents a natural but underexplored scaling axis. Understanding whether phrase-level representations remain useful—or must be fundamentally restructured—when embedded in document-level context is critical for building translation systems that produce coherent, human-quality discourse.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a standard encoder-decoder translation model with a hierarchical document-context mechanism will improve translation of discourse-dependent phenomena (pronoun resolution, lexical consistency, discourse connectives) without degrading local phrase-level translation quality, demonstrating that learned phrase representations compose productively with longer-range contextual signals rather than being supplanted by them.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture in which a sentence-level encoder first produces phrase-grounded representations, and a document-level encoder then contextualizes these representations using sparse cross-sentence attention over a sliding window of surrounding sentences. We will train on large-scale document-aligned parallel corpora (e.g., WMT document-level datasets, OpenSubtitles, TED talks) and evaluate on targeted test suites for anaphora resolution, lexical cohesion, and discourse connective translation, alongside standard BLEU and COMET metrics. Controlled ablations will isolate the contribution of document context at varying span lengths (2, 5, 10, and full-document sentences) to characterize the scaling behavior of the phrase representation space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic characterization of how learned phrase-level representations behave as input context scales from sentence to document level, identifying whether they gracefully absorb discourse signals or require architectural restructuring. The proposed hierarchical sparse-attention mechanism offers a practical, memory-efficient approach to document-level translation that can be integrated into existing encoder-decoder pipelines. The targeted evaluation methodology and ablation framework will serve as a benchmark protocol for future document-level translation research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior document-level NMT work that focuses on appending context as a flat auxiliary input, our approach explicitly preserves and probes the phrase-level representation space under scaling, treating the interaction between local and global representations as the primary object of study rather than a byproduct. The systematic span-length ablation design directly tests the compositionality of phrase representations, a question with implications beyond translation for any encoder-decoder system operating over structured language.

proposals.title: Open-World Phrase Pair Generation via Conditional Language Models for Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Current neural rescoring methods for phrase-based translation are limited to selecting among candidates already extracted by heuristic alignment, leaving significant coverage gaps for rare, compositional, and domain-specific expressions. Bridging this gap matters now because hybrid phrase-neural systems remain competitive in low-resource and domain-adapted settings, yet their phrase tables are inherently incomplete. An open-ended generation mechanism would allow the decoder to access translation options that no extraction heuristic could surface, directly improving recall and translation quality.
proposals.hypothesis: A conditional language model trained to generate target-language phrases given a source phrase and local context can produce novel, high-quality phrase pair candidates that, when dynamically inserted into the decoder's search, yield statistically significant improvements in BLEU and coverage over closed-set rescoring alone.
proposals.method: We train a sequence-to-sequence model with a variational latent variable to map source phrases and their sentential context to diverse target phrase candidates, using the existing phrase table as supervised signal and back-translated monolingual data for augmentation. At decode time, the generator is called on-the-fly for source spans whose top-k table entries fall below a confidence threshold; generated candidates are scored by a discriminator that estimates log-probability ratios against the phrase table distribution, and surviving candidates are merged into the beam. We evaluate on WMT low-resource pairs and a specialized biomedical domain where phrase table sparsity is acute.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled generative complement to closed-world phrase rescoring that unifies the strengths of phrase-based and fully neural translation, (2) a confidence-gated calling strategy that limits computational overhead to phrases where the table is demonstrably sparse, and (3) empirical evidence across low-resource and domain-specific benchmarks showing that open-world generation recovers translations inaccessible to extraction heuristics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats the phrase table as a fixed, immutable resource that neural models can only reweight, we invert the paradigm by treating the neural model as a phrase table author, generating candidates that never existed in the extraction pipeline. The variational formulation coupled with discriminative filtering is non-obvious because it must balance diversity of generation against precision of insertion without destabilizing beam search.

proposals.title: Neural Phrase Memory: Compressing Massive Phrase Tables into Continuous Representations for Scalable Statistical Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Phrase-based statistical machine translation systems rely on enormous phrase tables that grow superlinearly with training data, creating severe memory bottlenecks that limit deployment on resource-constrained hardware and hinder scaling to many language pairs. While neural machine translation has largely supplanted phrase-based SMT, hybrid systems and low-resource scenarios still benefit from explicit phrasal knowledge, yet the storage overhead remains prohibitive. A method that compresses symbolic phrase inventories into compact, queryable neural memories would unlock the coverage advantages of large phrase tables without their memory cost.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuous neural memory module trained via an encoder-decoder architecture with locality-sensitive hashing for fast retrieval can losslessly compress phrase tables by two orders of magnitude while preserving translation quality, because the distributional regularities among phrase pairs create a highly compressible latent manifold that explicit symbolic storage fails to exploit.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-encoder architecture in which source and target phrases are mapped to a shared low-dimensional continuous space using character-aware convolutional encoders, with a auxiliary decoder that reconstructs target phrases from their embeddings conditioned on source context. The neural memory is organized as a product-quantized vector store with learned codebooks, enabling sub-linear nearest-neighbor retrieval at inference time. Training uses a multi-task objective combining reconstruction loss, contrastive translation-pair alignment loss, and a knowledge distillation signal from the original phrase-table probabilities, optimized end-to-end on standard parallel corpora.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic framework for replacing explicit phrase tables with a compact neural memory that is orders of magnitude smaller yet maintains comparable BLEU scores across multiple language pairs. It establishes theoretical and empirical compression bounds relating phrase-table entropy to minimum embedding dimensionality, offering principled guidance for practitioners. The approach also opens a path toward hybrid neural-symbolic translation systems that combine the interpretability and coverage of phrase-based methods with the efficiency of neural representations.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on neural network joint models or simple phrase-table pruning heuristics, our approach treats the entire phrase table as a compressible information source and learns a structured continuous memory with guaranteed retrieval fidelity, bridging information-theoretic compression principles with representation learning in a way that has not been explored for translation memories.

proposals.title: Continual Phrase Representation Learning: Online Adaptation of Phrase Scoring Models via Streaming Domain Data and User Feedback
proposals.motivation: Large-scale phrase scoring models are typically trained on static corpora and deployed with frozen parameters, creating a fundamental mismatch as language use, domain terminology, and user needs evolve over time. This rigidity leads to degraded performance on emerging topics and prevents incorporation of valuable user corrections, a limitation that becomes increasingly costly as these models are deployed in dynamic real-world settings such as scientific literature search and clinical text mining. Developing online adaptation methods for phrase representations addresses a critical gap between static pre-training paradigms and the non-stationary nature of real-world language data.
proposals.hypothesis: Phrase representation models equipped with a continual learning objective that combines experience replay from a compact episodic memory with online gradient updates from streaming data can maintain or improve phrase scoring accuracy on new domains while avoiding catastrophic forgetting of previously learned phrase semantics, outperforming both periodic full retraining and naive fine-tuning baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-memory online adaptation framework where a fixed-capacity episodic buffer stores representative phrase-context pairs from past training via reservoir sampling, while a working memory module processes incoming streaming data and user correction signals. At each adaptation step, the model performs a joint gradient update combining a contrastive phrase scoring loss on the new data stream with a knowledge distillation regularizer anchored to replay samples from the episodic buffer. We employ a modular architecture with adapter layers inserted into a pre-trained transformer encoder, so that only lightweight parameters are updated online while the base representations remain stable, and we introduce a novelty-triggered adaptation schedule that activates updates only when distributional shift is detected via an online estimate of KL divergence between incoming and stored phrase distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic framework for continual online adaptation of phrase scoring models, demonstrating that streaming updates with episodic replay can match or exceed full retraining at a fraction of the computational cost. It establishes empirical benchmarks for catastrophic forgetting in phrase representation models across multiple domain-shift scenarios including biomedical, legal, and scientific text streams. The proposed novelty-triggered scheduling mechanism offers a principled and compute-efficient strategy for deciding when adaptation is necessary, applicable beyond phrase scoring to other representation learning systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior continual learning work focused on classification tasks, this approach addresses the unique challenges of dense phrase-level representation spaces where forgetting manifests as subtle semantic drift rather than discrete class confusion. The combination of distributional shift detection with selective adapter-based updates provides a non-obvious solution that avoids both the instability of unconstrained online learning and the inefficiency of periodic full retraining.

proposals.title: Inductive Biases for Neural Phrase Table Learning in Low-Resource and Specialized Translation Domains
proposals.motivation: State-of-the-art neural machine translation models rely on massive parallel corpora, yet the majority of the world's language pairs and specialized domains (medical, legal, scientific) operate under severe data scarcity. Understanding which architectural and regularization inductive biases enable reliable phrase table learning from small, noisy, or imbalanced bilingual data is critical for democratizing translation technology and ensuring safe, accurate deployment in high-stakes specialized settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicit structural inductive biases—specifically, graph-based phrase alignment constraints and curriculum-driven data balancing—can compensate for small and noisy phrase tables, achieving translation quality competitive with large-corpus baselines while requiring an order of magnitude less parallel data in specialized domains.
proposals.method: We propose a graph neural network-augmented phrase table encoder that models phrase-level alignment structure explicitly, drawing on benchmarking insights from graph neural network architectures. The model is trained with a three-stage curriculum: first on synthetic back-translated data to bootstrap coverage, then on filtered domain-specific parallel sentences scored by a learned noise classifier, and finally fine-tuned with a class-balanced loss that up-weights rare phrase pairs. We evaluate across four low-resource settings (Nepali-English, Sinhala-English, medical English-French, patent English-German) and systematically ablate each inductive bias to quantify its individual contribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic empirical characterization of which inductive biases matter most when phrase tables are tiny, noisy, or domain-skewed, yielding actionable guidelines for practitioners building translation systems under resource constraints. The proposed GNN-augmented phrase encoder offers a reusable architectural component, and our ablation framework establishes a reproducible benchmark for low-resource neural phrase table research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has scaled neural translation up, the interaction between graph-structured phrase representations and curriculum-based noise robustness in the low-resource regime is unexplored. Our approach is non-obvious because it reframes phrase table quality as a graph learning problem rather than a purely sequence-to-sequence challenge, leveraging structural priors that only become critical when data is scarce.

proposals.title: From Translation to Rewriting: Scaling Sequence-to-Sequence Architectures for Monolingual Paraphrase and Style Transfer via Dual Formulation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation architectures have achieved remarkable success by learning generic sequence transduction mappings, yet their full potential for monolingual tasks remains underexplored. Paraphrase generation and style-preserving rewriting are critical for data augmentation, text simplification, and controllable generation, but current approaches treat them as separate problems from translation rather than as a natural dual. Unifying bilingual translation and monolingual rewriting under a single transduction framework would enable direct transfer of scaling laws, training recipes, and architectural advances from the mature translation literature to these high-impact monolingual tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that encoder-decoder and sequence-to-sequence architectures trained on bilingual translation can be systematically repurposed for monolingual paraphrase generation and style transfer by treating the source and target as different surface realizations within the same language, and that scaling laws governing translation quality as a function of model size, data volume, and compute transfer predictably to this monolingual dual, yielding state-of-the-art rewriting performance without task-specific architectural modifications.
proposals.method: We will train Transformer-based sequence-to-sequence models of varying scale (125M to 3B parameters) on large-scale paraphrase corpora constructed from bilingual pivoting, back-translation, and naturally occurring paraphrase pairs, treating monolingual rewriting as a degenerate case of translation where source and target languages coincide. We will systematically measure scaling behavior across model size, dataset size, and compute budget, fitting power-law curves analogous to those established for bilingual translation. To disentangle content preservation from surface variation, we introduce a dual-objective training procedure combining a reconstruction loss with a latent-space divergence penalty that encourages diverse surface forms while anchoring semantic equivalence, leveraging insights from denoising autoencoders and variational sequence models.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes the first comprehensive scaling analysis for monolingual sequence transduction, demonstrating that paraphrase generation and style transfer obey predictable scaling laws analogous to those in bilingual translation. It provides a unified framework and set of training recipes that allow practitioners to directly leverage translation-scale infrastructure for rewriting tasks, eliminating the need for bespoke architectures. The resulting models are expected to achieve state-of-the-art paraphrase quality across multiple benchmarks while offering fine-grained control over the diversity-fidelity tradeoff.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has applied translation architectures to paraphrase generation in an ad hoc manner, no study has formally characterized the duality between bilingual and monolingual transduction or measured whether translation scaling laws transfer to the monolingual setting. Our dual-objective framework that jointly optimizes for semantic preservation and surface divergence within a single encoder-decoder is a principled departure from pipeline approaches that rely on separate paraphrase filtering stages.

proposals.title: Cooperative Adversarial Networks: Replacing Zero-Sum Dynamics with Negotiated Gradient Teaching in Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Generative Adversarial Networks suffer from well-documented training pathologies—mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and oscillatory non-convergence—that stem directly from their strict zero-sum formulation. Recent work on gradient penalty methods and spectral normalization treats symptoms rather than the root cause: the assumption that the discriminator's sole objective must be to maximally oppose the generator. Given Bengio's longstanding interest in training dynamics, credit assignment, and biologically motivated learning, revisiting this foundational adversarial assumption is both timely and aligned with growing evidence that cooperative multi-agent dynamics yield more stable equilibria.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the pure adversarial objective with a partially cooperative game—where the discriminator is jointly incentivized to classify real from fake and to provide maximally informative gradient signals to the generator—will yield a training equilibrium that is more stable, converges faster, and produces higher-quality samples than standard zero-sum GANs, without requiring ad hoc stabilization tricks.
proposals.method: We introduce Cooperative Adversarial Networks (CoopAN), where the discriminator's loss includes a novel teaching term that rewards it for producing gradients with high Fisher information with respect to the generator's parameters, effectively turning the discriminator into a negotiator rather than a pure adversary. Concretely, we augment the standard minimax objective with a regularizer that maximizes the alignment between the discriminator's gradient signal and the direction of steepest improvement in a sample quality metric (e.g., FID-proxy or a learned perceptual score). We analyze the resulting game-theoretic equilibrium using tools from cooperative game theory (Shapley values for credit attribution between generator and discriminator updates) and validate empirically on CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, and LSUN, comparing against WGAN-GP, SN-GAN, and StyleGAN2 baselines across convergence speed, mode coverage, and sample fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled reformulation of the GAN training objective grounded in cooperative game theory, providing both theoretical analysis of when partially cooperative equilibria dominate adversarial ones and practical algorithms that are drop-in replacements for standard GAN training loops. We expect to demonstrate that cooperative dynamics reduce mode collapse without sacrificing sample sharpness, offering the field a new design axis—cooperation degree—that interpolates between fully adversarial and fully cooperative training. The framework also opens connections to Bengio's broader research agenda on credit assignment and biologically plausible learning, where pure competition is rarely observed.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modified GAN losses to stabilize training, no existing approach explicitly reframes the discriminator as a cooperative teacher whose objective includes maximizing the informativeness of its gradient signal to the generator. The use of Fisher information alignment as a cooperation mechanism and the formal analysis via cooperative game theory equilibria are, to our knowledge, entirely new to the generative modeling literature.

proposals.title: Multi-Critic Adversarial Networks: Decomposing Discrimination into Specialist Modules for Improved Training Stability and Mode Coverage
proposals.motivation: Standard GANs rely on a single discriminator to simultaneously assess realism, diversity, semantic consistency, and adherence to constraints—objectives that can produce conflicting gradient signals and exacerbate mode collapse. As generative models are deployed in safety-critical domains such as medical imaging and drug design, the inability of a monolithic critic to provide fine-grained, decomposed feedback becomes a practical bottleneck.
