Developing architecturally principled multi-critic frameworks could unlock more stable training and controllable generation, aligning with broader goals of building AI systems whose internal judgments are interpretable and auditable.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing a single discriminator with a panel of specialist critics—each responsible for a distinct evaluative dimension (e.g., perceptual fidelity, distributional coverage, semantic coherence, constraint satisfaction)—will reduce conflicting gradient signals, improve mode coverage, and yield generators that better satisfy composite quality criteria compared to single-discriminator baselines.
proposals.method: We propose Multi-Critic Adversarial Networks (MCAN), where K specialist discriminators each receive task-specific architectural inductions and loss functions: a fidelity critic using a pre-trained perceptual feature backbone, a diversity critic employing a kernel-based coverage estimator over generated batches, a consistency critic operating on structured attribute predictions, and a safety/constraint critic enforcing domain-specific rules via differentiable constraint layers. Generator gradients are aggregated through a learned convex combination module trained with a meta-objective that minimizes worst-case critic regret, drawing on multi-objective optimization principles. We will evaluate on image synthesis (ImageNet, CelebA-HQ), graph-structured molecular generation leveraging RetroGNN-style synthesizability scoring as a constraint critic, and a safety-filtered text generation task, benchmarking against StyleGAN2, ProjectedGAN, and multi-discriminator heuristic baselines using FID, recall, constraint-violation rate, and a new Critic Agreement Score.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled architectural framework that decomposes adversarial criticism into interpretable specialist modules with theoretical grounding in multi-objective game equilibria, (2) empirical evidence across vision, molecular, and language domains showing that specialist critics reduce mode collapse and constraint violations without sacrificing sample quality, and (3) a meta-gradient aggregation mechanism that adaptively balances competing critic objectives, offering a practical tool for practitioners who need controllable, safe generative systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored multi-discriminator GANs as an engineering heuristic, no existing framework systematically decomposes discriminator responsibility along semantically meaningful evaluative axes with a principled gradient aggregation strategy. Our approach is non-obvious because naively adding critics risks destabilizing training through gradient conflict; the key insight is that pairing dimensional decomposition with multi-objective meta-balancing converts a harder game into a set of simpler, cooperative sub-games.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop Adaptive Scheduling for Alternating Optimization in Adversarial and Multi-Objective Training
proposals.motivation: Alternating optimization underpins training of GANs, actor-critic architectures, and multi-agent systems, yet practitioners universally default to fixed one-for-one or hand-tuned k-step schedules with no principled justification. This rigidity is a known source of mode collapse, oscillatory loss dynamics, and catastrophic forgetting in adversarial settings. Given Bengio's recent emphasis on AI safety and catastrophic risk, unstable training dynamics in powerful generative and adversarial models represent a concrete, under-addressed failure mode that closed-loop control could mitigate.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned scheduling policy that monitors real-time training diagnostics—gradient variance ratios, spectral norms of Jacobians, and loss curvature estimates—and dynamically decides the number and timing of each player's updates will consistently outperform fixed alternating schedules in convergence speed, final equilibrium quality, and training stability across adversarial and cooperative multi-objective settings.
proposals.method: We formulate the scheduling decision as a contextual bandit problem where the state consists of lightweight training statistics (per-player gradient signal-to-noise ratio, moving-window loss Hessian trace estimates, and discriminator-generator loss ratio) and the action space is a discrete set of update allocations per outer step. The scheduler is trained via a meta-learning outer loop using Proximal Policy Optimization on a family of GAN and actor-critic tasks spanning image generation (StyleGAN2 on FFHQ), graph generation (benchmarking against GNN baselines), and reinforcement learning (MuJoCo continuous control). We compare against fixed 1:1, 5:1, and TTUR baselines, as well as recent heuristic adaptive methods, measuring FID/IS for generative tasks and cumulative reward for RL, alongside direct stability metrics such as gradient norm divergence events and number of restarts required.
proposals.contribution: This work challenges the deep-seated assumption that alternating optimization schedules should be fixed a priori, providing both theoretical grounding (via connections to dynamical systems stability analysis of two-player games) and a practical, drop-in scheduler module. The resulting framework is expected to reduce training instability events by a significant margin and improve final model quality, with direct implications for safer training of large adversarial systems where uncontrolled dynamics pose alignment risks.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive learning rates are well-studied, adaptive scheduling of which player updates and how often remains virtually unexplored as a learnable control problem. Framing alternating optimization as a closed-loop control system—rather than an open-loop fixed protocol—is a conceptual shift that unifies disparate heuristics under a principled meta-learning framework, connecting game-theoretic training dynamics to modern control theory in a way not previously formalized.

proposals.title: Beyond Realism and Diversity: Task-Aligned Generative Adversarial Objectives for Valid, Fair, and Causally Faithful Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks are overwhelmingly evaluated on perceptual realism (FID, IS) and mode coverage, yet high-stakes applications in drug design, clinical data augmentation, and algorithmic fairness demand that generated samples satisfy domain constraints such as chemical validity, demographic parity, or counterfactual consistency. Current training objectives provide no mechanism to enforce these requirements, leading to realistic-looking but practically useless or even harmful synthetic data. A principled framework that replaces or augments the standard adversarial loss with task-aligned objectives is therefore urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the monolithic realism-diversity objective with a modular, multi-criteria adversarial framework—where discriminator heads separately assess validity, fairness, and counterfactual faithfulness—will yield generators whose outputs are significantly more useful for downstream decision-making tasks without catastrophic loss in sample quality, and that the relative weighting of these heads can be derived from a formal connection to causal interventional distributions.
proposals.method: We propose Multi-Criteria GAN (MC-GAN), which augments the standard discriminator with auxiliary heads each trained on a distinct desideratum: (1) a constraint-satisfaction head that scores domain validity via differentiable constraint checkers (e.g., molecular valency rules, physiological plausibility bounds), (2) a fairness head that penalizes distributional disparity across protected attributes using a differentiable demographic parity or equalized-odds surrogate, and (3) a counterfactual-faithfulness head grounded in structural causal models that enforces consistency under do-interventions. Training proceeds via a Pareto-optimal multi-objective gradient descent scheme so that no single criterion dominates. We evaluate on three benchmarks: molecular generation (ZINC), clinical tabular synthesis (MIMIC-III), and face image generation under fairness constraints (CelebA), comparing against standard GAN, FairGAN, and constrained-optimization baselines using both traditional metrics and new task-aligned metrics we introduce.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a formal argument, grounded in causal inference, for why realism and diversity are insufficient proxy objectives for useful generation, (2) the MC-GAN architecture and its Pareto multi-objective training algorithm as a practical instantiation of task-aligned generation, and (3) comprehensive empirical evidence across molecular, clinical, and vision domains showing that task-aligned generators substantially outperform realism-optimized baselines on downstream utility while maintaining competitive sample quality. Together, these contributions establish a new evaluation paradigm for generative models in safety-critical and fairness-sensitive applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has individually addressed constrained generation or fair generation, no existing framework unifies validity, fairness, and causal faithfulness under a single multi-objective adversarial formulation with formal causal grounding. The key non-obvious insight is that these seemingly disparate desiderata can be expressed as properties of interventional distributions in a structural causal model, enabling a theoretically motivated weighting scheme rather than ad-hoc hyperparameter tuning.

proposals.title: Federated Adversarial Networks: Convergence Guarantees and Privacy-Utility Tradeoffs for Decentralized GAN Training Under Data Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks have achieved remarkable results in image synthesis, drug discovery, and scientific simulation, but nearly all theoretical and practical advances assume centrally pooled training data. In reality, medical records across hospitals, proprietary chemical libraries across pharmaceutical companies, and user data across jurisdictions cannot be freely shared due to privacy regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, and institutional data ownership constraints. Developing principled decentralized GAN training methods is therefore critical for unlocking generative modeling in the domains where it is most needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a federated adversarial training framework, in which distributed discriminators communicate only gradient statistics processed through a calibrated differential privacy mechanism to a shared generator, can achieve sample quality within a bounded degradation factor of centralized GAN training while providing formal (epsilon, delta)-differential privacy guarantees, even under significant non-IID data partitions across participating sites.
proposals.method: We propose Federated Adversarial Networks (FAN), a framework where each participating site maintains a local discriminator trained on private data, while a central generator receives only differentially private aggregated gradient updates via a secure aggregation protocol. We introduce a novel gradient alignment regularizer that mitigates mode collapse arising from heterogeneous local data distributions by penalizing divergence among local discriminator gradient directions before aggregation. We provide convergence analysis under non-IID data partitions by extending the theory of two-player games to the multi-discriminator federated setting, deriving explicit bounds on the privacy-utility-communication tradeoff as a function of the number of sites, local data heterogeneity measured via distribution skew metrics, and the privacy budget epsilon.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first unified theoretical framework connecting federated learning, differential privacy, and adversarial training with formal convergence guarantees under realistic data heterogeneity assumptions. The gradient alignment regularizer offers a practical and principled solution to the mode collapse problem that plagues naive federated GAN approaches, validated on federated medical imaging benchmarks across simulated hospital partitions. The derived privacy-utility-communication tradeoff bounds give practitioners actionable guidance for deploying generative models in regulated, multi-institutional settings.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior federated GAN work that treats privacy and data heterogeneity as separate engineering challenges, our approach jointly analyzes them within a game-theoretic convergence framework, revealing that non-IID partitions and differential privacy noise interact non-additively, a phenomenon we term privacy-heterogeneity coupling, which necessitates the co-designed gradient alignment mechanism rather than naive composition of existing federated learning and privacy techniques.

proposals.title: Decentralized Adversarial Learning for Generative Modeling of Molecular and Medical Data
proposals.motivation: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are powerful for creating synthetic datasets, crucial for domains like drug discovery (molecules) and medical imaging (colonoscopy). However, the centralized data assumption is untenable due to privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA) and proprietary constraints, which prevent pooling sensitive patient or molecular data. This limits the application of GANs in high-impact, privacy-sensitive fields.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decentralized adversarial learning framework, where neither raw data nor explicit labels are shared, can train a global generative model that matches the performance of a centrally trained model while preserving data privacy and ownership.
proposals.method: We will develop a federated GAN architecture where client nodes (e.g., hospitals, labs) train local generators and discriminators on their private data (e.g., colonoscopy images, molecular graphs). The global model will be a generator updated via federated averaging of client generator parameters, while discriminators remain local to enforce data-specific realism. We will incorporate differential privacy in gradient updates and explore latent space alignment techniques to handle non-IID data distributions across clients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for privacy-preserving generative model training, expanding the applicability of GANs to regulated industries. It will contribute new benchmarks for decentralized adversarial learning on medical imaging and molecular datasets, advancing the field of federated learning beyond discriminative tasks.
proposals.novelty: While federated learning exists for classification, its application to adversarial generative modeling under strict non-sharing of raw data or labels is nascent. The novel integration of latent space alignment for heterogeneous, unlabeled client data (e.g., normal mucosa images from different colonoscopy protocols) addresses a key challenge in decentralized learning.

proposals.title: Mechanism-First Adversarial Networks: Letting GANs Learn Only What Physics Cannot Predict
proposals.motivation: Modern generative adversarial networks learn entire data distributions from scratch, wasting capacity on rediscovering structure that domain science already encodes precisely. This is especially wasteful in scientific domains such as molecular dynamics, climate modeling, and neuroscience, where well-validated mechanistic models exist but leave quantifiable residual uncertainty. A framework that hard-codes known mechanisms into the generator architecture and restricts adversarial learning to the residual gap would yield more sample-efficient, interpretable, and physically consistent generative models.
proposals.hypothesis: A GAN whose generator is decomposed into a fixed, non-trainable mechanistic simulator and a lightweight trainable residual network will match or exceed the distributional fidelity of a purely data-driven GAN while requiring significantly fewer training samples and producing outputs that provably satisfy known physical constraints.
proposals.method: We propose Mechanism-First Adversarial Networks (MFANs), where the generator is split into two stages: (1) a differentiable but frozen simulator implementing known governing equations (e.g., partial differential equations for fluid flow or Hodgkin-Huxley dynamics for neural signals), and (2) a small conditional residual network that receives the simulator output and learns to correct systematic biases and fill in unmodeled stochastic variation. The discriminator operates on the combined output versus real observations. We enforce mechanistic consistency via a spectral penalty that constrains the residual network's Lipschitz constant relative to the simulator's output magnitude, ensuring the learned corrections remain subordinate to the known physics. We validate on three benchmarks: turbulent flow super-resolution, molecular conformation generation using RetroGNN-style synthesizability priors, and visual cortex calcium-imaging trace synthesis informed by dendritic biophysical models.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled architectural pattern for embedding hard scientific priors into adversarial generative models, shifting the role of learning from full distribution discovery to residual uncertainty estimation. It provides theoretical analysis showing that constraining the learnable subspace reduces the effective complexity of the minimax game, improving convergence guarantees. Empirically, it demonstrates across three scientific domains that mechanism-first generation achieves comparable sample quality with 5-10x fewer training examples while maintaining zero constraint violations, addressing a key barrier to trustworthy AI in safety-critical scientific applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats domain knowledge as soft regularization or auxiliary loss terms layered atop data-driven generators, MFAN inverts this hierarchy: the mechanistic model is the primary generative pathway and adversarial learning is architecturally restricted to a bounded residual role. This inversion is non-obvious because it challenges the prevailing assumption that end-to-end learning should dominate, and it requires solving the under-explored problem of making legacy scientific simulators differentiable enough to participate in GAN training while keeping them frozen.

proposals.title: Towards Invert The Purely Data-Driven Generation Premise: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to GANs are commonly trained to discover structure from data alone, with domain knowledge added only as. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that invert the purely data-driven generation premise can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates assumption challenge principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: GANs are commonly trained to discover structure from data alone, with domain knowledge added only as an enhancement. An inversion is to begin from har.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the invert the purely data-driven generation premise problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Beyond Point-wise Critics: Set-Level and Graph-Level Discriminators for Distribution-Faithful Generative Modeling
proposals.motivation: Modern generative adversarial networks and score-based models rely on discriminators that evaluate individual samples in isolation, yet many real-world quality desiderata—distributional coverage, causal consistency, temporal coherence, and demographic fairness—are inherently collective properties that cannot be assessed from any single example. This mismatch allows generators to achieve low per-sample loss while systematically failing on mode coverage, producing causally incoherent sequences, or amplifying representational biases across protected groups. Addressing this gap is urgent as generative models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains such as drug design, clinical imaging, and policy-relevant synthetic data.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing or augmenting point-wise discriminators with set-level, sequence-level, and graph-level critics that explicitly evaluate collective properties—distributional coverage, causal graph consistency, and fairness disparity across subgroups—will yield generators that better satisfy these structural desiderata without sacrificing per-sample fidelity, and will do so more reliably than post-hoc filtering or regularization approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-granularity critic architecture with three components: (1) a DeepSets-based set critic that ingests mini-batches of generated and real samples to score distributional coverage via learned kernel embeddings, replacing or supplementing the standard per-sample discriminator; (2) a graph neural network critic that operates over causal or relational graphs constructed from sample batches to enforce structural consistency, building on recent GNN benchmarking methodology; and (3) a fairness-aware sequence critic that evaluates temporal or group-conditional statistics using differentiable hypothesis testing layers. We train these critics jointly with the generator in a modified adversarial framework, using gradient penalty stabilization and a curriculum that phases in collective critics after initial per-sample pretraining. We evaluate on four domains: image generation (CelebA with fairness attributes), molecular graph generation (retrosynthesis-valid molecule sets), synthetic video (temporal coherence), and tabular synthetic data (coverage and fairness).
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled framework that elevates discriminator design from point-wise scoring to collective property verification, providing both theoretical grounding (connecting set-level critics to integral probability metrics) and practical architectures that are modular and domain-agnostic. It establishes new evaluation protocols measuring coverage, causal consistency, and fairness alongside standard fidelity metrics, creating benchmarks that better reflect deployment-relevant quality. The framework directly addresses AI safety concerns by making it structurally harder for generators to produce outputs that satisfy per-sample quality checks while violating distributional or fairness constraints.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored mode-coverage penalties and diversity regularizers as add-ons, no existing framework reconceptualizes the discriminator itself as a collective-property verifier operating at set, sequence, and graph granularities simultaneously. The integration of GNN-based causal critics with differentiable fairness testing within the adversarial loop is novel and connects disparate threads—graph neural network evaluation, causal reasoning, and AI safety—into a unified architectural principle that challenges the foundational assumption of sample-level discrimination.

proposals.title: Asymmetric by Design: Scheduled Capacity Imbalance as a Stabilization Strategy for Generative Adversarial Training
proposals.motivation: GAN training notoriously suffers from mode collapse, oscillation, and vanishing gradients, problems widely attributed to the delicate balance between generator and discriminator. The prevailing heuristic of matching their capacities lacks theoretical grounding yet dominates architectural choices. If intentional capacity asymmetry could be shown to stabilize training or mitigate specific failure modes, it would overturn a core design assumption and open a new axis of GAN engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a scheduled capacity-asymmetric training regime—where the discriminator-to-generator parameter ratio is deliberately varied over the course of training according to a curriculum—yields more stable convergence and higher sample diversity than capacity-matched baselines, because different training phases are bottlenecked by different failure modes (early: vanishing gradients from an overpowered discriminator; late: mode collapse from an underpowered one).
proposals.method: We propose a Scheduled Asymmetric Capacity (SAC) framework that dynamically adjusts the effective capacity of the discriminator relative to the generator during training. Concretely, we use progressive channel-width scaling and selective layer freezing to implement three capacity schedules: discriminator-heavy-first (tapering to balanced), generator-heavy-first (tapering to balanced), and oscillating asymmetry. We evaluate on CIFAR-10, CelebA-HQ, and LSUN using FID, precision-recall, and a novel training stability metric (gradient variance ratio) across StyleGAN2 and BigGAN backbones. We further provide a theoretical analysis grounded in the neural tangent kernel regime, characterizing how capacity ratio affects the discriminator's approximation of the optimal critic at each training phase.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) the first systematic empirical study isolating the effect of capacity asymmetry schedules on GAN training dynamics across multiple architectures and datasets, (2) a lightweight SAC meta-algorithm that can be applied on top of existing GAN architectures without changing their loss functions, and (3) theoretical analysis connecting capacity ratio to the smoothness of the discriminator's decision boundary and its implications for gradient informativeness. Together these results challenge a long-standing design assumption and provide practitioners with a principled new lever for stabilizing adversarial training.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored learning-rate asymmetry and update-frequency asymmetry (e.g., TTUR), architectural capacity asymmetry has been treated as a nuisance rather than a design variable. Our approach is non-obvious because the community's intuition—rooted in game-theoretic equilibrium arguments—suggests that matched capacities are necessary for convergence, yet we show that the transient dynamics of training, not just the equilibrium, determine practical outcomes, and that these dynamics favor phase-dependent imbalance.

proposals.title: Adversarial Architecture Search: Joint Neural Architecture Optimization of Generator and Discriminator via Bilevel Co-Design
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks remain notoriously sensitive to the relative capacity and architectural choices of the generator and discriminator, yet these components are almost always designed independently through manual tuning or separate NAS procedures. This decoupled approach ignores the fundamental game-theoretic coupling between the two networks, leading to training instability, mode collapse, and suboptimal sample quality. A principled co-design framework that searches over paired architectures jointly could unlock more stable and efficient adversarial training at scale.
proposals.hypothesis: Jointly optimizing generator and discriminator architectures through a bilevel neural architecture search formulation—where the outer loop maximizes a stability-aware objective over architectural parameters and the inner loop solves the minimax game—will produce GAN configurations that achieve superior training stability, faster convergence, and higher sample quality compared to independently designed or sequentially searched architectures.
proposals.method: We propose a bilevel optimization framework where continuous architectural parameters for both generator and discriminator are jointly optimized in the outer loop using a composite objective that combines Fréchet Inception Distance, a spectral-norm-based stability penalty, and wall-clock training efficiency. The inner loop performs standard adversarial training with differentiable architecture search (DARTS-style) relaxations applied simultaneously to both networks, with shared architectural coupling constraints that enforce capacity balance. We introduce an architecture compatibility score derived from the Jacobian singular value spectrum of the generator-discriminator pair to regularize the search toward stable equilibria, and validate across image generation (CIFAR-10, LSUN), graph generation, and molecular design tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work introduces the first principled framework for treating GAN design as a coupled architecture optimization problem rather than two independent design tasks, providing both a practical search algorithm and theoretical grounding via game-theoretic stability analysis. The resulting co-designed architectures are expected to reduce the extensive manual tuning currently required for GAN deployment and democratize access to high-quality generative models. The framework also yields insights into what architectural properties make generator-discriminator pairs inherently more trainable.
proposals.novelty: While neural architecture search has been applied to generators or discriminators in isolation, no existing method co-optimizes both architectures under an explicit game-theoretic stability criterion. The introduction of a Jacobian-spectrum-based compatibility regularizer is a fundamentally new inductive bias that directly targets the coupled dynamics of adversarial training rather than treating architecture search as a standard supervised learning problem.

proposals.title: Towards Automated Co-Design Of Generators And Discriminators: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to Search jointly over generator and discriminator architectures instead of tuning them independently. . This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that automated co-design of generators and discriminators can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates future work principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: Search jointly over generator and discriminator architectures instead of tuning them independently. This would treat adversarial training as a coupled.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the automated co-design of generators and discriminators problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Phase-Adaptive Adversarial Training: A Curriculum of Objectives for Stable and High-Fidelity Generative Modeling
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks remain notoriously difficult to train, with practitioners resorting to extensive hyperparameter searches and ad-hoc tricks to balance mode coverage against sample fidelity. Different loss formulations—minimax, Wasserstein with gradient penalty, hinge loss—each excel during particular training regimes but degrade in others, suggesting that no single objective is optimal across the full training trajectory. A principled framework that adapts the adversarial objective to the current training phase could substantially reduce instability and improve final generation quality without requiring per-task tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned phase controller that monitors real-time training dynamics—discriminator accuracy, gradient norm statistics, and inception-based diversity metrics—and smoothly interpolates among a portfolio of adversarial objectives will achieve both faster convergence and higher sample quality than any fixed-objective baseline, by matching the loss landscape to the generator's current capacity at each stage of training.
proposals.method: We propose a meta-learning framework comprising a lightweight recurrent controller network that ingests a feature vector of training diagnostics (discriminator loss curvature, generator gradient signal-to-noise ratio, FID trajectory slope, and spectral norm statistics) at fixed intervals and outputs softmax blending weights over a portfolio of objective modules: the original minimax loss, Wasserstein distance with adaptive gradient penalty, a mode-seeking diversity regularizer, and a perceptual fidelity auxiliary loss. The controller is trained via a bilevel optimization scheme where the inner loop performs standard GAN updates under the blended objective and the outer loop optimizes the controller parameters to minimize a validation-set FID surrogate using truncated backpropagation through time. We will evaluate on CIFAR-10, LSUN-Bedroom, and FFHQ at multiple resolutions using StyleGAN2 and ProjectedGAN backbones, comparing against fixed-objective ablations and recent adaptive methods such as ADA and LeCam regularization.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a formally grounded, architecture-agnostic curriculum over adversarial objectives that can be dropped into existing GAN pipelines with minimal overhead. We expect to demonstrate state-of-the-art or competitive FID and recall scores while significantly reducing training variance across random seeds, effectively turning the choice of GAN loss from a brittle design decision into a learned, self-regulating process. The trained controller's attention over objectives across training phases will additionally yield interpretable insights into which loss properties matter at which stages of generative learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored individual loss modifications or static schedules, no existing method treats the adversarial objective itself as a dynamic, learnable curriculum informed by real-time training diagnostics. The bilevel optimization of a blending controller over a diverse objective portfolio is a fundamentally different paradigm from tuning a single loss, offering a compositional and interpretable approach to training stabilization that generalizes across architectures and datasets.

proposals.title: Utility-Aligned Evaluation Metrics for Generative Models: Connecting Sample Quality to Downstream Task Performance and Failure Costs
proposals.motivation: Standard generative model evaluation metrics such as FID, IS, and precision-recall operate on distributional similarity assumptions that are agnostic to how generated samples will actually be used. In safety-critical domains like medical imaging and drug discovery, a generated sample that scores well on generic fidelity metrics may nonetheless be useless or even harmful when deployed in downstream decision pipelines. There is an urgent need for evaluation frameworks that ground generative model assessment in task-specific utility, calibration quality, and the asymmetric costs of different failure modes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that evaluation metrics which explicitly incorporate downstream task performance, decision-theoretic cost functions, and calibration measures will provide a more faithful and actionable assessment of generative model quality than distribution-matching metrics alone, and that optimizing generators against such utility-aligned metrics will produce samples that yield measurably better downstream outcomes even when conventional scores are comparable.
proposals.method: We propose a three-component evaluation framework: (1) a task-conditional utility score that measures the marginal improvement in a downstream predictor's performance when training data is augmented with generated samples, evaluated across multiple domain-specific tasks including medical image classification and molecular property prediction; (2) a calibration-aware quality metric that assesses whether the generator's implicit density estimates are well-calibrated with respect to the true data distribution in task-relevant regions, using techniques from conformal prediction and Bayesian calibration analysis; and (3) a cost-sensitive failure metric that weights different generative failure modes (mode collapse, hallucinated features, distributional shift) by their downstream decision costs, formalized via a loss function inspired by proper scoring rules. We will validate the framework by benchmarking existing generative models (GANs, diffusion models, VAEs) on medical imaging (colonoscopy landmark detection) and molecular design (retrosynthesis-aware virtual screening) tasks, comparing rankings produced by our utility-aligned metrics against those from FID, IS, and density-coverage metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, domain-grounded evaluation methodology that shifts generative model assessment from purely distributional fidelity toward measurable downstream impact. It provides practitioners in high-stakes domains with actionable metrics that reflect real deployment value and failure risks, and it establishes formal connections between generative evaluation, calibration theory, and decision-theoretic cost analysis. The benchmark suite across medical imaging and molecular design will serve as a reusable testbed for future utility-aware generative modeling research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has separately studied GAN evaluation metrics and downstream task performance, no existing framework systematically unifies task utility, calibration quality, and asymmetric failure costs into a coherent evaluation methodology. Our approach is non-obvious because it reveals that standard distributional metrics can be orthogonal to—or even inversely correlated with—domain-specific utility, challenging the implicit assumption that better FID scores translate to better real-world usefulness.

proposals.title: GenBench: A Unified Cross-Domain Benchmark Suite for Evaluating Generative Adversarial Network Families Under Standardized Compute Budgets
proposals.motivation: Claims about the generality of GAN architectures are routinely made on the basis of image-only benchmarks, leaving it unknown whether observed advantages transfer to video, tabular, time-series, or graph-structured data. This gap inflates architecture-specific conclusions that may merely reflect dataset-specific inductive biases. A rigorous, compute-controlled cross-domain benchmark is now tractable given mature GAN variants and standardized training infrastructure, and would ground the field's understanding of when and why particular generator-discriminator designs succeed or fail.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative ranking of major GAN families (StyleGAN, BigGAN, Wasserstein GAN variants, conditional GAN architectures) changes substantially across data modalities when evaluated under equivalent compute budgets, implying that reported superiority in the image domain does not generalize and that modality-specific inductive biases, rather than architectural universality, drive current benchmark performance.
proposals.method: We will construct GenBench comprising five standardized evaluation tracks—images (CIFAR-10, LSUN), video (UCF-101 clips), tabular (census and medical records), time series (financial and physiological signals), and molecular graphs—each with fixed preprocessing pipelines and matched FLOPs budgets enforced via a custom training harness. Each track will evaluate at least six representative GAN families using modality-appropriate fidelity metrics (FID and precision-recall for images, Fréchet Video Distance for video, likelihood fitness and downstream utility for tabular and time series, and validity-uniqueness-novelty for molecules). We will apply multi-factor statistical analysis (mixed-effects models with architecture and domain as factors) to decompose variance into architecture-driven versus domain-driven components, directly quantifying generality claims.
proposals.contribution: GenBench will provide the first controlled empirical evidence on whether GAN architectural advances are domain-general or domain-specific, giving practitioners principled guidance for architecture selection beyond images. The open-source benchmark suite, including reproducible training configurations and evaluation code, will serve as a lasting community resource analogous to what MLPerf provides for supervised learning. Additionally, the statistical decomposition framework we introduce will offer a reusable methodology for evaluating generality claims about any generative model family.
proposals.novelty: While individual GAN benchmarks exist per modality, no prior work enforces identical compute budgets across five or more domains and statistically decomposes performance into architecture versus domain factors. This compute-equalized, multi-domain design is non-obvious because it requires reconciling fundamentally different data representations and evaluation metrics under a single experimental protocol, moving beyond the common practice of per-domain leaderboards toward a science of generative model generality.

proposals.title: Spectral Sentinels: Online Early-Warning Diagnostics for Predicting and Preventing Mode Collapse in Generative Adversarial Networks
proposals.motivation: Mode collapse remains one of the most pernicious failure modes in GAN training, often detected only after significant computational resources have been wasted generating low-diversity samples. Current mitigation strategies are reactive rather than predictive, intervening only after collapse has visibly manifested. A reliable early-warning system would enable preemptive corrective action, dramatically improving training efficiency and the reliability of generative models deployed in safety-critical applications such as drug design and medical imaging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mode collapse is preceded by detectable geometric and spectral signatures in the discriminator's learned representation space—specifically, a progressive collapse of the effective rank of intermediate feature covariance matrices and a characteristic sharpening of the Hessian spectrum of the discriminator loss—that manifest several hundred training steps before sample diversity visibly degrades, enabling reliable early prediction and automated intervention.
proposals.method: We propose a lightweight monitoring framework that tracks three complementary online diagnostics during GAN training: (1) the effective rank of discriminator hidden-layer activation covariance matrices as a proxy for feature coverage, (2) the top eigenvalue ratio of stochastic Hessian estimates of the discriminator loss to capture curvature sharpening, and (3) a running estimate of latent-space occupancy via random projection hashing of generator outputs. These signals are fused through a small recurrent anomaly detector trained on synthetic collapse trajectories from controlled experiments across CIFAR-10, CelebA, and LSUN. When the detector triggers an alert, the system automatically applies one of several corrective interventions—adaptive learning rate reduction, spectral normalization tightening, or targeted noise injection into the generator latent space—selected via a simple bandit policy that learns which intervention best suits the current training regime.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes the first systematic, predictive early-warning framework for mode collapse, shifting the paradigm from post-hoc detection to preemptive intervention. It provides empirical characterization of the precursor signatures of collapse across multiple GAN architectures and datasets, establishing a taxonomy of collapse dynamics. The open-source monitoring toolkit will be directly usable by practitioners, reducing wasted compute and improving the trustworthiness of generative models in high-stakes domains aligned with responsible AI deployment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing approaches that treat mode collapse as a binary event to be avoided through architectural design or regularization, our framework treats it as a dynamical process with predictable precursors, drawing on ideas from catastrophe theory and early-warning signals in complex systems. The combination of discriminator geometry tracking, Hessian spectral analysis, and latent-space occupancy monitoring into a unified predictive system with automated closed-loop intervention has not been previously explored.

proposals.title: ProveNGen: Embedding Cryptographic Provenance and Abuse-Detection Mechanisms Directly into GAN Training Objectives
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks can now produce photorealistic images, audio, and video, yet no widely adopted technical mechanism exists to trace a synthetic artifact back to its originating model or detect post-hoc misuse at scale. Current watermarking approaches are applied as fragile post-processing steps easily removed by adversarial perturbation. Integrating provenance guarantees into the generative process itself would shift the AI safety discourse from abstract ethical guidelines to concrete, measurable technical safeguards—a direction aligned with the growing urgency around catastrophic misuse of AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A GAN whose generator is jointly trained with a differentiable watermark encoder and a provenance-verification discriminator can embed robust, model-specific signatures into every generated sample without degrading output quality, and these signatures remain detectable even after common adversarial transformations such as cropping, compression, and noise injection.
proposals.method: We propose a tri-component architecture: (1) a standard GAN generator augmented with a lightweight neural watermark encoder that embeds a model-specific cryptographic hash into the latent feature maps before final pixel synthesis; (2) an auxiliary provenance discriminator trained adversarially against an attack network that attempts to remove or forge watermarks, ensuring robustness; and (3) a misuse-detection module that learns distributional signatures of downstream abuse patterns (e.g., face-swap pipelines, disinformation templates) via contrastive learning on curated misuse datasets. The watermark encoder is trained end-to-end with the GAN objective using a combined loss that balances Fréchet Inception Distance preservation, bit-accuracy of the embedded signature, and robustness to a learned set of differentiable image transformations. We evaluate on unconditional face generation (FFHQ), conditional image synthesis (COCO), and audio generation (LJSpeech), measuring watermark bit-accuracy under JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, and adversarial removal attacks.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide the first end-to-end trainable framework that unifies generation quality, cryptographic provenance, and misuse detection within a single GAN training loop, establishing a new paradigm for responsible generative modeling. It would produce open-source benchmarks and metrics—Provenance Bit-Accuracy under Attack (PBA) and Misuse Detection AUC—that enable reproducible comparison of future provenance-aware generative models. By demonstrating that safety mechanisms can be baked into architecture rather than bolted on, the work offers a concrete technical foundation for AI governance policies requiring generative model accountability.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing post-hoc watermarking or detector-only approaches, our method co-trains the watermark mechanism adversarially within the GAN objective itself, making the provenance signal an intrinsic property of the learned generative distribution rather than a removable artifact. The addition of a learned misuse-detection head that shares representations with the discriminator is, to our knowledge, the first attempt to unify generation, attribution, and abuse detection in a single differentiable framework.

proposals.title: Disentangled-by-Design GANs: Grounding Latent Factors in Human-Interpretable Concepts via Structured Causal Priors
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks produce high-fidelity samples, yet their latent spaces remain opaque, limiting their utility for scientific discovery, fairness auditing, and safe deployment. Recent work on disentangled representations has shown promise but typically relies on unsupervised heuristics (e.g., beta-VAE) that offer no guarantee that recovered factors align with meaningful domain concepts. As generative models are increasingly used in high-stakes domains such as medical imaging and drug design, the inability to inspect and control individual generative factors poses both a scientific and a safety bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: Imposing a structured causal prior over the generator's latent space—where each latent variable is explicitly tied to a human-specified or discovery-guided semantic concept through a differentiable structural equation model—will yield GAN architectures whose individual latent dimensions are verifiably interpretable and independently controllable, without significant degradation in sample quality compared to unconstrained baselines.
proposals.method: We propose Causal-Factor GANs (CF-GANs), which replace the standard isotropic Gaussian prior with a learnable structural causal model (SCM) whose graph skeleton is either provided by domain knowledge or discovered via a differentiable adjacency matrix with sparsity regularization. Each node in the SCM corresponds to a named semantic factor (e.g., age, lighting, molecular scaffold). The generator conditions on samples from this SCM, and we introduce three complementary training signals: (1) a concept-alignment loss that matches latent traversals to labeled attribute changes using a small set of annotated examples, (2) a disentanglement regularizer based on total correlation minimization adapted for adversarial training, and (3) an interventional consistency loss that enforces that do-calculus interventions on single latent nodes produce changes only in the targeted attribute. We will evaluate on CelebA-HQ for facial attributes, the MIMIC-CXR chest X-ray dataset for clinically meaningful factors, and a molecular generation benchmark using ZINC-250K.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled framework for building GANs whose latent spaces are interpretable by construction rather than by post-hoc analysis, bridging the gap between causal representation learning and generative modeling. The interventional consistency loss is a novel training objective that goes beyond correlational disentanglement to enforce causal semantics. Practically, CF-GANs will enable domain experts to generate targeted counterfactual samples for fairness auditing, data augmentation with known factor variation, and hypothesis-driven scientific exploration in medicine and chemistry.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior disentanglement approaches that operate in a purely unsupervised or weakly-supervised statistical setting, CF-GANs embed an explicit causal graph into the generative prior, enabling true interventional control rather than mere statistical independence. The combination of SCM-structured priors with adversarial training and the interventional consistency objective is, to our knowledge, unexplored and directly addresses the gap between associative disentanglement metrics and the causal controllability that downstream applications require.

proposals.title: Pareto-Optimal GAN Design via Evolutionary Multi-Objective Neural Architecture Search
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks remain notoriously difficult to train, requiring careful co-design of generator and discriminator architectures, loss functions, and learning rate schedules. Current GAN design relies heavily on expensive manual tuning or single-objective neural architecture search, which cannot capture the inherent tradeoffs among image fidelity, mode coverage, adversarial robustness, and computational cost. A principled multi-objective optimization framework is needed to expose the Pareto frontier of these competing desiderata and yield deployable GAN configurations across resource budgets.
proposals.hypothesis: An evolutionary multi-objective search algorithm that jointly optimizes GAN architecture topology, adversarial loss formulation, and training schedule can discover Pareto-dominant designs that simultaneously improve FID, recall, robustness to perturbation, and FLOPs efficiency compared to state-of-the-art hand-designed and single-objective NAS-discovered GANs.
proposals.method: We employ NSGA-III as the backbone evolutionary algorithm, encoding each individual as a triple of (architecture graph for generator and discriminator, loss function composition selected from a grammar over Wasserstein, hinge, and non-saturating objectives with spectral and gradient penalty terms, and a piecewise-linear learning rate and augmentation schedule). Fitness is evaluated on four objectives: Fréchet Inception Distance for fidelity, precision-recall coverage for diversity, certified accuracy under PGD perturbations for robustness, and multiply-accumulate operations for compute cost. We introduce a surrogate performance predictor trained on early-stopping signals at 20% of full training to reduce evaluation cost by approximately 5x, and apply weight-sharing supernet techniques adapted from one-shot NAS to amortize generator-discriminator parameter training across the population.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first comprehensive multi-objective search framework purpose-built for adversarial generative modeling, producing a Pareto set of GAN designs that practitioners can select from based on deployment constraints. It establishes empirical scaling laws relating architecture complexity, loss landscape geometry, and the achievable tradeoff surface on CIFAR-10, LSUN, and FFHQ benchmarks. The released search space specification and discovered architectures will serve as a reusable benchmark for future GAN and multi-objective NAS research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior GAN NAS methods that optimize a single scalar metric, our approach treats the adversarial training landscape as an inherently multi-objective problem, co-searching over the discrete space of loss formulations and schedules alongside architecture—a combination that has not been explored. The surrogate-assisted evolutionary strategy specifically accounts for the non-stationary fitness landscape induced by the generator-discriminator co-adaptation, making the search tractable where naive multi-objective approaches would fail.

proposals.title: CausalGEN: Structural Causal Models as Latent Architectures for Intervention-Aware Generative Adversarial Networks
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks produce high-fidelity samples but their latent spaces lack semantic structure, making controlled manipulation unreliable and preventing principled counterfactual reasoning. Causal inference provides a formal framework—structural causal models (SCMs)—for representing mechanisms and interventions, yet this machinery has not been systematically integrated into GAN training objectives. Bridging these fields would yield generators whose latent dimensions correspond to identifiable causal factors, enabling robust, interpretable, and intervention-consistent generation for applications in science, medicine, and policy simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: Imposing a learned structural causal model on the latent space of a GAN, with an auxiliary intervention-consistency loss that enforces do-calculus constraints during training, produces a generator whose latent dimensions are disentangled along causal mechanisms and whose outputs remain faithful under single-variable and multi-variable interventions, outperforming standard and disentangled baselines on both generation quality and causal consistency metrics.
proposals.method: We propose CausalGEN, which replaces the unstructured latent prior of a standard GAN with a parameterized directed acyclic graph whose edges are learned jointly with the generator and discriminator via a differentiable acyclicity constraint (NOTEARS-style). An intervention module allows do-operator application at any latent node during both training and inference; an intervention-consistency regularizer penalizes the generator when changing a parent node fails to propagate appropriately to its descendants or when changing a non-ancestor node alters a target variable. We validate on CelebA-HQ (facial attributes with known causal relations), a synthetic SCM benchmark with ground-truth interventional distributions, and a medical imaging dataset where counterfactual generation can be evaluated against paired clinical observations.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a principled training framework that unifies structural causal models with adversarial generation, providing the first GAN whose latent space provably respects interventional semantics under mild identifiability assumptions; (2) new quantitative metrics—Interventional Fréchet Distance and Counterfactual Consistency Score—for evaluating whether generative models respect causal structure; and (3) empirical demonstrations that causally structured latent spaces improve robustness to distribution shift induced by interventions while maintaining competitive sample quality.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior disentanglement methods that encourage statistical independence without causal semantics, CausalGEN enforces mechanism-level structure through do-calculus constraints integrated into the adversarial objective, making it the first approach where intervening on a single latent factor produces outputs consistent with the true interventional distribution rather than merely altering a correlated feature. The joint learning of graph structure and generator parameters avoids requiring a pre-specified causal graph, addressing a key limitation of earlier causally-inspired generative approaches.

proposals.title: CausalGAN: Integrating Structural Causal Models into Generative Adversarial Networks for Controllable and Interpretable Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current generative models, including GANs, learn entangled latent representations where directions correspond to arbitrary mixtures of semantic features, limiting precise control and robustness. This is a critical bottleneck for scientific applications, such as generating realistic molecular structures or medical images, where interventions on specific mechanisms (e.g., a functional group or a pathological lesion) must be faithfully modeled. Grounding latent variables in causal mechanisms, rather than statistical correlations, would enable more reliable counterfactual reasoning and structured generation.
proposals.hypothesis: By enforcing a structural causal model (SCM) as a prior on the latent space of a GAN, we can learn disentangled representations that correspond to independent, intervenable data-generating mechanisms, leading to more controllable and robust generation under targeted interventions.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel GAN architecture where the generator's latent input is partitioned according to a pre-specified causal graph (e.g., for molecules: rings, chains, functional groups). The generator will be conditioned on this graph, and an adversarial regularizer will enforce that interventions on one latent variable (using do-calculus) alter only the corresponding semantic attribute in the output. We will leverage Bengio's expertise in neural latent variable models and recent object-centric representation methods to implement the causal constraints. Evaluation will use pre-2026 benchmarks for disentanglement and a new metric for counterfactual fidelity on datasets of organic molecules and colonoscopy images.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for causally-structured generative modeling, moving beyond statistical correlation to mechanism-based generation. It will deliver a practical tool for generating counterfactual scenarios in high-stakes domains like drug discovery and medical AI, where understanding the effect of interventions is paramount. The resulting models will be more interpretable and robust to distribution shifts induced by targeted changes.
proposals.novelty: While causal representation learning and disentangled GANs exist separately, their integration to enforce a full SCM as a hard constraint within the GAN's generative process is novel. This approach explicitly models interventions and counterfactuals at generation time, a significant advance over post-hoc latent space traversal methods.

proposals.title: Persistent Homology Signatures for Mode Coverage Evaluation in Deep Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation metrics for generative models, such as FID and IS, rely on summary statistics that average over the generated distribution and can mask severe mode dropping, especially in multimodal or low-density regions of the data manifold. Topological data analysis (TDA) offers principled tools from computational geometry that characterize the shape, connectivity, and higher-order structure of point clouds, yet these have been largely unexplored as evaluation instruments for generative modeling. A topological evaluation framework would provide complementary, geometry-aware diagnostics that expose structural failures invisible to conventional aggregate scores.
proposals.hypothesis: Persistent homology signatures computed from generated samples will reveal mode collapse and incomplete manifold coverage that standard distributional metrics fail to detect, and the discrepancy between persistence diagrams of real and generated data will serve as a reliable, interpretable measure of mode coverage fidelity.
proposals.method: We propose computing persistent homology on both real data and generated samples projected into a shared learned feature space, producing persistence diagrams that encode connected components (H0), loops (H1), and higher-dimensional voids. We introduce a Topological Mode Coverage (TMC) score based on the Wasserstein distance between persistence diagrams of real and generated distributions, augmented with a filtration-aware matching that aligns topological features across scales. To handle computational scaling, we employ landmark-based approximations and witness complexes, and validate the metric on synthetic multimodal benchmarks, image generation tasks with known mode structure, and molecular generation where chemical space topology is critical.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, interpretable evaluation metric grounded in algebraic topology that directly quantifies whether a generative model preserves the topological structure of the target distribution. It provides the first systematic empirical study connecting persistent homology features to mode coverage in deep generative models across vision and molecular domains. The framework offers practitioners a diagnostic tool that complements existing metrics and can guide architecture and training decisions in settings where mode diversity is paramount.
proposals.novelty: While TDA has been applied to analyze neural network loss landscapes and latent representations, using persistent homology as a mode coverage evaluation metric for generative models is unexplored. The non-obvious insight is that topological invariants capture global manifold connectivity that local density-based metrics inherently miss, providing a fundamentally different and complementary evaluation axis.

proposals.title: Curriculum Discriminator: Active Sample Selection for Sample-Efficient Adversarial Training
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks waste significant discriminator capacity on regions of the data distribution that the generator has already learned, leading to inefficient training and instability. As GANs scale to high-resolution and multi-modal domains, the cost of redundant discriminator updates becomes a practical bottleneck. Borrowing principles from active learning and curriculum design can refocus discriminator supervision on the frontier of generator failure, accelerating convergence and improving final sample quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Selectively presenting the discriminator with real samples, augmentations, and hard negatives chosen by an acquisition function that targets high-uncertainty or high-loss regions of the data manifold will yield faster GAN convergence and higher generation quality compared to uniform sampling, without increasing the total number of training samples.
proposals.method: We introduce an acquisition module that maintains a lightweight density model over the discriminator's per-sample loss landscape and, at each training iteration, selects a mini-batch of real samples and their augmentations that maximize an expected information gain criterion analogous to BALD (Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement). Hard negatives are constructed by interpolating in the generator's latent space toward modes where the discriminator is most uncertain. The acquisition module is updated online with negligible overhead using a replay buffer of recent discriminator gradients, and the overall framework is evaluated on unconditional image generation (CIFAR-10, LSUN) and conditional class-specific synthesis (ImageNet 128×128) using StyleGAN2 and ProjectedGAN backbones.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first principled integration of active learning acquisition functions into the adversarial training loop, offering both a practical algorithm that reduces FID by an expected 10-20% at matched wall-clock time and a theoretical analysis connecting discriminator sample efficiency to the information-theoretic properties of the acquisition strategy. The resulting framework is backbone-agnostic and can be dropped into existing GAN pipelines as a plug-in data loader, lowering the barrier to training high-quality generative models under limited data budgets.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and data augmentation have been applied to GANs independently, no prior work formulates discriminator data selection as a sequential Bayesian experimental design problem. The key non-obvious insight is that coupling an information-gain acquisition function with latent-space hard-negative mining creates a self-reinforcing feedback loop: the generator is forced toward its weakest modes precisely because the discriminator is trained hardest there, breaking the mode-collapse equilibrium that plagues standard training.

proposals.title: Active Experimental Design for Discriminative Feedback in Generative Adversarial Networks
proposals.motivation: Training Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) remains highly sample-inefficient and unstable, with the discriminator often wasting capacity on regions of the data distribution it has already mastered. This inefficiency is particularly critical in domains with expensive or limited data, such as molecular generation or medical imaging, where Bengio's recent work has focused. An active learning paradigm for the discriminator could dramatically improve training dynamics and final model quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning strategy, which strategically selects real samples, augmentations, or synthetic hard negatives for the discriminator's next training batch, will lead to more stable GAN training, faster convergence, and higher fidelity generations compared to standard random sampling.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where the generator and a candidate pool (of real data, augmentations, or perturbed generator outputs) are evaluated by an acquisition function. This function, inspired by Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement (BALD) or predictive entropy, will quantify the expected information gain for the discriminator. The highest-scoring candidates will form the next discriminator batch. We will test this within progressive GAN and StyleGAN2 architectures, initially on CIFAR-10 and CelebA, before applying it to a molecular graph generation task using a JT-VAE generator.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, sample-efficient training protocol for adversarial frameworks, reducing the number of wasted gradient updates. It will yield a new perspective on stabilizing GANs by treating the discriminator's learning as an experimental design problem. The method will be validated on benchmark image datasets and a proof-of-concept molecular generation task, demonstrating broad applicability.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is well-established for classifiers, its application to *curate the training set of a GAN discriminator* is novel. This approach moves beyond simple hard negative mining to a formal, uncertainty-driven selection of all input types (real, augmented, synthetic) for the adversarial opponent.

proposals.title: SafeGAN: Formal Verification and Runtime Monitoring Frameworks for Constraining Generative Adversarial Network Outputs
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks are increasingly deployed in safety-relevant domains such as medical imaging synthesis, autonomous driving simulation, and content generation, yet they lack any formal guarantees about output safety. Drawing from Bengio's work on AI and catastrophic risk, the uncontrolled generation of harmful, deceptive, or out-of-distribution outputs represents a concrete and growing threat as these models scale. Methods from safety-critical systems engineering, including formal verification and runtime monitoring, offer a principled but unexplored path to providing such guarantees for generative models.
proposals.hypothesis: By encoding safety specifications as formal constraints over the latent and output spaces of GANs, and by deploying lightweight runtime monitors derived from these specifications, it is possible to provably prevent generators from producing outputs in forbidden regions of the output space while preserving generation quality and diversity.
proposals.method: We propose a three-component framework: (1) a specification language based on signal temporal logic (STL) adapted for image and structured data domains, enabling researchers to formally define forbidden output regions and mandatory safety properties; (2) a verification-aware training procedure that incorporates abstract interpretation over generator network layers to compute sound over-approximations of reachable output sets, pruning latent space regions that may violate specifications; and (3) a lightweight runtime monitor using neural network verification techniques (e.g., linear relaxation bounds) that intercepts generator outputs at inference time, blocking unsafe generations and triggering fallback policies such as nearest safe projection or rejection sampling. We will evaluate on medical image synthesis (ensuring anatomical plausibility, connecting to Bengio's co-authored work on anatomical landmark detection) and face generation (preventing generation of identifiable real individuals).
proposals.contribution: This work establishes the first formal bridge between safety-critical systems verification and generative model deployment, providing both theoretical guarantees and practical tools. It introduces a reusable specification language for defining safety constraints on generative outputs, a verification-aware training algorithm with soundness guarantees, and an efficient runtime monitoring system with provable worst-case latency bounds. These contributions directly address the governance gap identified in AI safety research by providing auditable, certifiable safety mechanisms for generative AI.
proposals.novelty: While formal verification has been applied to classify neural network robustness, extending these techniques to generative models requires fundamentally new abstractions because the verification target shifts from input perturbation bounds to output space reachability over an unconstrained latent space. The combination of compile-time verification via abstract interpretation with runtime monitoring via relaxation-based certificates is unprecedented in the generative modeling literature and creates a defense-in-depth architecture inspired by safety-critical avionics standards.

proposals.title: PhysGAN: Physics-Informed Adversarial Networks with Differentiable Conservation Constraints for Physically Plausible Sample Generation
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks have achieved remarkable success in image synthesis, yet their application to scientific domains—fluid dynamics, molecular design, climate modeling—remains limited because generated samples frequently violate fundamental physical laws such as energy conservation, mass continuity, or thermodynamic equilibria. Enforcing physical plausibility post hoc through rejection sampling is computationally wasteful and fails to shape the learned distribution. A principled integration of physics-informed constraints directly into the adversarial training loop would unlock reliable generative modeling for scientific computing, where realism is defined by adherence to governing equations rather than perceptual quality.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding differentiable physics residuals—derived from known partial differential equations and conservation laws—as auxiliary penalty terms in both the generator loss and a physics-aware discriminator will produce generative models whose samples satisfy governing physical constraints to within numerical tolerance, without sacrificing distributional diversity or training stability.
proposals.method: We propose PhysGAN, which augments a standard GAN architecture with three components: (1) a differentiable physics residual module that evaluates PDE residuals (e.g., Navier-Stokes, continuity equations) on generated samples using automatic differentiation, added as a soft constraint to the generator loss; (2) a physics-informed discriminator that receives both raw samples and their computed physics residual fields as input channels, enabling it to distinguish physically implausible samples even when they appear statistically realistic; and (3) a curriculum-based constraint scheduling strategy that gradually tightens physics tolerance during training to avoid mode collapse from overly rigid early constraints. We will evaluate on three benchmark domains—2D turbulent flow fields, molecular conformer generation with energy conservation, and climate reanalysis data with mass-energy balance—comparing against vanilla GANs, physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) used as post-filters, and constrained optimization baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes a general-purpose framework for incorporating known physical laws into adversarial generative models, providing both theoretical analysis of constraint satisfaction guarantees under mild assumptions and empirical demonstrations across multiple scientific domains. It bridges the gap between the generative modeling and scientific computing communities by showing that physics-informed learning principles, previously applied mainly to forward and inverse PDE solvers, transfer effectively to the generative setting. The resulting models will enable reliable data augmentation, surrogate simulation, and scenario generation in safety-critical scientific applications where unphysical samples could lead to erroneous downstream decisions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that either filters generated samples post hoc or uses soft physics losses without discriminator awareness, PhysGAN jointly informs both generator and discriminator with differentiable PDE residuals and introduces curriculum constraint scheduling to navigate the tension between physical fidelity and distributional coverage—a trade-off not addressed by existing physics-informed generative approaches.

proposals.title: Adversarial Training as an Online Decision Problem: Multi-Armed Bandit Selection of Loss Terms, Critics, and Update Ratios
proposals.motivation: Adversarial training procedures such as GANs and adversarial robustness methods rely on manually scheduled loss weightings, fixed critic-to-generator update ratios, and static augmentation pipelines—choices that are brittle and require expensive hyperparameter sweeps. Recent work shows that the optimal balance among competing objectives shifts dramatically during training, yet practitioners still treat these as fixed recipe parameters. A principled online selection mechanism would reduce tuning cost, improve stability, and generalize across domains where adversarial objectives appear, from image synthesis to safe AI alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing the per-iteration choice of which loss term to emphasize, which critic to query, and which augmentation or update ratio to apply as a non-stationary multi-armed bandit problem will yield training dynamics that are more stable and sample-efficient than fixed or heuristically scheduled baselines, because the bandit learner can track the shifting landscape of useful gradient signals throughout training.
proposals.method: We propose BanditTrain, a meta-controller that wraps any adversarial training loop. At each training step, a contextual bandit agent (using an EXP3 or discounted Thompson Sampling policy) selects from a discrete action set comprising (a) which loss term to weight most heavily, (b) which critic head or discriminator variant to use for the gradient update, (c) which data augmentation to apply, and (d) what generator-to-critic update ratio to use. The bandit reward signal is derived from a lightweight validation proxy—for GANs we use an exponential moving average of the Fréchet Inception Distance on a held-out shard, and for adversarial robustness we use certified radius estimates. We validate on CIFAR-10/ImageNet conditional generation with StyleGAN2 and BigGAN backbones, and on adversarial robustness training with PGD and TRADES losses on ResNet-50, comparing against grid-searched fixed schedules, population-based training, and recent adaptive loss-balancing heuristics.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) a formal reduction of adversarial training configuration to a non-stationary bandit problem with theoretical regret guarantees under mild smoothness assumptions, (2) the BanditTrain algorithm with open-source implementation that is architecture- and domain-agnostic, and (3) empirical evidence across generative and robustness settings showing that bandit-selected training configurations match or exceed heavily tuned fixed schedules while eliminating the need for expensive hyperparameter searches.
