The framework also provides interpretable action-frequency logs that reveal which objectives matter at which training phases, offering new insight into adversarial training dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has applied bandits to neural architecture search and hyperparameter optimization between runs, no existing method treats the within-run, per-step selection of competing adversarial objectives as a bandit problem. The key non-obvious insight is that adversarial training's notoriously unstable dynamics are better modeled as a non-stationary reward landscape for an online learner than as a static optimization problem, and that cheap proxy rewards suffice to guide selection without the overhead of full evaluation.

proposals.title: ThriftGAN: Pareto-Optimal Neural Architecture Search for On-Device Generative Adversarial Networks Under Joint Quality-Efficiency Constraints
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks have achieved remarkable sample quality but remain confined to cloud infrastructure due to their heavy memory, latency, and power requirements. Deploying generation on-device would unlock privacy-preserving data augmentation, real-time image adaptation, and personalized content synthesis without network connectivity. Existing compression techniques treat efficiency as a secondary post-hoc objective, leaving a significant gap in methods that co-optimize perceptual quality and computational cost from the ground up.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-objective neural architecture search framework that jointly optimizes FID and on-device latency within a GAN-specific search space—incorporating depthwise-separable convolutions, mixed-precision quantization-aware training, and progressive knowledge distillation from a full-precision teacher—can discover generator architectures achieving less than 2x FID degradation while reducing multiply-accumulate operations by 10x and peak memory by 8x relative to StyleGAN2, enabling real-time inference on mobile NPUs.
proposals.method: We design a GAN-tailored search space comprising depthwise-separable and grouped convolution blocks, channel-adaptive attention modules, and mixed-precision quantization choices at each layer. A differentiable multi-objective NAS procedure uses a Pareto-dominance reward signal combining FID, Fréchet CLIP Distance, on-device latency measured via hardware-in-the-loop profiling, and peak activation memory. The discovered architectures are further compressed through a two-stage progressive distillation pipeline: first distilling intermediate feature distributions from a pretrained StyleGAN2 teacher using sliced Wasserstein distance, then fine-tuning with quantization-aware training at INT8 and INT4 precision levels. We evaluate on FFHQ, LSUN-Church, and a new mobile-camera dataset, benchmarking on Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 and Apple A16 NPUs for wall-clock latency, power draw, and thermal throttling behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes the first systematic framework for Pareto-optimal GAN design under joint quality-efficiency constraints, producing a family of generator architectures spanning the efficiency-quality tradeoff frontier. The discovered ThriftGAN models will enable practical on-device generative applications—including privacy-preserving medical data augmentation and real-time style transfer—previously infeasible outside data centers. We will release the search space definition, discovered architectures, and a hardware-aware benchmarking toolkit to catalyze reproducible research on efficient generative models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior GAN compression work that applies generic pruning or distillation post-hoc, ThriftGAN treats the generator architecture, precision policy, and distillation schedule as a single joint optimization problem with hardware-measured efficiency as a first-class objective. The integration of Pareto-dominance NAS with progressive feature-distribution distillation specifically tailored to adversarial training dynamics—where naive compression causes mode collapse—is unexplored and addresses the fundamental instability that makes GAN compression harder than discriminative model compression.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Dual-Discriminator Adversarial Networks for Temporally Coherent Long-Horizon Video and Volumetric Scene Generation
proposals.motivation: Current generative adversarial approaches produce impressive single images but collapse when extended to long video sequences or 3D volumetric outputs, suffering from temporal flickering, geometric inconsistency, and mode collapse over extended horizons. The ability to generate coherent high-dimensional structured data—minutes-long videos or full volumetric scenes—would transform applications from scientific simulation to content creation, yet no principled adversarial framework jointly enforces local realism and global structural coherence. Bridging this gap requires rethinking the discriminator architecture itself, moving beyond frame-level or patch-level criticism to multi-scale spatiotemporal reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical dual-discriminator framework—pairing a local realism critic operating on short spatiotemporal patches with a global coherence critic that evaluates long-range temporal trajectories and cross-view geometric consistency via learned abstract state representations—will enable adversarial generation of temporally coherent videos exceeding 128 frames and geometrically consistent volumetric scenes, without the quality degradation observed in naive sequential or autoregressive GAN extensions.
proposals.method: We propose a generator architecture that produces outputs in a coarse-to-fine latent hierarchy: a global planning module first generates a compact spatiotemporal skeleton (keyframe embeddings for video, sparse voxel layouts for volumes), which a local synthesis module then upsamples into dense frames or volumetric grids. Two discriminators operate in tandem: a Local Realism Discriminator (LRD) that evaluates sliding spatiotemporal windows for perceptual quality, and a Global Coherence Discriminator (GCD) that ingests a learned trajectory embedding of the full sequence or multi-view rendering to assess long-range consistency. The GCD is trained with an auxiliary contrastive objective that distinguishes real temporal/geometric trajectories from shuffled or warped negatives, providing a structured gradient signal that prevents the generator from sacrificing coherence for local fidelity. We validate on long-horizon video generation (256+ frames on UCF-101 and Kinetics) and volumetric scene generation (ShapeNet volumes and multi-view consistent room-scale scenes).
proposals.contribution: This work contributes (1) the first adversarial framework that explicitly factorizes and jointly optimizes local realism and global coherence for high-dimensional structured outputs, (2) a trajectory-contrastive training objective for discriminators that provides stable gradients over long temporal or spatial horizons, and (3) systematic benchmarks demonstrating that the dual-discriminator approach substantially outperforms single-discriminator baselines and autoregressive GAN methods on both perceptual quality (FID/FVD) and coherence metrics (temporal consistency error, multi-view reprojection error) for sequences and volumes well beyond current adversarial generation capabilities.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior GAN extensions that simply add temporal discriminators or 3D convolutions, our approach decouples the adversarial objective into complementary local and global critics with distinct architectural inductive biases, and introduces trajectory-contrastive learning within the discriminator—a mechanism that provides geometrically and temporally grounded feedback rather than relying on the discriminator to implicitly discover coherence from raw pixels, addressing the fundamental reason long-horizon adversarial generation has remained intractable.

proposals.title: Generative Latent Flow Networks for Coherent Long-Horizon Volumetric Video Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Generative models like GANs and diffusion models excel at producing high-fidelity static images but struggle with long-horizon, structured outputs like videos or 3D volumes where coherence across time or space is paramount. This limitation hinders applications in scientific simulation (e.g., molecular dynamics), medical imaging (e.g., synthetic 4D colonoscopy sequences), and content creation. A scalable framework for generating coherent, high-dimensional structured data is a critical open challenge in deep learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical generative model, which first learns a structured latent space enforcing temporal and spatial consistency and then uses adversarial training on a decomposed set of discriminators, can scale to produce coherent, long-horizon volumetric sequences beyond the capabilities of frame-by-frame generation.
proposals.method: We will develop a Generative Latent Flow Network (GLFN). The architecture uses a two-stage VAE-GAN hybrid: a 3D convolutional VAE learns a compressed, disentangled latent representation of volumetric frames. A temporal transformer module then models latent trajectories, enforcing long-range coherence. For generation, a GAN framework with multiple discriminators is employed: one for single-frame realism, one for short-term consistency (akin to optical flow), and a novel 'global coherence' discriminator operating on latent sequence embeddings. Training will leverage datasets of sequential volumetric data, such as mouse visual cortex imaging stacks and colonoscopy video sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable architecture for generating coherent long-horizon structured data, moving beyond static images. It will deliver new benchmarks and metrics for evaluating coherence in generated sequences. The method could enable the creation of large-scale synthetic datasets for training medical AI systems and for simulating complex physical and biological processes.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit decomposition of the adversarial objective into local realism and long-horizon coherence tasks, combined with a transformer-based latent dynamics model. This moves beyond simply extending 2D image generators to 3D+time and instead directly architectures the learning problem for global consistency.

proposals.title: HierGAN: Hierarchical Cross-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks with Scale-Consistent Latent Constraints
proposals.motivation: Generative adversarial networks have achieved impressive results at fixed resolutions, yet they struggle to maintain structural coherence when outputs must be consistent across multiple spatial scales simultaneously. This limitation is critical in domains such as scientific imaging, medical visualization, and molecular design, where coarse global topology and fine-grained local detail must jointly satisfy physical or semantic constraints. Bridging the gap between low-resolution plausibility and high-resolution faithfulness within a unified framework is essential for deploying generative models in safety-critical and scientifically rigorous applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical GAN architecture that enforces explicit cross-resolution consistency constraints through shared latent representations and scale-equivariant discriminators will produce outputs that are simultaneously globally coherent and locally faithful, outperforming both single-resolution and naive multi-scale baselines on structural consistency metrics without sacrificing perceptual quality.
proposals.method: We propose HierGAN, a generator composed of resolution-specific sub-networks that share a structured latent space organized as a coarse-to-fine hierarchy, where each level conditions on the immediately coarser representation via learned upsampling and residual refinement modules. A set of scale-equivariant discriminators operate at each resolution level, augmented with a cross-resolution consistency discriminator that evaluates whether downsampled fine outputs match coarse outputs. Training proceeds with a novel dual-objective loss combining adversarial terms at each scale with a differentiable cross-resolution alignment penalty based on Laplacian pyramid decomposition, inspired by recent work on graph neural network benchmarking for principled multi-scale evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a principled framework for multi-resolution generation that treats scale consistency as a first-class training objective rather than an emergent property. It introduces cross-resolution consistency discriminators and a Laplacian alignment loss as reusable architectural components for any multi-scale generative task. We expect to demonstrate state-of-the-art results on hierarchical image generation benchmarks and provide theoretical analysis connecting scale consistency constraints to improved mode coverage and training stability.
proposals.novelty: Unlike progressive growing or super-resolution approaches that treat scale as a sequential pipeline, HierGAN enforces bidirectional consistency across all resolutions simultaneously during training, ensuring that no scale is treated as primary. The integration of scale-equivariant discrimination with explicit cross-resolution alignment constraints is, to our knowledge, unexplored and provides a dual scaling perspective where global plausibility and local fidelity are co-optimized rather than traded off.

proposals.title: Subpopulation-Aware Scaling Laws: Guaranteeing Performance on Long-Tail Modes Under Compute and Data Growth
proposals.motivation: Current neural scaling laws characterize how average loss decreases with model size, data, and compute, yet this aggregate view masks catastrophic failures on rare but safety-critical subpopulations. As large models are deployed in high-stakes domains—medical imaging, autonomous systems, and content moderation—coverage failures on underrepresented groups pose both fairness and catastrophic-risk concerns. A principled framework that extends scaling analysis from population-level averages to worst-case subpopulation guarantees is urgently needed to align capability scaling with responsible deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subpopulation-specific scaling exponents diverge systematically from aggregate scaling laws, with rare modes exhibiting slower power-law improvement rates, and that a distributionally robust optimization objective with adaptive data reweighting can provably close this scaling gap, yielding uniform performance guarantees across all subpopulations above a minimum prevalence threshold.
proposals.method: We propose a three-stage approach. First, we derive theoretical subpopulation scaling laws by decomposing the empirical risk into mixture components and analyzing per-component loss trajectories as functions of model size and data volume, leveraging concentration inequalities to bound the gap between subpopulation and aggregate exponents. Second, we introduce a distributionally robust scaling objective (DRO-Scale) that augments standard pre-training with a group-aware reweighting mechanism based on online estimation of subpopulation losses, using techniques from adaptive stochastic optimization to avoid the need for explicit group labels at training time via learned latent subpopulation embeddings. Third, we validate on vision benchmarks (CelebA, Waterbirds), medical imaging tasks (colonoscopy landmark detection following our prior work), and language modeling with demographic subgroups, measuring both aggregate scaling curves and per-subpopulation scaling exponents across five model scales spanning two orders of magnitude in parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes the first formal characterization of how scaling laws fractionate across subpopulations, providing practitioners with quantitative predictions of when scale alone will or will not resolve tail-group failures. The DRO-Scale training procedure offers a practical, label-free method to enforce subpopulation-aware scaling during pre-training. Together, these results bridge the gap between the scaling laws literature and the algorithmic fairness community, offering a unified lens on when scaling is sufficient for equitable performance and when targeted interventions remain necessary.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on distributionally robust optimization addresses worst-group accuracy at fixed model scale, no existing framework models how subpopulation performance evolves as a function of scaling compute and data jointly. Our latent-subpopulation discovery mechanism combined with per-group scaling exponent analysis is a fundamentally new analytical tool that reveals hidden failure modes invisible to both standard scaling analysis and static fairness audits.

proposals.title: CycleSim: Bidirectional Adversarial Networks for Joint Sim-to-Real Transfer and Inverse Simulator Calibration
proposals.motivation: Simulators are indispensable for training and evaluating machine learning models in domains where real data is scarce or expensive, yet the reality gap between simulated and real distributions remains a fundamental bottleneck. Current domain adaptation methods focus almost exclusively on the forward direction—making simulations look realistic—while ignoring the rich inverse signal that real observations provide for correcting simulator parameters. Closing this loop would yield both higher-fidelity synthetic training data and scientifically meaningful simulator calibration, with direct implications for safety-critical applications such as medical imaging and autonomous systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-cycle adversarial framework that jointly trains a forward generator (sim-to-real) and an inverse calibration network (real-to-sim parameter inference) will achieve superior domain transfer and more physically grounded simulator updates than unidirectional approaches, because the two directions provide complementary gradient signals that regularize each other and prevent mode collapse.
proposals.method: We propose CycleSim, a framework built on paired conditional GANs arranged in a dual cycle: a forward generator G_f translates simulator renderings to photorealistic images while an inverse network G_inv infers latent simulator parameters (lighting, texture, geometry distributions) from real observations. The two cycles are linked by a bidirectional consistency loss ensuring that applying G_inv to G_f's output recovers the original simulation parameters, and that re-simulating with G_inv's inferred parameters followed by G_f reproduces the target real image. We incorporate a differentiable renderer as the simulation backbone, enabling end-to-end gradient flow through the simulator, and train the system with a combination of adversarial, cycle-consistency, and physics-informed regularization losses that penalize physically implausible parameter configurations.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled dual-cycle architecture that unifies sim-to-real transfer and inverse simulator calibration within a single adversarial training framework, providing theoretical analysis of convergence properties under the bidirectional consistency constraint. It establishes new benchmarks for sim-to-real transfer on visual recognition tasks (colonoscopy landmark detection and autonomous driving scenes) while simultaneously yielding interpretable simulator parameter updates, bridging the gap between generative modeling and scientific simulation calibration.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing CycleGAN or domain randomization approaches that treat the simulator as a fixed black box, CycleSim closes the loop by making the simulator itself a learnable component whose parameters are continuously refined by an inverse adversarial network. The bidirectional consistency constraint between forward generation and inverse calibration is a non-obvious regularizer that provably tightens the bound on domain gap beyond what either direction alone can achieve.

proposals.title: Meta-Adversarial Personalization: One-Shot Local Adaptation of Generative Models via Structured Regularization on the Adversarial Loss Landscape
proposals.motivation: Large generative and adversarial models are trained on population-level data, yet deployment increasingly demands outputs tailored to a single patient, device, or environment—settings where only one or a handful of examples are available. Naïve fine-tuning on such scarce data leads to catastrophic overfitting and mode collapse, a problem that intensifies as model capacity grows. Understanding how to transfer adversarial structure from a population prior to a local context with a single example is both a practical deployment bottleneck and a fundamental question about the geometry of learned representations.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learned adversarial framework that decomposes the generator into a shared population backbone and a low-rank, locally-adaptive personalization head can achieve faithful one-shot adaptation by constraining updates to lie on a learned manifold of plausible local discriminator landscapes, thereby preventing catastrophic overfitting while preserving the diversity encoded in the population model.
proposals.method: We propose Meta-Adversarial Personalization (MAP), which trains a conditional GAN with a hyper-network that produces low-rank adaptation matrices for both generator and discriminator, conditioned on a single support example. During meta-training on diverse user/environment clusters, we optimize a bi-level objective: the inner loop adapts the low-rank parameters using a local adversarial loss on the support example, while the outer loop maximizes generation quality on held-out query samples from the same cluster. A structured spectral regularizer constrains the adapted discriminator's Jacobian to remain close to the population discriminator's spectral profile, preventing degenerate local minima. We evaluate on three domains—personalized medical image synthesis from single patient scans, device-specific sensor-signal generation for IoT anomaly detection, and user-conditioned text-to-image stylization—measuring both fidelity (FID, domain-specific metrics) and diversity (recall, intra-cluster variance) relative to population baselines and standard fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work establishes a principled framework for understanding the dual scaling regime—how adversarial training dynamics change when moving from population to individual scope—and provides the first theoretical and empirical characterization of the overfitting boundary in one-shot adversarial adaptation. The structured spectral regularization technique offers a general-purpose mechanism applicable beyond GANs to any adversarial or minimax learning system requiring local adaptation. Practically, MAP enables privacy-preserving on-device personalization without transmitting user data, directly addressing deployment concerns in healthcare and edge AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing personalization methods that treat adaptation as a supervised fine-tuning problem, MAP directly addresses the unique instability of adversarial objectives under extreme data scarcity by regularizing the discriminator's loss landscape geometry rather than the generator's parameter space. The spectral manifold constraint is a fundamentally different inductive bias from weight-space priors like EWC or parameter-efficient tuning, operating on the functional curvature of the minimax game itself.

proposals.title: One-Shot Latent Space Personalization for Robust and Catastrophe-Resistant Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale generative models are trained on population-level datasets, lacking the ability to efficiently adapt to individual users, patients, or specific environments without extensive fine-tuning. This is critical in domains like personalized medicine, where adapting a colonoscopy AI to a single patient's unique mucosal anatomy from a single baseline scan could improve detection, but risks catastrophic overfitting and loss of general robustness. Bridging the gap between massive pre-trained models and safe, data-efficient personalization remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative model's latent space can be structured to allow for one-shot personalization via a minimal adversarial update, enabling significant local adaptation for a single conditioning context (e.g., one patient) while provably preserving the model's core population-level knowledge and resisting catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-trained variational autoencoder (VAE) or diffusion model with a hypernetwork or a dedicated low-rank adaptation (LoRA) module that is conditioned on a single input sample (e.g., one colonoscopy frame of normal mucosa). This adapter will be trained adversarially against a fixed feature extractor to modify only a sparse, structured subspace of the latent representation. We will test adaptation on biomedical imaging (colonoscopy landmarks) and molecular property generation, measuring performance gain on the local context versus degradation on the original population validation set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and empirical evidence for the limits of one-shot personalization in deep generative models. It will contribute a novel method for local latent space adaptation that balances specificity with robustness, directly addressing concerns of catastrophic overfitting raised in AI safety research. The findings will be applicable to personalized healthcare AI and efficient device-specific model deployment.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning and personalization exist, the explicit focus on *one-shot* adaptation within a *generative* model's latent space, framed as an adversarial structure-learning problem to probe and prevent catastrophic overfitting, is novel. It connects Bengio's themes of latent representation and neural adaptation with concrete safety-oriented constraints.

proposals.title: Discrete Latent Alignment for Sequence-to-Sequence Models: Replacing Soft Attention with Structured Categorical Variables
proposals.motivation: Soft attention, as introduced in neural machine translation, computes a differentiable weighted average over source states, deliberately sidestepping the combinatorial challenge of treating alignment as a latent variable. While effective, this design produces dense, diffuse attention distributions that obscure interpretability, waste computation on irrelevant positions, and lack controllability for safety-critical or structured-output applications. With recent advances in gradient estimators for discrete variables—such as straight-through Gumbel-Softmax, REINFORCE with learned baselines, and the score-function trick with variance reduction—the original motivation for avoiding discrete alignment deserves rigorous re-examination.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling alignment as a discrete categorical latent variable, trained end-to-end with modern gradient estimation techniques, will yield attention distributions that are provably sparser, more interpretable through direct alignment inspection, and more controllable via structured priors, while matching or exceeding the translation quality of conventional soft attention when paired with appropriate variance-reduction strategies.
proposals.method: We propose a family of discrete alignment models where each decoder step samples a hard alignment index from a categorical distribution parameterized by encoder-decoder compatibility scores. Training uses a three-pronged gradient estimation strategy: (1) Gumbel-Softmax with learned temperature annealing for warm-start pretraining, (2) REINFORCE with a self-critical baseline for fine-tuning with true discrete samples, and (3) an optional structured prior (e.g., monotonicity or fertility constraints encoded as a hidden semi-Markov model) to inject linguistically motivated inductive biases. We evaluate on WMT translation benchmarks (En-De, En-Fr), document summarization (CNN/DailyMail), and a controlled synthetic task designed to test alignment fidelity under known ground-truth correspondences.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes: (1) the first systematic comparison of modern discrete gradient estimators applied to the alignment problem that soft attention was originally designed to circumvent, demonstrating when and why discrete alignment can be competitive; (2) quantitative evidence that discrete alignments yield higher sparsity and interpretability scores (measured via alignment error rate against gold annotations and human evaluation) without sacrificing task performance; and (3) a practical toolkit of structured priors and variance-reduction techniques that make discrete alignment training stable at scale, lowering the barrier for adoption in safety-critical NLP systems where attention transparency is essential.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on hard attention (e.g., Xu et al. 2015) demonstrated feasibility in image captioning, it predates modern variance-reduction techniques and was largely abandoned due to training instability. Our approach is non-obvious because it challenges the foundational design decision of the Bahdanau et al. attention mechanism by showing that the very problem soft attention was engineered to avoid—discrete combinatorial alignment—is now tractable with contemporary tools, and that the interpretability and controllability gains from discreteness constitute a qualitative advantage that weighted averaging fundamentally cannot provide.

proposals.title: Segment-Level Attention via Latent Constituency Induction for Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Standard attention mechanisms in sequence-to-sequence models align decoder states to individual source tokens, yet natural language meaning frequently resides in multi-word units such as idioms, collocations, and morphologically rich compounds. This token-level bottleneck forces the model to implicitly reconstruct phrasal semantics from scattered soft alignments, degrading translation quality for many-to-many correspondences and non-compositional expressions. Developing attention over explicitly learned segments would provide a more linguistically faithful retrieval unit and improve both accuracy and interpretability of neural translation.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing fixed token-level source annotations with dynamically induced latent segments—learned end-to-end via a differentiable segmentation module—will yield higher BLEU scores on phrase-heavy language pairs and produce attention maps that better correlate with human-judged phrasal alignments, compared to standard subword-level attention baselines.
proposals.method: We introduce a Latent Segment Attention (LSA) layer that sits between the encoder and the cross-attention mechanism. First, a lightweight boundary predictor (a single-layer BiLSTM with Gumbel-Softmax gating) proposes soft segment boundaries over encoder hidden states; segment representations are then formed by pooling consecutive encoder states within each induced span using an attention-weighted average. The decoder cross-attends over these variable-length segment representations instead of individual token states. The boundary predictor is trained jointly with the translation objective, regularized by an expected-length prior that encourages linguistically plausible segment sizes, and optionally warm-started with phrase-table statistics from unsupervised word alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first end-to-end trainable mechanism that lifts cross-attention from the token level to a learned phrasal level without requiring external syntactic parsers or phrase tables at inference time. We expect measurable BLEU improvements on language pairs with substantial phrasal divergence (e.g., English–Japanese, English–German compounds) and on idiomatic test suites. Additionally, the induced segments offer a new interpretability lens: researchers can inspect which source spans the model treats as atomic units, enabling qualitative analysis of what the encoder has learned about compositionality.
proposals.novelty: Prior approaches either attend over fixed n-gram windows or require supervised constituency parses to define spans; our method learns segment boundaries from the translation signal alone via a differentiable discrete-continuous relaxation, making it language-agnostic and fully end-to-end. The combination of Gumbel-Softmax boundary induction with segment-pooled cross-attention is, to our knowledge, unexplored and challenges the implicit assumption in Transformer attention that the natural retrieval granularity is the subword token.

proposals.title: Deliberative Decoding: Planning-Augmented Iterative Refinement for Non-Autoregressive Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Left-to-right autoregressive decoding forces each token decision to be made without knowledge of future context, leading to exposure bias, error propagation, and an inability to globally plan coherent outputs. This limitation is particularly acute in long-form generation and machine translation, where maintaining discourse coherence and satisfying distant dependencies requires awareness of the full target structure. Recent advances in non-autoregressive and iterative refinement methods have shown promise but lack a principled planning mechanism that explicitly reasons about future obligations before committing to local token choices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting iterative refinement decoding with a lightweight latent planning module—which constructs a coarse global sketch of the target sequence before fine-grained token generation—will significantly reduce coherence errors and improve translation quality compared to both standard autoregressive baselines and existing non-autoregressive methods, particularly on long sequences where future-context dependencies are most critical.
proposals.method: We propose a two-phase decode-then-refine architecture. In the first phase, a planning network conditioned on the source encoding produces a sequence of discrete latent plan tokens that capture high-level semantic and syntactic structure of the full target (e.g., sentence-level topic slots and syntactic templates), trained via a vector-quantized variational objective. In the second phase, a bidirectional conditional masked language model generates all target tokens in parallel, conditioned on both the source and the latent plan, then iteratively refines the output over K passes using full bidirectional target attention. We train end-to-end on WMT translation benchmarks and XSum summarization, using a scheduled plan-dropout curriculum to ensure robustness, and evaluate with BLEU, COMET, and a novel long-range dependency accuracy metric.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled framework that unifies global planning with iterative local refinement, directly addressing the theoretical limitation that autoregressive models cannot condition on future context. We expect to demonstrate state-of-the-art non-autoregressive translation quality that closes the gap with autoregressive models on long sequences while maintaining significant speedup, and to provide empirical evidence that explicit planning reduces coherence errors by over 30% on documents exceeding 50 tokens. The latent plan representations also offer an interpretable window into the model's generation strategy.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior non-autoregressive methods (e.g., CMLM, Levenshtein Transformer) that refine tokens without explicit future-awareness, and unlike plan-based methods that operate in continuous space, our approach introduces discrete latent plan tokens that serve as a compressed, interpretable global blueprint before any token-level decisions are made. This decouples the what-to-say planning from the how-to-say-it realization in a way that has not been explored in iterative refinement decoding.

proposals.title: Document-Conditioned Neural Machine Translation via Hierarchical Discourse Attention
proposals.motivation: Modern neural machine translation systems process each sentence as an isolated unit, discarding the rich discourse structure that human translators rely on to resolve anaphora, maintain lexical consistency, and preserve topic flow across paragraphs. This independence assumption is a key bottleneck for document-level translation quality, leading to incoherent pronoun choices, inconsistent terminology, and broken coreference chains. As transformer-based models scale to longer contexts, there is now a concrete opportunity to replace this assumption with architectures that explicitly condition on inter-sentential discourse relations.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical discourse-aware attention mechanism that maintains a dynamic document-level representation of entity states, topic threads, and coreference chains will significantly improve translation coherence across sentence boundaries, as measured by both automatic discourse metrics and human judgments of document-level adequacy, without degrading sentence-level BLEU.
proposals.method: We propose a two-tier transformer architecture in which a sentence-level encoder-decoder is augmented with a document-level discourse module. The discourse module maintains a memory of encoded representations from preceding and following sentences using a sparse cross-attention mechanism that attends selectively to entity mentions, discourse connectives, and coreferent spans identified by a lightweight coreference tagger. During decoding, a gated fusion layer combines the sentence-level decoder state with the discourse memory, allowing the model to condition lexical and pronominal choices on document context. We train end-to-end on document-aligned parallel corpora (IWSLT, WMT document-level tasks) with an auxiliary contrastive loss that encourages consistent entity translations across sentence boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work challenges the deeply embedded sentence-independence assumption in neural machine translation by providing both an architecture and a training objective that explicitly model inter-sentential discourse. We expect measurable gains on document-level evaluation benchmarks including pronoun translation accuracy (e.g., ContraPro), lexical consistency metrics, and human coherence ratings. The hierarchical discourse attention mechanism is modular and can be integrated into existing pretrained translation models, offering a practical path to deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior context-aware translation approaches simply concatenate adjacent sentences or use flat additional context, our method introduces structured discourse representations—coreference chains and topic continuity signals—as first-class inputs to a hierarchical attention mechanism. The combination of a lightweight coreference-guided sparse attention with a contrastive entity consistency loss is, to our knowledge, unexplored in machine translation and addresses the root linguistic causes of cross-sentence errors rather than relying on brute-force context expansion.

proposals.title: Beyond the Shortlist: Subword-Compositional Neural Machine Translation Without Fixed Vocabulary Boundaries
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation models have historically relied on a fixed shortlist of 30,000–80,000 frequent words, mapping all remaining tokens to a single UNK symbol. This crude truncation discards morphological regularity, fails catastrophically on agglutinative and low-resource languages, and creates an artificial bottleneck that prevents models from leveraging the compositional structure inherent in human language. As translation systems scale to hundreds of language pairs, the limitations of word-level vocabularies become an urgent practical and scientific problem.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing fixed-shortlist word vocabularies with learned subword segmentation units (byte-pair encodings, morpheme-aware tokenizers, or hybrid character-subword models) will yield statistically significant improvements in translation quality—measured by BLEU, chrF, and human evaluation—particularly on morphologically rich languages and rare or unseen words, without increasing model size or training cost.
proposals.method: We propose a controlled experimental framework comparing four vocabulary strategies on identical encoder-decoder architectures with attention: (1) the standard 30K word shortlist with UNK replacement, (2) byte-pair encoding (BPE) with varying merge counts (8K–32K), (3) a morpheme-aware segmentation using Morfessor-derived units, and (4) a hybrid character-subword model that falls back to character-level encoding for out-of-vocabulary segments. All models will be trained on WMT datasets across typologically diverse language pairs (English–German, English–Finnish, English–Turkish, English–Japanese) using matched compute budgets. We will analyze not only aggregate translation quality but also per-token accuracy as a function of word frequency rank, morphological complexity, and compounding depth.
proposals.contribution: This work provides the first systematic, controlled comparison of subword vocabulary strategies against the classical shortlist assumption within a unified neural MT framework, isolating the effect of tokenization from other architectural choices. It establishes empirical scaling laws relating vocabulary granularity to translation quality across language typologies, and offers practical guidelines for vocabulary design in multilingual settings. The results will demonstrate that compositional subword representations are not merely a convenience but a fundamental architectural improvement that better aligns model structure with linguistic reality.
proposals.novelty: While BPE and character-level models have been explored independently, no prior work has rigorously isolated the shortlist assumption itself as the variable under study across multiple segmentation strategies, language families, and frequency strata. By framing tokenization as a testable scientific assumption rather than an engineering default, this work reveals that the UNK-boundary is not an innocuous simplification but a systematic bias that distorts what neural translation models can learn about compositional morphology.

proposals.title: Linguistically-Grounded Multi-View Attention: Disentangling Lexical, Syntactic, and Morphological Context in Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Standard attention-based sequence-to-sequence models compress all source-side evidence into a single context vector per decoding step, forcing the model to merge heterogeneous linguistic signals—lexical choice, syntactic structure, morphological agreement, and long-range dependencies—into one blended representation. This conflation limits interpretability and may degrade translation quality on morphologically rich or syntactically divergent language pairs, where different linguistic dimensions demand distinct weighting patterns over the source. Designing an architecture that maintains multiple concurrent, linguistically-motivated attention views could yield both better translations and deeper insight into what information the decoder actually requires at each generation step.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the single attention context vector with multiple parallel attention heads that are explicitly encouraged—via auxiliary supervision and architectural constraints—to specialize on distinct linguistic dimensions (lexical identity, syntactic constituency, morphological features, and long-distance coreference) will improve translation quality on structurally challenging language pairs and produce attention distributions that are more interpretable and linguistically coherent than unconstrained multi-head attention.
proposals.method: We extend the encoder-decoder attention mechanism by introducing four parallel attention modules, each operating over a shared encoder output but with separate parameterization and a dedicated auxiliary loss that encourages specialization: (1) a lexical view supervised by word-alignment priors from statistical alignment tools, (2) a syntactic view guided by source-side dependency parse distances, (3) a morphological view regularized by subword-level feature agreement signals, and (4) a long-range view biased toward attending to distant positions via a learned relative-position penalty. The four resulting context vectors are combined through a lightweight gating network that learns a step-specific mixture at each decoder time-step. We train on WMT English-German, English-Turkish, and English-Japanese to test across typologically diverse pairs, and compare against standard single-head attention, vanilla multi-head attention (Vaswani et al., 2017), and recent structured-attention baselines. Ablation studies will isolate the contribution of each view and the auxiliary losses.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled architectural framework for multi-view attention that goes beyond the implicit specialization observed in standard multi-head attention by incorporating explicit linguistic inductive biases. It will provide empirical evidence on whether enforced head specialization yields consistent BLEU and human-evaluation gains over unconstrained heads, particularly on morphologically rich and syntactically distant language pairs. Additionally, the interpretability analysis—mapping each head's attention patterns to known linguistic phenomena—will offer concrete guidance for future attention mechanism design and deepen our understanding of what information neural decoders exploit during generation.
proposals.novelty: While multi-head attention allows multiple parallel projections, heads are not encouraged to capture distinct linguistic functions and often converge to redundant patterns. Our approach is non-obvious because it combines linguistically-motivated auxiliary supervision with a learned gating mechanism that dynamically selects among specialized views per decoding step, providing a structured middle ground between fully unconstrained heads and rigid hard-coded linguistic pipelines.

proposals.title: Global Target Planning for Attention: Conditioning Alignment on Sentence-Level Semantic Frames and Syntactic Plans
proposals.motivation: Standard attention mechanisms in sequence-to-sequence models condition alignment scores solely on the previous decoder hidden state and the source annotations, implicitly assuming that local decoding history is sufficient for determining which source positions to attend to. However, translation and generation require global coherence—long-range reordering, consistent lexical choice, and adherence to target-side syntactic structure—that local context cannot reliably capture. Improving alignment with explicit global planning signals could reduce hallucination, improve adequacy in long sentences, and yield more structurally faithful outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: Conditioning the attention mechanism on a learned global target plan—encoding anticipated sentence-level semantic frames, target syntax sketches, and discourse structure—will produce more accurate and globally coherent alignments than attention conditioned only on local decoder state, particularly for long and syntactically divergent sentence pairs.
proposals.method: We propose a two-pass decoder architecture. In the first pass, a lightweight planning network generates a coarse global target plan: a sequence of predicted semantic role labels, target-side dependency arc types, and a sentence-level topic vector, all derived from the source encoding via a structured latent variable model trained with amortized variational inference. In the second pass, the standard autoregressive decoder receives this plan as an additional conditioning signal when computing attention scores—specifically, the plan vector is concatenated with the decoder hidden state before the alignment function is applied. We train end-to-end on parallel corpora (WMT English–German, English–Japanese for high reordering divergence) with an auxiliary loss encouraging the plan to predict oracle target-side dependency parses. We compare against standard Bahdanau attention, Transformer self-attention, and recent deliberation network baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work challenges a foundational assumption of attention-based sequence-to-sequence models: that purely local decoder state suffices for alignment. By demonstrating that explicit global planning signals improve both translation quality (BLEU, COMET) and alignment accuracy (AER on gold alignments), we provide a principled mechanism for injecting top-down structure into neural generation. The framework generalizes beyond translation to any conditional generation task where output coherence depends on long-range structural decisions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on deliberation networks and non-autoregressive models has explored multi-pass decoding, none has explicitly constructed a structured semantic and syntactic plan as a first-class conditioning signal for the attention mechanism itself. Our approach uniquely decomposes the problem: the plan captures what to say and in what structure, while attention guided by this plan determines where to look—bridging the gap between neural attention and classical AI planning for language generation.

proposals.title: Beyond Concatenated BiRNN Annotations: Evaluating Hierarchical, Convolutional, and Graph-Based Source Representations for Attention-Based Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: The seminal attention-based encoder-decoder framework for neural machine translation relies on concatenated forward and backward RNN hidden states as source annotations, an architectural choice that was adopted without systematic comparison against structurally richer alternatives. As source sentences exhibit hierarchical syntactic structure, multi-scale phrase boundaries, and long-range dependencies that sequential BiRNNs may inadequately capture, there is a pressing need to determine whether alternative encoder architectures yield annotations more naturally aligned with the translation units that the attention mechanism must retrieve. Given recent advances in graph neural networks, tree-structured LSTMs, and dilated convolutional encoders, the field now has mature tools to rigorously challenge this foundational assumption.
proposals.hypothesis: Source annotations produced by encoders that explicitly model hierarchical linguistic structure—such as tree-structured LSTMs over constituency parses, graph neural networks over dependency graphs, or multi-scale convolutional encoders—will yield attention distributions more sharply aligned with linguistically meaningful translation units and consequently improve translation quality, particularly for syntactically divergent language pairs and long sentences where BiRNN annotations degrade.
proposals.method: We will implement and compare five encoder architectures within a controlled attention-based NMT framework: (1) the standard BiGRU baseline, (2) a tree-structured LSTM encoder operating over constituency parse trees, (3) a graph neural network encoder over dependency parse graphs using gated message passing, (4) a hierarchical encoder with word-level and phrase-level BiRNNs, and (5) a multi-scale dilated convolutional encoder. All encoders will feed into an identical Bahdanau-style attention decoder to isolate the effect of source representation. We will evaluate on WMT translation tasks across typologically diverse language pairs (English-German, English-Japanese, English-Turkish), measuring BLEU, attention alignment error rate against gold word alignments, and performance stratified by sentence length and syntactic divergence. Probing classifiers on the learned annotations will assess what linguistic information each encoder captures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled, large-scale empirical comparison of structurally diverse source encoders within the attention-based NMT paradigm, producing concrete evidence for or against the BiRNN annotation assumption that has shaped the field since 2014. The analysis will identify which source representation inductive biases best support attention-based retrieval for different linguistic phenomena, offering actionable encoder design principles. The linguistic probing analysis will additionally reveal how encoder architecture shapes the information content of annotations, bridging neural architecture choices with linguistic theory.
proposals.novelty: While individual alternative encoders have been explored in isolation, no prior work has systematically compared BiRNN, tree-structured, graph-based, hierarchical, and convolutional encoders under identical decoder and attention conditions specifically to challenge the foundational annotation assumption. Our combination of translation quality metrics, attention alignment analysis, and linguistic probing provides a uniquely comprehensive evaluation that moves beyond BLEU scores to explain why certain source representations better serve the attention mechanism.

proposals.title: Towards Question Birnn Annotations As The Best Source Representation: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to The encoder assumes concatenated forward and backward recurrent states provide the right annotations. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Question BiRNN annotations as the best source representation can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates assumption challenge principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: The encoder assumes concatenated forward and backward recurrent states provide the right annotations for retrieval. That can be challenged with hierar.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the Question BiRNN annotations as the best source representation problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Sequence-Level Training for Neural Machine Translation via Differentiable Risk Minimization
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation systems are trained to maximize token-level conditional log-likelihood yet evaluated using sequence-level metrics such as BLEU, introducing a fundamental train-test objective mismatch. This discrepancy means the model never directly optimizes the criterion it is judged on, leading to suboptimal translations that may be probable token-by-token yet poor holistically. Closing this gap is critical for deploying NMT systems where fluency, adequacy, and coherence at the sentence level determine real-world utility.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing or augmenting maximum likelihood training with a sequence-level risk minimization objective—where the expected loss under the model's own decoding distribution is directly minimized—will yield significant improvements in translation quality as measured by BLEU and human evaluation, without sacrificing training stability, compared to standard token-level MLE training.
proposals.method: We propose a two-phase training procedure: first pre-train the encoder-decoder model with standard token-level maximum likelihood to obtain a strong initialization, then fine-tune using a minimum risk training (MRT) objective that computes expected sequence-level loss (e.g., sentence-level BLEU or METEOR) over a sampled subset of candidate translations from the model's own distribution. To make this tractable, we employ a stratified sampling strategy that mixes high-probability beam outputs with stochastic samples to reduce variance in the gradient estimator. We further introduce a learned baseline reward network, conditioned on the source sentence, to act as a control variate and stabilize REINFORCE-style gradient updates, drawing on variance reduction techniques from the reinforcement learning literature.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a principled and practical framework for directly optimizing sequence-level translation metrics during NMT training, bridging the long-standing gap between training and evaluation objectives. We expect consistent BLEU improvements of 1-2 points over MLE baselines across multiple language pairs (EN-DE, EN-FR, EN-ZH), along with qualitative improvements in sentence coherence confirmed by human evaluation. The variance-reduced MRT framework we develop will serve as a general recipe applicable beyond translation to any structured prediction task suffering from exposure bias and metric mismatch.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored REINFORCE and minimum risk objectives for sequence models, the combination of stratified candidate sampling with a learned source-conditional baseline reward network for variance reduction in MRT has not been systematically investigated. This approach is non-obvious because it reframes the problem not as a pure reinforcement learning challenge but as a hybrid that leverages the geometry of the MLE-pretrained model's output distribution to make risk minimization both stable and sample-efficient.

proposals.title: Dual Monolingual Pretraining: Joint Source Encoder and Target Decoder Initialization for Low-Resource Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation systems remain heavily dependent on large parallel corpora, which are scarce for the vast majority of language pairs. While pretrained language models have revolutionized NLP, their integration into NMT has focused primarily on encoder-side representations, leaving target-side generation under-explored. A unified pretraining strategy that initializes both the encoder with source-language masked language modeling and the decoder with target-language causal language modeling could unlock significant quality gains, especially in low-resource and domain-adaptation scenarios where parallel data is limited.
proposals.hypothesis: Jointly initializing a sequence-to-sequence translation model with a source-language pretrained encoder and a target-language pretrained decoder, followed by a cross-attention alignment phase on modest parallel data, will yield significantly higher BLEU scores and greater robustness to domain shift compared to training from scratch or using encoder-only pretraining, particularly in low-resource settings below 1 million parallel sentences.
proposals.method: We pretrain a Transformer encoder using masked language modeling on large monolingual source-language corpora and separately pretrain a Transformer decoder using causal language modeling on large monolingual target-language corpora. We then assemble these into a full encoder-decoder architecture, introducing randomly initialized cross-attention layers, and fine-tune in two stages: first a cross-attention warm-up phase on parallel data with frozen encoder and decoder parameters, then full end-to-end fine-tuning. We evaluate across multiple language pairs at varying parallel data scales (10K, 100K, 500K, 1M sentences) and measure BLEU, chrF, and out-of-domain generalization on held-out test sets spanning news, biomedical, and legal domains.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a principled and reproducible framework for leveraging abundant monolingual resources on both sides of the translation pipeline, demonstrating that target-side pretraining yields complementary gains to source-side pretraining. It establishes scaling curves showing at which parallel data thresholds dual pretraining most benefits translation quality, offering practical guidance for low-resource NMT practitioners. The staged fine-tuning protocol for bridging independently pretrained components via cross-attention alignment constitutes a reusable methodological contribution applicable beyond machine translation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work such as XLM and mBART has explored multilingual or denoising pretraining, our approach distinctly decouples source and target monolingual pretraining using language-specific objectives tailored to each component's role—comprehension for the encoder and generation for the decoder—and introduces a novel staged cross-attention warm-up procedure that avoids catastrophic forgetting of pretrained representations during fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Towards Monolingual Pretraining For Encoder And Decoder: A Framework for Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale generative models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities but face fundamental challenges related to The authors note that they do not use monolingual data and suggest that a much larger monolingual co. This work addresses a critical gap in our understanding of how to systematically improve these models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Monolingual pretraining for encoder and decoder can be effectively implemented through structured optimization objectives that better align model training with downstream evaluation criteria.
proposals.method: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates future work principles. Specifically, we leverage neural architecture components with auxiliary regularization losses derived from the core insight: The authors note that they do not use monolingual data and suggest that a much larger monolingual corpus could pretrain an encoder. Extending this ide.
proposals.contribution: This work provides both theoretical foundations and empirical validation of the proposed approach, with experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrating significant improvements over baselines in terms of sample quality and diversity.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work, this approach directly addresses the Monolingual pretraining for encoder and decoder problem by treating it as a structured optimization problem rather than relying on heuristic design choices.

proposals.title: Beyond Monotonic Alignment: Evaluating and Extending Soft Attention for Neural Machine Translation of Morphologically Rich and Syntactically Divergent Language Pairs
proposals.motivation: The seminal work on attention-based neural machine translation demonstrated that soft attention can learn effective word alignments for language pairs like English-French, where word order is largely monotonic with only local reorderings. However, many typologically diverse language pairs—such as English-Japanese, English-Turkish, or English-Hindi—exhibit pervasive long-distance reordering and rich agglutinative or fusional morphology, posing a fundamental challenge to the implicit monotonic bias of standard soft attention. Understanding whether and how soft attention degrades on these harder alignment problems is critical for building truly language-universal translation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that standard soft attention mechanisms suffer systematic alignment degradation—manifesting as lower translation quality and less interpretable attention matrices—on language pairs with heavy long-distance reordering and rich morphology, and that augmenting the attention model with explicit reordering components and morphological decomposition can recover and surpass baseline performance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study across a typological spectrum of language pairs grouped by reordering complexity (monotonic: English-French; moderate: English-German; heavy: English-Japanese, English-Turkish, English-Hindi) using parallel corpora of comparable size. We will implement and compare four attention variants: (1) standard soft attention, (2) local attention with a learned monotonic bias, (3) a novel hierarchical attention mechanism that first attends over morpheme-level subword units and then composes phrase-level alignments, and (4) a hybrid model combining soft attention with an explicit permutation-based reordering module inspired by fertility and distortion concepts from statistical MT. Alignment quality will be measured against gold-standard word alignments (where available) using AER, and translation quality via BLEU, COMET, and human evaluation on syntactically complex constructions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, controlled analysis of how soft attention performance varies as a function of typological distance and reordering complexity, filling a gap left by the original attention paper's focus on English-French. The proposed hierarchical morpheme-phrase attention and hybrid reordering-attention architectures offer concrete, reusable model components for improving NMT on structurally divergent language pairs. The resulting typological difficulty benchmarks and diagnostic test suites for attention-alignment quality will serve as community resources for future NMT research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored local versus global attention or incorporated coverage mechanisms, no study has systematically disentangled the effects of reordering distance and morphological complexity on attention quality across a controlled typological spectrum. The proposed hierarchical morpheme-to-phrase attention mechanism is a novel architectural contribution that explicitly bridges the granularity mismatch between subword tokenization and phrase-level reordering, an issue that existing flat attention architectures ignore.

proposals.title: Differentiable Coverage and Fertility Priors for Soft Attention in Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Soft attention mechanisms in sequence-to-sequence models assign alignment weights at each decoding step independently, lacking any explicit memory of which source positions have already been consumed. This independence assumption leads to systematic pathologies on long and information-dense sentences—repeated translations of salient phrases, omission of low-salience content, and hallucinated output when the decoder loses track of source coverage. These failure modes remain a practical bottleneck for deploying neural machine translation in domains such as legal and biomedical text where faithfulness is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting the standard soft attention mechanism with a differentiable coverage vector that accumulates past attention mass and a learned fertility predictor that estimates the expected number of target words per source token will jointly reduce over-translation, under-translation, and repetition errors, particularly on sentences exceeding 30 tokens, without sacrificing BLEU on shorter inputs.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component extension to the additive attention framework. First, a coverage vector is maintained as a cumulative sum of previous attention distributions and fed as an additional input to the alignment scoring function, penalizing re-attendance through a learned gating mechanism rather than a fixed penalty. Second, a lightweight fertility module—a single-layer feedforward network conditioned on encoder hidden states—predicts a real-valued fertility for each source token; a soft auxiliary loss encourages the total attention received by each source position across all decoding steps to approximate its predicted fertility. Both components are trained end-to-end with the translation objective using standard backpropagation, requiring no discrete marginalization or reinforcement learning.
proposals.contribution: This work provides a principled, fully differentiable integration of two classical statistical MT concepts—coverage and fertility—into the modern attention-based encoder-decoder paradigm. We expect measurable reductions in repetition rate and omission rate on WMT benchmarks for multiple language pairs, with the largest gains on the long-tail of sentence lengths. The approach also yields interpretable diagnostics: the predicted fertility map and residual coverage at end-of-sentence offer direct visualization of where and why the model under- or over-translates.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored coverage penalties as fixed heuristics or fertility as a latent discrete variable requiring variational inference, our approach is the first to unify both mechanisms in a single differentiable framework with learned gating and a soft fertility loss, eliminating the need for approximate inference while preserving the statistical motivation of the original IBM alignment models.

proposals.title: Partially Monotonic Attention for Neural Machine Translation via Adaptive Gaussian Kernels
proposals.motivation: Standard soft attention in sequence-to-sequence translation models attends over all source positions at every decoding step, incurring quadratic cost and ignoring the broadly sequential nature of cross-lingual word order. Graves (2013) demonstrated that monotonic Gaussian mixture attention kernels are highly effective for handwriting synthesis, where alignment is strictly left-to-right, yet translation exhibits a mostly monotonic alignment with structured reorderings. A mechanism that exploits this partial monotonicity would reduce computational cost and improve alignment quality, particularly for morphologically rich and long-distance reordering language pairs.
proposals.hypothesis: An attention mechanism that constrains alignment to advance mostly monotonically via learned Gaussian kernels, while permitting discrete learned jumps at syntactically motivated positions, will match or exceed standard soft attention in translation quality while substantially reducing the effective attention search space and improving decoding speed.
proposals.method: We adapt Graves's location-based Gaussian mixture attention by introducing a partially monotonic formulation: at each decoder step, a controller network predicts (a) a monotonic advance parameter that shifts the Gaussian mean forward, and (b) a binary jump gate trained with the straight-through Gumbel-Softmax estimator that triggers non-local reorderings when syntactic structure demands it. The jump offset is predicted by a small auxiliary network conditioned on the decoder hidden state and a local source context window. We train end-to-end on standard WMT benchmarks (En-De, En-Ja, En-Tr) chosen to span low, medium, and high reordering difficulty, comparing against soft attention, hard monotonic attention (Raffel et al., 2017), and monotonic infinite lookback baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work bridges the gap between strictly monotonic attention models, which sacrifice quality on reordering-heavy pairs, and unconstrained soft attention, which wastes computation on implausible alignments. We expect to demonstrate a 30-50% reduction in effective attention computations with no degradation in BLEU on moderate-reordering pairs and improved BLEU on long sentences. The framework also provides interpretable alignment traces that explicitly mark where and why non-monotonic jumps occur, enabling linguistic analysis of learned reordering patterns.
proposals.novelty: Prior monotonic attention methods enforce a hard left-to-right constraint or relax it uniformly; our approach is the first to combine continuous monotonic advance with discrete, learned jump decisions, directly transferring the generative alignment mechanism from handwriting synthesis into a discriminative translation setting. The explicit factorization of monotonic flow and structured jumps is non-obvious because it requires reconciling continuous location modeling with discrete reordering decisions in a single differentiable framework.

proposals.title: Fertility-Regularized Attention: Incorporating Classical Alignment Fertility Priors into Transformer Attention Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Transformer-based sequence-to-sequence models rely on soft attention distributions that impose no explicit constraint on how many target positions attend to each source token, leading to well-documented pathologies such as over-translation, under-translation, and attention diffusion. Classical statistical MT alignment models (IBM Models 3–5) explicitly parameterize fertility—the number of target words generated per source word—yielding interpretable, linguistically grounded alignment structure. Bridging this gap is timely because modern large-scale models still exhibit these faithfulness failures, and principled structural inductive biases remain underexplored relative to scaling-driven improvements.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting neural attention with learned fertility priors—auxiliary per-source-token distributions over expected target attendance counts derived from the fertility parameterization of IBM Model 3—will reduce over- and under-translation errors and improve alignment interpretability without sacrificing translation quality, by providing a differentiable structural constraint that soft attention currently lacks.
proposals.method: We introduce a Fertility-Regularized Attention (FRA) module that extends standard multi-head attention with a lightweight fertility predictor: a single-layer MLP that, given each source token's encoder representation, outputs a Poisson or categorical distribution over expected target attendance counts. During training, we add a differentiable fertility loss term that penalizes the KL divergence between the predicted fertility distribution and the empirical attention mass (summed across heads and target positions) for each source token. The fertility predictor can optionally be warm-started using fertility tables extracted from GIZA++ alignments on the training bitext. We evaluate on WMT English–German and English–Chinese translation, measuring BLEU, COMET, alignment error rate against gold alignments, and over-/under-translation rates via lexical coverage metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a principled, modular mechanism for injecting classical alignment structure into neural attention that is architecture-agnostic and can be applied to any Transformer encoder-decoder model. It provides the first systematic empirical comparison between implicit neural fertility behavior and explicit classical fertility modeling, offering both quantitative translation improvements and qualitative interpretability gains. The fertility regularizer also opens a pathway toward more controllable generation, where practitioners can adjust fertility priors to handle domain-specific one-to-many or many-to-one translation patterns.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored coverage mechanisms and guided attention for neural MT, no existing approach directly imports the probabilistic fertility parameterization from IBM alignment models as a differentiable training objective. The non-obvious insight is that fertility—a discrete, latent variable in classical models—can be relaxed into a continuous regularizer that complements rather than competes with soft attention, providing structural guidance without imposing hard constraints that would break end-to-end differentiability.

proposals.title: Globally Normalized Attention: CRF-Structured Alignment for Sequence-to-Sequence Models
proposals.motivation: Attention mechanisms in modern encoder-decoder architectures normalize alignment scores independently at each decoder step, treating each alignment decision as locally optimal without regard for global coherence. This leads to well-documented pathologies such as over-translation (repeated coverage of the same source tokens), under-translation (source tokens never attended to), and implausible long-range crossing alignments. Structured prediction methods, particularly conditional random fields (CRFs), have long solved analogous problems in sequence labeling by enforcing global normalization over entire output structures, yet their application to soft attention alignment remains largely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing locally normalized softmax attention with a globally normalized CRF-style alignment layer—parameterized with learned transition potentials that encode coverage, monotonicity, and bounded-crossing constraints—will yield more coherent attention patterns and improve translation quality, particularly on long sentences and morphologically rich language pairs where alignment pathologies are most severe.
proposals.method: We propose a CRF-Attention layer that defines a linear-chain CRF over the sequence of alignment decisions across all decoder time steps. Emission potentials are derived from standard content-based attention scores, while transition potentials are parameterized by learned features capturing coverage counts, positional displacement between consecutive attention peaks, and crossing number. Global normalization is achieved via the forward-backward algorithm, and gradients are computed through automatic differentiation of the log-partition function. To maintain tractability, we employ a bucketed discretization of the source positions and apply beam-level pruning during inference. We evaluate on WMT English-German, English-Romanian, and English-Japanese translation benchmarks, with ablations isolating the contributions of coverage, monotonicity, and crossing constraints, and we compare against fertility-based and guided-alignment baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work bridges the gap between structured prediction and neural attention by introducing a principled, globally normalized alignment framework that replaces ad hoc coverage penalties and guided alignment heuristics with a unified probabilistic model. We expect measurable BLEU improvements on long sentences and distant language pairs, along with qualitatively more interpretable and linguistically plausible attention maps. The CRF-Attention module is architecture-agnostic and can be integrated into any encoder-decoder model, providing a reusable component for tasks beyond machine translation, including speech recognition and document summarization.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs have been combined with neural networks for sequence labeling (e.g., BiLSTM-CRF), applying global normalization directly to the attention alignment distribution across decoder steps is novel. The key non-obvious insight is that attention weights across time steps can be treated as a structured prediction problem with tractable exact inference, rather than as independent categorical distributions, and that learned transition features can softly encode linguistic priors like monotonicity without hard-coding them.

proposals.title: Memory-Augmented Source Attention: Readable and Writable External Memory over Encoder Annotations for Sequence-to-Sequence Models
proposals.motivation: Attention mechanisms in sequence-to-sequence models compute a stateless weighted sum over source annotations at each decoding step, discarding information about which sources have been consumed, revisited, or contradicted.
